reference: WELL NOW ENNIS I DECLARE YOU HAVE A HEAD AND SO HAS MY STICK

reference: HE TRIED TO THINK HOW IT COULD BE

transcript: [' but the dusk deepening in the schoolroom covered over his thoughts the bell rang']
reference: BUT THE DUSK DEEPENING IN THE SCHOOLROOM COVERED OVER HIS THOUGHTS THE BELL RANG

transcript: [' Then you can ask him questions on the catechism dedalus.']
reference: THEN YOU CAN ASK HIM QUESTIONS ON THE CATECHISM DEDALUS

reference: STEPHEN LEANING BACK AND DRAWING IDLY ON HIS SCRIBBLER LISTENED TO THE TALK ABOUT HIM WHICH HERON CHECKED FROM TIME TO TIME BY SAYING

reference: THE SENTENCE OF SAINT JAMES WHICH SAYS THAT HE WHO OFFENDS AGAINST ONE COMMANDMENT BECOMES GUILTY OF ALL HAD SEEMED TO HIM FIRST A SWOLLEN PHRASE UNTIL HE HAD BEGUN TO GROPE IN THE DARKNESS OF HIS OWN STATE

reference: IF A MAN HAD STOLEN A POUND IN HIS YOUTH AND HAD USED THAT POUND TO AMASS A HUGE FORTUNE HOW MUCH WAS HE OBLIGED TO GIVE BACK THE POUND HE HAD STOLEN ONLY OR THE POUND TOGETHER WITH THE COMPOUND INTEREST ACCRUING UPON IT OR ALL HIS HUGE FORTUNE

reference: IF A LAYMAN IN GIVING BAPTISM POUR THE WATER BEFORE SAYING THE WORDS IS THE CHILD BAPTIZED

reference: BEWARE OF MAKING THAT MISTAKE

reference: STEPHEN'S HEART BEGAN SLOWLY TO FOLD AND FADE WITH FEAR LIKE A WITHERING FLOWER

reference: HE IS CALLED AS YOU KNOW THE APOSTLE OF THE INDIES

transcript: [' a great saint, saint Francis Xavier.']
reference: A GREAT SAINT SAINT FRANCIS XAVIER

reference: THE RECTOR PAUSED AND THEN SHAKING HIS CLASPED HANDS BEFORE HIM WENT ON

transcript: [' He had the faith in him that moves mountains.']
reference: HE HAD THE FAITH IN HIM THAT MOVES MOUNTAINS

transcript: [' a great saint, saint Francis Xavier.']
reference: A GREAT SAINT SAINT FRANCIS XAVIER

transcript: [' In the silence their dark fire kindled the dusk into a tawny glow.']
reference: IN THE SILENCE THEIR DARK FIRE KINDLED THE DUSK INTO A TAWNY GLOW

reference: HE COULD WAIT NO LONGER

reference: FOR A FULL HOUR HE HAD PACED UP AND DOWN WAITING BUT HE COULD WAIT NO LONGER

reference: HE SET OFF ABRUPTLY FOR THE BULL WALKING RAPIDLY LEST HIS FATHER'S SHRILL WHISTLE MIGHT CALL HIM BACK AND IN A FEW MOMENTS HE HAD ROUNDED THE CURVE AT THE POLICE BARRACK AND WAS SAFE

transcript: [' The university.']
reference: THE UNIVERSITY

transcript: [' Pride after satisfaction uplifted him like long slow waves.']
reference: PRIDE AFTER SATISFACTION UPLIFTED HIM LIKE LONG SLOW WAVES

transcript: [' Whose feet are as the feet of hearts and underneath the everlasting arms.']
reference: WHOSE FEET ARE AS THE FEET OF HARTS AND UNDERNEATH THE EVERLASTING ARMS

transcript: [' The pride of that dim image brought back to his mind the dignity of the office he had refused.']
reference: THE PRIDE OF THAT DIM IMAGE BROUGHT BACK TO HIS MIND THE DIGNITY OF THE OFFICE HE HAD REFUSED

reference: SOON THE WHOLE BRIDGE WAS TREMBLING AND RESOUNDING

reference: THE UNCOUTH FACES PASSED HIM TWO BY TWO STAINED YELLOW OR RED OR LIVID BY THE SEA AND AS HE STROVE TO LOOK AT THEM WITH EASE AND INDIFFERENCE A FAINT STAIN OF PERSONAL SHAME AND COMMISERATION ROSE TO HIS OWN FACE

transcript: [' Angry with himself, he tried to hide his face from their eyes by gazing down sideways into the shallow swirling water under the bridge, but he still saw a reflection therein of their top heavy silk hats and humble tape like collars and loosely hanging clerical clothes. Brother Hickey!']
reference: ANGRY WITH HIMSELF HE TRIED TO HIDE HIS FACE FROM THEIR EYES BY GAZING DOWN SIDEWAYS INTO THE SHALLOW SWIRLING WATER UNDER THE BRIDGE BUT HE STILL SAW A REFLECTION THEREIN OF THEIR TOP HEAVY SILK HATS AND HUMBLE TAPE LIKE COLLARS AND LOOSELY HANGING CLERICAL CLOTHES BROTHER HICKEY

transcript: [' Brother mc Cardle Brother Keof']
reference: BROTHER MAC ARDLE BROTHER KEOGH

transcript: [' Their piety would be like their names like their faces like their clothes and it was idle for him to tell himself that their humble and contrite hearts it might be paid a far richer tribute of devotion than his had ever been a gift tenfold more acceptable than his elaborate adoration.']
reference: THEIR PIETY WOULD BE LIKE THEIR NAMES LIKE THEIR FACES LIKE THEIR CLOTHES AND IT WAS IDLE FOR HIM TO TELL HIMSELF THAT THEIR HUMBLE AND CONTRITE HEARTS IT MIGHT BE PAID A FAR RICHER TRIBUTE OF DEVOTION THAN HIS HAD EVER BEEN A GIFT TENFOLD MORE ACCEPTABLE THAN HIS ELABORATE ADORATION

reference: IT WAS IDLE FOR HIM TO MOVE HIMSELF TO BE GENEROUS TOWARDS THEM TO TELL HIMSELF THAT IF HE EVER CAME TO THEIR GATES STRIPPED OF HIS PRIDE BEATEN AND IN BEGGAR'S WEEDS THAT THEY WOULD BE GENEROUS TOWARDS HIM LOVING HIM AS THEMSELVES

transcript: [' idle and embittering finally to argue against his own dispassionate certitude that the commandment of love bade us not to love our neighbour as ourselves with the same amount and intensity of love but to love him as ourselves with the same kind of love']
reference: IDLE AND EMBITTERING FINALLY TO ARGUE AGAINST HIS OWN DISPASSIONATE CERTITUDE THAT THE COMMANDMENT OF LOVE BADE US NOT TO LOVE OUR NEIGHBOUR AS OURSELVES WITH THE SAME AMOUNT AND INTENSITY OF LOVE BUT TO LOVE HIM AS OURSELVES WITH THE SAME KIND OF LOVE

reference: THE PHRASE AND THE DAY AND THE SCENE HARMONIZED IN A CHORD

reference: WORDS WAS IT THEIR COLOURS

reference: THEY WERE VOYAGING ACROSS THE DESERTS OF THE SKY A HOST OF NOMADS ON THE MARCH VOYAGING HIGH OVER IRELAND WESTWARD BOUND

reference: THE EUROPE THEY HAD COME FROM LAY OUT THERE BEYOND THE IRISH SEA EUROPE OF STRANGE TONGUES AND VALLEYED AND WOODBEGIRT AND CITADELLED AND OF ENTRENCHED AND MARSHALLED RACES

reference: AGAIN AGAIN

reference: A VOICE FROM BEYOND THE WORLD WAS CALLING

reference: HELLO STEPHANOS HERE COMES THE DEDALUS

reference: THEIR DIVING STONE POISED ON ITS RUDE SUPPORTS AND ROCKING UNDER THEIR PLUNGES AND THE ROUGH HEWN STONES OF THE SLOPING BREAKWATER OVER WHICH THEY SCRAMBLED IN THEIR HORSEPLAY GLEAMED WITH COLD WET LUSTRE

reference: HE STOOD STILL IN DEFERENCE TO THEIR CALLS AND PARRIED THEIR BANTER WITH EASY WORDS

reference: IT WAS A PAIN TO SEE THEM AND A SWORD LIKE PAIN TO SEE THE SIGNS OF ADOLESCENCE THAT MADE REPELLENT THEIR PITIABLE NAKEDNESS

reference: STEPHANOS DEDALOS

reference: A MOMENT BEFORE THE GHOST OF THE ANCIENT KINGDOM OF THE DANES HAD LOOKED FORTH THROUGH THE VESTURE OF THE HAZEWRAPPED CITY

reference: YOU WILL FIND ME CONTINUALLY SPEAKING OF FOUR MEN TITIAN HOLBEIN TURNER AND TINTORET IN ALMOST THE SAME TERMS

transcript: [' They unite every quality and sometimes you will find me referring to them as colorists sometimes as chiaroscurists.']
reference: THEY UNITE EVERY QUALITY AND SOMETIMES YOU WILL FIND ME REFERRING TO THEM AS COLORISTS SOMETIMES AS CHIAROSCURISTS

reference: BY BEING STUDIOUS OF COLOR THEY ARE STUDIOUS OF DIVISION AND WHILE THE CHIAROSCURIST DEVOTES HIMSELF TO THE REPRESENTATION OF DEGREES OF FORCE IN ONE THING UNSEPARATED LIGHT THE COLORISTS HAVE FOR THEIR FUNCTION THE ATTAINMENT OF BEAUTY BY ARRANGEMENT OF THE DIVISIONS OF LIGHT

transcript: [' My first and principal reason was that they enforced beyond all resistance on any student who might attempt to copy them this method of laying portions of distinct hue side by side.']
reference: MY FIRST AND PRINCIPAL REASON WAS THAT THEY ENFORCED BEYOND ALL RESISTANCE ON ANY STUDENT WHO MIGHT ATTEMPT TO COPY THEM THIS METHOD OF LAYING PORTIONS OF DISTINCT HUE SIDE BY SIDE

reference: SOME OF THE TOUCHES INDEED WHEN THE TINT HAS BEEN MIXED WITH MUCH WATER HAVE BEEN LAID IN LITTLE DROPS OR PONDS SO THAT THE PIGMENT MIGHT CRYSTALLIZE HARD AT THE EDGE

reference: IT IS THE HEAD OF A PARROT WITH A LITTLE FLOWER IN HIS BEAK FROM A PICTURE OF CARPACCIO'S ONE OF HIS SERIES OF THE LIFE OF SAINT GEORGE

transcript: [' Then he comes to the beak of it.']
reference: THEN HE COMES TO THE BEAK OF IT

transcript: [' The brown ground beneath is left for the most part one touch of black is put for the hollow two delicate lines of dark gray define the outer curve and one little quivering touch of white draws the inner edge of the mandible.']
reference: THE BROWN GROUND BENEATH IS LEFT FOR THE MOST PART ONE TOUCH OF BLACK IS PUT FOR THE HOLLOW TWO DELICATE LINES OF DARK GRAY DEFINE THE OUTER CURVE AND ONE LITTLE QUIVERING TOUCH OF WHITE DRAWS THE INNER EDGE OF THE MANDIBLE

transcript: [' For believe me the final philosophy of art can only ratify their opinion that the beauty of a cock robin is to be red and of a grass plot to be green, and the best skill of art is in instantly seizing on the manifold deliciousness of light which you can only seize by precision of instantaneous touch.']
reference: FOR BELIEVE ME THE FINAL PHILOSOPHY OF ART CAN ONLY RATIFY THEIR OPINION THAT THE BEAUTY OF A COCK ROBIN IS TO BE RED AND OF A GRASS PLOT TO BE GREEN AND THE BEST SKILL OF ART IS IN INSTANTLY SEIZING ON THE MANIFOLD DELICIOUSNESS OF LIGHT WHICH YOU CAN ONLY SEIZE BY PRECISION OF INSTANTANEOUS TOUCH

transcript: [' Now you will see in these studies that the moment the white is enclosed properly and harmonized with the other hues it becomes somehow more precious and pearly than the white paper and that I am not afraid to leave a whole field of untreated white paper all round it being sure that even the little diamonds in the round window will tell as jewels if they are gradated justly.']
reference: NOW YOU WILL SEE IN THESE STUDIES THAT THE MOMENT THE WHITE IS INCLOSED PROPERLY AND HARMONIZED WITH THE OTHER HUES IT BECOMES SOMEHOW MORE PRECIOUS AND PEARLY THAN THE WHITE PAPER AND THAT I AM NOT AFRAID TO LEAVE A WHOLE FIELD OF UNTREATED WHITE PAPER ALL ROUND IT BEING SURE THAT EVEN THE LITTLE DIAMONDS IN THE ROUND WINDOW WILL TELL AS JEWELS IF THEY ARE GRADATED JUSTLY

transcript: [' But in this vignette copied from Turner, you have the two principles brought out perfectly.']
reference: BUT IN THIS VIGNETTE COPIED FROM TURNER YOU HAVE THE TWO PRINCIPLES BROUGHT OUT PERFECTLY

transcript: [' They are beyond all other works that I know existing dependent for their effect on low subdued tones their favorite choice in time of day being either dawn or twilight and even their brightest sunsets produced chiefly out of gray paper.']
reference: THEY ARE BEYOND ALL OTHER WORKS THAT I KNOW EXISTING DEPENDENT FOR THEIR EFFECT ON LOW SUBDUED TONES THEIR FAVORITE CHOICE IN TIME OF DAY BEING EITHER DAWN OR TWILIGHT AND EVEN THEIR BRIGHTEST SUNSETS PRODUCED CHIEFLY OUT OF GRAY PAPER

transcript: [' It may be that a great colorist will use his utmost force of color as a singer his full power of voice but loud or low the virtue is in both cases always in refinement never in loudness.']
reference: IT MAY BE THAT A GREAT COLORIST WILL USE HIS UTMOST FORCE OF COLOR AS A SINGER HIS FULL POWER OF VOICE BUT LOUD OR LOW THE VIRTUE IS IN BOTH CASES ALWAYS IN REFINEMENT NEVER IN LOUDNESS

transcript: [' It must remember be one or the other.']
reference: IT MUST REMEMBER BE ONE OR THE OTHER

transcript: [' Do not therefore think that the gothic school is an easy one.']
reference: DO NOT THEREFORE THINK THAT THE GOTHIC SCHOOL IS AN EASY ONE

transcript: [' The law of that school is that everything shall be seen clearly or at least only in such mist or faintness as shall be delightful and I have no doubt that the best introduction to it would be the elementary practice of painting every study on a golden ground.']
reference: THE LAW OF THAT SCHOOL IS THAT EVERYTHING SHALL BE SEEN CLEARLY OR AT LEAST ONLY IN SUCH MIST OR FAINTNESS AS SHALL BE DELIGHTFUL AND I HAVE NO DOUBT THAT THE BEST INTRODUCTION TO IT WOULD BE THE ELEMENTARY PRACTICE OF PAINTING EVERY STUDY ON A GOLDEN GROUND

transcript: [' This at once compels you to understand that the work is to be imaginative and decorative that it represents beautiful things in the clearest way but not under existing conditions and that in fact you are producing jewelers work rather than pictures.']
reference: THIS AT ONCE COMPELS YOU TO UNDERSTAND THAT THE WORK IS TO BE IMAGINATIVE AND DECORATIVE THAT IT REPRESENTS BEAUTIFUL THINGS IN THE CLEAREST WAY BUT NOT UNDER EXISTING CONDITIONS AND THAT IN FACT YOU ARE PRODUCING JEWELER'S WORK RATHER THAN PICTURES

transcript: [' That a style is restrained or severe does not mean that it is also erroneous.']
reference: THAT A STYLE IS RESTRAINED OR SEVERE DOES NOT MEAN THAT IT IS ALSO ERRONEOUS

transcript: [' In all early gothic art indeed you will find failures of this kind especially distortion and rigidity which are in many respects painfully to be compared with the splendid repose of classic art.']
reference: IN ALL EARLY GOTHIC ART INDEED YOU WILL FIND FAILURE OF THIS KIND ESPECIALLY DISTORTION AND RIGIDITY WHICH ARE IN MANY RESPECTS PAINFULLY TO BE COMPARED WITH THE SPLENDID REPOSE OF CLASSIC ART

transcript: [' The large letter contains indeed entirely feeble and ill drawn figures that is merely childish and failing work of an inferior hand it is not characteristic of gothic or any other school.']
reference: THE LARGE LETTER CONTAINS INDEED ENTIRELY FEEBLE AND ILL DRAWN FIGURES THAT IS MERELY CHILDISH AND FAILING WORK OF AN INFERIOR HAND IT IS NOT CHARACTERISTIC OF GOTHIC OR ANY OTHER SCHOOL

transcript: [' But observe, you can only do this on one condition, that of striving also to create in reality the beauty which you seek in imagination.']
reference: BUT OBSERVE YOU CAN ONLY DO THIS ON ONE CONDITION THAT OF STRIVING ALSO TO CREATE IN REALITY THE BEAUTY WHICH YOU SEEK IN IMAGINATION

transcript: [' It will be wholly impossible for you to retain the tranquillity of temper and felicity of faith necessary for noble purist painting unless you are actively engaged in promoting the felicity and peace of practical life.']
reference: IT WILL BE WHOLLY IMPOSSIBLE FOR YOU TO RETAIN THE TRANQUILLITY OF TEMPER AND FELICITY OF FAITH NECESSARY FOR NOBLE PURIST PAINTING UNLESS YOU ARE ACTIVELY ENGAGED IN PROMOTING THE FELICITY AND PEACE OF PRACTICAL LIFE

transcript: [' You must look him in the face fight him conquer him with what scathe you may. You need not think to keep out of the way of him.']
reference: YOU MUST LOOK AT HIM IN THE FACE FIGHT HIM CONQUER HIM WITH WHAT SCATHE YOU MAY YOU NEED NOT THINK TO KEEP OUT OF THE WAY OF HIM

transcript: [' The colorist says, first of all, as my delicious perroquet was ruby, so this nasty viper shall be black. And then is the question, can I round him off, even though he is black, and make him slimy, and yet springy and close down, clotted like a pool of black blood on the earth, all the same?']
reference: THE COLORIST SAYS FIRST OF ALL AS MY DELICIOUS PAROQUET WAS RUBY SO THIS NASTY VIPER SHALL BE BLACK AND THEN IS THE QUESTION CAN I ROUND HIM OFF EVEN THOUGH HE IS BLACK AND MAKE HIM SLIMY AND YET SPRINGY AND CLOSE DOWN CLOTTED LIKE A POOL OF BLACK BLOOD ON THE EARTH ALL THE SAME

transcript: [' Nothing will be more precious to you, I think, in the practical study of art than the conviction which will force itself on you more and more every hour of the way all things are bound together little and great in spirit and in matter.']
reference: NOTHING WILL BE MORE PRECIOUS TO YOU I THINK IN THE PRACTICAL STUDY OF ART THAN THE CONVICTION WHICH WILL FORCE ITSELF ON YOU MORE AND MORE EVERY HOUR OF THE WAY ALL THINGS ARE BOUND TOGETHER LITTLE AND GREAT IN SPIRIT AND IN MATTER

transcript: [' You know I have just been telling you how this school of materialism in clay involved itself at last in cloud and fire.']
reference: YOU KNOW I HAVE JUST BEEN TELLING YOU HOW THIS SCHOOL OF MATERIALISM AND CLAY INVOLVED ITSELF AT LAST IN CLOUD AND FIRE

transcript: [" Here is an equally typical Greek school landscape by Wilson lost wholly in golden mist the trees so slightly drawn that you don't know if they are trees or towers and no care for color whatsoever perfectly deceptive and marvelous effect of sunshine through the mist Apollo and the python"]
reference: HERE IS AN EQUALLY TYPICAL GREEK SCHOOL LANDSCAPE BY WILSON LOST WHOLLY IN GOLDEN MIST THE TREES SO SLIGHTLY DRAWN THAT YOU DON'T KNOW IF THEY ARE TREES OR TOWERS AND NO CARE FOR COLOR WHATEVER PERFECTLY DECEPTIVE AND MARVELOUS EFFECT OF SUNSHINE THROUGH THE MIST APOLLO AND THE PYTHON

transcript: [' Now here is Raphael exactly between the two trees still drawn leaf by leaf wholly formal but beautiful mist coming gradually into the distance.']
reference: NOW HERE IS RAPHAEL EXACTLY BETWEEN THE TWO TREES STILL DRAWN LEAF BY LEAF WHOLLY FORMAL BUT BEAUTIFUL MIST COMING GRADUALLY INTO THE DISTANCE

transcript: [" Well then, last, here is Turner's Greek school of the highest class, and you define his art absolutely as first the displaying intensely and with the sternest intellect of natural form as it is, and then the envelopment of it with cloud and fire."]
reference: WELL THEN LAST HERE IS TURNER'S GREEK SCHOOL OF THE HIGHEST CLASS AND YOU DEFINE HIS ART ABSOLUTELY AS FIRST THE DISPLAYING INTENSELY AND WITH THE STERNEST INTELLECT OF NATURAL FORM AS IT IS AND THEN THE ENVELOPMENT OF IT WITH CLOUD AND FIRE

transcript: [' Only, there are two sorts of cloud and fire.']
reference: ONLY THERE ARE TWO SORTS OF CLOUD AND FIRE

transcript: [' He knows them both.']
reference: HE KNOWS THEM BOTH

transcript: [" There's one and there's another, the Dudley and the Flint."]
reference: THERE'S ONE AND THERE'S ANOTHER THE DUDLEY AND THE FLINT

reference: IT IS ONLY A PENCIL OUTLINE BY EDWARD BURNE JONES IN ILLUSTRATION OF THE STORY OF PSYCHE IT IS THE INTRODUCTION OF PSYCHE AFTER ALL HER TROUBLES INTO HEAVEN

transcript: [' Every plant in the grass is set formally, grows perfectly, and may be realized completely.']
reference: EVERY PLANT IN THE GRASS IS SET FORMALLY GROWS PERFECTLY AND MAY BE REALIZED COMPLETELY

transcript: [' Exquisite order and universal with eternal life and light, this is the faith and effort of the schools of crystal. And you may describe and complete their work quite literally by taking any verses of Chaucer in his tender mood and observing how he insists on the clearness and brightness first and then on the order.']
reference: EXQUISITE ORDER AND UNIVERSAL WITH ETERNAL LIFE AND LIGHT THIS IS THE FAITH AND EFFORT OF THE SCHOOLS OF CRYSTAL AND YOU MAY DESCRIBE AND COMPLETE THEIR WORK QUITE LITERALLY BY TAKING ANY VERSES OF CHAUCER IN HIS TENDER MOOD AND OBSERVING HOW HE INSISTS ON THE CLEARNESS AND BRIGHTNESS FIRST AND THEN ON THE ORDER

transcript: [" Thus in Chaucer's dream,"]
reference: THUS IN CHAUCER'S DREAM

transcript: [' In both these high mythical subjects the surrounding nature, though suffering, is still dignified and beautiful.']
reference: IN BOTH THESE HIGH MYTHICAL SUBJECTS THE SURROUNDING NATURE THOUGH SUFFERING IS STILL DIGNIFIED AND BEAUTIFUL

transcript: [' Every line in which the master traces it, even where seemingly negligent, is lovely and set down with a meditative calmness which makes these two etchings capable of being placed beside the most tranquil work of Holbein or durer.']
reference: EVERY LINE IN WHICH THE MASTER TRACES IT EVEN WHERE SEEMINGLY NEGLIGENT IS LOVELY AND SET DOWN WITH A MEDITATIVE CALMNESS WHICH MAKES THESE TWO ETCHINGS CAPABLE OF BEING PLACED BESIDE THE MOST TRANQUIL WORK OF HOLBEIN OR DUERER

reference: WITH MANY VARIATIONS SUGGESTED BY THE NATURE OF HIS BUILDING MATERIALS DIVERSITY OF CLIMATE AND A DIFFERENT MODE OF SOCIAL LIFE GOVERNOR BELLINGHAM HAD PLANNED HIS NEW HABITATION AFTER THE RESIDENCES OF GENTLEMEN OF FAIR ESTATE IN HIS NATIVE LAND

transcript: [' On the table in token that the sentiment of old English hospitality had not been left behind stood a large pewter tankard at the bottom of which had hester or pearl peeped into it they might have seen the frothy remnant of a recent draught of ale.']
reference: ON THE TABLE IN TOKEN THAT THE SENTIMENT OF OLD ENGLISH HOSPITALITY HAD NOT BEEN LEFT BEHIND STOOD A LARGE PEWTER TANKARD AT THE BOTTOM OF WHICH HAD HESTER OR PEARL PEEPED INTO IT THEY MIGHT HAVE SEEN THE FROTHY REMNANT OF A RECENT DRAUGHT OF ALE

transcript: [' Little Pearl who was as greatly pleased with the gleaming armor as she had been with the glittering frontispiece of the house spent some time looking into the polished mirror of the breastplate']
reference: LITTLE PEARL WHO WAS AS GREATLY PLEASED WITH THE GLEAMING ARMOUR AS SHE HAD BEEN WITH THE GLITTERING FRONTISPIECE OF THE HOUSE SPENT SOME TIME LOOKING INTO THE POLISHED MIRROR OF THE BREASTPLATE

transcript: [' mother cried she, I see you here. Look, look.']
reference: MOTHER CRIED SHE I SEE YOU HERE LOOK LOOK

transcript: [' In truth, she seemed absolutely hidden behind it.']
reference: IN TRUTH SHE SEEMED ABSOLUTELY HIDDEN BEHIND IT

transcript: [' Pearl accordingly ran to the bow window at the further end of the hall and looked along the vista of a garden walk carpeted with closely shaven grass and bordered with some rude and immature attempt at shrubbery.']
reference: PEARL ACCORDINGLY RAN TO THE BOW WINDOW AT THE FURTHER END OF THE HALL AND LOOKED ALONG THE VISTA OF A GARDEN WALK CARPETED WITH CLOSELY SHAVEN GRASS AND BORDERED WITH SOME RUDE AND IMMATURE ATTEMPT AT SHRUBBERY

transcript: [' But the proprietor appeared already to have relinquished as hopeless the effort to perpetuate on this side of the Atlantic in a hard soil and amid the close struggle for subsistence the native English taste for ornamental gardening.']
reference: BUT THE PROPRIETOR APPEARED ALREADY TO HAVE RELINQUISHED AS HOPELESS THE EFFORT TO PERPETUATE ON THIS SIDE OF THE ATLANTIC IN A HARD SOIL AND AMID THE CLOSE STRUGGLE FOR SUBSISTENCE THE NATIVE ENGLISH TASTE FOR ORNAMENTAL GARDENING

transcript: [' There were a few rose bushes, however, and a number of apple trees probably the descendants of those planted by the reverend mister Blackstone, the first settler of the peninsula, that half mythological personage who rides through our early annals seated on the back of a bull.']
reference: THERE WERE A FEW ROSE BUSHES HOWEVER AND A NUMBER OF APPLE TREES PROBABLY THE DESCENDANTS OF THOSE PLANTED BY THE REVEREND MISTER BLACKSTONE THE FIRST SETTLER OF THE PENINSULA THAT HALF MYTHOLOGICAL PERSONAGE WHO RIDES THROUGH OUR EARLY ANNALS SEATED ON THE BACK OF A BULL

transcript: [' Pearl seeing the rose bushes began to cry for a red rose and would not be pacified.']
reference: PEARL SEEING THE ROSE BUSHES BEGAN TO CRY FOR A RED ROSE AND WOULD NOT BE PACIFIED

transcript: [' He wore blue silk stockings, blue knee pants with gold buckles, a blue ruffled waist and a jacket of bright blue braided with gold.']
reference: HE WORE BLUE SILK STOCKINGS BLUE KNEE PANTS WITH GOLD BUCKLES A BLUE RUFFLED WAIST AND A JACKET OF BRIGHT BLUE BRAIDED WITH GOLD

transcript: [' His hat had a peaked crown and a flat brim and around the brim was a row of tiny golden bells that tinkled when he moved.']
reference: HIS HAT HAD A PEAKED CROWN AND A FLAT BRIM AND AROUND THE BRIM WAS A ROW OF TINY GOLDEN BELLS THAT TINKLED WHEN HE MOVED

transcript: [' Instead of shoes the old man wore boots with turnover tops and his blue coat had wide cuffs of gold braid.']
reference: INSTEAD OF SHOES THE OLD MAN WORE BOOTS WITH TURNOVER TOPS AND HIS BLUE COAT HAD WIDE CUFFS OF GOLD BRAID

transcript: [' For a long time he had wished to explore the beautiful land of oz in which they lived.']
reference: FOR A LONG TIME HE HAD WISHED TO EXPLORE THE BEAUTIFUL LAND OF OZ IN WHICH THEY LIVED

transcript: [' When they were outside unc simply latched the door and started up the path.']
reference: WHEN THEY WERE OUTSIDE UNC SIMPLY LATCHED THE DOOR AND STARTED UP THE PATH

transcript: [' No one would disturb their little house even if any one came so far into the thick forest while they were gone.']
reference: NO ONE WOULD DISTURB THEIR LITTLE HOUSE EVEN IF ANYONE CAME SO FAR INTO THE THICK FOREST WHILE THEY WERE GONE

transcript: [' At the foot of the mountain that separated the country of the munchkins from the country of the gillikins, the path divided.']
reference: AT THE FOOT OF THE MOUNTAIN THAT SEPARATED THE COUNTRY OF THE MUNCHKINS FROM THE COUNTRY OF THE GILLIKINS THE PATH DIVIDED

transcript: [' He knew it would take them to the house of the crooked magician whom he had never seen but who was their nearest neighbor.']
reference: HE KNEW IT WOULD TAKE THEM TO THE HOUSE OF THE CROOKED MAGICIAN WHOM HE HAD NEVER SEEN BUT WHO WAS THEIR NEAREST NEIGHBOR

transcript: [' All the morning they trudged up the mountain path and at noon unc and ojo sat on a fallen tree trunk and ate the last of the bread which the old munchkin had placed in his pocket.']
reference: ALL THE MORNING THEY TRUDGED UP THE MOUNTAIN PATH AND AT NOON UNC AND OJO SAT ON A FALLEN TREE TRUNK AND ATE THE LAST OF THE BREAD WHICH THE OLD MUNCHKIN HAD PLACED IN HIS POCKET

transcript: [' Then they started on again and two hours later came in sight of the house of doctor Pipt.']
reference: THEN THEY STARTED ON AGAIN AND TWO HOURS LATER CAME IN SIGHT OF THE HOUSE OF DOCTOR PIPT

transcript: [' Unk knocked at the door of the house and a chubby pleasant faced woman dressed all in blue opened it and greeted the visitors with a smile']
reference: UNC KNOCKED AT THE DOOR OF THE HOUSE AND A CHUBBY PLEASANT FACED WOMAN DRESSED ALL IN BLUE OPENED IT AND GREETED THE VISITORS WITH A SMILE

transcript: [' I am, my dear, and all strangers are welcome to my home.']
reference: I AM MY DEAR AND ALL STRANGERS ARE WELCOME TO MY HOME

transcript: [' We have come from a far lonelier place than this a lonelier place?']
reference: WE HAVE COME FROM A FAR LONELIER PLACE THAN THIS A LONELIER PLACE

transcript: [' And you must be ojo the Unlucky she added.']
reference: AND YOU MUST BE OJO THE UNLUCKY SHE ADDED

transcript: [' Ojo had never eaten such a fine meal in all his life.']
reference: OJO HAD NEVER EATEN SUCH A FINE MEAL IN ALL HIS LIFE

transcript: [' We are traveling replied ojo and we stopped at your house just to rest and refresh ourselves']
reference: WE ARE TRAVELING REPLIED OJO AND WE STOPPED AT YOUR HOUSE JUST TO REST AND REFRESH OURSELVES

transcript: [' The woman seemed thoughtful.']
reference: THE WOMAN SEEMED THOUGHTFUL

transcript: [' At one end stood a great fireplace in which a blue log was blazing with a blue flame and over the fire hung four kettles in a row all bubbling and steaming at a great rate.']
reference: AT ONE END STOOD A GREAT FIREPLACE IN WHICH A BLUE LOG WAS BLAZING WITH A BLUE FLAME AND OVER THE FIRE HUNG FOUR KETTLES IN A ROW ALL BUBBLING AND STEAMING AT A GREAT RATE

transcript: [' It takes me several years to make this magic powder, but at this moment I am pleased to say it is nearly done. You see, I am making it for my good wife Margolotte, who wants to use some of it for a purpose of her own.']
reference: IT TAKES ME SEVERAL YEARS TO MAKE THIS MAGIC POWDER BUT AT THIS MOMENT I AM PLEASED TO SAY IT IS NEARLY DONE YOU SEE I AM MAKING IT FOR MY GOOD WIFE MARGOLOTTE WHO WANTS TO USE SOME OF IT FOR A PURPOSE OF HER OWN

transcript: [' You must know said Margalot when they were all seated together on the broad window seat that my husband foolishly gave away all the powder of life he first made to old Mombi the witch who used to live in the country of the Gillikins to the north of here.']
reference: YOU MUST KNOW SAID MARGOLOTTE WHEN THEY WERE ALL SEATED TOGETHER ON THE BROAD WINDOW SEAT THAT MY HUSBAND FOOLISHLY GAVE AWAY ALL THE POWDER OF LIFE HE FIRST MADE TO OLD MOMBI THE WITCH WHO USED TO LIVE IN THE COUNTRY OF THE GILLIKINS TO THE NORTH OF HERE

transcript: [' The first lot we tested on our glass cat, which not only began to live but has lived ever since.']
reference: THE FIRST LOT WE TESTED ON OUR GLASS CAT WHICH NOT ONLY BEGAN TO LIVE BUT HAS LIVED EVER SINCE

transcript: [' I think the next glass cat the magician makes will have neither brains nor heart for then it will not object to catching mice and may prove of some use to us.']
reference: I THINK THE NEXT GLASS CAT THE MAGICIAN MAKES WILL HAVE NEITHER BRAINS NOR HEART FOR THEN IT WILL NOT OBJECT TO CATCHING MICE AND MAY PROVE OF SOME USE TO US

transcript: [" I'm afraid I don't know much about the land of oz."]
reference: I'M AFRAID I DON'T KNOW MUCH ABOUT THE LAND OF OZ

transcript: [" You see, I've lived all my life with unc nunkie the Silent One and there was no one to tell me anything."]
reference: YOU SEE I'VE LIVED ALL MY LIFE WITH UNC NUNKIE THE SILENT ONE AND THERE WAS NO ONE TO TELL ME ANYTHING

transcript: [' That is one reason you are ojo the unlucky said the woman in a sympathetic tone.']
reference: THAT IS ONE REASON YOU ARE OJO THE UNLUCKY SAID THE WOMAN IN A SYMPATHETIC TONE

transcript: [" I think I must show you my patchwork girl said Margolotte laughing at the boy's astonishment for she is rather difficult to explain."]
reference: I THINK I MUST SHOW YOU MY PATCHWORK GIRL SAID MARGOLOTTE LAUGHING AT THE BOY'S ASTONISHMENT FOR SHE IS RATHER DIFFICULT TO EXPLAIN

transcript: [' But first I will tell you that for many years I have longed for a servant to help me with the housework and to cook the meals and wash the dishes.']
reference: BUT FIRST I WILL TELL YOU THAT FOR MANY YEARS I HAVE LONGED FOR A SERVANT TO HELP ME WITH THE HOUSEWORK AND TO COOK THE MEALS AND WASH THE DISHES

transcript: [' Yet that task was not so easy as you may suppose.']
reference: YET THAT TASK WAS NOT SO EASY AS YOU MAY SUPPOSE

transcript: [' a bed quilt made of patches of different kinds and colors of cloth all neatly sewed together']
reference: A BED QUILT MADE OF PATCHES OF DIFFERENT KINDS AND COLORS OF CLOTH ALL NEATLY SEWED TOGETHER

transcript: [' Sometimes it is called a crazy quilt because the patches and colors are so mixed up.']
reference: SOMETIMES IT IS CALLED A CRAZY QUILT BECAUSE THE PATCHES AND COLORS ARE SO MIXED UP

transcript: [' When I found it, I said to myself that it would do nicely for my servant girl for when she was brought to life she would not be proud nor haughty as the glass cat is, for such a dreadful mixture of colors would discourage her from trying to be as dignified as the blue munchkins are.']
reference: WHEN I FOUND IT I SAID TO MYSELF THAT IT WOULD DO NICELY FOR MY SERVANT GIRL FOR WHEN SHE WAS BROUGHT TO LIFE SHE WOULD NOT BE PROUD NOR HAUGHTY AS THE GLASS CAT IS FOR SUCH A DREADFUL MIXTURE OF COLORS WOULD DISCOURAGE HER FROM TRYING TO BE AS DIGNIFIED AS THE BLUE MUNCHKINS ARE

transcript: [' At the Emerald City, where our Princess Ozma lives, green is the popular color.']
reference: AT THE EMERALD CITY WHERE OUR PRINCESS OZMA LIVES GREEN IS THE POPULAR COLOR

transcript: [' I will show you what a good job I did. And she went to a tall cupboard and threw open the doors.']
reference: I WILL SHOW YOU WHAT A GOOD JOB I DID AND SHE WENT TO A TALL CUPBOARD AND THREW OPEN THE DOORS

transcript: [' Ojo examined this curious contrivance with wonder.']
reference: OJO EXAMINED THIS CURIOUS CONTRIVANCE WITH WONDER

transcript: [" Margolotte had first made the girl's form from the patchwork quilt and then she had dressed it with a patchwork skirt and an apron with pockets in it using the same gay material throughout."]
reference: MARGOLOTTE HAD FIRST MADE THE GIRL'S FORM FROM THE PATCHWORK QUILT AND THEN SHE HAD DRESSED IT WITH A PATCHWORK SKIRT AND AN APRON WITH POCKETS IN IT USING THE SAME GAY MATERIAL THROUGHOUT

transcript: [' The head of the Patchwork Girl was the most curious part of her.']
reference: THE HEAD OF THE PATCHWORK GIRL WAS THE MOST CURIOUS PART OF HER

transcript: [' The hair was of brown yarn and hung down on her neck in several neat braids.']
reference: THE HAIR WAS OF BROWN YARN AND HUNG DOWN ON HER NECK IN SEVERAL NEAT BRAIDS

transcript: [' Gold is the most common metal in the Land of Oz and is used for many purposes because it is soft and pliable.']
reference: GOLD IS THE MOST COMMON METAL IN THE LAND OF OZ AND IS USED FOR MANY PURPOSES BECAUSE IT IS SOFT AND PLIABLE

transcript: [' No, I forgot all about the brains exclaimed the woman.']
reference: NO I FORGOT ALL ABOUT THE BRAINS EXCLAIMED THE WOMAN

transcript: [' Well, that may be true agreed Margolotte, but on the contrary a servant with too much brains is sure to become independent and high and mighty and feel above her work.']
reference: WELL THAT MAY BE TRUE AGREED MARGOLOTTE BUT ON THE CONTRARY A SERVANT WITH TOO MUCH BRAINS IS SURE TO BECOME INDEPENDENT AND HIGH AND MIGHTY AND FEEL ABOVE HER WORK

transcript: [' She poured into the dish a quantity from each of these bottles.']
reference: SHE POURED INTO THE DISH A QUANTITY FROM EACH OF THESE BOTTLES

transcript: [' I think that will do, she continued, for the other qualities are not needed in a servant.']
reference: I THINK THAT WILL DO SHE CONTINUED FOR THE OTHER QUALITIES ARE NOT NEEDED IN A SERVANT

transcript: [" She ran to her husband's side at once and helped him lift the four kettles from the fire."]
reference: SHE RAN TO HER HUSBAND'S SIDE AT ONCE AND HELPED HIM LIFT THE FOUR KETTLES FROM THE FIRE

transcript: [' Their contents had all boiled away leaving in the bottom of each kettle a few grains of fine white powder.']
reference: THEIR CONTENTS HAD ALL BOILED AWAY LEAVING IN THE BOTTOM OF EACH KETTLE A FEW GRAINS OF FINE WHITE POWDER

transcript: [' Very carefully the magician removed this powder placing it all together in a golden dish where he mixed it with a golden spoon.']
reference: VERY CAREFULLY THE MAGICIAN REMOVED THIS POWDER PLACING IT ALL TOGETHER IN A GOLDEN DISH WHERE HE MIXED IT WITH A GOLDEN SPOON

transcript: [' No one saw him do this, for all were looking at the powder of life, but soon the woman remembered what she had been doing and came back to the cupboard.']
reference: NO ONE SAW HIM DO THIS FOR ALL WERE LOOKING AT THE POWDER OF LIFE BUT SOON THE WOMAN REMEMBERED WHAT SHE HAD BEEN DOING AND CAME BACK TO THE CUPBOARD

transcript: [' Ojo became a bit uneasy at this for he had already put quite a lot of the cleverness powder in the dish but he dared not interfere and so he comforted himself with the thought that one cannot have too much cleverness']
reference: OJO BECAME A BIT UNEASY AT THIS FOR HE HAD ALREADY PUT QUITE A LOT OF THE CLEVERNESS POWDER IN THE DISH BUT HE DARED NOT INTERFERE AND SO HE COMFORTED HIMSELF WITH THE THOUGHT THAT ONE CANNOT HAVE TOO MUCH CLEVERNESS

transcript: [' He selected a small gold bottle with a pepper box top so that the powder might be sprinkled on any object through the small holes.']
reference: HE SELECTED A SMALL GOLD BOTTLE WITH A PEPPER BOX TOP SO THAT THE POWDER MIGHT BE SPRINKLED ON ANY OBJECT THROUGH THE SMALL HOLES

transcript: [' Most people talk too much, so it is a relief to find one who talks too little.']
reference: MOST PEOPLE TALK TOO MUCH SO IT IS A RELIEF TO FIND ONE WHO TALKS TOO LITTLE

transcript: [' I am not allowed to perform magic except for my own amusement, he told his visitors as he lighted a pipe with a crooked stem and began to smoke.']
reference: I AM NOT ALLOWED TO PERFORM MAGIC EXCEPT FOR MY OWN AMUSEMENT HE TOLD HIS VISITORS AS HE LIGHTED A PIPE WITH A CROOKED STEM AND BEGAN TO SMOKE

transcript: [" The Wizard of Oz, who used to be a humbug and knew no magic at all, has been taking lessons of Glinda and I'm told he is getting to be a pretty good wizard, but he is merely the assistant of the great sorceress."]
reference: THE WIZARD OF OZ WHO USED TO BE A HUMBUG AND KNEW NO MAGIC AT ALL HAS BEEN TAKING LESSONS OF GLINDA AND I'M TOLD HE IS GETTING TO BE A PRETTY GOOD WIZARD BUT HE IS MERELY THE ASSISTANT OF THE GREAT SORCERESS

transcript: [' It truly is, asserted the magician.']
reference: IT TRULY IS ASSERTED THE MAGICIAN

transcript: [' I now use them as ornamental statuary in my garden.']
reference: I NOW USE THEM AS ORNAMENTAL STATUARY IN MY GARDEN

transcript: [" Dear me, what a chatterbox you're getting to be, Unc remarked the magician who was pleased with the compliment."]
reference: DEAR ME WHAT A CHATTERBOX YOU'RE GETTING TO BE UNC REMARKED THE MAGICIAN WHO WAS PLEASED WITH THE COMPLIMENT

transcript: [' asked the voice in scornful accents.']
reference: ASKED THE VOICE IN SCORNFUL ACCENTS

transcript: [' The grateful applause of the clergy has consecrated the memory of a prince who indulged their passions and promoted their interest.']
reference: THE GRATEFUL APPLAUSE OF THE CLERGY HAS CONSECRATED THE MEMORY OF A PRINCE WHO INDULGED THEIR PASSIONS AND PROMOTED THEIR INTEREST

transcript: [' The edict of Milan, the great charter of toleration, had conferred to each individual of the Roman world the privilege of choosing and professing his own religion.']
reference: THE EDICT OF MILAN THE GREAT CHARTER OF TOLERATION HAD CONFIRMED TO EACH INDIVIDUAL OF THE ROMAN WORLD THE PRIVILEGE OF CHOOSING AND PROFESSING HIS OWN RELIGION

transcript: [' But this inestimable privilege was soon violated with the knowledge of truth the emperor imbibed the maxims of persecution and the sects which dissented from the catholic church were afflicted and oppressed by the triumph of christianity.']
reference: BUT THIS INESTIMABLE PRIVILEGE WAS SOON VIOLATED WITH THE KNOWLEDGE OF TRUTH THE EMPEROR IMBIBED THE MAXIMS OF PERSECUTION AND THE SECTS WHICH DISSENTED FROM THE CATHOLIC CHURCH WERE AFFLICTED AND OPPRESSED BY THE TRIUMPH OF CHRISTIANITY

transcript: [' Constantine easily believed that the heretics who presumed to dispute his opinions or to oppose his commands were guilty of the most absurd and criminal obstinacy and that a seasonable application of moderate severities might save those unhappy men from the danger of an everlasting condemnation.']
reference: CONSTANTINE EASILY BELIEVED THAT THE HERETICS WHO PRESUMED TO DISPUTE HIS OPINIONS OR TO OPPOSE HIS COMMANDS WERE GUILTY OF THE MOST ABSURD AND CRIMINAL OBSTINACY AND THAT A SEASONABLE APPLICATION OF MODERATE SEVERITIES MIGHT SAVE THOSE UNHAPPY MEN FROM THE DANGER OF AN EVERLASTING CONDEMNATION

transcript: [' Some of the penal regulations were copied from the edicts of Diocletian and this method of conversion was applauded by the same bishops who had felt the hand of oppression and pleaded for the rights of humanity.']
reference: SOME OF THE PENAL REGULATIONS WERE COPIED FROM THE EDICTS OF DIOCLETIAN AND THIS METHOD OF CONVERSION WAS APPLAUDED BY THE SAME BISHOPS WHO HAD FELT THE HAND OF OPPRESSION AND PLEADED FOR THE RIGHTS OF HUMANITY

transcript: [' They asserted with confidence and almost with exultation that the apostolical succession was interrupted that all the bishops of Europe and Asia were infected by the contagion of guilt and schism and that the prerogatives of the catholic church were confined to the chosen portion of the african believers who alone had preserved inviolate the integrity of their faith and discipline.']
reference: THEY ASSERTED WITH CONFIDENCE AND ALMOST WITH EXULTATION THAT THE APOSTOLICAL SUCCESSION WAS INTERRUPTED THAT ALL THE BISHOPS OF EUROPE AND ASIA WERE INFECTED BY THE CONTAGION OF GUILT AND SCHISM AND THAT THE PREROGATIVES OF THE CATHOLIC CHURCH WERE CONFINED TO THE CHOSEN PORTION OF THE AFRICAN BELIEVERS WHO ALONE HAD PRESERVED INVIOLATE THE INTEGRITY OF THEIR FAITH AND DISCIPLINE

transcript: [' Bishops virgins and even spotless infants were subjected to the disgrace of a public penance before they could be admitted to the communion of the Donatists.']
reference: BISHOPS VIRGINS AND EVEN SPOTLESS INFANTS WERE SUBJECTED TO THE DISGRACE OF A PUBLIC PENANCE BEFORE THEY COULD BE ADMITTED TO THE COMMUNION OF THE DONATISTS

transcript: [' Proscribed by the civil and ecclesiastical powers of the empire, the Donatists still maintained in some provinces, particularly in Numidia, their superior numbers, and four hundred bishops acknowledged the jurisdiction of their primate.']
reference: PROSCRIBED BY THE CIVIL AND ECCLESIASTICAL POWERS OF THE EMPIRE THE DONATISTS STILL MAINTAINED IN SOME PROVINCES PARTICULARLY IN NUMIDIA THEIR SUPERIOR NUMBERS AND FOUR HUNDRED BISHOPS ACKNOWLEDGED THE JURISDICTION OF THEIR PRIMATE

transcript: [' Since the period of our tale the active spirit of the country has surrounded it with a belt of rich and thriving settlements though none but the hunter or the savage is ever known even now to penetrate its wild recesses.']
reference: SINCE THE PERIOD OF OUR TALE THE ACTIVE SPIRIT OF THE COUNTRY HAS SURROUNDED IT WITH A BELT OF RICH AND THRIVING SETTLEMENTS THOUGH NONE BUT THE HUNTER OR THE SAVAGE IS EVER KNOWN EVEN NOW TO PENETRATE ITS WILD RECESSES

transcript: [' The dews were suffered to exhale and the sun had dispersed the mists and was shedding a strong and clear light in the forests when the travellers resumed their journey.']
reference: THE DEWS WERE SUFFERED TO EXHALE AND THE SUN HAD DISPERSED THE MISTS AND WAS SHEDDING A STRONG AND CLEAR LIGHT IN THE FOREST WHEN THE TRAVELERS RESUMED THEIR JOURNEY

transcript: [' After proceeding a few miles the progress of Hawkeye who led the advance became more deliberate and watchful.']
reference: AFTER PROCEEDING A FEW MILES THE PROGRESS OF HAWKEYE WHO LED THE ADVANCE BECAME MORE DELIBERATE AND WATCHFUL

transcript: [' He often stopped to examine the trees nor did he cross a rivulet without attentively considering the quantity the velocity and the color of its waters.']
reference: HE OFTEN STOPPED TO EXAMINE THE TREES NOR DID HE CROSS A RIVULET WITHOUT ATTENTIVELY CONSIDERING THE QUANTITY THE VELOCITY AND THE COLOR OF ITS WATERS

transcript: [' Distrusting his own judgment, his appeals to the opinion of chingachgook were frequent and earnest.']
reference: DISTRUSTING HIS OWN JUDGMENT HIS APPEALS TO THE OPINION OF CHINGACHGOOK WERE FREQUENT AND EARNEST

transcript: [' Yet here are we within a short range of the scaroons and not a sign of a trail have we crossed.']
reference: YET HERE ARE WE WITHIN A SHORT RANGE OF THE SCAROONS AND NOT A SIGN OF A TRAIL HAVE WE CROSSED

transcript: [' Let us retrace our steps and examine as we go with keener eyes']
reference: LET US RETRACE OUR STEPS AND EXAMINE AS WE GO WITH KEENER EYES

transcript: [' Chingachgook had caught the look and motioning with his hand he bade him speak']
reference: CHINGACHGOOK HAD CAUGHT THE LOOK AND MOTIONING WITH HIS HAND HE BADE HIM SPEAK

transcript: [' The eyes of the whole party followed the unexpected movement and read their success in the air of triumph that the youth assumed.']
reference: THE EYES OF THE WHOLE PARTY FOLLOWED THE UNEXPECTED MOVEMENT AND READ THEIR SUCCESS IN THE AIR OF TRIUMPH THAT THE YOUTH ASSUMED

transcript: [' It would have been more wonderful had he spoken without a bidding.']
reference: IT WOULD HAVE BEEN MORE WONDERFUL HAD HE SPOKEN WITHOUT A BIDDING

transcript: [' See said uncas pointing north and south at the evident marks of the broad trail on either side of him the dark hair has gone toward the forest']
reference: SEE SAID UNCAS POINTING NORTH AND SOUTH AT THE EVIDENT MARKS OF THE BROAD TRAIL ON EITHER SIDE OF HIM THE DARK HAIR HAS GONE TOWARD THE FOREST

transcript: [' If a rock or a rivulet or a bit of earth harder than common severed the links of the clew they followed the true eye of the scout recovered them at a distance and seldom rendered the delay of a single moment necessary.']
reference: IF A ROCK OR A RIVULET OR A BIT OF EARTH HARDER THAN COMMON SEVERED THE LINKS OF THE CLEW THEY FOLLOWED THE TRUE EYE OF THE SCOUT RECOVERED THEM AT A DISTANCE AND SELDOM RENDERED THE DELAY OF A SINGLE MOMENT NECESSARY

transcript: [' extinguished brands were lying around a spring the offals of a deer were scattered about the place and the trees bore evident marks of having been browsed by the horses']
reference: EXTINGUISHED BRANDS WERE LYING AROUND A SPRING THE OFFALS OF A DEER WERE SCATTERED ABOUT THE PLACE AND THE TREES BORE EVIDENT MARKS OF HAVING BEEN BROWSED BY THE HORSES

transcript: [' A circle of a few hundred feet in circumference was drawn and each of the party took a segment for his portion.']
reference: A CIRCLE OF A FEW HUNDRED FEET IN CIRCUMFERENCE WAS DRAWN AND EACH OF THE PARTY TOOK A SEGMENT FOR HIS PORTION

transcript: [' The examination however resulted in no discovery.']
reference: THE EXAMINATION HOWEVER RESULTED IN NO DISCOVERY

transcript: [' The whole party crowded to the spot where uncas pointed out the impression of a moccasin in the moist alluvion.']
reference: THE WHOLE PARTY CROWDED TO THE SPOT WHERE UNCAS POINTED OUT THE IMPRESSION OF A MOCCASIN IN THE MOIST ALLUVION

transcript: [" Run back uncas and bring me the size of the singer's foot."]
reference: RUN BACK UNCAS AND BRING ME THE SIZE OF THE SINGER'S FOOT

transcript: [' Notwithstanding the high resolution of Hawkeye, he fully comprehended all the difficulties and danger he was about to incur.']
reference: NOTWITHSTANDING THE HIGH RESOLUTION OF HAWKEYE HE FULLY COMPREHENDED ALL THE DIFFICULTIES AND DANGER HE WAS ABOUT TO INCUR

transcript: [' In his return to the camp his acute and practised intellects were intently engaged in devising means to counteract a watchfulness and suspicion on the part of his enemies that he knew were in no degree inferior to his own.']
reference: IN HIS RETURN TO THE CAMP HIS ACUTE AND PRACTISED INTELLECTS WERE INTENTLY ENGAGED IN DEVISING MEANS TO COUNTERACT A WATCHFULNESS AND SUSPICION ON THE PART OF HIS ENEMIES THAT HE KNEW WERE IN NO DEGREE INFERIOR TO HIS OWN

transcript: [" In other words, while he had implicit faith in the ability of balaam's ass to speak, he was somewhat sceptical on the subject of a bear's singing, and yet he had been assured of the latter on the testimony of his own exquisite organs."]
reference: IN OTHER WORDS WHILE HE HAD IMPLICIT FAITH IN THE ABILITY OF BALAAM'S ASS TO SPEAK HE WAS SOMEWHAT SKEPTICAL ON THE SUBJECT OF A BEAR'S SINGING AND YET HE HAD BEEN ASSURED OF THE LATTER ON THE TESTIMONY OF HIS OWN EXQUISITE ORGANS

transcript: [' There was something in his air and manner that betrayed to the scout the utter confusion of the state of his mind.']
reference: THERE WAS SOMETHING IN HIS AIR AND MANNER THAT BETRAYED TO THE SCOUT THE UTTER CONFUSION OF THE STATE OF HIS MIND

transcript: [' The ingenious Hawkeye who recalled the hasty manner in which the other had abandoned his post at the bedside of the sick woman was not without his suspicions concerning the subject of so much solemn deliberation.']
reference: THE INGENIOUS HAWKEYE WHO RECALLED THE HASTY MANNER IN WHICH THE OTHER HAD ABANDONED HIS POST AT THE BEDSIDE OF THE SICK WOMAN WAS NOT WITHOUT HIS SUSPICIONS CONCERNING THE SUBJECT OF SO MUCH SOLEMN DELIBERATION

transcript: [' The bear shook his shaggy sides and then a well known voice replied']
reference: THE BEAR SHOOK HIS SHAGGY SIDES AND THEN A WELL KNOWN VOICE REPLIED

transcript: [' Can these things be? returned david breathing more freely as the truth began to dawn upon him.']
reference: CAN THESE THINGS BE RETURNED DAVID BREATHING MORE FREELY AS THE TRUTH BEGAN TO DAWN UPON HIM

transcript: [' Come come returned Hawkeye uncasing his honest countenance the better to assure the wavering confidence of his companion you may see a skin which if it be not as white as one of the gentle ones has no tinge of red to it that the winds of the heaven and the sun have not bestowed now let us to business']
reference: COME COME RETURNED HAWKEYE UNCASING HIS HONEST COUNTENANCE THE BETTER TO ASSURE THE WAVERING CONFIDENCE OF HIS COMPANION YOU MAY SEE A SKIN WHICH IF IT BE NOT AS WHITE AS ONE OF THE GENTLE ONES HAS NO TINGE OF RED TO IT THAT THE WINDS OF THE HEAVEN AND THE SUN HAVE NOT BESTOWED NOW LET US TO BUSINESS

transcript: [' The young man is in bondage and much I fear his death is decreed.']
reference: THE YOUNG MAN IS IN BONDAGE AND MUCH I FEAR HIS DEATH IS DECREED

transcript: [' I greatly mourn that one so well disposed should die in his ignorance and I have sought a goodly hymn Can you lead me to him?']
reference: I GREATLY MOURN THAT ONE SO WELL DISPOSED SHOULD DIE IN HIS IGNORANCE AND I HAVE SOUGHT A GOODLY HYMN CAN YOU LEAD ME TO HIM

transcript: [' The task will not be difficult returned david hesitating though I greatly fear your presence would rather increase than mitigate his unhappy fortunes.']
reference: THE TASK WILL NOT BE DIFFICULT RETURNED DAVID HESITATING THOUGH I GREATLY FEAR YOUR PRESENCE WOULD RATHER INCREASE THAN MITIGATE HIS UNHAPPY FORTUNES

transcript: [' The lodge in which uncas was confined was in the very center of the village and in a situation perhaps more difficult than any other to approach or leave without observation.']
reference: THE LODGE IN WHICH UNCAS WAS CONFINED WAS IN THE VERY CENTER OF THE VILLAGE AND IN A SITUATION PERHAPS MORE DIFFICULT THAN ANY OTHER TO APPROACH OR LEAVE WITHOUT OBSERVATION

transcript: [' Four or five of the latter only lingered about the door of the prison of uncas wary but close observers of the manner of their captive.']
reference: FOUR OR FIVE OF THE LATTER ONLY LINGERED ABOUT THE DOOR OF THE PRISON OF UNCAS WARY BUT CLOSE OBSERVERS OF THE MANNER OF THEIR CAPTIVE

transcript: [' delivered in a strong tone of assent announced the gratification the savage would receive in witnessing such an exhibition of weakness in an enemy so long hated and so much feared']
reference: DELIVERED IN A STRONG TONE OF ASSENT ANNOUNCED THE GRATIFICATION THE SAVAGE WOULD RECEIVE IN WITNESSING SUCH AN EXHIBITION OF WEAKNESS IN AN ENEMY SO LONG HATED AND SO MUCH FEARED

transcript: [' They drew back a little from the entrance and motioned to the supposed conjuror to enter.']
reference: THEY DREW BACK A LITTLE FROM THE ENTRANCE AND MOTIONED TO THE SUPPOSED CONJURER TO ENTER

transcript: [' But the bear instead of obeying maintained the seat it had taken and growled.']
reference: BUT THE BEAR INSTEAD OF OBEYING MAINTAINED THE SEAT IT HAD TAKEN AND GROWLED

transcript: [' The cunning man is afraid that his breath will blow upon his brothers and take away their courage too continued david improving the hint he received they must stand further off']
reference: THE CUNNING MAN IS AFRAID THAT HIS BREATH WILL BLOW UPON HIS BROTHERS AND TAKE AWAY THEIR COURAGE TOO CONTINUED DAVID IMPROVING THE HINT HE RECEIVED THEY MUST STAND FURTHER OFF

transcript: [' Then as if satisfied of their safety the scout left his position and slowly entered the place.']
reference: THEN AS IF SATISFIED OF THEIR SAFETY THE SCOUT LEFT HIS POSITION AND SLOWLY ENTERED THE PLACE

transcript: [' It was silent and gloomy being tenanted solely by the captive and lighted by the dying embers of a fire which had been used for the purpose of cookery.']
reference: IT WAS SILENT AND GLOOMY BEING TENANTED SOLELY BY THE CAPTIVE AND LIGHTED BY THE DYING EMBERS OF A FIRE WHICH HAD BEEN USED FOR THE PURPOSED OF COOKERY

transcript: [' Uncas occupied a distant corner in a reclining attitude being rigidly bound both hands and feet by strong and painful widths.']
reference: UNCAS OCCUPIED A DISTANT CORNER IN A RECLINING ATTITUDE BEING RIGIDLY BOUND BOTH HANDS AND FEET BY STRONG AND PAINFUL WITHES

transcript: [' The scout, who had left David at the door to ascertain they were not observed, thought it prudent to preserve his disguise until assured of their privacy.']
reference: THE SCOUT WHO HAD LEFT DAVID AT THE DOOR TO ASCERTAIN THEY WERE NOT OBSERVED THOUGHT IT PRUDENT TO PRESERVE HIS DISGUISE UNTIL ASSURED OF THEIR PRIVACY

transcript: [' What shall we do with the mingoes at the door? They count six, and this singer is as good as nothing.']
reference: WHAT SHALL WE DO WITH THE MINGOES AT THE DOOR THEY COUNT SIX AND THIS SINGER IS AS GOOD AS NOTHING

transcript: [' The Delawares are children of the tortoise and they outstrip the deer.']
reference: THE DELAWARES ARE CHILDREN OF THE TORTOISE AND THEY OUTSTRIP THE DEER

transcript: [' Uncas who had already approached the door in readiness to lead the way now recoiled and placed himself once more in the bottom of the lodge.']
reference: UNCAS WHO HAD ALREADY APPROACHED THE DOOR IN READINESS TO LEAD THE WAY NOW RECOILED AND PLACED HIMSELF ONCE MORE IN THE BOTTOM OF THE LODGE

transcript: [' But Hawkeye who was too much occupied with his own thoughts to note the movement continued speaking more to himself than to his companion.']
reference: BUT HAWKEYE WHO WAS TOO MUCH OCCUPIED WITH HIS OWN THOUGHTS TO NOTE THE MOVEMENT CONTINUED SPEAKING MORE TO HIMSELF THAN TO HIS COMPANION

transcript: [' So uncas, you had better take the lead while I will put on the skin again and trust to cunning for want of speed.']
reference: SO UNCAS YOU HAD BETTER TAKE THE LEAD WHILE I WILL PUT ON THE SKIN AGAIN AND TRUST TO CUNNING FOR WANT OF SPEED

transcript: [" Well, what can't be done by main courage in war must be done by circumvention."]
reference: WELL WHAT CAN'T BE DONE BY MAIN COURAGE IN WAR MUST BE DONE BY CIRCUMVENTION

transcript: [' As soon as these dispositions were made the scout turned to david and gave him his parting instructions.']
reference: AS SOON AS THESE DISPOSITIONS WERE MADE THE SCOUT TURNED TO DAVID AND GAVE HIM HIS PARTING INSTRUCTIONS

transcript: [' My pursuits are peaceful and my temper I humbly trust is greatly given to mercy and love returned david a little nettled at so direct an attack on his manhood but there are none who can say that I have ever forgotten my faith in the Lord even in the greatest straits.']
reference: MY PURSUITS ARE PEACEFUL AND MY TEMPER I HUMBLY TRUST IS GREATLY GIVEN TO MERCY AND LOVE RETURNED DAVID A LITTLE NETTLED AT SO DIRECT AN ATTACK ON HIS MANHOOD BUT THERE ARE NONE WHO CAN SAY THAT I HAVE EVER FORGOTTEN MY FAITH IN THE LORD EVEN IN THE GREATEST STRAITS

transcript: [" If you are not then knocked on the head your being a non composite will protect you, and you'll then have a good reason to expect to die in your bed."]
reference: IF YOU ARE NOT THEN KNOCKED ON THE HEAD YOUR BEING A NON COMPOSSER WILL PROTECT YOU AND YOU'LL THEN HAVE A GOOD REASON TO EXPECT TO DIE IN YOUR BED

transcript: [' So choose for yourself to make a rush or tarry here.']
reference: SO CHOOSE FOR YOURSELF TO MAKE A RUSH OR TARRY HERE

transcript: [' Bravely and generously has he battled in my behalf and this and more will I dare in his service.']
reference: BRAVELY AND GENEROUSLY HAS HE BATTLED IN MY BEHALF AND THIS AND MORE WILL I DARE IN HIS SERVICE

transcript: [' Keep silent as long as may be and it would be wise when you do speak to break out suddenly in one of your shoutings which will serve to remind the Indians that you are not altogether as responsible as men should be.']
reference: KEEP SILENT AS LONG AS MAY BE AND IT WOULD BE WISE WHEN YOU DO SPEAK TO BREAK OUT SUDDENLY IN ONE OF YOUR SHOUTINGS WHICH WILL SERVE TO REMIND THE INDIANS THAT YOU ARE NOT ALTOGETHER AS RESPONSIBLE AS MEN SHOULD BE

transcript: [' If however they take your scalp, as I trust and believe they will not, depend on it uncas and I will not forget the deed but revenge it as becomes true warriors and trusty friends.']
reference: IF HOWEVER THEY TAKE YOUR SCALP AS I TRUST AND BELIEVE THEY WILL NOT DEPEND ON IT UNCAS AND I WILL NOT FORGET THE DEED BUT REVENGE IT AS BECOMES TRUE WARRIORS AND TRUSTY FRIENDS

transcript: [' Hold said David perceiving that with this assurance they were about to leave him I am an unworthy and humble follower of one who taught not the damnable principle of revenge.']
reference: HOLD SAID DAVID PERCEIVING THAT WITH THIS ASSURANCE THEY WERE ABOUT TO LEAVE HIM I AM AN UNWORTHY AND HUMBLE FOLLOWER OF ONE WHO TAUGHT NOT THE DAMNABLE PRINCIPLE OF REVENGE

transcript: [' Then heaving a heavy sigh probably among the last he ever drew in pining for a condition he had so long abandoned he added it is what I would wish to practise myself as one without a cross of blood though it is not always easy to deal with an indian as you would with a fellow christian.']
reference: THEN HEAVING A HEAVY SIGH PROBABLY AMONG THE LAST HE EVER DREW IN PINING FOR A CONDITION HE HAD SO LONG ABANDONED HE ADDED IT IS WHAT I WOULD WISH TO PRACTISE MYSELF AS ONE WITHOUT A CROSS OF BLOOD THOUGH IT IS NOT ALWAYS EASY TO DEAL WITH AN INDIAN AS YOU WOULD WITH A FELLOW CHRISTIAN

transcript: [' God bless you friend I do believe your scent is not greatly wrong when the matter is duly considered and keeping eternity before the eyes though much depends on the natural gifts and the force of temptation']
reference: GOD BLESS YOU FRIEND I DO BELIEVE YOUR SCENT IS NOT GREATLY WRONG WHEN THE MATTER IS DULY CONSIDERED AND KEEPING ETERNITY BEFORE THE EYES THOUGH MUCH DEPENDS ON THE NATURAL GIFTS AND THE FORCE OF TEMPTATION

transcript: [" The Delaware dog he said leaning forward and peering through the dim light to catch the expression of the other's features is he afraid?"]
reference: THE DELAWARE DOG HE SAID LEANING FORWARD AND PEERING THROUGH THE DIM LIGHT TO CATCH THE EXPRESSION OF THE OTHER'S FEATURES IS HE AFRAID

transcript: [' Will the Hurons hear his groans?']
reference: WILL THE HURONS HEAR HIS GROANS

transcript: [' The Mohican started on his feet and shook his shaggy covering as though the animal he counterfeited was about to make some desperate effort.']
reference: THE MOHICAN STARTED ON HIS FEET AND SHOOK HIS SHAGGY COVERING AS THOUGH THE ANIMAL HE COUNTERFEITED WAS ABOUT TO MAKE SOME DESPERATE EFFORT

transcript: [' He had no occasion to delay for at the next instant a burst of cries filled the outer air and ran along the whole extent of the village.']
reference: HE HAD NO OCCASION TO DELAY FOR AT THE NEXT INSTANT A BURST OF CRIES FILLED THE OUTER AIR AND RAN ALONG THE WHOLE EXTENT OF THE VILLAGE

transcript: [' Uncas cast his skin and stepped forth in his own beautiful proportions.']
reference: UNCAS CAST HIS SKIN AND STEPPED FORTH IN HIS OWN BEAUTIFUL PROPORTIONS

transcript: [' I will endeavor in my statement to avoid such terms as would serve to limit the events to any particular place or give a clue as to the people concerned.']
reference: I WILL ENDEAVOUR IN MY STATEMENT TO AVOID SUCH TERMS AS WOULD SERVE TO LIMIT THE EVENTS TO ANY PARTICULAR PLACE OR GIVE A CLUE AS TO THE PEOPLE CONCERNED

transcript: [' I had always known him to be restless in his manner, but on this particular occasion he was in such a state of uncontrollable agitation that it was clear something very unusual had occurred.']
reference: I HAD ALWAYS KNOWN HIM TO BE RESTLESS IN HIS MANNER BUT ON THIS PARTICULAR OCCASION HE WAS IN SUCH A STATE OF UNCONTROLLABLE AGITATION THAT IT WAS CLEAR SOMETHING VERY UNUSUAL HAD OCCURRED

transcript: [" My friend's temper had not improved since he had been deprived of the congenial surroundings of Baker Street."]
reference: MY FRIEND'S TEMPER HAD NOT IMPROVED SINCE HE HAD BEEN DEPRIVED OF THE CONGENIAL SURROUNDINGS OF BAKER STREET

transcript: [' Without his scrapbooks his chemicals and his homely untidiness, he was an uncomfortable man.']
reference: WITHOUT HIS SCRAPBOOKS HIS CHEMICALS AND HIS HOMELY UNTIDINESS HE WAS AN UNCOMFORTABLE MAN

transcript: [' I had to read it over carefully as the text must be absolutely correct.']
reference: I HAD TO READ IT OVER CAREFULLY AS THE TEXT MUST BE ABSOLUTELY CORRECT

transcript: [' I was absent rather more than an hour.']
reference: I WAS ABSENT RATHER MORE THAN AN HOUR

transcript: [' The only duplicate which existed so far as I knew was that which belonged to my servant Bannister a man who has looked after my room for ten years and whose honesty is absolutely above suspicion.']
reference: THE ONLY DUPLICATE WHICH EXISTED SO FAR AS I KNEW WAS THAT WHICH BELONGED TO MY SERVANT BANNISTER A MAN WHO HAS LOOKED AFTER MY ROOM FOR TEN YEARS AND WHOSE HONESTY IS ABSOLUTELY ABOVE SUSPICION

transcript: [' The moment I looked at my table, I was aware that some one had rummaged among my papers.']
reference: THE MOMENT I LOOKED AT MY TABLE I WAS AWARE THAT SOMEONE HAD RUMMAGED AMONG MY PAPERS

transcript: [' The proof was in three long slips. I had left them all together.']
reference: THE PROOF WAS IN THREE LONG SLIPS I HAD LEFT THEM ALL TOGETHER

transcript: [' The alternative was that some one passing had observed the key in the door had known that I was out and had entered to look at the papers.']
reference: THE ALTERNATIVE WAS THAT SOMEONE PASSING HAD OBSERVED THE KEY IN THE DOOR HAD KNOWN THAT I WAS OUT AND HAD ENTERED TO LOOK AT THE PAPERS

transcript: [' I gave him a little brandy and left him collapsed in a chair while I made a most careful examination of the room.']
reference: I GAVE HIM A LITTLE BRANDY AND LEFT HIM COLLAPSED IN A CHAIR WHILE I MADE A MOST CAREFUL EXAMINATION OF THE ROOM

transcript: [' A broken tip of lead was lying there also.']
reference: A BROKEN TIP OF LEAD WAS LYING THERE ALSO

transcript: [' Not only this, but on the table I found a small ball of black dough or clay with specks of something which looked like sawdust in it.']
reference: NOT ONLY THIS BUT ON THE TABLE I FOUND A SMALL BALL OF BLACK DOUGH OR CLAY WITH SPECKS OF SOMETHING WHICH LOOKS LIKE SAWDUST IN IT

transcript: [' above all things I desire to settle the matter quietly and discreetly.']
reference: ABOVE ALL THINGS I DESIRE TO SETTLE THE MATTER QUIETLY AND DISCREETLY

transcript: [' To the best of my belief, they were rolled up.']
reference: TO THE BEST OF MY BELIEF THEY WERE ROLLED UP

transcript: [' Did any one know that these proofs would be there? No one save the printer!']
reference: DID ANYONE KNOW THAT THESE PROOFS WOULD BE THERE NO ONE SAVE THE PRINTER

transcript: [' I was in such a hurry to come to you. You left your door open?']
reference: I WAS IN SUCH A HURRY TO COME TO YOU YOU LEFT YOUR DOOR OPEN

transcript: [' So it seems to me.']
reference: SO IT SEEMS TO ME

transcript: [' Now, mister soames, at your disposal.']
reference: NOW MISTER SOAMES AT YOUR DISPOSAL

transcript: [' Above were three students, one on each story.']
reference: ABOVE WERE THREE STUDENTS ONE ON EACH STORY

transcript: [' Then he approached it and standing on tiptoe with his neck craned he looked into the room.']
reference: THEN HE APPROACHED IT AND STANDING ON TIPTOE WITH HIS NECK CRANED HE LOOKED INTO THE ROOM

transcript: [' There is no opening except the one pane, said our learned guide.']
reference: THERE IS NO OPENING EXCEPT THE ONE PANE SAID OUR LEARNED GUIDE

transcript: [' I am afraid there are no signs here, said he.']
reference: I AM AFRAID THERE ARE NO SIGNS HERE SAID HE

transcript: [' One could hardly hope for any upon so dry a day.']
reference: ONE COULD HARDLY HOPE FOR ANY UPON SO DRY A DAY

transcript: [' You left him in a chair you say? Which chair? By the window there.']
reference: YOU LEFT HIM IN A CHAIR YOU SAY WHICH CHAIR BY THE WINDOW THERE

transcript: [' The men entered and took the papers sheet by sheet from the central table.']
reference: THE MAN ENTERED AND TOOK THE PAPERS SHEET BY SHEET FROM THE CENTRAL TABLE

transcript: [' As a matter of fact he could not said solmes for I entered by the side door.']
reference: AS A MATTER OF FACT HE COULD NOT SAID SOAMES FOR I ENTERED BY THE SIDE DOOR

transcript: [' How long would it take him to do that using every possible contraction a quarter of an hour not less']
reference: HOW LONG WOULD IT TAKE HIM TO DO THAT USING EVERY POSSIBLE CONTRACTION A QUARTER OF AN HOUR NOT LESS

transcript: [' Then he tossed it down and seized the next.']
reference: THEN HE TOSSED IT DOWN AND SEIZED THE NEXT

transcript: [' He was in the midst of that when your return caused him to make a very hurried retreat very hurried since he had not time to replace the papers which would tell you that he had been there.']
reference: HE WAS IN THE MIDST OF THAT WHEN YOUR RETURN CAUSED HIM TO MAKE A VERY HURRIED RETREAT VERY HURRIED SINCE HE HAD NOT TIME TO REPLACE THE PAPERS WHICH WOULD TELL YOU THAT HE HAD BEEN THERE

transcript: [' mister solmes was somewhat overwhelmed by this flood of information.']
reference: MISTER SOAMES WAS SOMEWHAT OVERWHELMED BY THIS FLOOD OF INFORMATION

transcript: [' Holmes held out a small chip with the letters n n and a space of clear wood after them you see']
reference: HOLMES HELD OUT A SMALL CHIP WITH THE LETTERS N N AND A SPACE OF CLEAR WOOD AFTER THEM YOU SEE

transcript: [' Watson, I have always done you an injustice. There are others.']
reference: WATSON I HAVE ALWAYS DONE YOU AN INJUSTICE THERE ARE OTHERS

transcript: [' I was hoping that if the paper on which he wrote was thin some trace of it might come through upon this polished surface no, I see nothing.']
reference: I WAS HOPING THAT IF THE PAPER ON WHICH HE WROTE WAS THIN SOME TRACE OF IT MIGHT COME THROUGH UPON THIS POLISHED SURFACE NO I SEE NOTHING

transcript: [' As Holmes drew the curtain I was aware from some little rigidity and alertness of his attitude that he was prepared for an emergency.']
reference: AS HOLMES DREW THE CURTAIN I WAS AWARE FROM SOME LITTLE RIGIDITY AND ALERTNESS OF HIS ATTITUDE THAT HE WAS PREPARED FOR AN EMERGENCY

transcript: [' Holmes turned away and stooped suddenly to the floor hallo what is this?']
reference: HOLMES TURNED AWAY AND STOOPED SUDDENLY TO THE FLOOR HALLOA WHAT'S THIS

transcript: [' Holmes held it out on his open palm in the glare of the electric light.']
reference: HOLMES HELD IT OUT ON HIS OPEN PALM IN THE GLARE OF THE ELECTRIC LIGHT

transcript: [' What could he do? He caught up everything which would betray him and he rushed into your bedroom to conceal himself.']
reference: WHAT COULD HE DO HE CAUGHT UP EVERYTHING WHICH WOULD BETRAY HIM AND HE RUSHED INTO YOUR BEDROOM TO CONCEAL HIMSELF

transcript: [' I understand you to say that there are three students who use this stair and are in the habit of passing your door? Yes, there are.']
reference: I UNDERSTAND YOU TO SAY THAT THERE ARE THREE STUDENTS WHO USE THIS STAIR AND ARE IN THE HABIT OF PASSING YOUR DOOR YES THERE ARE

transcript: [' And they are all in for this examination? Yes.']
reference: AND THEY ARE ALL IN FOR THIS EXAMINATION YES

transcript: [' One hardly likes to throw suspicion where there are no proofs.']
reference: ONE HARDLY LIKES TO THROW SUSPICION WHERE THERE ARE NO PROOFS

transcript: [' Let us hear the suspicions, I will look after the proofs.']
reference: LET US HEAR THE SUSPICIONS I WILL LOOK AFTER THE PROOFS

transcript: [' My scholar has been left very poor, but he is hard working and industrious he will do well.']
reference: MY SCHOLAR HAS BEEN LEFT VERY POOR BUT HE IS HARD WORKING AND INDUSTRIOUS HE WILL DO WELL

transcript: [' The top floor belongs to miles mc laren.']
reference: THE TOP FLOOR BELONGS TO MILES MC LAREN

transcript: [' I dare not go so far as that but of the three he is perhaps the least unlikely']
reference: I DARE NOT GO SO FAR AS THAT BUT OF THE THREE HE IS PERHAPS THE LEAST UNLIKELY

transcript: [' He was still suffering from this sudden disturbance of the quiet routine of his life.']
reference: HE WAS STILL SUFFERING FROM THIS SUDDEN DISTURBANCE OF THE QUIET ROUTINE OF HIS LIFE

transcript: [' But I have occasionally done the same thing at other times.']
reference: BUT I HAVE OCCASIONALLY DONE THE SAME THING AT OTHER TIMES

transcript: [' Did you look at these papers on the table?']
reference: DID YOU LOOK AT THESE PAPERS ON THE TABLE

transcript: [' How came you to leave the key in the door?']
reference: HOW CAME YOU TO LEAVE THE KEY IN THE DOOR

transcript: [' Any one in the room could get out? Yes, sir.']
reference: ANYONE IN THE ROOM COULD GET OUT YES SIR

transcript: [" I really don't think he knew much about it, mister Holmes."]
reference: I REALLY DON'T THINK HE KNEW MUCH ABOUT IT MISTER HOLMES

transcript: [' only for a minute or so.']
reference: ONLY FOR A MINUTE OR SO

transcript: [' Oh, I would not venture to say, sir.']
reference: OH I WOULD NOT VENTURE TO SAY SIR

transcript: [" You haven't seen any of them? No, sir."]
reference: YOU HAVEN'T SEEN ANY OF THEM NO SIR

transcript: [' It was the Indian whose dark silhouette appeared suddenly upon his blind.']
reference: IT WAS THE INDIAN WHOSE DARK SILHOUETTE APPEARED SUDDENLY UPON HIS BLIND

transcript: [' He was pacing swiftly up and down his room.']
reference: HE WAS PACING SWIFTLY UP AND DOWN HIS ROOM

transcript: [' This set of rooms is quite the oldest in the college and it is not unusual for visitors to go over them.']
reference: THIS SET OF ROOMS IS QUITE THE OLDEST IN THE COLLEGE AND IT IS NOT UNUSUAL FOR VISITORS TO GO OVER THEM

transcript: [" No names please said Holmes as we knocked at Gilchrist's door."]
reference: NO NAMES PLEASE SAID HOLMES AS WE KNOCKED AT GILCHRIST'S DOOR

transcript: [' Of course he did not realize that it was I who was knocking, but none the less his conduct was very uncourteous and indeed under the circumstances rather suspicious.']
reference: OF COURSE HE DID NOT REALIZE THAT IT WAS I WHO WAS KNOCKING BUT NONE THE LESS HIS CONDUCT WAS VERY UNCOURTEOUS AND INDEED UNDER THE CIRCUMSTANCES RATHER SUSPICIOUS

transcript: [' That is very important said Holmes.']
reference: THAT IS VERY IMPORTANT SAID HOLMES

transcript: [" You don't seem to realize the position."]
reference: YOU DON'T SEEM TO REALIZE THE POSITION

transcript: [' To morrow is the examination.']
reference: TO MORROW IS THE EXAMINATION

transcript: [' I cannot allow the examination to be held if one of the papers has been tampered with. The situation must be faced.']
reference: I CANNOT ALLOW THE EXAMINATION TO BE HELD IF ONE OF THE PAPERS HAS BEEN TAMPERED WITH THE SITUATION MUST BE FACED

transcript: [' It is possible that I may be in a position then to indicate some course of action.']
reference: IT IS POSSIBLE THAT I MAY BE IN A POSITION THEN TO INDICATE SOME COURSE OF ACTION

transcript: [' I will take the black clay with me also the pencil cuttings good bye']
reference: I WILL TAKE THE BLACK CLAY WITH ME ALSO THE PENCIL CUTTINGS GOOD BYE

transcript: [' When we were out in the darkness of the quadrangle we again looked up at the windows.']
reference: WHEN WE WERE OUT IN THE DARKNESS OF THE QUADRANGLE WE AGAIN LOOKED UP AT THE WINDOWS

transcript: [' The Foul Mouthed Fellow at the Top']
reference: THE FOUL MOUTHED FELLOW AT THE TOP

transcript: [' He is the one with the worst record.']
reference: HE IS THE ONE WITH THE WORST RECORD

transcript: [" Why Bannister the servant, what's his game in the matter?"]
reference: WHY BANNISTER THE SERVANT WHAT'S HIS GAME IN THE MATTER

transcript: [' He impressed me as being a perfectly honest man.']
reference: HE IMPRESSED ME AS BEING A PERFECTLY HONEST MAN

transcript: [' My friend did not appear to be depressed by his failure, but shrugged his shoulders in half humorous resignation.']
reference: MY FRIEND DID NOT APPEAR TO BE DEPRESSED BY HIS FAILURE BUT SHRUGGED HIS SHOULDERS IN HALF HUMOROUS RESIGNATION

transcript: [' No good, my dear Watson.']
reference: NO GOOD MY DEAR WATSON

transcript: [' I think so you have formed a conclusion']
reference: I THINK SO YOU HAVE FORMED A CONCLUSION

transcript: [' Yes, my dear Watson, I have solved the mystery.']
reference: YES MY DEAR WATSON I HAVE SOLVED THE MYSTERY

transcript: [' Look at that! He held out his hand.']
reference: LOOK AT THAT HE HELD OUT HIS HAND

transcript: [' on the palm were three little pyramids of black doughy clay.']
reference: ON THE PALM WERE THREE LITTLE PYRAMIDS OF BLACK DOUGHY CLAY

transcript: [' And one more this morning.']
reference: AND ONE MORE THIS MORNING

transcript: [' In a few hours the examination would commence and he was still in the dilemma between making the facts public and allowing the culprit to compete for the valuable scholarship.']
reference: IN A FEW HOURS THE EXAMINATION WOULD COMMENCE AND HE WAS STILL IN THE DILEMMA BETWEEN MAKING THE FACTS PUBLIC AND ALLOWING THE CULPRIT TO COMPETE FOR THE VALUABLE SCHOLARSHIP

transcript: [' He could hardly stand still so great was his mental agitation and he ran towards Holmes with two eager hands outstretched thank heaven that you have come.']
reference: HE COULD HARDLY STAND STILL SO GREAT WAS HIS MENTAL AGITATION AND HE RAN TOWARDS HOLMES WITH TWO EAGER HANDS OUTSTRETCHED THANK HEAVEN THAT YOU HAVE COME

transcript: [' You know him? I think so.']
reference: YOU KNOW HIM I THINK SO

transcript: [' If this matter is not to become public we must give ourselves certain powers and resolve ourselves into a small private court martial.']
reference: IF THIS MATTER IS NOT TO BECOME PUBLIC WE MUST GIVE OURSELVES CERTAIN POWERS AND RESOLVE OURSELVES INTO A SMALL PRIVATE COURT MARTIAL

transcript: [' No, sir, certainly not.']
reference: NO SIR CERTAINLY NOT

transcript: [' There was no man, sir.']
reference: THERE WAS NO MAN SIR

transcript: [' His troubled blue eyes glanced at each of us and finally rested with an expression of blank dismay upon Bannister in the farther corner.']
reference: HIS TROUBLED BLUE EYES GLANCED AT EACH OF US AND FINALLY RESTED WITH AN EXPRESSION OF BLANK DISMAY UPON BANNISTER IN THE FARTHER CORNER

transcript: [' Just close the door said Holmes.']
reference: JUST CLOSE THE DOOR SAID HOLMES

transcript: [' We want to know, mister Gilchrist, how you an honorable man ever came to commit such an action as that of yesterday.']
reference: WE WANT TO KNOW MISTER GILCHRIST HOW YOU AN HONOURABLE MAN EVER CAME TO COMMIT SUCH AN ACTION AS THAT OF YESTERDAY

transcript: [' For a moment Gilchrist with upraised hand tried to control his writhing features.']
reference: FOR A MOMENT GILCHRIST WITH UPRAISED HAND TRIED TO CONTROL HIS WRITHING FEATURES

transcript: [' Come come said Holmes kindly it is human to err and at least no one can accuse you of being a callous criminal.']
reference: COME COME SAID HOLMES KINDLY IT IS HUMAN TO ERR AND AT LEAST NO ONE CAN ACCUSE YOU OF BEING A CALLOUS CRIMINAL

transcript: [" Well, well, don't trouble to answer. Listen and see that I do you no injustice."]
reference: WELL WELL DON'T TROUBLE TO ANSWER LISTEN AND SEE THAT I DO YOU NO INJUSTICE

transcript: [' He could examine the papers in his own office.']
reference: HE COULD EXAMINE THE PAPERS IN HIS OWN OFFICE

transcript: [' The Indian I also thought nothing of.']
reference: THE INDIAN I ALSO THOUGHT NOTHING OF

transcript: [' When I approached your room, I examined the window.']
reference: WHEN I APPROACHED YOUR ROOM I EXAMINED THE WINDOW

transcript: [' No one less than that would have a chance.']
reference: NO ONE LESS THAN THAT WOULD HAVE A CHANCE

transcript: [' I entered, and I took you into my confidence as to the suggestions of the side table.']
reference: I ENTERED AND I TOOK YOU INTO MY CONFIDENCE AS TO THE SUGGESTIONS OF THE SIDE TABLE

transcript: [' He returned carrying his jumping shoes which are provided as you are aware with several sharp spikes.']
reference: HE RETURNED CARRYING HIS JUMPING SHOES WHICH ARE PROVIDED AS YOU ARE AWARE WITH SEVERAL SHARP SPIKES

transcript: [' No harm would have been done had it not been that as he passed your door he perceived the key which had been left by the carelessness of your servant.']
reference: NO HARM WOULD HAVE BEEN DONE HAD IT NOT BEEN THAT AS HE PASSED YOUR DOOR HE PERCEIVED THE KEY WHICH HAD BEEN LEFT BY THE CARELESSNESS OF YOUR SERVANT

transcript: [' A sudden impulse came over him to enter and see if they were indeed the proofs.']
reference: A SUDDEN IMPULSE CAME OVER HIM TO ENTER AND SEE IF THEY WERE INDEED THE PROOFS

transcript: [' He put his shoes on the table.']
reference: HE PUT HIS SHOES ON THE TABLE

transcript: [' Gloves! said the young man.']
reference: GLOVES SAID THE YOUNG MAN

transcript: [' Suddenly he heard him at the very door. There was no possible escape.']
reference: SUDDENLY HE HEARD HIM AT THE VERY DOOR THERE WAS NO POSSIBLE ESCAPE

transcript: [' Have I told the truth, mister Gilchrist?']
reference: HAVE I TOLD THE TRUTH MISTER GILCHRIST

transcript: [' I have a letter here, mister Solmes, which I wrote to you early this morning in the middle of a restless night.']
reference: I HAVE A LETTER HERE MISTER SOAMES WHICH I WROTE TO YOU EARLY THIS MORNING IN THE MIDDLE OF A RESTLESS NIGHT

transcript: [' It will be clear to you from what I have said that only you could have let this young man out since you were left in the room and must have locked the door when you went out.']
reference: IT WILL BE CLEAR TO YOU FROM WHAT I HAVE SAID THAT ONLY YOU COULD HAVE LET THIS YOUNG MAN OUT SINCE YOU WERE LEFT IN THE ROOM AND MUST HAVE LOCKED THE DOOR WHEN YOU WENT OUT

transcript: [' It was simple enough, sir, if you only had known, but with all your cleverness it was impossible that you could know.']
reference: IT WAS SIMPLE ENOUGH SIR IF YOU ONLY HAD KNOWN BUT WITH ALL YOUR CLEVERNESS IT WAS IMPOSSIBLE THAT YOU COULD KNOW

transcript: [' If mister Solmes saw them the game was up.']
reference: IF MISTER SOAMES SAW THEM THE GAME WAS UP

transcript: [' In the debate between the senior societies her defense of the fifteenth amendment had been not only a notable bit of reasoning but delivered with real enthusiasm.']
reference: IN THE DEBATE BETWEEN THE SENIOR SOCIETIES HER DEFENCE OF THE FIFTEENTH AMENDMENT HAD BEEN NOT ONLY A NOTABLE BIT OF REASONING BUT DELIVERED WITH REAL ENTHUSIASM

transcript: [' The south she had not thought of seriously and yet knowing of its delightful hospitality and mild climate she was not averse to charleston or new orleans.']
reference: THE SOUTH SHE HAD NOT THOUGHT OF SERIOUSLY AND YET KNOWING OF ITS DELIGHTFUL HOSPITALITY AND MILD CLIMATE SHE WAS NOT AVERSE TO CHARLESTON OR NEW ORLEANS

transcript: [' John Taylor who had supported her through college was interested in cotton.']
reference: JOHN TAYLOR WHO HAD SUPPORTED HER THROUGH COLLEGE WAS INTERESTED IN COTTON

transcript: [' Better go, he had counseled sententiously.']
reference: BETTER GO HE HAD COUNSELLED SENTENTIOUSLY

transcript: [' Might learn something useful down there.']
reference: MIGHT LEARN SOMETHING USEFUL DOWN THERE

transcript: [" But, john, there's no society, just elementary work."]
reference: BUT JOHN THERE'S NO SOCIETY JUST ELEMENTARY WORK

transcript: [' Been looking up Tombs county.']
reference: BEEN LOOKING UP TOOMS COUNTY

transcript: [' find some cresswells there big plantations rated at two hundred and fifty thousand dollars']
reference: FIND SOME CRESSWELLS THERE BIG PLANTATIONS RATED AT TWO HUNDRED AND FIFTY THOUSAND DOLLARS

transcript: [' Some others too big cotton county.']
reference: SOME OTHERS TOO BIG COTTON COUNTY

transcript: [" You ought to know, john, if I teach negroes, I'll scarcely see much of people in my own class."]
reference: YOU OUGHT TO KNOW JOHN IF I TEACH NEGROES I'LL SCARCELY SEE MUCH OF PEOPLE IN MY OWN CLASS

transcript: [' At any rate, I say go.']
reference: AT ANY RATE I SAY GO

transcript: [' Here she was teaching dirty children and the smell of confused odors and bodily perspiration was to her at times unbearable.']
reference: HERE SHE WAS TEACHING DIRTY CHILDREN AND THE SMELL OF CONFUSED ODORS AND BODILY PERSPIRATION WAS TO HER AT TIMES UNBEARABLE

transcript: [' She wanted a glance of the new books and periodicals and talk of great philanthropies and reforms.']
reference: SHE WANTED A GLANCE OF THE NEW BOOKS AND PERIODICALS AND TALK OF GREAT PHILANTHROPIES AND REFORMS

transcript: [' So for the hundredth time she was thinking to day as she walked alone up the lane back of the barn and then slowly down through the bottoms.']
reference: SO FOR THE HUNDREDTH TIME SHE WAS THINKING TODAY AS SHE WALKED ALONE UP THE LANE BACK OF THE BARN AND THEN SLOWLY DOWN THROUGH THE BOTTOMS

transcript: [' Carton she paused.']
reference: COTTON SHE PAUSED

transcript: [' She had almost forgotten that it was here within touch and sight.']
reference: SHE HAD ALMOST FORGOTTEN THAT IT WAS HERE WITHIN TOUCH AND SIGHT

transcript: [' the glimmering sea of delicate leaves whispered and murmured before her stretching away to the northward']
reference: THE GLIMMERING SEA OF DELICATE LEAVES WHISPERED AND MURMURED BEFORE HER STRETCHING AWAY TO THE NORTHWARD
