transcript: [' There might be a bit of poetry here and there, but most of this place was such desperate prose.']
reference: THERE MIGHT BE A BIT OF POETRY HERE AND THERE BUT MOST OF THIS PLACE WAS SUCH DESPERATE PROSE

transcript: [' Her regard shifted to the green stalks and leaves again and she started to move away.']
reference: HER REGARD SHIFTED TO THE GREEN STALKS AND LEAVES AGAIN AND SHE STARTED TO MOVE AWAY

transcript: [' Cotton is a wonderful thing, is it not boys? she said rather primly.']
reference: COTTON IS A WONDERFUL THING IS IT NOT BOYS SHE SAID RATHER PRIMLY

transcript: [' Miss Taylor did not know much about cotton, but at least one more remark seemed called for.']
reference: MISS TAYLOR DID NOT KNOW MUCH ABOUT COTTON BUT AT LEAST ONE MORE REMARK SEEMED CALLED FOR

transcript: [" Don't know well of all things inwardly commented miss taylor literally born in cotton and oh well as much as to ask what's the use she turned again to go"]
reference: DON'T KNOW WELL OF ALL THINGS INWARDLY COMMENTED MISS TAYLOR LITERALLY BORN IN COTTON AND OH WELL AS MUCH AS TO ASK WHAT'S THE USE SHE TURNED AGAIN TO GO

transcript: [" I suppose though it's too early for them. Then came the explosion."]
reference: I SUPPOSE THOUGH IT'S TOO EARLY FOR THEM THEN CAME THE EXPLOSION

transcript: [" Goobels don't grow on de tops o vines but on de ground on de roots lak yams is that so"]
reference: GOOBERS DON'T GROW ON THE TOPS OF VINES BUT UNDERGROUND ON THE ROOTS LIKE YAMS IS THAT SO

transcript: [" The golden fleece. It's the silver fleece. He hearkened."]
reference: THE GOLDEN FLEECE IT'S THE SILVER FLEECE HE HARKENED

transcript: [" Sometime you'll tell me please won't you?"]
reference: SOME TIME YOU'LL TELL ME PLEASE WON'T YOU

transcript: [' Now for one little half hour she had been a woman talking to a boy. No, not even that she had been talking, just talking. There were no persons in the conversation, just things, one thing cotton.']
reference: NOW FOR ONE LITTLE HALF HOUR SHE HAD BEEN A WOMAN TALKING TO A BOY NO NOT EVEN THAT SHE HAD BEEN TALKING JUST TALKING THERE WERE NO PERSONS IN THE CONVERSATION JUST THINGS ONE THING COTTON

transcript: [" The Honorable Charles Smith, Miss Sarah's brother, was walking swiftly up town from mister Easterly's Wall Street office and his face was pale."]
reference: THE HON CHARLES SMITH MISS SARAH'S BROTHER WAS WALKING SWIFTLY UPTOWN FROM MISTER EASTERLY'S WALL STREET OFFICE AND HIS FACE WAS PALE

transcript: [' At last the cotton combine was to all appearances an assured fact and he was slated for the senate.']
reference: AT LAST THE COTTON COMBINE WAS TO ALL APPEARANCES AN ASSURED FACT AND HE WAS SLATED FOR THE SENATE

transcript: [' Why should he not be as other men?']
reference: WHY SHOULD HE NOT BE AS OTHER MEN

transcript: [' She was not herself a notably intelligent woman she greatly admired intelligence or whatever looked to her like intelligence in others.']
reference: SHE WAS NOT HERSELF A NOTABLY INTELLIGENT WOMAN SHE GREATLY ADMIRED INTELLIGENCE OR WHATEVER LOOKED TO HER LIKE INTELLIGENCE IN OTHERS

transcript: [' As she awaited her guest she surveyed the table with both satisfaction and disquietude for her social functions were few. To night there were she checked them off on her fingers Sir James Crichton the rich English manufacturer and Lady Crichton, mister and missus vanderpool, mister Harry Cresswell and his sister, John Taylor and his sister, and mister Charles Smith whom the evening papers mentioned as likely to be United States Senator from New Jersey, a selection of guests that had been determined unknown to the hostess by the meeting of cotton interests earlier in the day.']
reference: AS SHE AWAITED HER GUESTS SHE SURVEYED THE TABLE WITH BOTH SATISFACTION AND DISQUIETUDE FOR HER SOCIAL FUNCTIONS WERE FEW TONIGHT THERE WERE SHE CHECKED THEM OFF ON HER FINGERS SIR JAMES CREIGHTON THE RICH ENGLISH MANUFACTURER AND LADY CREIGHTON MISTER AND MISSUS VANDERPOOL MISTER HARRY CRESSWELL AND HIS SISTER JOHN TAYLOR AND HIS SISTER AND MISTER CHARLES SMITH WHOM THE EVENING PAPERS MENTIONED AS LIKELY TO BE UNITED STATES SENATOR FROM NEW JERSEY A SELECTION OF GUESTS THAT HAD BEEN DETERMINED UNKNOWN TO THE HOSTESS BY THE MEETING OF COTTON INTERESTS EARLIER IN THE DAY

transcript: [' Missus Gray had met Southerners before but not intimately and she always had in mind vividly their cruelty to poor negroes a subject she made a point of introducing forthwith.']
reference: MISSUS GREY HAD MET SOUTHERNERS BEFORE BUT NOT INTIMATELY AND SHE ALWAYS HAD IN MIND VIVIDLY THEIR CRUELTY TO POOR NEGROES A SUBJECT SHE MADE A POINT OF INTRODUCING FORTHWITH

transcript: [' She was therefore most agreeably surprised to hear mister Cresswell express himself so cordially as approving of negro education.']
reference: SHE WAS THEREFORE MOST AGREEABLY SURPRISED TO HEAR MISTER CRESSWELL EXPRESS HIMSELF SO CORDIALLY AS APPROVING OF NEGRO EDUCATION

transcript: [' Do you believe in some education? asked Mary Taylor.']
reference: BUT YOU BELIEVE IN SOME EDUCATION ASKED MARY TAYLOR

transcript: [' I believe in the training of people to their highest capacity. The Englishman here heartily seconded him.']
reference: I BELIEVE IN THE TRAINING OF PEOPLE TO THEIR HIGHEST CAPACITY THE ENGLISHMAN HERE HEARTILY SECONDED HIM

transcript: [' But, Cresswell added significantly, capacity differs enormously between races.']
reference: BUT CRESSWELL ADDED SIGNIFICANTLY CAPACITY DIFFERS ENORMOUSLY BETWEEN RACES

transcript: [' The Vanderpools were sure of this and the Englishman instancing India became quite eloquent missus Gray was mystified but hardly dared admit it the general trend of the conversation seemed to be that most individuals needed to be submitted to the sharpest scrutiny before being allowed much education and as for the lower races it was simply criminal to open such useless opportunities to them.']
reference: THE VANDERPOOLS WERE SURE OF THIS AND THE ENGLISHMAN INSTANCING INDIA BECAME QUITE ELOQUENT MISSUS GREY WAS MYSTIFIED BUT HARDLY DARED ADMIT IT THE GENERAL TREND OF THE CONVERSATION SEEMED TO BE THAT MOST INDIVIDUALS NEEDED TO BE SUBMITTED TO THE SHARPEST SCRUTINY BEFORE BEING ALLOWED MUCH EDUCATION AND AS FOR THE LOWER RACES IT WAS SIMPLY CRIMINAL TO OPEN SUCH USELESS OPPORTUNITIES TO THEM

transcript: [' positively heroic," added Cresswell, avoiding his sister\'s eyes.']
reference: POSITIVELY HEROIC ADDED CRESSWELL AVOIDING HIS SISTER'S EYES

transcript: [" but we're not er xactly welcome"]
reference: BUT WE'RE NOT ER EXACTLY WELCOMED

transcript: [" Mary Taylor however related the tale of Zora to missus Gray's private ear later."]
reference: MARY TAYLOR HOWEVER RELATED THE TALE OF ZORA TO MISSUS GREY'S PRIVATE EAR LATER

transcript: [' Fortunately, said mister vanderpoel, northerners and southerners are arriving at a better mutual understanding on most of these matters.']
reference: FORTUNATELY SAID MISTER VANDERPOOL NORTHERNERS AND SOUTHERNERS ARE ARRIVING AT A BETTER MUTUAL UNDERSTANDING ON MOST OF THESE MATTERS

transcript: [" He knew the Silver Fleece, his and Zorra's, must be ruined."]
reference: HE KNEW THE SILVER FLEECE HIS AND ZORA'S MUST BE RUINED

transcript: [' It was the first great sorrow of his life. It was not so much the loss of the cotton itself, but the fantasy, the hopes, the dreams built around it.']
reference: IT WAS THE FIRST GREAT SORROW OF HIS LIFE IT WAS NOT SO MUCH THE LOSS OF THE COTTON ITSELF BUT THE FANTASY THE HOPES THE DREAMS BUILT AROUND IT

transcript: [' Ah, the swamp, the cruel swamp!']
reference: AH THE SWAMP THE CRUEL SWAMP

transcript: [' The revelation of his love lighted and brightened slowly till it flamed like a sunrise over him and left him in burning wonder.']
reference: THE REVELATION OF HIS LOVE LIGHTED AND BRIGHTENED SLOWLY TILL IT FLAMED LIKE A SUNRISE OVER HIM AND LEFT HIM IN BURNING WONDER

transcript: [' He panted to know if she too knew or knew and cared not or cared and knew not.']
reference: HE PANTED TO KNOW IF SHE TOO KNEW OR KNEW AND CARED NOT OR CARED AND KNEW NOT

transcript: [' She was so strange and human a creature.']
reference: SHE WAS SO STRANGE AND HUMAN A CREATURE

transcript: [' The world was water veiled in mists.']
reference: THE WORLD WAS WATER VEILED IN MISTS

transcript: [' Then of a sudden at midday the sun shot out hot and still no breath of air stirred the sky was like blue steel the earth steamed.']
reference: THEN OF A SUDDEN AT MIDDAY THE SUN SHOT OUT HOT AND STILL NO BREATH OF AIR STIRRED THE SKY WAS LIKE BLUE STEEL THE EARTH STEAMED

transcript: [' Where was the use of imagining?']
reference: WHERE WAS THE USE OF IMAGINING

transcript: [' The lagoon had been level with the dikes a week ago and now?']
reference: THE LAGOON HAD BEEN LEVEL WITH THE DYKES A WEEK AGO AND NOW

transcript: [' Perhaps she too might be there, waiting, weeping.']
reference: PERHAPS SHE TOO MIGHT BE THERE WAITING WEEPING

transcript: [' He started at the thought. He hurried forth sadly.']
reference: HE STARTED AT THE THOUGHT HE HURRIED FORTH SADLY

transcript: [' He splashed and stamped along farther and farther onward until he neared the rampart of the clearing and put foot upon the tree bridge.']
reference: HE SPLASHED AND STAMPED ALONG FARTHER AND FARTHER ONWARD UNTIL HE NEARED THE RAMPART OF THE CLEARING AND PUT FOOT UPON THE TREE BRIDGE

transcript: [' Then he looked down. The lagoon was dry.']
reference: THEN HE LOOKED DOWN THE LAGOON WAS DRY

transcript: [' He stood a moment bewildered then turned and rushed upon the island a great sheet of dazzling sunlight swept the place and beneath lay a mighty mass of olive green thick tall wet and willowy.']
reference: HE STOOD A MOMENT BEWILDERED THEN TURNED AND RUSHED UPON THE ISLAND A GREAT SHEET OF DAZZLING SUNLIGHT SWEPT THE PLACE AND BENEATH LAY A MIGHTY MASS OF OLIVE GREEN THICK TALL WET AND WILLOWY

transcript: [' The squares of cotton sharp edged heavy were just about to burst to bowls.']
reference: THE SQUARES OF COTTON SHARP EDGED HEAVY WERE JUST ABOUT TO BURST TO BOLLS

transcript: [' For one long moment he paused stupid agape with utter amazement then leaned dizzily against a tree.']
reference: FOR ONE LONG MOMENT HE PAUSED STUPID AGAPE WITH UTTER AMAZEMENT THEN LEANED DIZZILY AGAINST A TREE

transcript: [' He gazed about perplexed, astonished.']
reference: HE GAZED ABOUT PERPLEXED ASTONISHED

transcript: [' Here lay the reading of the riddle with infinite work and pain some one had dug a canal from the lagoon to the creek into which the former had drained by a long and crooked way thus allowing it to empty directly.']
reference: HERE LAY THE READING OF THE RIDDLE WITH INFINITE WORK AND PAIN SOME ONE HAD DUG A CANAL FROM THE LAGOON TO THE CREEK INTO WHICH THE FORMER HAD DRAINED BY A LONG AND CROOKED WAY THUS ALLOWING IT TO EMPTY DIRECTLY

transcript: [' He sat down weak, bewildered, and one thought was uppermost Zora!']
reference: HE SAT DOWN WEAK BEWILDERED AND ONE THOUGHT WAS UPPERMOST ZORA

transcript: [' The years of the days of her dying were ten.']
reference: THE YEARS OF THE DAYS OF HER DYING WERE TEN

transcript: [' The hope and dream of harvest was upon the land.']
reference: THE HOPE AND DREAM OF HARVEST WAS UPON THE LAND

transcript: [' Up in the sick room Zora lay on the little white bed.']
reference: UP IN THE SICK ROOM ZORA LAY ON THE LITTLE WHITE BED

transcript: [' The net and web of endless things had been crawling and creeping around her. She had struggled in dumb speechless terror against some mighty grasping that strove for her life with gnarled and creeping fingers, but now at last weakly she opened her eyes and questioned.']
reference: THE NET AND WEB OF ENDLESS THINGS HAD BEEN CRAWLING AND CREEPING AROUND HER SHE HAD STRUGGLED IN DUMB SPEECHLESS TERROR AGAINST SOME MIGHTY GRASPING THAT STROVE FOR HER LIFE WITH GNARLED AND CREEPING FINGERS BUT NOW AT LAST WEAKLY SHE OPENED HER EYES AND QUESTIONED

transcript: [' For a while she lay in her chair in happy dreamy pleasure at sun and bird and tree.']
reference: FOR A WHILE SHE LAY IN HER CHAIR IN HAPPY DREAMY PLEASURE AT SUN AND BIRD AND TREE

transcript: [' She rose with a fleeting glance gathered the shawl around her then gliding forward wavering tremulous slipped across the road and into the swamp.']
reference: SHE ROSE WITH A FLEETING GLANCE GATHERED THE SHAWL ROUND HER THEN GLIDING FORWARD WAVERING TREMULOUS SLIPPED ACROSS THE ROAD AND INTO THE SWAMP

transcript: [' She had been born within its borders within its borders she had lived and grown and within its borders she had met her love.']
reference: SHE HAD BEEN BORN WITHIN ITS BORDERS WITHIN ITS BORDERS SHE HAD LIVED AND GROWN AND WITHIN ITS BORDERS SHE HAD MET HER LOVE

transcript: [' On she hurried until sweeping down to the lagoon and the island lo the cotton lay before her.']
reference: ON SHE HURRIED UNTIL SWEEPING DOWN TO THE LAGOON AND THE ISLAND LO THE COTTON LAY BEFORE HER

transcript: [' The chair was empty, but he knew.']
reference: THE CHAIR WAS EMPTY BUT HE KNEW

transcript: [' He darted through the trees and paused a tall man strongly but slimly made.']
reference: HE DARTED THROUGH THE TREES AND PAUSED A TALL MAN STRONGLY BUT SLIMLY MADE

transcript: [' It is a very fine old place of red brick softened by a pale powdery lichen which has dispersed itself with happy irregularity so as to bring the red brick into terms of friendly companionship with the limestone ornaments surrounding the three gables the windows and the door place.']
reference: IT IS A VERY FINE OLD PLACE OF RED BRICK SOFTENED BY A PALE POWDERY LICHEN WHICH HAS DISPERSED ITSELF WITH HAPPY IRREGULARITY SO AS TO BRING THE RED BRICK INTO TERMS OF FRIENDLY COMPANIONSHIP WITH THE LIMESTONE ORNAMENTS SURROUNDING THE THREE GABLES THE WINDOWS AND THE DOOR PLACE

transcript: [' But the windows are patched with wooden panes and the door, I think, is like the gate. It is never opened.']
reference: BUT THE WINDOWS ARE PATCHED WITH WOODEN PANES AND THE DOOR I THINK IS LIKE THE GATE IT IS NEVER OPENED

transcript: [' For it is a solid heavy handsome door and must once have been in the habit of shutting with a sonorous bang behind the liveried lackey who had just seen his master and mistress off the grounds in a carriage and pair.']
reference: FOR IT IS A SOLID HEAVY HANDSOME DOOR AND MUST ONCE HAVE BEEN IN THE HABIT OF SHUTTING WITH A SONOROUS BANG BEHIND A LIVERIED LACKEY WHO HAD JUST SEEN HIS MASTER AND MISTRESS OFF THE GROUNDS IN A CARRIAGE AND PAIR

transcript: [' a large open fireplace with rusty dogs in it and a bare boarded floor at the far end fleeces of wool stacked up in the middle of the floor some empty corn bags']
reference: A LARGE OPEN FIREPLACE WITH RUSTY DOGS IN IT AND A BARE BOARDED FLOOR AT THE FAR END FLEECES OF WOOL STACKED UP IN THE MIDDLE OF THE FLOOR SOME EMPTY CORN BAGS

transcript: [' And what through the left hand window?']
reference: AND WHAT THROUGH THE LEFT HAND WINDOW

transcript: [' several clothes horses a pillion a spinning wheel and an old box wide open and stuffed full of colored rags']
reference: SEVERAL CLOTHES HORSES A PILLION A SPINNING WHEEL AND AN OLD BOX WIDE OPEN AND STUFFED FULL OF COLOURED RAGS

transcript: [' At the edge of this box there lies a great wooden doll which so far as mutilation is concerned bears a strong resemblance to the finest greek sculpture and especially in the total loss of its nose.']
reference: AT THE EDGE OF THIS BOX THERE LIES A GREAT WOODEN DOLL WHICH SO FAR AS MUTILATION IS CONCERNED BEARS A STRONG RESEMBLANCE TO THE FINEST GREEK SCULPTURE AND ESPECIALLY IN THE TOTAL LOSS OF ITS NOSE

transcript: [' The history of the house is plain now.']
reference: THE HISTORY OF THE HOUSE IS PLAIN NOW

transcript: [' But there is always a stronger sense of life when the sun is brilliant after rain and now he is pouring down his beams and making sparkles among the wet straw and lighting up every patch of vivid green moss on the red tiles of the cowshed and turning even the muddy water that is hurrying along the channel to the drain into a mirror for the yellow billed ducks who are seizing the opportunity of getting a drink with as much body in it as possible.']
reference: BUT THERE IS ALWAYS A STRONGER SENSE OF LIFE WHEN THE SUN IS BRILLIANT AFTER RAIN AND NOW HE IS POURING DOWN HIS BEAMS AND MAKING SPARKLES AMONG THE WET STRAW AND LIGHTING UP EVERY PATCH OF VIVID GREEN MOSS ON THE RED TILES OF THE COW SHED AND TURNING EVEN THE MUDDY WATER THAT IS HURRYING ALONG THE CHANNEL TO THE DRAIN INTO A MIRROR FOR THE YELLOW BILLED DUCKS WHO ARE SEIZING THE OPPORTUNITY OF GETTING A DRINK WITH AS MUCH BODY IN IT AS POSSIBLE

transcript: [' For the great barn doors are thrown wide open and men are busy there mending the harness under the superintendence of mister Goby the widow otherwise saddler who entertains them with the latest treddleston gossip.']
reference: FOR THE GREAT BARN DOORS ARE THROWN WIDE OPEN AND MEN ARE BUSY THERE MENDING THE HARNESS UNDER THE SUPERINTENDENCE OF MISTER GOBY THE WHITTAW OTHERWISE SADDLER WHO ENTERTAINS THEM WITH THE LATEST TREDDLESTON GOSSIP

transcript: [" Hetty Searle often took the opportunity when her aunt's back was turned of looking at the pleasing reflection of herself in those polished services for the oak table was usually turned up like a screen and was more for ornament than for use and she could see herself sometimes in the great round pewter dishes that were ranged on the shelves above the long deal dinner table or in the hobs of the grate which always shone like jasper."]
reference: HETTY SORREL OFTEN TOOK THE OPPORTUNITY WHEN HER AUNT'S BACK WAS TURNED OF LOOKING AT THE PLEASING REFLECTION OF HERSELF IN THOSE POLISHED SURFACES FOR THE OAK TABLE WAS USUALLY TURNED UP LIKE A SCREEN AND WAS MORE FOR ORNAMENT THAN FOR USE AND SHE COULD SEE HERSELF SOMETIMES IN THE GREAT ROUND PEWTER DISHES THAT WERE RANGED ON THE SHELVES ABOVE THE LONG DEAL DINNER TABLE OR IN THE HOBS OF THE GRATE WHICH ALWAYS SHONE LIKE JASPER

transcript: [' Do not suppose however that missus Poyser was elderly or shrewish in her appearance she was a good looking woman not more than eight and thirty of fair complexion and sandy hair well shapen light footed']
reference: DO NOT SUPPOSE HOWEVER THAT MISSUS POYSER WAS ELDERLY OR SHREWISH IN HER APPEARANCE SHE WAS A GOOD LOOKING WOMAN NOT MORE THAN EIGHT AND THIRTY OF FAIR COMPLEXION AND SANDY HAIR WELL SHAPEN LIGHT FOOTED

transcript: [" The family likeness between her and her niece dinah Morris with the contrast between her keenness and dinah's seraphic gentleness of expression might have served a painter as an excellent suggestion for a martha and mary."]
reference: THE FAMILY LIKENESS BETWEEN HER AND HER NIECE DINAH MORRIS WITH THE CONTRAST BETWEEN HER KEENNESS AND DINAH'S SERAPHIC GENTLENESS OF EXPRESSION MIGHT HAVE SERVED A PAINTER AS AN EXCELLENT SUGGESTION FOR A MARTHA AND MARY

transcript: [' Her tongue was not less keen than her eye, and whenever a damsel came within earshot seemed to take up an unfinished lecture as a barrel organ takes up a tune precisely at the point where it had left off.']
reference: HER TONGUE WAS NOT LESS KEEN THAN HER EYE AND WHENEVER A DAMSEL CAME WITHIN EARSHOT SEEMED TO TAKE UP AN UNFINISHED LECTURE AS A BARREL ORGAN TAKES UP A TUNE PRECISELY AT THE POINT WHERE IT HAD LEFT OFF

transcript: [' The fact that it was churning day was another reason why it was inconvenient to have the widows and why consequently missus poyser should scold molly the housemaid with unusual severity']
reference: THE FACT THAT IT WAS CHURNING DAY WAS ANOTHER REASON WHY IT WAS INCONVENIENT TO HAVE THE WHITTAWS AND WHY CONSEQUENTLY MISSUS POYSER SHOULD SCOLD MOLLY THE HOUSEMAID WITH UNUSUAL SEVERITY

transcript: [' To all appearance molly had got through her after dinner work in an exemplary manner had cleaned herself with great dispatch and now came to ask submissively if she should sit down to her spinning till milking time']
reference: TO ALL APPEARANCE MOLLY HAD GOT THROUGH HER AFTER DINNER WORK IN AN EXEMPLARY MANNER HAD CLEANED HERSELF WITH GREAT DISPATCH AND NOW CAME TO ASK SUBMISSIVELY IF SHE SHOULD SIT DOWN TO HER SPINNING TILL MILKING TIME

transcript: [' Spinning indeed.']
reference: SPINNING INDEED

transcript: [' I never knew your equals for gallowsness.']
reference: I NEVER KNEW YOUR EQUALS FOR GALLOWSNESS

transcript: [' Who taught you to scrub a floor, I should like to know?']
reference: WHO TAUGHT YOU TO SCRUB A FLOOR I SHOULD LIKE TO KNOW

transcript: [' Comb the wool for the widows indeed.']
reference: COMB THE WOOL FOR THE WHITTAWS INDEED

transcript: [" That's what you'd like to be doing, is it?"]
reference: THAT'S WHAT YOU'D LIKE TO BE DOING IS IT

transcript: [" That's the way with you. That's the road you'd all like to go headlongs to ruin."]
reference: THAT'S THE WAY WITH YOU THAT'S THE ROAD YOU'D ALL LIKE TO GO HEADLONGS TO RUIN

transcript: [" mister Oatley's indeed."]
reference: MISTER OTTLEY'S INDEED

transcript: [" You are a rare un for sitting down to your work a little while after it's time to put by."]
reference: YOU'RE A RARE UN FOR SITTING DOWN TO YOUR WORK A LITTLE WHILE AFTER IT'S TIME TO PUT BY

transcript: [" Money, my iron's quite told. Please put it down to warm."]
reference: MUNNY MY IRON'S TWITE TOLD PEASE PUT IT DOWN TO WARM

transcript: [' cold is it my darling bless your sweet face']
reference: COLD IS IT MY DARLING BLESS YOUR SWEET FACE

transcript: [" She's going to put the ironing things away."]
reference: SHE'S GOING TO PUT THE IRONING THINGS AWAY

transcript: [' money i did like to do into the barn to tommy to see the widowed']
reference: MUNNY I TOULD IKE TO DO INTO DE BARN TO TOMMY TO SEE DE WHITTAWD

transcript: [" No, no, no, totty'd get her feet wet, said missus Poyser carrying away her iron."]
reference: NO NO NO TOTTY UD GET HER FEET WET SAID MISSUS POYSER CARRYING AWAY HER IRON

transcript: [' Did ever anybody see the like? screamed missus poyser running towards the table when her eye had fallen on the blue stream.']
reference: DID EVER ANYBODY SEE THE LIKE SCREAMED MISSUS POYSER RUNNING TOWARDS THE TABLE WHEN HER EYE HAD FALLEN ON THE BLUE STREAM

transcript: [' Totty however had descended from her chair with great swiftness and was already in retreat towards the dairy with a sort of waddling run and an amount of fat on the nape of her neck which made her look like the metamorphosis of a white sucking pig.']
reference: TOTTY HOWEVER HAD DESCENDED FROM HER CHAIR WITH GREAT SWIFTNESS AND WAS ALREADY IN RETREAT TOWARDS THE DAIRY WITH A SORT OF WADDLING RUN AND AN AMOUNT OF FAT ON THE NAPE OF HER NECK WHICH MADE HER LOOK LIKE THE METAMORPHOSIS OF A WHITE SUCKLING PIG

transcript: [' And she was very fond of you too, aunt Rachel.']
reference: AND SHE WAS VERY FOND OF YOU TOO AUNT RACHEL

transcript: [' I often heard her talk of you in the same sort of way']
reference: I OFTEN HEARD HER TALK OF YOU IN THE SAME SORT OF WAY

transcript: [" When she had that bad illness and I was only eleven years old she used to say you'll have a friend on earth in your aunt Rachel if I'm taken from you for she has a kind heart and I'm sure I've found it so"]
reference: WHEN SHE HAD THAT BAD ILLNESS AND I WAS ONLY ELEVEN YEARS OLD SHE USED TO SAY YOU'LL HAVE A FRIEND ON EARTH IN YOUR AUNT RACHEL IF I'M TAKEN FROM YOU FOR SHE HAS A KIND HEART AND I'M SURE I'VE FOUND IT SO

transcript: [" And there's linen in the house as I could well spare you for I got lots of sheeting and table clothing and towelling as isn't made up."]
reference: AND THERE'S LINEN IN THE HOUSE AS I COULD WELL SPARE YOU FOR I'VE GOT LOTS O SHEETING AND TABLE CLOTHING AND TOWELLING AS ISN'T MADE UP

transcript: [" But not more than what's in the bible, aunt said dinah."]
reference: BUT NOT MORE THAN WHAT'S IN THE BIBLE AUNT SAID DINAH

transcript: [' Nay dear aunt, you never heard me say that all people are called to forsake their work and their families.']
reference: NAY DEAR AUNT YOU NEVER HEARD ME SAY THAT ALL PEOPLE ARE CALLED TO FORSAKE THEIR WORK AND THEIR FAMILIES

transcript: [' We can all be servants of God wherever our lot is cast, but he gives us different sorts of work according as he fits us for it and calls us to it.']
reference: WE CAN ALL BE SERVANTS OF GOD WHEREVER OUR LOT IS CAST BUT HE GIVES US DIFFERENT SORTS OF WORK ACCORDING AS HE FITS US FOR IT AND CALLS US TO IT

transcript: [" I can no more help spending my life in trying to do what I can for the souls of others than you could help running if you heard little tottie crying at the other end of the house the voice would go to your heart you would think the dear child was in trouble or in danger and you couldn't rest without running to help her and comfort her"]
reference: I CAN NO MORE HELP SPENDING MY LIFE IN TRYING TO DO WHAT I CAN FOR THE SOULS OF OTHERS THAN YOU COULD HELP RUNNING IF YOU HEARD LITTLE TOTTY CRYING AT THE OTHER END OF THE HOUSE THE VOICE WOULD GO TO YOUR HEART YOU WOULD THINK THE DEAR CHILD WAS IN TROUBLE OR IN DANGER AND YOU COULDN'T REST WITHOUT RUNNING TO HELP HER AND COMFORT HER

transcript: [" I've strong assurance that no evil will happen to you and my uncle and the children from anything I've done."]
reference: I'VE STRONG ASSURANCE THAT NO EVIL WILL HAPPEN TO YOU AND MY UNCLE AND THE CHILDREN FROM ANYTHING I'VE DONE

transcript: [" I didn't preach without direction."]
reference: I DIDN'T PREACH WITHOUT DIRECTION

transcript: [' Direction']
reference: DIRECTION

transcript: [' I had a common patience with you.']
reference: I HANNA COMMON PATIENCE WITH YOU

transcript: [' By this time the two gentlemen had reached the palings and had got down from their horses it was plain they meant to come in.']
reference: BY THIS TIME THE TWO GENTLEMEN HAD REACHED THE PALINGS AND HAD GOT DOWN FROM THEIR HORSES IT WAS PLAIN THEY MEANT TO COME IN

transcript: [' said mister Irwin with his stately cordiality.']
reference: SAID MISTER IRWINE WITH HIS STATELY CORDIALITY

transcript: [" Oh, sir, don't mention it, said missus Poyser."]
reference: OH SIR DON'T MENTION IT SAID MISSUS POYSER

transcript: [' I delight in your kitchen.']
reference: I DELIGHT IN YOUR KITCHEN

transcript: [' Poyser is not at home is he?']
reference: POYSER IS NOT AT HOME IS HE

transcript: [' said Captain Donnythorne seating himself where he could see along the short passage to the open dairy door.']
reference: SAID CAPTAIN DONNITHORNE SEATING HIMSELF WHERE HE COULD SEE ALONG THE SHORT PASSAGE TO THE OPEN DAIRY DOOR

transcript: [" No, sir, he isn't. He's gone to rossiter to see mister West, the factor, about the wool."]
reference: NO SIR HE ISN'T HE'S GONE TO ROSSETER TO SEE MISTER WEST THE FACTOR ABOUT THE WOOL

transcript: [" But there's father in the barn, sir, if he'd be of any use."]
reference: BUT THERE'S FATHER THE BARN SIR IF HE'D BE OF ANY USE

transcript: [" No, thank you. I'll just look at the whelps and leave a message about them with your shepherd."]
reference: NO THANK YOU I'LL JUST LOOK AT THE WHELPS AND LEAVE A MESSAGE ABOUT THEM WITH YOUR SHEPHERD

transcript: [' I must come another day and see your husband. I want to have a consultation with him about horses.']
reference: I MUST COME ANOTHER DAY AND SEE YOUR HUSBAND I WANT TO HAVE A CONSULTATION WITH HIM ABOUT HORSES

transcript: [" For if he's anywhere on the farm we can send for him in a minute."]
reference: FOR IF HE'S ANYWHERE ON THE FARM WE CAN SEND FOR HIM IN A MINUTE

transcript: [" Oh, sir said missus poyser rather alarmed you wouldn't like it at all."]
reference: OH SIR SAID MISSUS POYSER RATHER ALARMED YOU WOULDN'T LIKE IT AT ALL

transcript: [' But you know more about that than I do sir.']
reference: BUT YOU KNOW MORE ABOUT THAT THAN I DO SIR

transcript: [' I think I should be doing you a service to turn you out of such a place.']
reference: I THINK I SHOULD BE DOING YOU A SERVICE TO TURN YOU OUT OF SUCH A PLACE

transcript: [" I know his farm is in better order than any other within ten miles of us and as for the kitchen he added smiling I don't believe there's one in the kingdom to beat it"]
reference: I KNOW HIS FARM IS IN BETTER ORDER THAN ANY OTHER WITHIN TEN MILES OF US AND AS FOR THE KITCHEN HE ADDED SMILING I DON'T BELIEVE THERE'S ONE IN THE KINGDOM TO BEAT IT

transcript: [' By the bye, I have never seen your dairy. I must see your dairy, missus Poyser.']
reference: BY THE BY I'VE NEVER SEEN YOUR DAIRY I MUST SEE YOUR DAIRY MISSUS POYSER

transcript: [' This missus Poyser said blushing and believing that the captain was really interested in her milk pans and would adjust his opinion of her to the appearance of her dairy']
reference: THIS MISSUS POYSER SAID BLUSHING AND BELIEVING THAT THE CAPTAIN WAS REALLY INTERESTED IN HER MILK PANS AND WOULD ADJUST HIS OPINION OF HER TO THE APPEARANCE OF HER DAIRY

transcript: [" Oh, I've no doubt. It's in capital order."]
reference: OH I'VE NO DOUBT IT'S IN CAPITAL ORDER

transcript: [' The Paris plant, like that at the Crystal Palace, was a temporary exhibit.']
reference: THE PARIS PLANT LIKE THAT AT THE CRYSTAL PALACE WAS A TEMPORARY EXHIBIT

transcript: [' The London plant was less temporary but not permanent supplying before it was torn out no fewer than three thousand lamps in hotels churches stores and dwellings in the vicinity of holborn viaduct.']
reference: THE LONDON PLANT WAS LESS TEMPORARY BUT NOT PERMANENT SUPPLYING BEFORE IT WAS TORN OUT NO FEWER THAN THREE THOUSAND LAMPS IN HOTELS CHURCHES STORES AND DWELLINGS IN THE VICINITY OF HOLBORN VIADUCT

transcript: [' There messrs Johnson and Hammer put into practice many of the ideas now standard in the art and secured much useful data for the work in New York of which the story has just been told.']
reference: THERE MESSRS JOHNSON AND HAMMER PUT INTO PRACTICE MANY OF THE IDEAS NOW STANDARD IN THE ART AND SECURED MUCH USEFUL DATA FOR THE WORK IN NEW YORK OF WHICH THE STORY HAS JUST BEEN TOLD

transcript: [' The dynamo electric machine though small was robust for under all the varying speeds of water power and the vicissitudes of the plant to which it belonged it continued in active use until eighteen ninety nine seventeen years.']
reference: THE DYNAMO ELECTRIC MACHINE THOUGH SMALL WAS ROBUST FOR UNDER ALL THE VARYING SPEEDS OF WATER POWER AND THE VICISSITUDES OF THE PLANT TO WHICH IT BELONGED IT CONTINUED IN ACTIVE USE UNTIL EIGHTEEN NINETY NINE SEVENTEEN YEARS

transcript: [' Owing to his insistence on low pressure direct current for use in densely populated districts as the only safe and truly universal profitable way of delivering electrical energy to the consumers, Edison has been frequently spoken of as an opponent of the alternating current.']
reference: OWING TO HIS INSISTENCE ON LOW PRESSURE DIRECT CURRENT FOR USE IN DENSELY POPULATED DISTRICTS AS THE ONLY SAFE AND TRULY UNIVERSAL PROFITABLE WAY OF DELIVERING ELECTRICAL ENERGY TO THE CONSUMERS EDISON HAS BEEN FREQUENTLY SPOKEN OF AS AN OPPONENT OF THE ALTERNATING CURRENT

transcript: [' Why, if we erect a station at the falls, it is a great economy to get it up to the city.']
reference: WHY IF WE ERECT A STATION AT THE FALLS IT IS A GREAT ECONOMY TO GET IT UP TO THE CITY

transcript: [' There seems no good reason for believing that it will change.']
reference: THERE SEEMS NO GOOD REASON FOR BELIEVING THAT IT WILL CHANGE

transcript: [' Broad as the prairies and free in thought as the winds that swept them, he is idiosyncratically opposed to loose and wasteful methods, to plans of empire that neglect the poor at the gate.']
reference: BROAD AS THE PRAIRIES AND FREE IN THOUGHT AS THE WINDS THAT SWEEP THEM HE IS IDIOSYNCRATICALLY OPPOSED TO LOOSE AND WASTEFUL METHODS TO PLANS OF EMPIRE THAT NEGLECT THE POOR AT THE GATE

transcript: [' Everything he has done has been aimed at the conservation of energy, the contraction of space, the intensification of culture.']
reference: EVERYTHING HE HAS DONE HAS BEEN AIMED AT THE CONSERVATION OF ENERGY THE CONTRACTION OF SPACE THE INTENSIFICATION OF CULTURE

transcript: [' For some years it was not found feasible to operate motors on alternating current circuits, and that reason was often urged against it seriously.']
reference: FOR SOME YEARS IT WAS NOT FOUND FEASIBLE TO OPERATE MOTORS ON ALTERNATING CURRENT CIRCUITS AND THAT REASON WAS OFTEN URGED AGAINST IT SERIOUSLY

transcript: [' It could not be used for electroplating or deposition, nor could it charge storage batteries, all of which are easily within the ability of the direct current.']
reference: IT COULD NOT BE USED FOR ELECTROPLATING OR DEPOSITION NOR COULD IT CHARGE STORAGE BATTERIES ALL OF WHICH ARE EASILY WITHIN THE ABILITY OF THE DIRECT CURRENT

transcript: [' But when it came to be a question of lighting a scattered suburb a group of dwellings on the outskirts a remote country residence or a farm house the alternating current in all elements save its danger was and is ideal.']
reference: BUT WHEN IT CAME TO BE A QUESTION OF LIGHTING A SCATTERED SUBURB A GROUP OF DWELLINGS ON THE OUTSKIRTS A REMOTE COUNTRY RESIDENCE OR A FARM HOUSE THE ALTERNATING CURRENT IN ALL ELEMENTS SAVE ITS DANGER WAS AND IS IDEAL

transcript: [' Edison was intolerant of sham and shoddy, and nothing would satisfy him that could not stand cross examination by microscope test tube and galvanometer.']
reference: EDISON WAS INTOLERANT OF SHAM AND SHODDY AND NOTHING WOULD SATISFY HIM THAT COULD NOT STAND CROSS EXAMINATION BY MICROSCOPE TEST TUBE AND GALVANOMETER

transcript: [' Unless he could secure an engine of smoother running and more exactly governed and regulated than those available for his dynamo and lamp, Edison realized that he would find it almost impossible to give a steady light.']
reference: UNLESS HE COULD SECURE AN ENGINE OF SMOOTHER RUNNING AND MORE EXACTLY GOVERNED AND REGULATED THAN THOSE AVAILABLE FOR HIS DYNAMO AND LAMP EDISON REALIZED THAT HE WOULD FIND IT ALMOST IMPOSSIBLE TO GIVE A STEADY LIGHT

transcript: [' mister edison was a leader far ahead of the time.']
reference: MISTER EDISON WAS A LEADER FAR AHEAD OF THE TIME

transcript: [' He obtained the desired speed and load with a friction brake, also regulator of speed, but waited for an indicator to verify it.']
reference: HE OBTAINED THE DESIRED SPEED AND LOAD WITH A FRICTION BRAKE ALSO REGULATOR OF SPEED BUT WAITED FOR AN INDICATOR TO VERIFY IT

transcript: [' Then again there was no known way to lubricate an engine for continuous running, and mister Edison informed me that as a marine engine started before the ship left New York and continued running until it reached its home port, so an engine for his purposes must produce light at all times.']
reference: THEN AGAIN THERE WAS NO KNOWN WAY TO LUBRICATE AN ENGINE FOR CONTINUOUS RUNNING AND MISTER EDISON INFORMED ME THAT AS A MARINE ENGINE STARTED BEFORE THE SHIP LEFT NEW YORK AND CONTINUED RUNNING UNTIL IT REACHED ITS HOME PORT SO AN ENGINE FOR HIS PURPOSES MUST PRODUCE LIGHT AT ALL TIMES

transcript: [' Edison had installed his historic first great central station system in New York on the multiple arc system covered by his feeder and main invention, which resulted in a notable saving in the cost of conductors as against a straight two wire system throughout of the tree kind.']
reference: EDISON HAD INSTALLED HIS HISTORIC FIRST GREAT CENTRAL STATION SYSTEM IN NEW YORK ON THE MULTIPLE ARC SYSTEM COVERED BY HIS FEEDER AND MAIN INVENTION WHICH RESULTED IN A NOTABLE SAVING IN THE COST OF CONDUCTORS AS AGAINST A STRAIGHT TWO WIRE SYSTEM THROUGHOUT OF THE TREE KIND

transcript: [' He soon foresaw that still greater economy would be necessary for commercial success not alone for the larger territory opening but for the compact district of large cities.']
reference: HE SOON FORESAW THAT STILL GREATER ECONOMY WOULD BE NECESSARY FOR COMMERCIAL SUCCESS NOT ALONE FOR THE LARGER TERRITORY OPENING BUT FOR THE COMPACT DISTRICTS OF LARGE CITIES

transcript: [' The strong position held by the edison system under the strenuous competition that was already springing up was enormously improved by the introduction of the three wire system and it gave an immediate impetus to incandescent lighting.']
reference: THE STRONG POSITION HELD BY THE EDISON SYSTEM UNDER THE STRENUOUS COMPETITION THAT WAS ALREADY SPRINGING UP WAS ENORMOUSLY IMPROVED BY THE INTRODUCTION OF THE THREE WIRE SYSTEM AND IT GAVE AN IMMEDIATE IMPETUS TO INCANDESCENT LIGHTING

transcript: [' It was specially suited for a trial plant also in the early days when a yield of six or eight lamps to the horse power was considered subject for congratulation.']
reference: IT WAS SPECIALLY SUITED FOR A TRIAL PLANT ALSO IN THE EARLY DAYS WHEN A YIELD OF SIX OR EIGHT LAMPS TO THE HORSE POWER WAS CONSIDERED SUBJECT FOR CONGRATULATION

transcript: [' The street conductors were of the overhead pole line construction and were installed by the construction company that had been organized by edison to build and equip central stations.']
reference: THE STREET CONDUCTORS WERE OF THE OVERHEAD POLE LINE CONSTRUCTION AND WERE INSTALLED BY THE CONSTRUCTION COMPANY THAT HAD BEEN ORGANIZED BY EDISON TO BUILD AND EQUIP CENTRAL STATIONS

transcript: [' Meanwhile, he had called upon me to make a report of the three wire system known in England as the Hopkinson, both doctor John Hopkinson and mister Edison being independent inventors at practically the same time.']
reference: MEANWHILE HE HAD CALLED UPON ME TO MAKE A REPORT OF THE THREE WIRE SYSTEM KNOWN IN ENGLAND AS THE HOPKINSON BOTH DOCTOR JOHN HOPKINSON AND MISTER EDISON BEING INDEPENDENT INVENTORS AT PRACTICALLY THE SAME TIME

transcript: [' I think he was perhaps more appreciative that I was of the discipline of the Edison construction department and thought it would be well for us to wait until the morning of the fourth before we started up.']
reference: I THINK HE WAS PERHAPS MORE APPRECIATIVE THAN I WAS OF THE DISCIPLINE OF THE EDISON CONSTRUCTION DEPARTMENT AND THOUGHT IT WOULD BE WELL FOR US TO WAIT UNTIL THE MORNING OF THE FOURTH BEFORE WE STARTED UP

transcript: [' But the plant ran and it was the first three wire station in this country.']
reference: BUT THE PLANT RAN AND IT WAS THE FIRST THREE WIRE STATION IN THIS COUNTRY

transcript: [' They were later used as reserve machines and finally with the engine retired from service as part of the collection of Edisonia. But they remain in practically as good condition as when installed in eighteen eighty three.']
reference: THEY WERE LATER USED AS RESERVE MACHINES AND FINALLY WITH THE ENGINE RETIRED FROM SERVICE AS PART OF THE COLLECTION OF EDISONIA BUT THEY REMAIN IN PRACTICALLY AS GOOD CONDITION AS WHEN INSTALLED IN EIGHTEEN EIGHTY THREE

transcript: [" The arc lamp installed outside a customer's premises or in a circuit for public street lighting burned so many hours nightly so many nights in the month and was paid for at that rate subject to rebate for hours when the lamp might be out through accident."]
reference: THE ARC LAMP INSTALLED OUTSIDE A CUSTOMER'S PREMISES OR IN A CIRCUIT FOR PUBLIC STREET LIGHTING BURNED SO MANY HOURS NIGHTLY SO MANY NIGHTS IN THE MONTH AND WAS PAID FOR AT THAT RATE SUBJECT TO REBATE FOR HOURS WHEN THE LAMP MIGHT BE OUT THROUGH ACCIDENT

transcript: [' Edison held that the electricity sold must be measured just like gas or water, and he proceeded to develop a meter.']
reference: EDISON HELD THAT THE ELECTRICITY SOLD MUST BE MEASURED JUST LIKE GAS OR WATER AND HE PROCEEDED TO DEVELOP A METER

transcript: [' There was infinite scepticism around him on the subject and while other inventors were also giving the subject their thought the public took it for granted that anything so utterly intangible as electricity that could not be seen or weighed and only gave secondary evidence of itself at the exact point of use could not be brought to accurate registration.']
reference: THERE WAS INFINITE SCEPTICISM AROUND HIM ON THE SUBJECT AND WHILE OTHER INVENTORS WERE ALSO GIVING THE SUBJECT THEIR THOUGHT THE PUBLIC TOOK IT FOR GRANTED THAT ANYTHING SO UTTERLY INTANGIBLE AS ELECTRICITY THAT COULD NOT BE SEEN OR WEIGHED AND ONLY GAVE SECONDARY EVIDENCE OF ITSELF AT THE EXACT POINT OF USE COULD NOT BE BROUGHT TO ACCURATE REGISTRATION

transcript: [' Hence the Edison electrolytic meter is no longer used, despite its excellent qualities.']
reference: HENCE THE EDISON ELECTROLYTIC METER IS NO LONGER USED DESPITE ITS EXCELLENT QUALITIES

transcript: [' The principle employed in the Edison electrolytic meter is that which exemplifies the power of electricity to decompose a chemical substance.']
reference: THE PRINCIPLE EMPLOYED IN THE EDISON ELECTROLYTIC METER IS THAT WHICH EXEMPLIFIES THE POWER OF ELECTRICITY TO DECOMPOSE A CHEMICAL SUBSTANCE

transcript: [' Associated with this simple form of apparatus were various ingenious details and refinements to secure regularity of operation freedom from inaccuracy and immunity from such tampering as would permit theft of current or damage.']
reference: ASSOCIATED WITH THIS SIMPLE FORM OF APPARATUS WERE VARIOUS INGENIOUS DETAILS AND REFINEMENTS TO SECURE REGULARITY OF OPERATION FREEDOM FROM INACCURACY AND IMMUNITY FROM SUCH TAMPERING AS WOULD PERMIT THEFT OF CURRENT OR DAMAGE

transcript: [' The standard Edison meter practice was to remove the cells once a month to the meter room of the central station company for examination, another set being substituted.']
reference: THE STANDARD EDISON METER PRACTICE WAS TO REMOVE THE CELLS ONCE A MONTH TO THE METER ROOM OF THE CENTRAL STATION COMPANY FOR EXAMINATION ANOTHER SET BEING SUBSTITUTED

transcript: [' In December eighteen eighty eight mister w j Jenks read an interesting paper before the American Institute of Electrical Engineers on the six years of practical experience had up to that time with the meter then more generally in use than any other.']
reference: IN DECEMBER EIGHTEEN EIGHTY EIGHT MISTER W J JENKS READ AN INTERESTING PAPER BEFORE THE AMERICAN INSTITUTE OF ELECTRICAL ENGINEERS ON THE SIX YEARS OF PRACTICAL EXPERIENCE HAD UP TO THAT TIME WITH THE METER THEN MORE GENERALLY IN USE THAN ANY OTHER

transcript: [' the others having been in operation too short a time to show definite results, although they also went quickly to a dividend basis.']
reference: THE OTHERS HAVING BEEN IN OPERATION TOO SHORT A TIME TO SHOW DEFINITE RESULTS ALTHOUGH THEY ALSO WENT QUICKLY TO A DIVIDEND BASIS

transcript: [' In this connection it should be mentioned that the association of edison illuminating companies in the same year adopted resolutions unanimously to the effect that the edison meter was accurate and that its use was not expensive for stations above one thousand lights and that the best financial results were invariably secured in a station selling current by meter.']
reference: IN THIS CONNECTION IT SHOULD BE MENTIONED THAT THE ASSOCIATION OF EDISON ILLUMINATING COMPANIES IN THE SAME YEAR ADOPTED RESOLUTIONS UNANIMOUSLY TO THE EFFECT THAT THE EDISON METER WAS ACCURATE AND THAT ITS USE WAS NOT EXPENSIVE FOR STATIONS ABOVE ONE THOUSAND LIGHTS AND THAT THE BEST FINANCIAL RESULTS WERE INVARIABLY SECURED IN A STATION SELLING CURRENT BY METER

transcript: [' The meter continued in general service during eighteen ninety nine and probably up to the close of the century.']
reference: THE METER CONTINUED IN GENERAL SERVICE DURING EIGHTEEN NINETY NINE AND PROBABLY UP TO THE CLOSE OF THE CENTURY

transcript: [' He weighed and re weighed the meter plates and pursued every line of investigation imaginable but all in vain.']
reference: HE WEIGHED AND REWEIGHED THE METER PLATES AND PURSUED EVERY LINE OF INVESTIGATION IMAGINABLE BUT ALL IN VAIN

transcript: [' He felt he was up against it and that perhaps another kind of a job would suit him better.']
reference: HE FELT HE WAS UP AGAINST IT AND THAT PERHAPS ANOTHER KIND OF A JOB WOULD SUIT HIM BETTER

transcript: [' The problem was solved.']
reference: THE PROBLEM WAS SOLVED

transcript: [' We were more interested in the technical condition of the station than in the commercial part.']
reference: WE WERE MORE INTERESTED IN THE TECHNICAL CONDITION OF THE STATION THAN IN THE COMMERCIAL PART

transcript: [' We had meters in which there were two bottles of liquid.']
reference: WE HAD METERS IN WHICH THERE WERE TWO BOTTLES OF LIQUID

transcript: [" here she would stay comforted and soothed among the lovely plants and rich exotics rejoicing the heart of old turner the gardener who since polly's first rapturous entrance had taken her into his good graces for all time"]
reference: HERE SHE WOULD STAY COMFORTED AND SOOTHED AMONG THE LOVELY PLANTS AND RICH EXOTICS REJOICING THE HEART OF OLD TURNER THE GARDENER WHO SINCE POLLY'S FIRST RAPTUROUS ENTRANCE HAD TAKEN HER INTO HIS GOOD GRACES FOR ALL TIME

transcript: [' every chance she could steal after practice hours were over and after the clamorous demands of the boys upon her time were fully satisfied was seized to fly on the wings of the wind to the flowers']
reference: EVERY CHANCE SHE COULD STEAL AFTER PRACTICE HOURS WERE OVER AND AFTER THE CLAMOROUS DEMANDS OF THE BOYS UPON HER TIME WERE FULLY SATISFIED WAS SEIZED TO FLY ON THE WINGS OF THE WIND TO THE FLOWERS

transcript: [' then dear said missus Whitney you must be kinder to her than ever think what it would be for one of you to be away from home even among friends']
reference: THEN DEAR SAID MISSUS WHITNEY YOU MUST BE KINDER TO HER THAN EVER THINK WHAT IT WOULD BE FOR ONE OF YOU TO BE AWAY FROM HOME EVEN AMONG FRIENDS

transcript: [' somehow of all the days when the home feeling was the strongest, this day it seemed as if she could bear it no longer.']
reference: SOMEHOW OF ALL THE DAYS WHEN THE HOME FEELING WAS THE STRONGEST THIS DAY IT SEEMED AS IF SHE COULD BEAR IT NO LONGER

transcript: [' If she could only see phronsie for just one moment.']
reference: IF SHE COULD ONLY SEE PHRONSIE FOR JUST ONE MOMENT

transcript: [" Oh, she's always at the piano said van. She must be there now somewhere. And then somebody laughed."]
reference: OH SHE'S ALWAYS AT THE PIANO SAID VAN SHE MUST BE THERE NOW SOMEWHERE AND THEN SOMEBODY LAUGHED

transcript: [' At this the bundle opened suddenly and out popped phronsie.']
reference: AT THIS THE BUNDLE OPENED SUDDENLY AND OUT POPPED PHRONSIE

transcript: [" But Polly couldn't speak and if jasper hadn't caught her just in time she would have tumbled over backward from the stool phronsie and all."]
reference: BUT POLLY COULDN'T SPEAK AND IF JASPER HADN'T CAUGHT HER JUST IN TIME SHE WOULD HAVE TUMBLED OVER BACKWARD FROM THE STOOL PHRONSIE AND ALL

transcript: [" asked phronsie with her little face close to polly's own"]
reference: ASKED PHRONSIE WITH HER LITTLE FACE CLOSE TO POLLY'S OWN

transcript: [" Now you'll stay cried anne. Say, Polly, won't you?"]
reference: NOW YOU'LL STAY CRIED VAN SAY POLLY WON'T YOU

transcript: [' Oh, you are the dearest and best mister king I ever saw. But how did you make mammy let her come?']
reference: OH YOU ARE THE DEAREST AND BEST MISTER KING I EVER SAW BUT HOW DID YOU MAKE MAMMY LET HER COME

transcript: [" isn't he splendid cried jasper in intense pride swelling up father knew how to do it"]
reference: ISN'T HE SPLENDID CRIED JASPER IN INTENSE PRIDE SWELLING UP FATHER KNEW HOW TO DO IT

transcript: [' There there he said soothingly patting her brown fuzzy head.']
reference: THERE THERE HE SAID SOOTHINGLY PATTING HER BROWN FUZZY HEAD

transcript: [" I know gasped polly controlling her sobs I won't only I can't thank you"]
reference: I KNOW GASPED POLLY CONTROLLING HER SOBS I WON'T ONLY I CAN'T THANK YOU

transcript: [" asked phronsie in intense interest slipping down out of polly's arms and crowding up close to jasper's side"]
reference: ASKED PHRONSIE IN INTENSE INTEREST SLIPPING DOWN OUT OF POLLY'S ARMS AND CROWDING UP CLOSE TO JASPER'S SIDE

transcript: [' Yes, all alone by himself asserted jasper vehemently and winking furiously to the others to stop their laughing he did now truly, phronsie.']
reference: YES ALL ALONE BY HIMSELF ASSERTED JASPER VEHEMENTLY AND WINKING FURIOUSLY TO THE OTHERS TO STOP THEIR LAUGHING HE DID NOW TRULY PHRONSIE

transcript: [' Oh no, Jabber, I must go by my very own self.']
reference: OH NO JASPER I MUST GO BY MY VERY OWN SELF

transcript: [" There Jap, you've caught it laughed Percy while the others screamed at the sight of Jasper's face."]
reference: THERE JAP YOU'VE CAUGHT IT LAUGHED PERCY WHILE THE OTHERS SCREAMED AT THE SIGHT OF JASPER'S FACE

transcript: [" Don't mind it, Polly whispered jasper. 'Twasn't her fault."]
reference: DON'T MIND IT POLLY WHISPERED JASPER TWASN'T HER FAULT

transcript: [' "Dear me!" ejaculated the old gentleman in the utmost amazement and such a time as I\'ve had to get her here, too.']
reference: DEAR ME EJACULATED THE OLD GENTLEMAN IN THE UTMOST AMAZEMENT AND SUCH A TIME AS I'VE HAD TO GET HER HERE TOO

transcript: [' How did her mother ever let her go?']
reference: HOW DID HER MOTHER EVER LET HER GO

transcript: [" she asked impulsively. I didn't believe you could persuade her, father."]
reference: SHE ASKED IMPULSIVELY I DIDN'T BELIEVE YOU COULD PERSUADE HER FATHER

transcript: [" I didn't have any fears if I worked it rightly, said the old gentleman complacently."]
reference: I DIDN'T HAVE ANY FEARS IF I WORKED IT RIGHTLY SAID THE OLD GENTLEMAN COMPLACENTLY

transcript: [' he cried in high dudgeon just as if he owned the whole of the peppers and could dispose of them all to suit his fancy.']
reference: HE CRIED IN HIGH DUDGEON JUST AS IF HE OWNED THE WHOLE OF THE PEPPERS AND COULD DISPOSE OF THEM ALL TO SUIT HIS FANCY

transcript: [' And the old gentleman was so delighted with his success that he had to burst out into a series of short happy bits of laughter that occupied quite a space of time.']
reference: AND THE OLD GENTLEMAN WAS SO DELIGHTED WITH HIS SUCCESS THAT HE HAD TO BURST OUT INTO A SERIES OF SHORT HAPPY BITS OF LAUGHTER THAT OCCUPIED QUITE A SPACE OF TIME

transcript: [' At last he came out of them and wiped his face vigorously.']
reference: AT LAST HE CAME OUT OF THEM AND WIPED HIS FACE VIGOROUSLY

transcript: [' It is sixteen years since John Bergson died.']
reference: IT IS SIXTEEN YEARS SINCE JOHN BERGSON DIED

transcript: [' His wife now lies beside him and the white shaft that marks their graves gleams across the wheat fields.']
reference: HIS WIFE NOW LIES BESIDE HIM AND THE WHITE SHAFT THAT MARKS THEIR GRAVES GLEAMS ACROSS THE WHEAT FIELDS

transcript: [' From the Norwegian graveyard one looks out over a vast checkerboard marked off in squares of wheat and corn light and dark dark and light.']
reference: FROM THE NORWEGIAN GRAVEYARD ONE LOOKS OUT OVER A VAST CHECKER BOARD MARKED OFF IN SQUARES OF WHEAT AND CORN LIGHT AND DARK DARK AND LIGHT

transcript: [' From the graveyard gate one can count a dozen gaily painted farmhouses the gilded weather vanes on the big red barns wink at each other across the green and brown and yellow fields']
reference: FROM THE GRAVEYARD GATE ONE CAN COUNT A DOZEN GAYLY PAINTED FARMHOUSES THE GILDED WEATHER VANES ON THE BIG RED BARNS WINK AT EACH OTHER ACROSS THE GREEN AND BROWN AND YELLOW FIELDS

transcript: [' The air and the earth are curiously mated and intermingled as if the one were the breath of the other.']
reference: THE AIR AND THE EARTH ARE CURIOUSLY MATED AND INTERMINGLED AS IF THE ONE WERE THE BREATH OF THE OTHER

transcript: [' He was a splendid figure of a boy tall and straight as a young pine tree with a handsome head and stormy gray eyes deeply set under a serious brow.']
reference: HE WAS A SPLENDID FIGURE OF A BOY TALL AND STRAIGHT AS A YOUNG PINE TREE WITH A HANDSOME HEAD AND STORMY GRAY EYES DEEPLY SET UNDER A SERIOUS BROW

transcript: [" That's not much of a job for an athlete. Here, I've been to town and back."]
reference: THAT'S NOT MUCH OF A JOB FOR AN ATHLETE HERE I'VE BEEN TO TOWN AND BACK

transcript: [' Alexandra lets you sleep late.']
reference: ALEXANDRA LETS YOU SLEEP LATE

transcript: [' She gathered up her reins.']
reference: SHE GATHERED UP HER REINS

transcript: [' Please wait for me, Marie, Emil coaxed.']
reference: PLEASE WAIT FOR ME MARIE EMIL COAXED

transcript: [" I never see Lou's scythe over here."]
reference: I NEVER SEE LOU'S SCYTHE OVER HERE

transcript: [" How brown you've got since you came home. I wish I had an athlete to mow my orchard."]
reference: HOW BROWN YOU'VE GOT SINCE YOU CAME HOME I WISH I HAD AN ATHLETE TO MOW MY ORCHARD

transcript: [' I get wet to my knees when I go down to pick cherries.']
reference: I GET WET TO MY KNEES WHEN I GO DOWN TO PICK CHERRIES

transcript: [' indeed he had looked away with the purpose of not seeing it.']
reference: INDEED HE HAD LOOKED AWAY WITH THE PURPOSE OF NOT SEEING IT

transcript: [" They think you're proud because you've been away to school or something."]
reference: THEY THINK YOU'RE PROUD BECAUSE YOU'VE BEEN AWAY TO SCHOOL OR SOMETHING

transcript: [' There was something individual about the great farm a most unusual trimness and care for detail.']
reference: THERE WAS SOMETHING INDIVIDUAL ABOUT THE GREAT FARM A MOST UNUSUAL TRIMNESS AND CARE FOR DETAIL

transcript: [' On either side of the road for a mile before you reached the foot of the hill stood tall osage orange hedges their glossy green marking off the yellow fields.']
reference: ON EITHER SIDE OF THE ROAD FOR A MILE BEFORE YOU REACHED THE FOOT OF THE HILL STOOD TALL OSAGE ORANGE HEDGES THEIR GLOSSY GREEN MARKING OFF THE YELLOW FIELDS

transcript: [' Any one thereabouts would have told you that this was one of the richest farms on the divide and that the farmer was a woman Alexandra Bergson.']
reference: ANY ONE THEREABOUTS WOULD HAVE TOLD YOU THAT THIS WAS ONE OF THE RICHEST FARMS ON THE DIVIDE AND THAT THE FARMER WAS A WOMAN ALEXANDRA BERGSON

transcript: [' There is even a white row of beehives in the orchard under the walnut trees.']
reference: THERE IS EVEN A WHITE ROW OF BEEHIVES IN THE ORCHARD UNDER THE WALNUT TREES

transcript: [' Frank read English slowly and the more he read about this divorce case the angrier he grew.']
reference: FRANK READ ENGLISH SLOWLY AND THE MORE HE READ ABOUT THIS DIVORCE CASE THE ANGRIER HE GREW

transcript: [' Marie sighed.']
reference: MARIE SIGHED

transcript: [' A brisk wind had come up and was driving puffy white clouds across the sky.']
reference: A BRISK WIND HAD COME UP AND WAS DRIVING PUFFY WHITE CLOUDS ACROSS THE SKY

transcript: [' The orchard was sparkling and rippling in the sun']
reference: THE ORCHARD WAS SPARKLING AND RIPPLING IN THE SUN

transcript: [' That invitation decided her.']
reference: THAT INVITATION DECIDED HER

transcript: [" Oh, but I'm glad to get this place mowed."]
reference: OH BUT I'M GLAD TO GET THIS PLACE MOWED

transcript: [' Just smell the wild roses they are always so spicy after a rain.']
reference: JUST SMELL THE WILD ROSES THEY ARE ALWAYS SO SPICY AFTER A RAIN

transcript: [' We never had so many of them in here before.']
reference: WE NEVER HAD SO MANY OF THEM IN HERE BEFORE

transcript: [" I suppose it's the wet season will you have to cut them too?"]
reference: I SUPPOSE IT'S THE WET SEASON WILL YOU HAVE TO CUT THEM TOO

transcript: [" I suppose that's the wet season too then."]
reference: I SUPPOSE THAT'S THE WET SEASON TOO THEN

transcript: [" It's exciting to see everything growing so fast and to get the grass cut."]
reference: IT'S EXCITING TO SEE EVERYTHING GROWING SO FAST AND TO GET THE GRASS CUT

transcript: [" Aren't you splashed? Look at the spider webs all over the grass."]
reference: AREN'T YOU SPLASHED LOOK AT THE SPIDER WEBS ALL OVER THE GRASS

transcript: [' In a few moments he heard the cherries dropping smartly into the pail and he began to swing his scythe with that long even stroke that few american boys ever learn.']
reference: IN A FEW MOMENTS HE HEARD THE CHERRIES DROPPING SMARTLY INTO THE PAIL AND HE BEGAN TO SWING HIS SCYTHE WITH THAT LONG EVEN STROKE THAT FEW AMERICAN BOYS EVER LEARN

transcript: [' Marie picked cherries and sang softly to herself stripping one glittering branch after another shivering when she caught a shower of rain drops on her neck and hair']
reference: MARIE PICKED CHERRIES AND SANG SOFTLY TO HERSELF STRIPPING ONE GLITTERING BRANCH AFTER ANOTHER SHIVERING WHEN SHE CAUGHT A SHOWER OF RAINDROPS ON HER NECK AND HAIR

transcript: [' And Amiel mowed his way slowly down towards the cherry trees.']
reference: AND EMIL MOWED HIS WAY SLOWLY DOWN TOWARD THE CHERRY TREES

transcript: [' That summer the rains had been so many and opportune that it was almost more than shabbatta and his man could do to keep up with the corn the orchard was a neglected wilderness.']
reference: THAT SUMMER THE RAINS HAD BEEN SO MANY AND OPPORTUNE THAT IT WAS ALMOST MORE THAN SHABATA AND HIS MAN COULD DO TO KEEP UP WITH THE CORN THE ORCHARD WAS A NEGLECTED WILDERNESS

transcript: [" I don't know all of them, but I know Lyndon's are."]
reference: I DON'T KNOW ALL OF THEM BUT I KNOW LINDENS ARE

transcript: [' If I feel that way, I feel that way.']
reference: IF I FEEL THAT WAY I FEEL THAT WAY

transcript: [' He reached up among the branches and began to pick the sweet insipid fruit long ivory coloured berries tipped with faint pink like white coral that fall to the ground unheeded all summer through.']
reference: HE REACHED UP AMONG THE BRANCHES AND BEGAN TO PICK THE SWEET INSIPID FRUIT LONG IVORY COLORED BERRIES TIPPED WITH FAINT PINK LIKE WHITE CORAL THAT FALL TO THE GROUND UNHEEDED ALL SUMMER THROUGH

transcript: [' He dropped a handful into her lap.']
reference: HE DROPPED A HANDFUL INTO HER LAP

transcript: [" Yes, don't you?"]
reference: YES DON'T YOU

transcript: [' Oh, ever so much only he seems kind of staid and schoolteachery.']
reference: OH EVER SO MUCH ONLY HE SEEMS KIND OF STAID AND SCHOOL TEACHERY

transcript: [" When she used to tell me about him, I always wondered whether she wasn't a little in love with him."]
reference: WHEN SHE USED TO TELL ME ABOUT HIM I ALWAYS WONDERED WHETHER SHE WASN'T A LITTLE IN LOVE WITH HIM

transcript: [' It would serve you all right if she walked off with karl.']
reference: IT WOULD SERVE YOU ALL RIGHT IF SHE WALKED OFF WITH CARL

transcript: [' I like to talk to Karl about New York and what a fellow can do there.']
reference: I LIKE TO TALK TO CARL ABOUT NEW YORK AND WHAT A FELLOW CAN DO THERE

transcript: [' Oh, Emil!']
reference: OH EMIL

transcript: [' surely you are not thinking of going off there']
reference: SURELY YOU ARE NOT THINKING OF GOING OFF THERE

transcript: [" Marie's face fell under his brooding gaze."]
reference: MARIE'S FACE FELL UNDER HIS BROODING GAZE

transcript: [" I'm sure Alexandra hopes you will stay on here she murmured"]
reference: I'M SURE ALEXANDRA HOPES YOU WILL STAY ON HERE SHE MURMURED

transcript: [" I don't want to stand around and look on."]
reference: I DON'T WANT TO STAND AROUND AND LOOK ON

transcript: [' I want to be doing something on my own account.']
reference: I WANT TO BE DOING SOMETHING ON MY OWN ACCOUNT

transcript: [" Sometimes I don't want to do anything at all and sometimes I want to pull the four corners of the divide together' he threw out his arm and brought it back with a jerk' so like a table cloth"]
reference: SOMETIMES I DON'T WANT TO DO ANYTHING AT ALL AND SOMETIMES I WANT TO PULL THE FOUR CORNERS OF THE DIVIDE TOGETHER HE THREW OUT HIS ARM AND BROUGHT IT BACK WITH A JERK SO LIKE A TABLE CLOTH

transcript: [' I get tired of seeing men and horses going up and down, up and down.']
reference: I GET TIRED OF SEEING MEN AND HORSES GOING UP AND DOWN UP AND DOWN

transcript: [" I wish you weren't so restless and didn't get so worked up over things she said sadly"]
reference: I WISH YOU WEREN'T SO RESTLESS AND DIDN'T GET SO WORKED UP OVER THINGS SHE SAID SADLY

transcript: [' Thank you, he returned shortly.']
reference: THANK YOU HE RETURNED SHORTLY

transcript: [' And you never used to be cross to me.']
reference: AND YOU NEVER USED TO BE CROSS TO ME

transcript: [" I can't play with you like a little boy any more, he said slowly. That's what you miss, Marie."]
reference: I CAN'T PLAY WITH YOU LIKE A LITTLE BOY ANY MORE HE SAID SLOWLY THAT'S WHAT YOU MISS MARIE

transcript: [' But Emil, if I understand, then all our good times are over. We can never do nice things together any more.']
reference: BUT EMIL IF I UNDERSTAND THEN ALL OUR GOOD TIMES ARE OVER WE CAN NEVER DO NICE THINGS TOGETHER ANY MORE

transcript: [" And anyhow, there's nothing to understand."]
reference: AND ANYHOW THERE'S NOTHING TO UNDERSTAND

transcript: [" That won't last, it will go away and things will be just as they used to."]
reference: THAT WON'T LAST IT WILL GO AWAY AND THINGS WILL BE JUST AS THEY USED TO

transcript: [" I pray for you, but that's not the same as if you prayed yourself."]
reference: I PRAY FOR YOU BUT THAT'S NOT THE SAME AS IF YOU PRAYED YOURSELF

transcript: [" I can't pray to have the things I want, he said slowly, and I won't pray not to have them, not if I'm damned for it."]
reference: I CAN'T PRAY TO HAVE THE THINGS I WANT HE SAID SLOWLY AND I WON'T PRAY NOT TO HAVE THEM NOT IF I'M DAMNED FOR IT

transcript: [' Then all our good times are over.']
reference: THEN ALL OUR GOOD TIMES ARE OVER

transcript: [' Saturday august fifteenth the sea unbroken all round no land in sight']
reference: SATURDAY AUGUST FIFTEENTH THE SEA UNBROKEN ALL ROUND NO LAND IN SIGHT

transcript: [' The horizon seems extremely distant.']
reference: THE HORIZON SEEMS EXTREMELY DISTANT

transcript: [' All my danger and sufferings were needed to strike a spark of human feeling out of him but now that I am well his nature has resumed its sway.']
reference: ALL MY DANGER AND SUFFERINGS WERE NEEDED TO STRIKE A SPARK OF HUMAN FEELING OUT OF HIM BUT NOW THAT I AM WELL HIS NATURE HAS RESUMED ITS SWAY

transcript: [' You seem anxious my uncle, I said, seeing him continually with his glass to his eye anxious?']
reference: YOU SEEM ANXIOUS MY UNCLE I SAID SEEING HIM CONTINUALLY WITH HIS GLASS TO HIS EYE ANXIOUS

transcript: [' One might be with less reason than now.']
reference: ONE MIGHT BE WITH LESS REASON THAN NOW

transcript: [' I am not complaining that the rate is slow, but that the sea is so wide.']
reference: I AM NOT COMPLAINING THAT THE RATE IS SLOW BUT THAT THE SEA IS SO WIDE

transcript: [' We are losing time and the fact is I have not come all this way to take a little sail upon a pond on a raft.']
reference: WE ARE LOSING TIME AND THE FACT IS I HAVE NOT COME ALL THIS WAY TO TAKE A LITTLE SAIL UPON A POND ON A RAFT

transcript: [' He called this sea a pond and our long voyage taking a little sail.']
reference: HE CALLED THIS SEA A POND AND OUR LONG VOYAGE TAKING A LITTLE SAIL

transcript: [" Therefore don't talk to me about views and prospects."]
reference: THEREFORE DON'T TALK TO ME ABOUT VIEWS AND PROSPECTS

transcript: [' I take this as my answer and I leave the professor to bite his lips with impatience.']
reference: I TAKE THIS AS MY ANSWER AND I LEAVE THE PROFESSOR TO BITE HIS LIPS WITH IMPATIENCE

transcript: [' sunday august sixteenth']
reference: SUNDAY AUGUST SIXTEENTH

transcript: [' Nothing new, weather unchanged, the wind freshens.']
reference: NOTHING NEW WEATHER UNCHANGED THE WIND FRESHENS

transcript: [' But there seemed no reason to fear.']
reference: BUT THERE SEEMED NO REASON TO FEAR

transcript: [' The shadow of the raft was clearly outlined upon the surface of the waves.']
reference: THE SHADOW OF THE RAFT WAS CLEARLY OUTLINED UPON THE SURFACE OF THE WAVES

transcript: [' Truly this sea is of infinite width.']
reference: TRULY THIS SEA IS OF INFINITE WIDTH

transcript: [' It must be as wide as the Mediterranean or the Atlantic, and why not?']
reference: IT MUST BE AS WIDE AS THE MEDITERRANEAN OR THE ATLANTIC AND WHY NOT

transcript: [' These thoughts agitated me all day, and my imagination scarcely calmed down after several hours sleep.']
reference: THESE THOUGHTS AGITATED ME ALL DAY AND MY IMAGINATION SCARCELY CALMED DOWN AFTER SEVERAL HOURS SLEEP

transcript: [' I shudder as I recall these monsters to my remembrance.']
reference: I SHUDDER AS I RECALL THESE MONSTERS TO MY REMEMBRANCE

transcript: [' I saw at the Hamburg Museum the skeleton of one of these creatures thirty feet in length.']
reference: I SAW AT THE HAMBURG MUSEUM THE SKELETON OF ONE OF THESE CREATURES THIRTY FEET IN LENGTH

transcript: [' I suppose professor liedenbrock was of my opinion too and even shared my fears for after having examined the pick his eyes traversed the ocean from side to side']
reference: I SUPPOSE PROFESSOR LIEDENBROCK WAS OF MY OPINION TOO AND EVEN SHARED MY FEARS FOR AFTER HAVING EXAMINED THE PICK HIS EYES TRAVERSED THE OCEAN FROM SIDE TO SIDE

transcript: [' tuesday august eighteenth']
reference: TUESDAY AUGUST EIGHTEENTH

transcript: [' During his watch, I slept.']
reference: DURING HIS WATCH I SLEPT

transcript: [' Two hours afterwards a terrible shock awoke me.']
reference: TWO HOURS AFTERWARDS A TERRIBLE SHOCK AWOKE ME

transcript: [' The raft was heaved up on a watery mountain and pitched down again at a distance of twenty fathoms.']
reference: THE RAFT WAS HEAVED UP ON A WATERY MOUNTAIN AND PITCHED DOWN AGAIN AT A DISTANCE OF TWENTY FATHOMS

transcript: [' There is a whale! a whale! cried the professor.']
reference: THERE'S A WHALE A WHALE CRIED THE PROFESSOR

transcript: [' Flight was out of the question now the reptiles rose. They wheeled around our little raft with a rapidity greater than that of express trains.']
reference: FLIGHT WAS OUT OF THE QUESTION NOW THE REPTILES ROSE THEY WHEELED AROUND OUR LITTLE RAFT WITH A RAPIDITY GREATER THAN THAT OF EXPRESS TRAINS

transcript: [' Two monsters only were creating all this commotion and before my eyes are two reptiles of the primitive world.']
reference: TWO MONSTERS ONLY WERE CREATING ALL THIS COMMOTION AND BEFORE MY EYES ARE TWO REPTILES OF THE PRIMITIVE WORLD

transcript: [" I could distinguish the eye of the ichthyosaurus glowing like a red hot coal and as large as a man's head."]
reference: I CAN DISTINGUISH THE EYE OF THE ICHTHYOSAURUS GLOWING LIKE A RED HOT COAL AND AS LARGE AS A MAN'S HEAD

transcript: [' Its jaw is enormous and according to naturalists it is armed with no less than one hundred and eighty two teeth.']
reference: ITS JAW IS ENORMOUS AND ACCORDING TO NATURALISTS IT IS ARMED WITH NO LESS THAN ONE HUNDRED AND EIGHTY TWO TEETH

transcript: [' Those huge creatures attacked each other with the greatest animosity.']
reference: THOSE HUGE CREATURES ATTACKED EACH OTHER WITH THE GREATEST ANIMOSITY

transcript: [' Suddenly the ichthyosaurus and the plesiosaurus disappear below, leaving a whirlpool eddying in the water.']
reference: SUDDENLY THE ICHTHYOSAURUS AND THE PLESIOSAURUS DISAPPEAR BELOW LEAVING A WHIRLPOOL EDDYING IN THE WATER

transcript: [' As for the ichthyosaurus has he returned to his submarine cavern?']
reference: AS FOR THE ICHTHYOSAURUS HAS HE RETURNED TO HIS SUBMARINE CAVERN

transcript: [' The roarings become lost in the distance.']
reference: THE ROARINGS BECOME LOST IN THE DISTANCE

transcript: [' The weather, if we may use the term, will change before long.']
reference: THE WEATHER IF WE MAY USE THAT TERM WILL CHANGE BEFORE LONG

transcript: [' The atmosphere is charged with vapors, pervaded with the electricity generated by the evaporation of saline waters.']
reference: THE ATMOSPHERE IS CHARGED WITH VAPOURS PERVADED WITH THE ELECTRICITY GENERATED BY THE EVAPORATION OF SALINE WATERS

transcript: [' The electric light can scarcely penetrate through the dense curtain which is dropped over the theatre on which the battle of the elements is about to be waged.']
reference: THE ELECTRIC LIGHT CAN SCARCELY PENETRATE THROUGH THE DENSE CURTAIN WHICH HAS DROPPED OVER THE THEATRE ON WHICH THE BATTLE OF THE ELEMENTS IS ABOUT TO BE WAGED

transcript: [' The air is heavy. The sea is calm.']
reference: THE AIR IS HEAVY THE SEA IS CALM

transcript: [' From time to time a fleecy tuft of mist with yet some gleaming light left upon it drops down upon the dense floor of grey and loses itself in the opaque and impenetrable mass.']
reference: FROM TIME TO TIME A FLEECY TUFT OF MIST WITH YET SOME GLEAMING LIGHT LEFT UPON IT DROPS DOWN UPON THE DENSE FLOOR OF GREY AND LOSES ITSELF IN THE OPAQUE AND IMPENETRABLE MASS

transcript: [' The atmosphere is evidently charged and surcharged with electricity.']
reference: THE ATMOSPHERE IS EVIDENTLY CHARGED AND SURCHARGED WITH ELECTRICITY

transcript: [' The wind never lulls but to acquire increased strength. The vast bank of heavy clouds is a huge reservoir of fearful windy gusts and rushing storms.']
reference: THE WIND NEVER LULLS BUT TO ACQUIRE INCREASED STRENGTH THE VAST BANK OF HEAVY CLOUDS IS A HUGE RESERVOIR OF FEARFUL WINDY GUSTS AND RUSHING STORMS

transcript: [" There's a heavy storm coming on, I cried, pointing towards the horizon."]
reference: THERE'S A HEAVY STORM COMING ON I CRIED POINTING TOWARDS THE HORIZON

transcript: [' Those clouds seem as if they were going to crush the sea.']
reference: THOSE CLOUDS SEEM AS IF THEY WERE GOING TO CRUSH THE SEA

transcript: [" On the mast already I see the light play of a lament saint Elwell's fire the outstretched sail catches not a breath of wind and hangs like a sheet of lead."]
reference: ON THE MAST ALREADY I SEE THE LIGHT PLAY OF A LAMBENT SAINT ELMO'S FIRE THE OUTSTRETCHED SAIL CATCHES NOT A BREATH OF WIND AND HANGS LIKE A SHEET OF LEAD

transcript: [' But if we have now ceased to advance why do we yet leave that sail loose which at the first shock of a tempest may capsize us in a moment?']
reference: BUT IF WE HAVE NOW CEASED TO ADVANCE WHY DO WE YET LEAVE THAT SAIL LOOSE WHICH AT THE FIRST SHOCK OF THE TEMPEST MAY CAPSIZE US IN A MOMENT

transcript: [' That will be safest. No, no, never!']
reference: THAT WILL BE SAFEST NO NO NEVER

transcript: [' The piled up vapors condensed into water and the air put into violent action to supply the vacuum left by the condensation of the mists rouses itself into a whirlwind.']
reference: THE PILED UP VAPOURS CONDENSE INTO WATER AND THE AIR PUT INTO VIOLENT ACTION TO SUPPLY THE VACUUM LEFT BY THE CONDENSATION OF THE MISTS ROUSES ITSELF INTO A WHIRLWIND

transcript: [' Hans stirs not.']
reference: HANS STIRS NOT

transcript: [' From the under surface of the clouds there are continual emissions of lurid light electric matter is in continual evolution from their component molecules the gaseous elements of the air need to be slaked with moisture for innumerable columns of water rush upwards into the air and fall back again in white foam.']
reference: FROM THE UNDER SURFACE OF THE CLOUDS THERE ARE CONTINUAL EMISSIONS OF LURID LIGHT ELECTRIC MATTER IS IN CONTINUAL EVOLUTION FROM THEIR COMPONENT MOLECULES THE GASEOUS ELEMENTS OF THE AIR NEED TO BE SLAKED WITH MOISTURE FOR INNUMERABLE COLUMNS OF WATER RUSH UPWARDS INTO THE AIR AND FALL BACK AGAIN IN WHITE FOAM

transcript: [' I refer to the thermometer, it indicates. The figure is obliterated.']
reference: I REFER TO THE THERMOMETER IT INDICATES THE FIGURE IS OBLITERATED

transcript: [' Is the atmospheric condition having once reached this density to become final?']
reference: IS THE ATMOSPHERIC CONDITION HAVING ONCE REACHED THIS DENSITY TO BECOME FINAL

transcript: [' The raft bears on still to the southeast.']
reference: THE RAFT BEARS ON STILL TO THE SOUTH EAST

transcript: [' At noon the violence of the storm redoubles.']
reference: AT NOON THE VIOLENCE OF THE STORM REDOUBLES

transcript: [' Each of us is lashed to some part of the raft']
reference: EACH OF US IS LASHED TO SOME PART OF THE RAFT

transcript: [' The waves rise above our heads.']
reference: THE WAVES RISE ABOVE OUR HEADS

transcript: [' They seem to be we are lost, but I am not sure.']
reference: THEY SEEM TO BE WE ARE LOST BUT I AM NOT SURE

transcript: [' He nods his consent.']
reference: HE NODS HIS CONSENT

transcript: [' The fire ball half of it white half azure blue and the size of a ten inch shell moved slowly about the raft but revolving on its own axis with astonishing velocity as if whipped round by the force of the whirlwind.']
reference: THE FIREBALL HALF OF IT WHITE HALF AZURE BLUE AND THE SIZE OF A TEN INCH SHELL MOVED SLOWLY ABOUT THE RAFT BUT REVOLVING ON ITS OWN AXIS WITH ASTONISHING VELOCITY AS IF WHIPPED ROUND BY THE FORCE OF THE WHIRLWIND

transcript: [' Here it comes there it glides now it is up the ragged stump of the mast thence it lightly leaps on the provision bag descends with a light bound and just skims the powder magazine horrible!']
reference: HERE IT COMES THERE IT GLIDES NOW IT IS UP THE RAGGED STUMP OF THE MAST THENCE IT LIGHTLY LEAPS ON THE PROVISION BAG DESCENDS WITH A LIGHT BOUND AND JUST SKIMS THE POWDER MAGAZINE HORRIBLE

transcript: [' We shall be blown up! But no, the dazzling disk of mysterious light nimbly leaps aside. It approaches Hans, who fixes his blue eye upon it steadily. It threatens the head of my uncle, who falls upon his knees with his head down to avoid it.']
reference: WE SHALL BE BLOWN UP BUT NO THE DAZZLING DISK OF MYSTERIOUS LIGHT NIMBLY LEAPS ASIDE IT APPROACHES HANS WHO FIXES HIS BLUE EYE UPON IT STEADILY IT THREATENS THE HEAD OF MY UNCLE WHO FALLS UPON HIS KNEES WITH HIS HEAD DOWN TO AVOID IT

transcript: [' A suffocating smell of nitrogen fills the air it enters the throat it fills the lungs.']
reference: A SUFFOCATING SMELL OF NITROGEN FILLS THE AIR IT ENTERS THE THROAT IT FILLS THE LUNGS

transcript: [' We suffer stifling pains.']
reference: WE SUFFER STIFLING PAINS

transcript: [' And how odd the directions will look!']
reference: AND HOW ODD THE DIRECTIONS WILL LOOK

transcript: [' Poor alice!']
reference: POOR ALICE

transcript: [' It was the white rabbit returning splendidly dressed with a pair of white kid gloves in one hand and a large fan in the other he came trotting along in a great hurry muttering to himself as he came oh the duchess the duchess']
reference: IT WAS THE WHITE RABBIT RETURNING SPLENDIDLY DRESSED WITH A PAIR OF WHITE KID GLOVES IN ONE HAND AND A LARGE FAN IN THE OTHER HE CAME TROTTING ALONG IN A GREAT HURRY MUTTERING TO HIMSELF AS HE CAME OH THE DUCHESS THE DUCHESS

transcript: [" Oh, won't she be savage if I've kept her waiting!"]
reference: OH WON'T SHE BE SAVAGE IF I'VE KEPT HER WAITING

transcript: [' Alice took up the fan and gloves and as the hall was very hot she kept fanning herself all the time she went on talking dear dear how queer everything is to day']
reference: ALICE TOOK UP THE FAN AND GLOVES AND AS THE HALL WAS VERY HOT SHE KEPT FANNING HERSELF ALL THE TIME SHE WENT ON TALKING DEAR DEAR HOW QUEER EVERYTHING IS TO DAY

transcript: [' And yesterday things went on just as usual.']
reference: AND YESTERDAY THINGS WENT ON JUST AS USUAL

transcript: [" I wonder if I've been changed in the night."]
reference: I WONDER IF I'VE BEEN CHANGED IN THE NIGHT

transcript: [' I almost think I can remember feeling a little different.']
reference: I ALMOST THINK I CAN REMEMBER FEELING A LITTLE DIFFERENT

transcript: [" I'll try if I know all the things I used to know."]
reference: I'LL TRY IF I KNOW ALL THE THINGS I USED TO KNOW

transcript: [' I shall never get to twenty at that rate.']
reference: I SHALL NEVER GET TO TWENTY AT THAT RATE

transcript: [' How cheerfully he seems to grin, how neatly spread his claws and welcome little fishes in with gently smiling jaws.']
reference: HOW CHEERFULLY HE SEEMS TO GRIN HOW NEATLY SPREAD HIS CLAWS AND WELCOME LITTLE FISHES IN WITH GENTLY SMILING JAWS

transcript: [" No, I've made up my mind about it. If I'm Mabel, I'll stay down here."]
reference: NO I'VE MADE UP MY MIND ABOUT IT IF I'M MABEL I'LL STAY DOWN HERE

transcript: [" It'll be no use their putting their heads down and saying come up again, dear."]
reference: IT'LL BE NO USE THEIR PUTTING THEIR HEADS DOWN AND SAYING COME UP AGAIN DEAR

transcript: [' I am so very tired of being all alone here.']
reference: I AM SO VERY TIRED OF BEING ALL ALONE HERE

transcript: [" And I declare it's too bad that it is."]
reference: AND I DECLARE IT'S TOO BAD THAT IT IS

transcript: [" I wish I hadn't cried so much said Alice as she swam about trying to find her way out."]
reference: I WISH I HADN'T CRIED SO MUCH SAID ALICE AS SHE SWAM ABOUT TRYING TO FIND HER WAY OUT

transcript: [' I shall be punished for it now, I suppose, by being drowned in my own tears.']
reference: I SHALL BE PUNISHED FOR IT NOW I SUPPOSE BY BEING DROWNED IN MY OWN TEARS

transcript: [' That will be a queer thing, to be sure.']
reference: THAT WILL BE A QUEER THING TO BE SURE

transcript: [' I am very tired of swimming about here. Oh, Mouse!']
reference: I AM VERY TIRED OF SWIMMING ABOUT HERE O MOUSE

transcript: [' cried Alice again, for this time the mouse was bristling all over, and she felt certain it must be really offended.']
reference: CRIED ALICE AGAIN FOR THIS TIME THE MOUSE WAS BRISTLING ALL OVER AND SHE FELT CERTAIN IT MUST BE REALLY OFFENDED

transcript: [" We won't talk about her any more if you'd rather not we indeed"]
reference: WE WON'T TALK ABOUT HER ANY MORE IF YOU'D RATHER NOT WE INDEED

transcript: [" We want you to help us publish some leading work of Luther's for the general American market. Will you do it?"]
reference: WE WANT YOU TO HELP US PUBLISH SOME LEADING WORK OF LUTHER'S FOR THE GENERAL AMERICAN MARKET WILL YOU DO IT

transcript: [' The condition is that I will be permitted to make Luther talk American streamline him, so to speak, because you will never get people whether in or outside the Lutheran Church actually to read Luther unless we make him talk as he would talk to day to Americans.']
reference: THE CONDITION IS THAT I WILL BE PERMITTED TO MAKE LUTHER TALK AMERICAN STREAMLINE HIM SO TO SPEAK BECAUSE YOU WILL NEVER GET PEOPLE WHETHER IN OR OUTSIDE THE LUTHERAN CHURCH ACTUALLY TO READ LUTHER UNLESS WE MAKE HIM TALK AS HE WOULD TALK TODAY TO AMERICANS

transcript: [' Let us begin with that, his commentary on Galatians.']
reference: LET US BEGIN WITH THAT HIS COMMENTARY ON GALATIANS

transcript: [' The undertaking which seems so attractive when viewed as a literary task proved a most difficult one and at times became oppressive.']
reference: THE UNDERTAKING WHICH SEEMED SO ATTRACTIVE WHEN VIEWED AS A LITERARY TASK PROVED A MOST DIFFICULT ONE AND AT TIMES BECAME OPPRESSIVE

transcript: [' It was written in Latin.']
reference: IT WAS WRITTEN IN LATIN

transcript: [' The work had to be condensed.']
reference: THE WORK HAD TO BE CONDENSED

transcript: [" A word should now be said about the origin of Luther's commentary on Galatians."]
reference: A WORD SHOULD NOW BE SAID ABOUT THE ORIGIN OF LUTHER'S COMMENTARY ON GALATIANS

transcript: [' Much later, when a friend of his was preparing an edition of all his latin works, he remarked to his home circle, If I had my way about it, they would republish only those of my books which have doctrine, my Galatians for instance.']
reference: MUCH LATER WHEN A FRIEND OF HIS WAS PREPARING AN EDITION OF ALL HIS LATIN WORKS HE REMARKED TO HIS HOME CIRCLE IF I HAD MY WAY ABOUT IT THEY WOULD REPUBLISH ONLY THOSE OF MY BOOKS WHICH HAVE DOCTRINE MY GALATIANS FOR INSTANCE

transcript: [' In other words, these three men took down the lectures which luther addressed to his students in the course of Galatians, and Roerer prepared the manuscript for the printer.']
reference: IN OTHER WORDS THESE THREE MEN TOOK DOWN THE LECTURES WHICH LUTHER ADDRESSED TO HIS STUDENTS IN THE COURSE OF GALATIANS AND ROERER PREPARED THE MANUSCRIPT FOR THE PRINTER
