transcript: [" It presents like no other of Luther's writings the central thought of Christianity, the justification of the sinner for the sake of Christ's merits alone."]
reference: IT PRESENTS LIKE NO OTHER OF LUTHER'S WRITINGS THE CENTRAL THOUGHT OF CHRISTIANITY THE JUSTIFICATION OF THE SINNER FOR THE SAKE OF CHRIST'S MERITS ALONE

transcript: [" But the essence of Luther's lectures is there."]
reference: BUT THE ESSENCE OF LUTHER'S LECTURES IS THERE

transcript: [' The Lord who has given us power to teach and to hear, let him also give us the power to serve and to do. Luke two.']
reference: THE LORD WHO HAS GIVEN US POWER TO TEACH AND TO HEAR LET HIM ALSO GIVE US THE POWER TO SERVE AND TO DO LUKE TWO

transcript: [' The word of our God shall stand forever.']
reference: THE WORD OF OUR GOD SHALL STAND FOREVER

transcript: [' In every way they sought to undermine the authority of saint Paul.']
reference: IN EVERY WAY THEY SOUGHT TO UNDERMINE THE AUTHORITY OF SAINT PAUL

transcript: [' They said to the galatians, You have no right to think highly of Paul.']
reference: THEY SAID TO THE GALATIANS YOU HAVE NO RIGHT TO THINK HIGHLY OF PAUL

transcript: [' He was the last to turn to Christ.']
reference: HE WAS THE LAST TO TURN TO CHRIST

transcript: [' Paul came later and is beneath us.']
reference: PAUL CAME LATER AND IS BENEATH US

transcript: [' Indeed, he persecuted the church of Christ for a long time.']
reference: INDEED HE PERSECUTED THE CHURCH OF CHRIST FOR A LONG TIME

transcript: [' Do you suppose that God for the sake of a few lutheran heretics would disown his entire church?']
reference: DO YOU SUPPOSE THAT GOD FOR THE SAKE OF A FEW LUTHERAN HERETICS WOULD DISOWN HIS ENTIRE CHURCH

transcript: [' Against these boasting false apostles, Paul boldly defends his apostolic authority and ministry.']
reference: AGAINST THESE BOASTING FALSE APOSTLES PAUL BOLDLY DEFENDS HIS APOSTOLIC AUTHORITY AND MINISTRY

transcript: [' As the ambassador of a government is honored for his office and not for his private person, so the minister of Christ should exalt his office in order to gain authority among men.']
reference: AS THE AMBASSADOR OF A GOVERNMENT IS HONORED FOR HIS OFFICE AND NOT FOR HIS PRIVATE PERSON SO THE MINISTER OF CHRIST SHOULD EXALT HIS OFFICE IN ORDER TO GAIN AUTHORITY AMONG MEN

transcript: [' Paul takes pride in his ministry, not to his own praise but to the praise of God.']
reference: PAUL TAKES PRIDE IN HIS MINISTRY NOT TO HIS OWN PRAISE BUT TO THE PRAISE OF GOD

transcript: [' Paul an apostle, not of men, et cetera.']
reference: PAUL AN APOSTLE NOT OF MEN ET CETERA

transcript: [' Either he calls ministers through the agency of men, or he calls them directly as he called the prophets and apostles.']
reference: EITHER HE CALLS MINISTERS THROUGH THE AGENCY OF MEN OR HE CALLS THEM DIRECTLY AS HE CALLED THE PROPHETS AND APOSTLES

transcript: [' Paul declares that the false apostles were called or sent neither by men nor by man.']
reference: PAUL DECLARES THAT THE FALSE APOSTLES WERE CALLED OR SENT NEITHER BY MEN NOR BY MAN

transcript: [' The most they could claim is that they were sent by others.']
reference: THE MOST THEY COULD CLAIM IS THAT THEY WERE SENT BY OTHERS

transcript: [' He mentions the apostles first because they were appointed directly by God.']
reference: HE MENTIONS THE APOSTLES FIRST BECAUSE THEY WERE APPOINTED DIRECTLY BY GOD

transcript: [' The call is not to be taken lightly.']
reference: THE CALL IS NOT TO BE TAKEN LIGHTLY

transcript: [' For a person to possess knowledge is not enough.']
reference: FOR A PERSON TO POSSESS KNOWLEDGE IS NOT ENOUGH

transcript: [" It spoils one's best work."]
reference: IT SPOILS ONE'S BEST WORK

transcript: [' When I was a young man I thought Paul was making too much of his call.']
reference: WHEN I WAS A YOUNG MAN I THOUGHT PAUL WAS MAKING TOO MUCH OF HIS CALL

transcript: [' I did not then realize the importance of the ministry.']
reference: I DID NOT THEN REALIZE THE IMPORTANCE OF THE MINISTRY

transcript: [' I knew nothing of the doctrine of faith because we were taught sophistry instead of certainty and nobody understood spiritual boasting.']
reference: I KNEW NOTHING OF THE DOCTRINE OF FAITH BECAUSE WE WERE TAUGHT SOPHISTRY INSTEAD OF CERTAINTY AND NOBODY UNDERSTOOD SPIRITUAL BOASTING

transcript: [' This is no sinful pride, it is holy pride.']
reference: THIS IS NO SINFUL PRIDE IT IS HOLY PRIDE

transcript: [' And God the Father who raised him from the dead.']
reference: AND GOD THE FATHER WHO RAISED HIM FROM THE DEAD

transcript: [' The claw seemed superfluous on first sight.']
reference: THE CLAUSE SEEMS SUPERFLUOUS ON FIRST SIGHT

transcript: [' These perverters of the righteousness of Christ resist the Father and the Son and the works of them both.']
reference: THESE PERVERTERS OF THE RIGHTEOUSNESS OF CHRIST RESIST THE FATHER AND THE SON AND THE WORKS OF THEM BOTH

transcript: [' In this whole epistle Paul treats of the resurrection of Christ.']
reference: IN THIS WHOLE EPISTLE PAUL TREATS OF THE RESURRECTION OF CHRIST

transcript: [' By his resurrection Christ won the victory over law sin flesh world devil death hell and every evil.']
reference: BY HIS RESURRECTION CHRIST WON THE VICTORY OVER LAW SIN FLESH WORLD DEVIL DEATH HELL AND EVERY EVIL

transcript: [' First two.']
reference: VERSE TWO

transcript: [' and all the brethren which are with me.']
reference: AND ALL THE BRETHREN WHICH ARE WITH ME

transcript: [' This should go far in shutting the mouths of the false apostles.']
reference: THIS SHOULD GO FAR IN SHUTTING THE MOUTHS OF THE FALSE APOSTLES

transcript: [' Although the brethren with me are not apostles like myself, yet they are all of one mind with me, think, write, and teach as I do.']
reference: ALTHOUGH THE BRETHREN WITH ME ARE NOT APOSTLES LIKE MYSELF YET THEY ARE ALL OF ONE MIND WITH ME THINK WRITE AND TEACH AS I DO

transcript: [' They do not go where the enemies of the gospel predominate. They go where the Christians are.']
reference: THEY DO NOT GO WHERE THE ENEMIES OF THE GOSPEL PREDOMINATE THEY GO WHERE THE CHRISTIANS ARE

transcript: [' Why do they not invade the catholic provinces and preach their doctrine to godless princes bishops and doctors as we have done by the help of God?']
reference: WHY DO THEY NOT INVADE THE CATHOLIC PROVINCES AND PREACH THEIR DOCTRINE TO GODLESS PRINCES BISHOPS AND DOCTORS AS WE HAVE DONE BY THE HELP OF GOD

transcript: [' We look for that reward which eye hath not seen nor ear heard neither hath entered into the heart of man.']
reference: WE LOOK FOR THAT REWARD WHICH EYE HATH NOT SEEN NOR EAR HEARD NEITHER HATH ENTERED INTO THE HEART OF MAN

transcript: [' Not all the Galatians had become perverted.']
reference: NOT ALL THE GALATIANS HAD BECOME PERVERTED

transcript: [' These means cannot be contaminated.']
reference: THESE MEANS CANNOT BE CONTAMINATED

transcript: [" They remain divine regardless of men's opinion."]
reference: THEY REMAIN DIVINE REGARDLESS OF MEN'S OPINION

transcript: [' Wherever the means of grace are found there is the holy church, even though antichrist reigns there.']
reference: WHEREVER THE MEANS OF GRACE ARE FOUND THERE IS THE HOLY CHURCH EVEN THOUGH ANTICHRIST REIGNS THERE

transcript: [' So much for the title of the epistle now follows the greeting of the apostle verse three']
reference: SO MUCH FOR THE TITLE OF THE EPISTLE NOW FOLLOWS THE GREETING OF THE APOSTLE VERSE THREE

transcript: [' Grace be to you and peace from God the Father and from our Lord Jesus Christ.']
reference: GRACE BE TO YOU AND PEACE FROM GOD THE FATHER AND FROM OUR LORD JESUS CHRIST

transcript: [' The terms of grace and peace are common terms with Paul, and are now pretty well understood.']
reference: THE TERMS OF GRACE AND PEACE ARE COMMON TERMS WITH PAUL AND ARE NOW PRETTY WELL UNDERSTOOD

transcript: [' The greeting of the apostle is refreshing.']
reference: THE GREETING OF THE APOSTLE IS REFRESHING

transcript: [' Grace involves the remission of sins, peace, and a happy conscience.']
reference: GRACE INVOLVES THE REMISSION OF SINS PEACE AND A HAPPY CONSCIENCE

transcript: [' The world brands this a pernicious doctrine.']
reference: THE WORLD BRANDS THIS A PERNICIOUS DOCTRINE

transcript: [' Experience proves this.']
reference: EXPERIENCE PROVES THIS

transcript: [' However, the grace and peace of God will.']
reference: HOWEVER THE GRACE AND PEACE OF GOD WILL

transcript: [' Men should not speculate about the nature of God.']
reference: MEN SHOULD NOT SPECULATE ABOUT THE NATURE OF GOD

transcript: [' Was it not enough to say from God the Father?']
reference: WAS IT NOT ENOUGH TO SAY FROM GOD THE FATHER

transcript: [' To do so is to lose God altogether because God becomes intolerable when we seek to measure and to comprehend his infinite majesty.']
reference: TO DO SO IS TO LOSE GOD ALTOGETHER BECAUSE GOD BECOMES INTOLERABLE WHEN WE SEEK TO MEASURE AND TO COMPREHEND HIS INFINITE MAJESTY

transcript: [' He came down to earth lived among men suffered was crucified and then he died standing clearly before us so that our hearts and eyes may fasten upon him.']
reference: HE CAME DOWN TO EARTH LIVED AMONG MEN SUFFERED WAS CRUCIFIED AND THEN HE DIED STANDING CLEARLY BEFORE US SO THAT OUR HEARTS AND EYES MAY FASTEN UPON HIM

transcript: [' Embrace him and forget about the nature of God.']
reference: EMBRACE HIM AND FORGET ABOUT THE NATURE OF GOD

transcript: [' Did not Christ himself say, I am the way and the truth and the life, no man cometh unto the Father but by me?']
reference: DID NOT CHRIST HIMSELF SAY I AM THE WAY AND THE TRUTH AND THE LIFE NO MAN COMETH UNTO THE FATHER BUT BY ME

transcript: [' When you argue about the nature of God apart from the question of justification, you may be as profound as you like.']
reference: WHEN YOU ARGUE ABOUT THE NATURE OF GOD APART FROM THE QUESTION OF JUSTIFICATION YOU MAY BE AS PROFOUND AS YOU LIKE

transcript: [' We are to hear Christ who has been appointed by the Father as our divine teacher.']
reference: WE ARE TO HEAR CHRIST WHO HAS BEEN APPOINTED BY THE FATHER AS OUR DIVINE TEACHER

transcript: [' At the same time, Paul confirms our creed that Christ is very God.']
reference: AT THE SAME TIME PAUL CONFIRMS OUR CREED THAT CHRIST IS VERY GOD

transcript: [' That Christ is very God is apparent in that Paul ascribes to him divine powers equally with the Father, as for instance the power to dispense grace and peace.']
reference: THAT CHRIST IS VERY GOD IS APPARENT IN THAT PAUL ASCRIBES TO HIM DIVINE POWERS EQUALLY WITH THE FATHER AS FOR INSTANCE THE POWER TO DISPENSE GRACE AND PEACE

transcript: [' To bestow peace and grace lies in the province of God who alone can create these blessings the angels cannot']
reference: TO BESTOW PEACE AND GRACE LIES IN THE PROVINCE OF GOD WHO ALONE CAN CREATE THESE BLESSINGS THE ANGELS CANNOT

transcript: [' Otherwise Paul should have written grace from God the Father and peace from our Lord Jesus Christ.']
reference: OTHERWISE PAUL SHOULD HAVE WRITTEN GRACE FROM GOD THE FATHER AND PEACE FROM OUR LORD JESUS CHRIST

transcript: [' The arians took Christ for a noble and perfect creature superior even to the angels because by him God created heaven and earth.']
reference: THE ARIANS TOOK CHRIST FOR A NOBLE AND PERFECT CREATURE SUPERIOR EVEN TO THE ANGELS BECAUSE BY HIM GOD CREATED HEAVEN AND EARTH

transcript: [' Mohammed also speaks highly of Christ.']
reference: MOHAMMED ALSO SPEAKS HIGHLY OF CHRIST

transcript: [' Paul sticks to his theme.']
reference: PAUL STICKS TO HIS THEME

transcript: [' He never loses sight of the purpose of his epistle.']
reference: HE NEVER LOSES SIGHT OF THE PURPOSE OF HIS EPISTLE

transcript: [' Not gold or silver or paschal lambs or an angel, but himself. What for?']
reference: NOT GOLD OR SILVER OR PASCHAL LAMBS OR AN ANGEL BUT HIMSELF WHAT FOR

transcript: [' Not for a crown or a kingdom or our goodness, but for our sins.']
reference: NOT FOR A CROWN OR A KINGDOM OR OUR GOODNESS BUT FOR OUR SINS

transcript: [' Underscore these words, for they are full of comfort for sore consciences.']
reference: UNDERSCORE THESE WORDS FOR THEY ARE FULL OF COMFORT FOR SORE CONSCIENCES

transcript: [' How may we obtain remission of our sins?']
reference: HOW MAY WE OBTAIN REMISSION OF OUR SINS

transcript: [' Paul answers, the man who is named Jesus Christ and the Son of God gave himself for our sins.']
reference: PAUL ANSWERS THE MAN WHO IS NAMED JESUS CHRIST AND THE SON OF GOD GAVE HIMSELF FOR OUR SINS

transcript: [' Since Christ was given for our sins it stands to reason that they could not be put away by our own efforts.']
reference: SINCE CHRIST WAS GIVEN FOR OUR SINS IT STANDS TO REASON THAT THEY CANNOT BE PUT AWAY BY OUR OWN EFFORTS

transcript: [' This sentence also defines our sins as great, so great in fact that the whole world could not make amends for a single sin.']
reference: THIS SENTENCE ALSO DEFINES OUR SINS AS GREAT SO GREAT IN FACT THAT THE WHOLE WORLD COULD NOT MAKE AMENDS FOR A SINGLE SIN

transcript: [' The greatness of the ransom, Christ the Son of God, indicates this.']
reference: THE GREATNESS OF THE RANSOM CHRIST THE SON OF GOD INDICATES THIS

transcript: [' The vicious character of sin is brought out by the words, who gave himself for our sins.']
reference: THE VICIOUS CHARACTER OF SIN IS BROUGHT OUT BY THE WORDS WHO GAVE HIMSELF FOR OUR SINS

transcript: [' But we are careless. We make light of sin.']
reference: BUT WE ARE CARELESS WE MAKE LIGHT OF SIN

transcript: [' We think that by some little work or merit we can dismiss sin.']
reference: WE THINK THAT BY SOME LITTLE WORK OR MERIT WE CAN DISMISS SIN

transcript: [' This passage then bears out the fact that all men are sold under sin.']
reference: THIS PASSAGE THEN BEARS OUT THE FACT THAT ALL MEN ARE SOLD UNDER SIN

transcript: [' This attitude springs from a false conception of sin, the conception that sin is a small matter easily taken care of by good works, that we must present ourselves unto God with a good conscience, that we must feel no sin before we may feel that Christ was given for our sins.']
reference: THIS ATTITUDE SPRINGS FROM A FALSE CONCEPTION OF SIN THE CONCEPTION THAT SIN IS A SMALL MATTER EASILY TAKEN CARE OF BY GOOD WORKS THAT WE MUST PRESENT OURSELVES UNTO GOD WITH A GOOD CONSCIENCE THAT WE MUST FEEL NO SIN BEFORE WE MAY FEEL THAT CHRIST WAS GIVEN FOR OUR SINS

transcript: [' This attitude is universal and particularly developed in those who consider themselves better than others.']
reference: THIS ATTITUDE IS UNIVERSAL AND PARTICULARLY DEVELOPED IN THOSE WHO CONSIDER THEMSELVES BETTER THAN OTHERS

transcript: [' But the real significance and comfort of the words for our sins is lost upon them.']
reference: BUT THE REAL SIGNIFICANCE AND COMFORT OF THE WORDS FOR OUR SINS IS LOST UPON THEM

transcript: [' On the other hand, we are not to regard them as so terrible that we must despair.']
reference: ON THE OTHER HAND WE ARE NOT TO REGARD THEM AS SO TERRIBLE THAT WE MUST DESPAIR

transcript: [' He passes abruptly from persons to ideas and numbers, and from ideas and numbers to persons, from the heavens to man, from astronomy to physiology. He confuses, or rather does not distinguish, subject and object, first and final causes, and is dreaming of geometrical figures lost in a flux of sense.']
reference: HE PASSES ABRUPTLY FROM PERSONS TO IDEAS AND NUMBERS AND FROM IDEAS AND NUMBERS TO PERSONS FROM THE HEAVENS TO MAN FROM ASTRONOMY TO PHYSIOLOGY HE CONFUSES OR RATHER DOES NOT DISTINGUISH SUBJECT AND OBJECT FIRST AND FINAL CAUSES AND IS DREAMING OF GEOMETRICAL FIGURES LOST IN A FLUX OF SENSE

transcript: [' The influence which the Timaeus has exercised upon posterity is due partly to a misunderstanding.']
reference: THE INFLUENCE WITH THE TIMAEUS HAS EXERCISED UPON POSTERITY IS DUE PARTLY TO A MISUNDERSTANDING

transcript: [' In the supposed depths of this dialogue the neo platonists found hidden meanings and connections with the jewish and christian scriptures and out of them they elicited doctrines quite at variance with the spirit of Plato.']
reference: IN THE SUPPOSED DEPTHS OF THIS DIALOGUE THE NEO PLATONISTS FOUND HIDDEN MEANINGS AND CONNECTIONS WITH THE JEWISH AND CHRISTIAN SCRIPTURES AND OUT OF THEM THEY ELICITED DOCTRINES QUITE AT VARIANCE WITH THE SPIRIT OF PLATO

transcript: [' They were absorbed in his theology and were under the dominion of his name while that which was truly great and truly characteristic in him, his effort to realise and connect abstractions, was not understood by them at all.']
reference: THEY WERE ABSORBED IN HIS THEOLOGY AND WERE UNDER THE DOMINION OF HIS NAME WHILE THAT WHICH WAS TRULY GREAT AND TRULY CHARACTERISTIC IN HIM HIS EFFORT TO REALIZE AND CONNECT ABSTRACTIONS WAS NOT UNDERSTOOD BY THEM AT ALL

transcript: [' There is no danger of the modern commentators on the Timaeus falling into the absurdities of the neo platonists.']
reference: THERE IS NO DANGER OF THE MODERN COMMENTATORS ON THE TIMAEUS FALLING INTO THE ABSURDITIES OF THE NEO PLATONISTS

transcript: [' In the present day we are well aware that an ancient philosopher is to be interpreted from himself and by the contemporary history of thought.']
reference: IN THE PRESENT DAY WE ARE WELL AWARE THAT AN ANCIENT PHILOSOPHER IS TO BE INTERPRETED FROM HIMSELF AND BY THE CONTEMPORARY HISTORY OF THOUGHT

transcript: [' The fancies of the neo platonists are only interesting to us because they exhibit a phase of the human mind which prevailed widely in the first centuries of the Christian era and is not wholly extinct in our own day.']
reference: THE FANCIES OF THE NEO PLATONISTS ARE ONLY INTERESTING TO US BECAUSE THEY EXHIBIT A PHASE OF THE HUMAN MIND WHICH PREVAILED WIDELY IN THE FIRST CENTURIES OF THE CHRISTIAN ERA AND IS NOT WHOLLY EXTINCT IN OUR OWN DAY

transcript: [' But they have nothing to do with the interpretation of Plato, and in spirit they are opposed to him.']
reference: BUT THEY HAVE NOTHING TO DO WITH THE INTERPRETATION OF PLATO AND IN SPIRIT THEY ARE OPPOSED TO HIM

transcript: [' We do not know how Plato would have arranged his own dialogues or whether the thought of arranging any of them besides the two trilogies which he has expressly connected was ever present to his mind.']
reference: WE DO NOT KNOW HOW PLATO WOULD HAVE ARRANGED HIS OWN DIALOGUES OR WHETHER THE THOUGHT OF ARRANGING ANY OF THEM BESIDES THE TWO TRILOGIES WHICH HE HAS EXPRESSLY CONNECTED WAS EVER PRESENT TO HIS MIND

transcript: [' The dialogue is primarily concerned with the animal creation, including under this term the heavenly bodies, and with man only as one among the animals.']
reference: THE DIALOGUE IS PRIMARILY CONCERNED WITH THE ANIMAL CREATION INCLUDING UNDER THIS TERM THE HEAVENLY BODIES AND WITH MAN ONLY AS ONE AMONG THE ANIMALS

transcript: [' But he has not as yet defined this intermediate territory which lies somewhere between medicine and mathematics and he would have felt that there was as great an impiety in ranking theories of physics first in the order of knowledge as in placing the body before the soul.']
reference: BUT HE HAS NOT AS YET DEFINED THIS INTERMEDIATE TERRITORY WHICH LIES SOMEWHERE BETWEEN MEDICINE AND MATHEMATICS AND HE WOULD HAVE FELT THAT THERE WAS AS GREAT AN IMPIETY IN RANKING THEORIES OF PHYSICS FIRST IN THE ORDER OF KNOWLEDGE AS IN PLACING THE BODY BEFORE THE SOUL

transcript: [' With Heraclitus he acknowledges the perpetual flux like Anaxagoras he asserts the predominance of mind although admitting an element of necessity which reason is incapable of subduing like the Pythagoreans he supposes the mystery of the world to be contained in number.']
reference: WITH HERACLEITUS HE ACKNOWLEDGES THE PERPETUAL FLUX LIKE ANAXAGORAS HE ASSERTS THE PREDOMINANCE OF MIND ALTHOUGH ADMITTING AN ELEMENT OF NECESSITY WHICH REASON IS INCAPABLE OF SUBDUING LIKE THE PYTHAGOREANS HE SUPPOSES THE MYSTERY OF THE WORLD TO BE CONTAINED IN NUMBER

transcript: [' Many, if not all, the elements of the pre socratic philosophy are included in the Timaeus.']
reference: MANY IF NOT ALL THE ELEMENTS OF THE PRE SOCRATIC PHILOSOPHY ARE INCLUDED IN THE TIMAEUS

transcript: [' It is probable that the relation of the ideas to God or of God to the world was differently conceived by him at different times of his life.']
reference: IT IS PROBABLE THAT THE RELATION OF THE IDEAS TO GOD OR OF GOD TO THE WORLD WAS DIFFERENTLY CONCEIVED BY HIM AT DIFFERENT TIMES OF HIS LIFE

transcript: [' The ideas also remain, but they have become types in nature forms of men animals birds fishes.']
reference: THE IDEAS ALSO REMAIN BUT THEY HAVE BECOME TYPES IN NATURE FORMS OF MEN ANIMALS BIRDS FISHES

transcript: [' The style and plan of the Timaeus differ greatly from that of any other of the Platonic dialogues.']
reference: THE STYLE AND PLAN OF THE TIMAEUS DIFFER GREATLY FROM THAT OF ANY OTHER OF THE PLATONIC DIALOGUES

transcript: [' But Plato has not the same mastery over his instrument which he exhibits in the Phaedrus or Symposium.']
reference: BUT PLATO HAS NOT THE SAME MASTERY OVER HIS INSTRUMENT WHICH HE EXHIBITS IN THE PHAEDRUS OR SYMPOSIUM

transcript: [' Nothing can exceed the beauty or art of the introduction in which he is using words after his accustomed manner.']
reference: NOTHING CAN EXCEED THE BEAUTY OR ART OF THE INTRODUCTION IN WHICH HE IS USING WORDS AFTER HIS ACCUSTOMED MANNER

transcript: [' But in the rest of the work the power of language seems to fail him and the dramatic form is wholly given up.']
reference: BUT IN THE REST OF THE WORK THE POWER OF LANGUAGE SEEMS TO FAIL HIM AND THE DRAMATIC FORM IS WHOLLY GIVEN UP

transcript: [' He could write in one style but not in another, and the Greek language had not as yet been fashioned by any poet or philosopher to describe physical phenomena.']
reference: HE COULD WRITE IN ONE STYLE BUT NOT IN ANOTHER AND THE GREEK LANGUAGE HAD NOT AS YET BEEN FASHIONED BY ANY POET OR PHILOSOPHER TO DESCRIBE PHYSICAL PHENOMENA

transcript: [' And hence we find the same sort of clumsiness in the Timaeus of Plato which characterizes the philosophical poem of Lucretius.']
reference: AND HENCE WE FIND THE SAME SORT OF CLUMSINESS IN THE TIMAEUS OF PLATO WHICH CHARACTERIZES THE PHILOSOPHICAL POEM OF LUCRETIUS

transcript: [" There is a want of flow and often a defect of rhythm the meaning is sometimes obscure and there is a greater use of apposition and more of repetition than occurs in plato's earlier writings."]
reference: THERE IS A WANT OF FLOW AND OFTEN A DEFECT OF RHYTHM THE MEANING IS SOMETIMES OBSCURE AND THERE IS A GREATER USE OF APPOSITION AND MORE OF REPETITION THAN OCCURS IN PLATO'S EARLIER WRITINGS

transcript: [' Plato had not the command of his materials which would have enabled him to produce a perfect work of art.']
reference: PLATO HAD NOT THE COMMAND OF HIS MATERIALS WHICH WOULD HAVE ENABLED HIM TO PRODUCE A PERFECT WORK OF ART

transcript: [' Socrates begins the Timaeus with a summary of the republic.']
reference: SOCRATES BEGINS THE TIMAEUS WITH A SUMMARY OF THE REPUBLIC

transcript: [' And now he desires to see the ideal state set in motion. He would like to know how she behaved in some great struggle.']
reference: AND NOW HE DESIRES TO SEE THE IDEAL STATE SET IN MOTION HE WOULD LIKE TO KNOW HOW SHE BEHAVED IN SOME GREAT STRUGGLE

transcript: [' And therefore to you I turn timaeus citizen of Locris who are at once a philosopher and a statesman and to you Critias whom all Athenians know to be similarly accomplished and to Hermocrates who is also fitted by nature and education to share in our discourse.']
reference: AND THEREFORE TO YOU I TURN TIMAEUS CITIZEN OF LOCRIS WHO ARE AT ONCE A PHILOSOPHER AND A STATESMAN AND TO YOU CRITIAS WHOM ALL ATHENIANS KNOW TO BE SIMILARLY ACCOMPLISHED AND TO HERMOCRATES WHO IS ALSO FITTED BY NATURE AND EDUCATION TO SHARE IN OUR DISCOURSE

transcript: [' I will, if Timaeus approves. I approve.']
reference: I WILL IF TIMAEUS APPROVES I APPROVE

transcript: [" Listen then, Socrates, to a tale of Solon's who being the friend of Dropidas my great grandfather told it to my grandfather Critias, and he told me."]
reference: LISTEN THEN SOCRATES TO A TALE OF SOLON'S WHO BEING THE FRIEND OF DROPIDAS MY GREAT GRANDFATHER TOLD IT TO MY GRANDFATHER CRITIAS AND HE TOLD ME

transcript: [' Some poems of Solon were recited by the boys.']
reference: SOME POEMS OF SOLON WERE RECITED BY THE BOYS

transcript: [' And what was the subject of the poem said the person who made the remark?']
reference: AND WHAT WAS THE SUBJECT OF THE POEM SAID THE PERSON WHO MADE THE REMARK

transcript: [' The subject was a very noble one. He described the most famous action in which the Athenian people were ever engaged.']
reference: THE SUBJECT WAS A VERY NOBLE ONE HE DESCRIBED THE MOST FAMOUS ACTION IN WHICH THE ATHENIAN PEOPLE WERE EVER ENGAGED

transcript: [' But the memory of their exploits had passed away owing to the lapse of time and the extinction of the actors.']
reference: BUT THE MEMORY OF THEIR EXPLOITS HAS PASSED AWAY OWING TO THE LAPSE OF TIME AND THE EXTINCTION OF THE ACTORS

transcript: [' Tell us said the other the whole story and where Solomon heard this story.']
reference: TELL US SAID THE OTHER THE WHOLE STORY AND WHERE SOLON HEARD THE STORY

transcript: [' But in Egypt the traditions of our own and other lands are by us registered for ever in our temples.']
reference: BUT IN EGYPT THE TRADITIONS OF OUR OWN AND OTHER LANDS ARE BY US REGISTERED FOR EVER IN OUR TEMPLES

transcript: [" The genealogies which you have recited to us out of your own annals, Solon, are a mere children's story."]
reference: THE GENEALOGIES WHICH YOU HAVE RECITED TO US OUT OF YOUR OWN ANNALS SOLON ARE A MERE CHILDREN'S STORY

transcript: [' For in the times before the great flood Athens was the greatest and best of cities and did the noblest deeds and had the best constitution of any under the face of heaven.']
reference: FOR IN THE TIMES BEFORE THE GREAT FLOOD ATHENS WAS THE GREATEST AND BEST OF CITIES AND DID THE NOBLEST DEEDS AND HAD THE BEST CONSTITUTION OF ANY UNDER THE FACE OF HEAVEN

transcript: [' Sullain marveled and desired to be informed of the particulars.']
reference: SOLON MARVELLED AND DESIRED TO BE INFORMED OF THE PARTICULARS

transcript: [' Nine thousand years have elapsed since she founded yours, and eight thousand since she founded ours, as our annals record.']
reference: NINE THOUSAND YEARS HAVE ELAPSED SINCE SHE FOUNDED YOURS AND EIGHT THOUSAND SINCE SHE FOUNDED OURS AS OUR ANNALS RECORD

transcript: [' Many laws exist among us which are the counterpart of yours as they were in the olden time.']
reference: MANY LAWS EXIST AMONG US WHICH ARE THE COUNTERPART OF YOURS AS THEY WERE IN THE OLDEN TIME

transcript: [' I will briefly describe them to you, and you shall read the account of them at your leisure in the sacred registers.']
reference: I WILL BRIEFLY DESCRIBE THEM TO YOU AND YOU SHALL READ THE ACCOUNT OF THEM AT YOUR LEISURE IN THE SACRED REGISTERS

transcript: [' Observe again what care the law took in the pursuit of wisdom searching out the deep things of the world and applying them to the use of men.']
reference: OBSERVE AGAIN WHAT CARE THE LAW TOOK IN THE PURSUIT OF WISDOM SEARCHING OUT THE DEEP THINGS OF THE WORLD AND APPLYING THEM TO THE USE OF MAN

transcript: [' The most famous of them all was the overthrow of the island of Atlantis.']
reference: THE MOST FAMOUS OF THEM ALL WAS THE OVERTHROW OF THE ISLAND OF ATLANTIS

transcript: [' For at the peril of her own existence and when the other Hellenes had deserted her she repelled the invader and of her own accord gave liberty to all the nations within the pillars.']
reference: FOR AT THE PERIL OF HER OWN EXISTENCE AND WHEN THE OTHER HELLENES HAD DESERTED HER SHE REPELLED THE INVADER AND OF HER OWN ACCORD GAVE LIBERTY TO ALL THE NATIONS WITHIN THE PILLARS

transcript: [' This is the explanation of the shallows which are found in that part of the Atlantic Ocean.']
reference: THIS IS THE EXPLANATION OF THE SHALLOWS WHICH ARE FOUND IN THAT PART OF THE ATLANTIC OCEAN

transcript: [' But I would not speak at the time because I wanted to refresh my memory.']
reference: BUT I WOULD NOT SPEAK AT THE TIME BECAUSE I WANTED TO REFRESH MY MEMORY

transcript: [' Then now let me explain to you the order of our entertainment. First, Timaeus, who is a natural philosopher, will speak of the origin of the world going down to the creation of men. And then I shall receive the men whom he has created and some of whom will have been educated by you and introduce them to you as the lost Athenian citizens of whom the Egyptian records spoke.']
reference: THEN NOW LET ME EXPLAIN TO YOU THE ORDER OF OUR ENTERTAINMENT FIRST TIMAEUS WHO IS A NATURAL PHILOSOPHER WILL SPEAK OF THE ORIGIN OF THE WORLD GOING DOWN TO THE CREATION OF MAN AND THEN I SHALL RECEIVE THE MEN WHOM HE HAS CREATED AND SOME OF WHOM WILL HAVE BEEN EDUCATED BY YOU AND INTRODUCE THEM TO YOU AS THE LOST ATHENIAN CITIZENS OF WHOM THE EGYPTIAN RECORD SPOKE

transcript: [' But already at a point in economic evolution far antedating the emergence of the lady specialized consumption of goods as an evidence of pecuniary strength had begun to work out in a more or less elaborate system.']
reference: BUT ALREADY AT A POINT IN ECONOMIC EVOLUTION FAR ANTEDATING THE EMERGENCE OF THE LADY SPECIALISED CONSUMPTION OF GOODS AS AN EVIDENCE OF PECUNIARY STRENGTH HAD BEGUN TO WORK OUT IN A MORE OR LESS ELABORATE SYSTEM

transcript: [' The utility of consumption as an evidence of wealth is to be classed as a derivative growth.']
reference: THE UTILITY OF CONSUMPTION AS AN EVIDENCE OF WEALTH IS TO BE CLASSED AS A DERIVATIVE GROWTH

transcript: [' Such consumption as falls to the women is merely incidental to their work it is a means to their continued labor and not a consumption directed to their own comfort and fullness of life.']
reference: SUCH CONSUMPTION AS FALLS TO THE WOMEN IS MERELY INCIDENTAL TO THEIR WORK IT IS A MEANS TO THEIR CONTINUED LABOUR AND NOT A CONSUMPTION DIRECTED TO THEIR OWN COMFORT AND FULNESS OF LIFE

transcript: [' With a further advance in culture this taboo may change into simple custom of a more or less rigorous character but whatever be the theoretical basis of the distinction which is maintained, whether it be a taboo or a larger conventionality, the features of the conventional scheme of consumption do not change easily.']
reference: WITH A FURTHER ADVANCE IN CULTURE THIS TABU MAY CHANGE INTO SIMPLE CUSTOM OF A MORE OR LESS RIGOROUS CHARACTER BUT WHATEVER BE THE THEORETICAL BASIS OF THE DISTINCTION WHICH IS MAINTAINED WHETHER IT BE A TABU OR A LARGER CONVENTIONALITY THE FEATURES OF THE CONVENTIONAL SCHEME OF CONSUMPTION DO NOT CHANGE EASILY

transcript: [' In the nature of things luxuries and the comforts of life belong to the leisure class.']
reference: IN THE NATURE OF THINGS LUXURIES AND THE COMFORTS OF LIFE BELONG TO THE LEISURE CLASS

transcript: [' Under the taboo certain victuals and more particularly certain beverages are strictly reserved for the use of the superior class.']
reference: UNDER THE TABU CERTAIN VICTUALS AND MORE PARTICULARLY CERTAIN BEVERAGES ARE STRICTLY RESERVED FOR THE USE OF THE SUPERIOR CLASS

transcript: [' Drunkenness and the other pathological consequences of the free use of stimulants therefore tend in their turn to become honorific as being a mark at the second remove of the superior status of those who are able to afford the indulgence.']
reference: DRUNKENNESS AND THE OTHER PATHOLOGICAL CONSEQUENCES OF THE FREE USE OF STIMULANTS THEREFORE TEND IN THEIR TURN TO BECOME HONORIFIC AS BEING A MARK AT THE SECOND REMOVE OF THE SUPERIOR STATUS OF THOSE WHO ARE ABLE TO AFFORD THE INDULGENCE

transcript: [' It has even happened that the name for certain diseased conditions of the body arising from such an origin has passed into everyday speech as a synonym for noble or gentle.']
reference: IT HAS EVEN HAPPENED THAT THE NAME FOR CERTAIN DISEASED CONDITIONS OF THE BODY ARISING FROM SUCH AN ORIGIN HAS PASSED INTO EVERYDAY SPEECH AS A SYNONYM FOR NOBLE OR GENTLE

transcript: [' The consumption of luxuries in the true sense is a consumption directed to the comfort of the consumer himself and is therefore a mark of the master.']
reference: THE CONSUMPTION OF LUXURIES IN THE TRUE SENSE IS A CONSUMPTION DIRECTED TO THE COMFORT OF THE CONSUMER HIMSELF AND IS THEREFORE A MARK OF THE MASTER

transcript: [' With many qualifications with more qualifications as the patriarchal tradition has gradually weakened the general rule is felt to be right and binding that women should consume only for the benefit of their masters.']
reference: WITH MANY QUALIFICATIONS WITH MORE QUALIFICATIONS AS THE PATRIARCHAL TRADITION HAS GRADUALLY WEAKENED THE GENERAL RULE IS FELT TO BE RIGHT AND BINDING THAT WOMEN SHOULD CONSUME ONLY FOR THE BENEFIT OF THEIR MASTERS

transcript: [" The objection of course presents itself that expenditure on women's dress and household paraphernalia is an obvious exception to this rule, but it will appear in the sequel that this exception is much more obvious than substantial."]
reference: THE OBJECTION OF COURSE PRESENTS ITSELF THAT EXPENDITURE ON WOMEN'S DRESS AND HOUSEHOLD PARAPHERNALIA IS AN OBVIOUS EXCEPTION TO THIS RULE BUT IT WILL APPEAR IN THE SEQUEL THAT THIS EXCEPTION IS MUCH MORE OBVIOUS THAN SUBSTANTIAL

transcript: [' The custom of festive gatherings probably originated in motives of conviviality and religion these motives are also present in the later development but they do not continue to be the sole motives.']
reference: THE CUSTOM OF FESTIVE GATHERINGS PROBABLY ORIGINATED IN MOTIVES OF CONVIVIALITY AND RELIGION THESE MOTIVES ARE ALSO PRESENT IN THE LATER DEVELOPMENT BUT THEY DO NOT CONTINUE TO BE THE SOLE MOTIVES

transcript: [' There is a more or less elaborate system of ranking rates.']
reference: THERE IS A MORE OR LESS ELABORATE SYSTEM OF RANK AND GRADES

transcript: [' This differentiation is furthered by the inheritance of wealth and the consequent inheritance of gentility.']
reference: THIS DIFFERENTIATION IS FURTHERED BY THE INHERITANCE OF WEALTH AND THE CONSEQUENT INHERITANCE OF GENTILITY

transcript: [' Many of these affiliated gentlemen of leisure are at the same time lesser men of substance in their own right so that some of them are scarcely at all others only partially to be rated as vicarious consumers.']
reference: MANY OF THESE AFFILIATED GENTLEMEN OF LEISURE ARE AT THE SAME TIME LESSER MEN OF SUBSTANCE IN THEIR OWN RIGHT SO THAT SOME OF THEM ARE SCARCELY AT ALL OTHERS ONLY PARTIALLY TO BE RATED AS VICARIOUS CONSUMERS

transcript: [' So many of them however as make up the retainer and hangers on of the patron may be classed as vicarious consumer without qualification.']
reference: SO MANY OF THEM HOWEVER AS MAKE UP THE RETAINER AND HANGERS ON OF THE PATRON MAY BE CLASSED AS VICARIOUS CONSUMER WITHOUT QUALIFICATION

transcript: [' Many of these again, and also many of the other aristocracy of less degree, have in turn attached to their persons a more or less comprehensive group of vicarious consumer in the persons of their wives and children their servants retainers, et cetera.']
reference: MANY OF THESE AGAIN AND ALSO MANY OF THE OTHER ARISTOCRACY OF LESS DEGREE HAVE IN TURN ATTACHED TO THEIR PERSONS A MORE OR LESS COMPREHENSIVE GROUP OF VICARIOUS CONSUMER IN THE PERSONS OF THEIR WIVES AND CHILDREN THEIR SERVANTS RETAINERS ET CETERA

transcript: [' The wearing of uniforms or liveries implies a considerable degree of dependence and may even be said to be a mark of servitude real or ostensible.']
reference: THE WEARING OF UNIFORMS OR LIVERIES IMPLIES A CONSIDERABLE DEGREE OF DEPENDENCE AND MAY EVEN BE SAID TO BE A MARK OF SERVITUDE REAL OR OSTENSIBLE

transcript: [' The wearers of uniforms and liveries may be roughly divided into two classes the free and the servile or the noble and the ignoble.']
reference: THE WEARERS OF UNIFORMS AND LIVERIES MAY BE ROUGHLY DIVIDED INTO TWO CLASSES THE FREE AND THE SERVILE OR THE NOBLE AND THE IGNOBLE

transcript: [' But the general distinction is not on that account to be overlooked.']
reference: BUT THE GENERAL DISTINCTION IS NOT ON THAT ACCOUNT TO BE OVERLOOKED

transcript: [' So those offices which are by right the proper employment of the leisure class are noble such as government fighting hunting the care of arms and accoutrements and the like in short those which may be classed as ostensibly predatory employments.']
reference: SO THOSE OFFICES WHICH ARE BY RIGHT THE PROPER EMPLOYMENT OF THE LEISURE CLASS ARE NOBLE SUCH AS GOVERNMENT FIGHTING HUNTING THE CARE OF ARMS AND ACCOUTREMENTS AND THE LIKE IN SHORT THOSE WHICH MAY BE CLASSED AS OSTENSIBLY PREDATORY EMPLOYMENTS

transcript: [' Whenever as in these cases the menial service in question has to do directly with the primary leisure employments of fighting and hunting it easily acquires a reflected honorific character.']
reference: WHENEVER AS IN THESE CASES THE MENIAL SERVICE IN QUESTION HAS TO DO DIRECTLY WITH THE PRIMARY LEISURE EMPLOYMENTS OF FIGHTING AND HUNTING IT EASILY ACQUIRES A REFLECTED HONORIFIC CHARACTER

transcript: [' The livery becomes obnoxious to nearly all who are required to wear it.']
reference: THE LIVERY BECOMES OBNOXIOUS TO NEARLY ALL WHO ARE REQUIRED TO WEAR IT

transcript: [' In a general way, though not wholly nor consistently, these two groups coincide.']
reference: IN A GENERAL WAY THOUGH NOT WHOLLY NOR CONSISTENTLY THESE TWO GROUPS COINCIDE

transcript: [' The dependent who was first delegated for these duties was the wife or the chief wife and as would be expected in the later development of the institution when the number of persons by whom these duties are customarily performed gradually narrows the wife remains the last.']
reference: THE DEPENDENT WHO WAS FIRST DELEGATED FOR THESE DUTIES WAS THE WIFE OR THE CHIEF WIFE AND AS WOULD BE EXPECTED IN THE LATER DEVELOPMENT OF THE INSTITUTION WHEN THE NUMBER OF PERSONS BY WHOM THESE DUTIES ARE CUSTOMARILY PERFORMED GRADUALLY NARROWS THE WIFE REMAINS THE LAST

transcript: [' But as we descend the social scale the point is presently reached where the duties of vicarious leisure and consumption devolve upon the wife alone.']
reference: BUT AS WE DESCEND THE SOCIAL SCALE THE POINT IS PRESENTLY REACHED WHERE THE DUTIES OF VICARIOUS LEISURE AND CONSUMPTION DEVOLVE UPON THE WIFE ALONE

transcript: [' In the communities of the western culture, this point is at present found among the lower middle class.']
reference: IN THE COMMUNITIES OF THE WESTERN CULTURE THIS POINT IS AT PRESENT FOUND AMONG THE LOWER MIDDLE CLASS

transcript: [' If beauty or comfort is achieved, and it is a more or less fortuitous circumstance if they are, they must be achieved by means and methods that commend themselves to the great economic law of wasted effort.']
reference: IF BEAUTY OR COMFORT IS ACHIEVED AND IT IS A MORE OR LESS FORTUITOUS CIRCUMSTANCE IF THEY ARE THEY MUST BE ACHIEVED BY MEANS AND METHODS THAT COMMEND THEMSELVES TO THE GREAT ECONOMIC LAW OF WASTED EFFORT

transcript: [" The man of the household also can do something in this direction and indeed he commonly does but with a still lower descent into the levels of indigence along the margin of the slums the man and presently also the children virtually cease to consume valuable goods for appearances and the woman remains virtually the sole exponent of the household's pecuniary decency"]
reference: THE MAN OF THE HOUSEHOLD ALSO CAN DO SOMETHING IN THIS DIRECTION AND INDEED HE COMMONLY DOES BUT WITH A STILL LOWER DESCENT INTO THE LEVELS OF INDIGENCE ALONG THE MARGIN OF THE SLUMS THE MAN AND PRESENTLY ALSO THE CHILDREN VIRTUALLY CEASE TO CONSUME VALUABLE GOODS FOR APPEARANCES AND THE WOMAN REMAINS VIRTUALLY THE SOLE EXPONENT OF THE HOUSEHOLD'S PECUNIARY DECENCY

transcript: [' Very much of squalor and discomfort will be endured before the last trinket or the last pretence of pecuniary decency is put away.']
reference: VERY MUCH OF SQUALOR AND DISCOMFORT WILL BE ENDURED BEFORE THE LAST TRINKET OR THE LAST PRETENSE OF PECUNIARY DECENCY IS PUT AWAY

transcript: [' There is no class and no country that has yielded so abjectly before the pressure of physical want as to deny themselves all gratification of this higher or spiritual need.']
reference: THERE IS NO CLASS AND NO COUNTRY THAT HAS YIELDED SO ABJECTLY BEFORE THE PRESSURE OF PHYSICAL WANT AS TO DENY THEMSELVES ALL GRATIFICATION OF THIS HIGHER OR SPIRITUAL NEED

transcript: [' The question is which of the two methods will most effectually reach the persons whose convictions it is desired to affect.']
reference: THE QUESTION IS WHICH OF THE TWO METHODS WILL MOST EFFECTIVELY REACH THE PERSONS WHOSE CONVICTIONS IT IS DESIRED TO AFFECT

transcript: [' Each will therefore serve about equally well during the earlier stages of social growth.']
reference: EACH WILL THEREFORE SERVE ABOUT EQUALLY WELL DURING THE EARLIER STAGES OF SOCIAL GROWTH

transcript: [' The modern organization of industry works in the same direction also by another line.']
reference: THE MODERN ORGANIZATION OF INDUSTRY WORKS IN THE SAME DIRECTION ALSO BY ANOTHER LINE

transcript: [' It is evident therefore that the present trend of the development is in the direction of heightening the utility of conspicuous consumption as compared with leisure.']
reference: IT IS EVIDENT THEREFORE THAT THE PRESENT TREND OF THE DEVELOPMENT IS IN THE DIRECTION OF HEIGHTENING THE UTILITY OF CONSPICUOUS CONSUMPTION AS COMPARED WITH LEISURE

transcript: [' It is also noticeable that the serviceability of consumption as a means of repute as well as the insistence on it as an element of decency is at its best in those portions of the community where the human contact of the individual is widest and the mobility of the population is greatest.']
reference: IT IS ALSO NOTICEABLE THAT THE SERVICEABILITY OF CONSUMPTION AS A MEANS OF REPUTE AS WELL AS THE INSISTENCE ON IT AS AN ELEMENT OF DECENCY IS AT ITS BEST IN THOSE PORTIONS OF THE COMMUNITY WHERE THE HUMAN CONTACT OF THE INDIVIDUAL IS WIDEST AND THE MOBILITY OF THE POPULATION IS GREATEST

transcript: [' Consumption becomes a larger element in the standard of living in the city than in the country.']
reference: CONSUMPTION BECOMES A LARGER ELEMENT IN THE STANDARD OF LIVING IN THE CITY THAN IN THE COUNTRY

transcript: [' Among the country population its place is to some extent taken by savings and home comforts known through the medium of neighborhood gossip sufficiently to serve the like general purpose of pecuniary repute.']
reference: AMONG THE COUNTRY POPULATION ITS PLACE IS TO SOME EXTENT TAKEN BY SAVINGS AND HOME COMFORTS KNOWN THROUGH THE MEDIUM OF NEIGHBORHOOD GOSSIP SUFFICIENTLY TO SERVE THE LIKE GENERAL PURPOSE OF PECUNIARY REPUTE

transcript: [' The result is a great mobility of the labor employed in printing, perhaps greater than in any other equally well defined and considerable body of workmen.']
reference: THE RESULT IS A GREAT MOBILITY OF THE LABOR EMPLOYED IN PRINTING PERHAPS GREATER THAN IN ANY OTHER EQUALLY WELL DEFINED AND CONSIDERABLE BODY OF WORKMEN

transcript: [' Under the simple test of effectiveness for advertising, we should expect to find leisure and the conspicuous consumption of goods dividing the field of pecuniary emulation pretty evenly between them at the outset.']
reference: UNDER THE SIMPLE TEST OF EFFECTIVENESS FOR ADVERTISING WE SHOULD EXPECT TO FIND LEISURE AND THE CONSPICUOUS CONSUMPTION OF GOODS DIVIDING THE FIELD OF PECUNIARY EMULATION PRETTY EVENLY BETWEEN THEM AT THE OUTSET

transcript: [' But the actual course of development has been somewhat different from this ideal scheme leisure held the first place at the start and came to hold a rank very much above wasteful consumption of goods both as a direct exponent of wealth and as an element in the standard of decency during the quasi peaceable culture.']
reference: BUT THE ACTUAL COURSE OF DEVELOPMENT HAS BEEN SOMEWHAT DIFFERENT FROM THIS IDEAL SCHEME LEISURE HELD THE FIRST PLACE AT THE START AND CAME TO HOLD A RANK VERY MUCH ABOVE WASTEFUL CONSUMPTION OF GOODS BOTH AS A DIRECT EXPONENT OF WEALTH AND AS AN ELEMENT IN THE STANDARD OF DECENCY DURING THE QUASI PEACEABLE CULTURE

transcript: [' Other circumstances permitting, that instinct disposes men to look with favor upon productive efficiency and on whatever is of human use.']
reference: OTHER CIRCUMSTANCES PERMITTING THAT INSTINCT DISPOSES MEN TO LOOK WITH FAVOR UPON PRODUCTIVE EFFICIENCY AND ON WHATEVER IS OF HUMAN USE

transcript: [' A reconciliation between the two conflicting requirements is effected by a resort to make believe many and intricate polite observances and social duties of a ceremonial nature are developed many organizations are founded with some specious object of amelioration embodied in their official style and title there is much coming and going and a deal of talk to the end that the talkers may not have occasion to reflect on what is the effectual economic value of their traffic.']
reference: A RECONCILIATION BETWEEN THE TWO CONFLICTING REQUIREMENTS IS EFFECTED BY A RESORT TO MAKE BELIEVE MANY AND INTRICATE POLITE OBSERVANCES AND SOCIAL DUTIES OF A CEREMONIAL NATURE ARE DEVELOPED MANY ORGANIZATIONS ARE FOUNDED WITH SOME SPECIOUS OBJECT OF AMELIORATION EMBODIED IN THEIR OFFICIAL STYLE AND TITLE THERE IS MUCH COMING AND GOING AND A DEAL OF TALK TO THE END THAT THE TALKERS MAY NOT HAVE OCCASION TO REFLECT ON WHAT IS THE EFFECTUAL ECONOMIC VALUE OF THEIR TRAFFIC

transcript: [' The salient features of this development of domestic service have already been indicated.']
reference: THE SALIENT FEATURES OF THIS DEVELOPMENT OF DOMESTIC SERVICE HAVE ALREADY BEEN INDICATED

transcript: [" Throughout the entire evolution of conspicuous expenditure whether of goods or of services or human life runs the obvious implication that in order to effectually mend the consumer's good fame it must be an expenditure of superfluities."]
reference: THROUGHOUT THE ENTIRE EVOLUTION OF CONSPICUOUS EXPENDITURE WHETHER OF GOODS OR OF SERVICES OR HUMAN LIFE RUNS THE OBVIOUS IMPLICATION THAT IN ORDER TO EFFECTUALLY MEND THE CONSUMER'S GOOD FAME IT MUST BE AN EXPENDITURE OF SUPERFLUITIES

transcript: [' As used in the speech of everyday life, the word carries an undertone of deprecation.']
reference: AS USED IN THE SPEECH OF EVERYDAY LIFE THE WORD CARRIES AN UNDERTONE OF DEPRECATION

transcript: [' The use of the word waste as a technical term therefore implies no deprecation of the motives or of the ends sought by the consumer under this canon of conspicuous waste.']
reference: THE USE OF THE WORD WASTE AS A TECHNICAL TERM THEREFORE IMPLIES NO DEPRECATION OF THE MOTIVES OR OF THE ENDS SOUGHT BY THE CONSUMER UNDER THIS CANON OF CONSPICUOUS WASTE

transcript: [' But it is on other grounds worth noting that the term waste in the language of everyday life implies depreciation of what is characterized as wasteful.']
reference: BUT IT IS ON OTHER GROUNDS WORTH NOTING THAT THE TERM WASTE IN THE LANGUAGE OF EVERYDAY LIFE IMPLIES DEPRECATION OF WHAT IS CHARACTERIZED AS WASTEFUL

transcript: [' In strict accuracy nothing should be included under the head of conspicuous waste but such expenditure as is incurred on the ground of an invidious pecuniary comparison']
reference: IN STRICT ACCURACY NOTHING SHOULD BE INCLUDED UNDER THE HEAD OF CONSPICUOUS WASTE BUT SUCH EXPENDITURE AS IS INCURRED ON THE GROUND OF AN INVIDIOUS PECUNIARY COMPARISON

transcript: [' An article may be useful and wasteful both and its utility to the consumer may be made up of use and waste in the most varying proportions.']
reference: AN ARTICLE MAY BE USEFUL AND WASTEFUL BOTH AND ITS UTILITY TO THE CONSUMER MAY BE MADE UP OF USE AND WASTE IN THE MOST VARYING PROPORTIONS

transcript: [' And often has my mother said while on her lap I laid my head, She feared for time I was not made, But for eternity.']
reference: AND OFTEN HAS MY MOTHER SAID WHILE ON HER LAP I LAID MY HEAD SHE FEARED FOR TIME I WAS NOT MADE BUT FOR ETERNITY

transcript: [" Why are we to be denied each other's society?"]
reference: WHY ARE WE TO BE DENIED EACH OTHER'S SOCIETY

transcript: [' Why are we to be divided?']
reference: WHY ARE WE TO BE DIVIDED

transcript: [' Surely it must be because we are in danger of loving each other too well, of losing sight of the creator in idolatry of the creature.']
reference: SURELY IT MUST BE BECAUSE WE ARE IN DANGER OF LOVING EACH OTHER TOO WELL OF LOSING SIGHT OF THE CREATOR IN IDOLATRY OF THE CREATURE

transcript: [' We used to dispute about politics and religion.']
reference: WE USED TO DISPUTE ABOUT POLITICS AND RELIGION

transcript: [" She, a Toryan clergyman's daughter, was always in a minority of one in our house of violent dissent and radicalism."]
reference: SHE A TORY AND CLERGYMAN'S DAUGHTER WAS ALWAYS IN A MINORITY OF ONE IN OUR HOUSE OF VIOLENT DISSENT AND RADICALISM

transcript: [' Her feeble health gave her her yielding manner, for she could never oppose any one without gathering up all her strength for the struggle.']
reference: HER FEEBLE HEALTH GAVE HER HER YIELDING MANNER FOR SHE COULD NEVER OPPOSE ANY ONE WITHOUT GATHERING UP ALL HER STRENGTH FOR THE STRUGGLE

transcript: [' He spoke French perfectly, I have been told, when need was, but delighted usually in talking the broadest Yorkshire.']
reference: HE SPOKE FRENCH PERFECTLY I HAVE BEEN TOLD WHEN NEED WAS BUT DELIGHTED USUALLY IN TALKING THE BROADEST YORKSHIRE

transcript: [" And so life and death have dispersed the circle of violent radicals and dissenters into which twenty years ago the little quiet resolute clergyman's daughter was received and by whom she was truly loved and honored."]
reference: AND SO LIFE AND DEATH HAVE DISPERSED THE CIRCLE OF VIOLENT RADICALS AND DISSENTERS INTO WHICH TWENTY YEARS AGO THE LITTLE QUIET RESOLUTE CLERGYMAN'S DAUGHTER WAS RECEIVED AND BY WHOM SHE WAS TRULY LOVED AND HONOURED

transcript: [' January and February of eighteen thirty seven had passed away and still there was no reply from southey.']
reference: JANUARY AND FEBRUARY OF EIGHTEEN THIRTY SEVEN HAD PASSED AWAY AND STILL THERE WAS NO REPLY FROM SOUTHEY

transcript: [' I am not depreciating it when I say that in these times it is not rare.']
reference: I AM NOT DEPRECIATING IT WHEN I SAY THAT IN THESE TIMES IT IS NOT RARE

transcript: [' But it is not with a view to distinction that you should cultivate this talent, if you consult your own happiness.']
reference: BUT IT IS NOT WITH A VIEW TO DISTINCTION THAT YOU SHOULD CULTIVATE THIS TALENT IF YOU CONSULT YOUR OWN HAPPINESS

transcript: [' You will say that a woman has no need of such a caution. There can be no peril in it for her.']
reference: YOU WILL SAY THAT A WOMAN HAS NO NEED OF SUCH A CAUTION THERE CAN BE NO PERIL IN IT FOR HER

transcript: [' The more she is engaged in her proper duties, the less leisure will she have for it, even as an accomplishment and a recreation.']
reference: THE MORE SHE IS ENGAGED IN HER PROPER DUTIES THE LESS LEISURE WILL SHE HAVE FOR IT EVEN AS AN ACCOMPLISHMENT AND A RECREATION

transcript: [' To those duties you have not yet been called, and when you are you will be less eager for celebrity.']
reference: TO THOSE DUTIES YOU HAVE NOT YET BEEN CALLED AND WHEN YOU ARE YOU WILL BE LESS EAGER FOR CELEBRITY

transcript: [' But do not suppose that I disparage the gift which you possess, nor that I would discourage you from exercising it. I only exhort you so to think of it and so to use it as to render it conducive to your own permanent good.']
reference: BUT DO NOT SUPPOSE THAT I DISPARAGE THE GIFT WHICH YOU POSSESS NOR THAT I WOULD DISCOURAGE YOU FROM EXERCISING IT I ONLY EXHORT YOU SO TO THINK OF IT AND SO TO USE IT AS TO RENDER IT CONDUCIVE TO YOUR OWN PERMANENT GOOD

transcript: [' Farewell, madame.']
reference: FAREWELL MADAM

transcript: [' Though I may be but an ungracious adviser, you will allow me therefore to subscribe myself with the best wishes for your happiness here and hereafter your true friend, Robert Southey.']
reference: THOUGH I MAY BE BUT AN UNGRACIOUS ADVISER YOU WILL ALLOW ME THEREFORE TO SUBSCRIBE MYSELF WITH THE BEST WISHES FOR YOUR HAPPINESS HERE AND HEREAFTER YOUR TRUE FRIEND ROBERT SOUTHEY

transcript: [' Sir, march sixteenth.']
reference: SIR MARCH SIXTEENTH

transcript: [' I have not ventured to hope for such a reply, so considerate in its tone, so noble in its spirit.']
reference: I HAD NOT VENTURED TO HOPE FOR SUCH A REPLY SO CONSIDERATE IN ITS TONE SO NOBLE IN ITS SPIRIT

transcript: [' I know the first letter I wrote to you was all senseless trash from beginning to end, but I am not altogether the idle dreaming being it would seem to denote.']
reference: I KNOW THE FIRST LETTER I WROTE TO YOU WAS ALL SENSELESS TRASH FROM BEGINNING TO END BUT I AM NOT ALTOGETHER THE IDLE DREAMING BEING IT WOULD SEEM TO DENOTE

transcript: [' I thought it therefore my duty when I left school to become a governess.']
reference: I THOUGHT IT THEREFORE MY DUTY WHEN I LEFT SCHOOL TO BECOME A GOVERNESS

transcript: [' In the evenings, I confess, I do think, but I never trouble any one else with my thoughts.']
reference: IN THE EVENINGS I CONFESS I DO THINK BUT I NEVER TROUBLE ANY ONE ELSE WITH MY THOUGHTS

transcript: [' I carefully avoid any appearance of preoccupation and eccentricity which might lead those I live amongst to suspect the nature of my pursuits.']
reference: I CAREFULLY AVOID ANY APPEARANCE OF PREOCCUPATION AND ECCENTRICITY WHICH MIGHT LEAD THOSE I LIVE AMONGST TO SUSPECT THE NATURE OF MY PURSUITS

transcript: [" I don't always succeed, for sometimes when I'm teaching or sewing I would rather be reading or writing, but I try to deny myself, and my father's approbation amply rewarded me for the privation."]
reference: I DON'T ALWAYS SUCCEED FOR SOMETIMES WHEN I'M TEACHING OR SEWING I WOULD RATHER BE READING OR WRITING BUT I TRY TO DENY MYSELF AND MY FATHER'S APPROBATION AMPLY REWARDED ME FOR THE PRIVATION

transcript: [' Again I thank you this incident I suppose will be renewed no more if I live to be an old woman I shall remember it thirty years hence as a bright dream.']
reference: AGAIN I THANK YOU THIS INCIDENT I SUPPOSE WILL BE RENEWED NO MORE IF I LIVE TO BE AN OLD WOMAN I SHALL REMEMBER IT THIRTY YEARS HENCE AS A BRIGHT DREAM

transcript: [' p s pray sir excuse me for writing to you a second time I could not help writing partly to tell you how thankful I am for your kindness and partly to let you know that your advice shall not be wasted however sorrowfully and reluctantly it may be at first followed c b']
reference: P S PRAY SIR EXCUSE ME FOR WRITING TO YOU A SECOND TIME I COULD NOT HELP WRITING PARTLY TO TELL YOU HOW THANKFUL I AM FOR YOUR KINDNESS AND PARTLY TO LET YOU KNOW THAT YOUR ADVICE SHALL NOT BE WASTED HOWEVER SORROWFULLY AND RELUCTANTLY IT MAY BE AT FIRST FOLLOWED C B

transcript: [" I cannot deny myself the gratification of inserting Southey's reply."]
reference: I CANNOT DENY MYSELF THE GRATIFICATION OF INSERTING SOUTHEY'S REPLY

transcript: [' Keswick, march twenty second eighteen thirty seven. Dear madam,']
reference: KESWICK MARCH TWENTY SECOND EIGHTEEN THIRTY SEVEN DEAR MADAM

transcript: [' Your letter has given me great pleasure and I should not forgive myself if I did not tell you so']
reference: YOUR LETTER HAS GIVEN ME GREAT PLEASURE AND I SHOULD NOT FORGIVE MYSELF IF I DID NOT TELL YOU SO

transcript: [' Of this second letter also she spoke and told me that it contained an invitation for her to go and see the poet if ever she visited the lakes.']
reference: OF THIS SECOND LETTER ALSO SHE SPOKE AND TOLD ME THAT IT CONTAINED AN INVITATION FOR HER TO GO AND SEE THE POET IF EVER SHE VISITED THE LAKES

transcript: [' On august twenty seventh eighteen thirty seven she writes,']
reference: ON AUGUST TWENTY SEVENTH EIGHTEEN THIRTY SEVEN SHE WRITES

transcript: [' Come, come, I am getting really tired of your absence.']
reference: COME COME I AM GETTING REALLY TIRED OF YOUR ABSENCE

transcript: [' Saturday after Saturday comes around and I can have no hope of hearing your knock at the door and then being told that Miss E is come oh dear!']
reference: SATURDAY AFTER SATURDAY COMES ROUND AND I CAN HAVE NO HOPE OF HEARING YOUR KNOCK AT THE DOOR AND THEN BEING TOLD THAT MISS E IS COME OH DEAR

transcript: [' In this monotonous life of mine, that was a pleasant event.']
reference: IN THIS MONOTONOUS LIFE OF MINE THAT WAS A PLEASANT EVENT

transcript: [' I wish it would recur again, but it will take two or three interviews before the stiffness, the estrangement of this long separation will wear away.']
reference: I WISH IT WOULD RECUR AGAIN BUT IT WILL TAKE TWO OR THREE INTERVIEWS BEFORE THE STIFFNESS THE ESTRANGEMENT OF THIS LONG SEPARATION WILL WEAR AWAY

transcript: [' My eyes fill with tears when I contrast the bliss of such a state, brightened by hopes of the future, with the melancholy state I now live in, uncertain that I ever felt true contrition, wandering in thought and deed, longing for holiness which I shall never, never attain, smitten at times to the heart with the conviction that ghastly Calvinistic doctrines are true, darkened, in short, by the very shadows of spiritual death.']
reference: MY EYES FILL WITH TEARS WHEN I CONTRAST THE BLISS OF SUCH A STATE BRIGHTENED BY HOPES OF THE FUTURE WITH THE MELANCHOLY STATE I NOW LIVE IN UNCERTAIN THAT I EVER FELT TRUE CONTRITION WANDERING IN THOUGHT AND DEED LONGING FOR HOLINESS WHICH I SHALL NEVER NEVER OBTAIN SMITTEN AT TIMES TO THE HEART WITH THE CONVICTION THAT GHASTLY CALVINISTIC DOCTRINES ARE TRUE DARKENED IN SHORT BY THE VERY SHADOWS OF SPIRITUAL DEATH

transcript: [' If Christian perfection be necessary to salvation, I shall never be saved. My heart is a very hotbed for sinful thoughts, and when I decide on an action, I scarcely remember to look to my Redeemer for direction.']
reference: IF CHRISTIAN PERFECTION BE NECESSARY TO SALVATION I SHALL NEVER BE SAVED MY HEART IS A VERY HOTBED FOR SINFUL THOUGHTS AND WHEN I DECIDE ON AN ACTION I SCARCELY REMEMBER TO LOOK TO MY REDEEMER FOR DIRECTION

transcript: [' And meantime, I know the greatness of Jehovah. I acknowledge the perfection of his word. I adore the purity of the Christian faith. My theory is right, my practice horribly wrong.']
reference: AND MEANTIME I KNOW THE GREATNESS OF JEHOVAH I ACKNOWLEDGE THE PERFECTION OF HIS WORD I ADORE THE PURITY OF THE CHRISTIAN FAITH MY THEORY IS RIGHT MY PRACTICE HORRIBLY WRONG

transcript: [' The Christmas holidays came and she and Anne returned to the parsonage and to that happy home circle in which alone their natures expanded amongst all other people they shriveled up more or less.']
reference: THE CHRISTMAS HOLIDAYS CAME AND SHE AND ANNE RETURNED TO THE PARSONAGE AND TO THAT HAPPY HOME CIRCLE IN WHICH ALONE THEIR NATURES EXPANDED AMONGST ALL OTHER PEOPLE THEY SHRIVELLED UP MORE OR LESS

transcript: [' Indeed, there were only one or two strangers who could be admitted among the sisters without producing the same result.']
reference: INDEED THERE WERE ONLY ONE OR TWO STRANGERS WHO COULD BE ADMITTED AMONG THE SISTERS WITHOUT PRODUCING THE SAME RESULT

transcript: [' She was gone out into the village on some errand when, as she was descending the steep street, her foot slipped on the ice and she fell. It was dark and no one saw her mischance till after a time her groans attracted the attention of a passer by.']
reference: SHE WAS GONE OUT INTO THE VILLAGE ON SOME ERRAND WHEN AS SHE WAS DESCENDING THE STEEP STREET HER FOOT SLIPPED ON THE ICE AND SHE FELL IT WAS DARK AND NO ONE SAW HER MISCHANCE TILL AFTER A TIME HER GROANS ATTRACTED THE ATTENTION OF A PASSER BY

transcript: [" Unfortunately the fracture could not be set till six o'clock the next morning as no surgeon was to be had before that time and she now lies at our house in a very doubtful and dangerous state."]
reference: UNFORTUNATELY THE FRACTURE COULD NOT BE SET TILL SIX O'CLOCK THE NEXT MORNING AS NO SURGEON WAS TO BE HAD BEFORE THAT TIME AND SHE NOW LIES AT OUR HOUSE IN A VERY DOUBTFUL AND DANGEROUS STATE

transcript: [' However, remembering what you told me, namely that you had commended the matter to a higher decision than ours, and that you were resolved to submit with resignation to that decision, whatever it might be, I hold it my duty to yield also and to be silent, and may be all for the best.']
reference: HOWEVER REMEMBERING WHAT YOU TOLD ME NAMELY THAT YOU HAD COMMENDED THE MATTER TO A HIGHER DECISION THAN OURS AND THAT YOU WERE RESOLVED TO SUBMIT WITH RESIGNATION TO THAT DECISION WHATEVER IT MIGHT BE I HOLD IT MY DUTY TO YIELD ALSO AND TO BE SILENT IT MAY BE ALL FOR THE BEST

transcript: [' After this disappointment I never dare reckon with certainty on the enjoyment of a pleasure again it seems as if some fatality stood between you and me.']
reference: AFTER THIS DISAPPOINTMENT I NEVER DARE RECKON WITH CERTAINTY ON THE ENJOYMENT OF A PLEASURE AGAIN IT SEEMS AS IF SOME FATALITY STOOD BETWEEN YOU AND ME

transcript: [' I am not good enough for you, and you must be kept from the contamination of too intimate society.']
reference: I AM NOT GOOD ENOUGH FOR YOU AND YOU MUST BE KEPT FROM THE CONTAMINATION OF TOO INTIMATE SOCIETY

transcript: [" A good neighbour of the Bronteys, a clever intelligent Yorkshire woman who keeps a druggist's shop in Haworth and from her occupation her experience and excellent sense holds the position of village doctress and nurse and as such has been a friend in many a time of trial and sickness and death in the households round, told me a characteristic little incident connected with Tabby's fractured leg."]
reference: A GOOD NEIGHBOUR OF THE BRONTES A CLEVER INTELLIGENT YORKSHIRE WOMAN WHO KEEPS A DRUGGIST'S SHOP IN HAWORTH AND FROM HER OCCUPATION HER EXPERIENCE AND EXCELLENT SENSE HOLDS THE POSITION OF VILLAGE DOCTRESS AND NURSE AND AS SUCH HAS BEEN A FRIEND IN MANY A TIME OF TRIAL AND SICKNESS AND DEATH IN THE HOUSEHOLDS ROUND TOLD ME A CHARACTERISTIC LITTLE INCIDENT CONNECTED WITH TABBY'S FRACTURED LEG

transcript: [' Tabby had lived with them for ten or twelve years and was, as Charlotte expressed it, one of the family.']
reference: TABBY HAD LIVED WITH THEM FOR TEN OR TWELVE YEARS AND WAS AS CHARLOTTE EXPRESSED IT ONE OF THE FAMILY

transcript: [' He refused at first to listen to the careful advice, it was repugnant to his liberal nature.']
reference: HE REFUSED AT FIRST TO LISTEN TO THE CAREFUL ADVICE IT WAS REPUGNANT TO HIS LIBERAL NATURE

transcript: [' This decision was communicated to the girls.']
reference: THIS DECISION WAS COMMUNICATED TO THE GIRLS

transcript: [' Tabby had tended them in their childhood they and none other should tend her in her infirmity and age.']
reference: TABBY HAD TENDED THEM IN THEIR CHILDHOOD THEY AND NONE OTHER SHOULD TEND HER IN HER INFIRMITY AND AGE

transcript: [' At tea time they were sad and silent and the meal went away untouched by any of the three.']
reference: AT TEA TIME THEY WERE SAD AND SILENT AND THE MEAL WENT AWAY UNTOUCHED BY ANY OF THE THREE

transcript: [' She had another weight on her mind this Christmas.']
reference: SHE HAD ANOTHER WEIGHT ON HER MIND THIS CHRISTMAS

transcript: [' But Anne had begun to suffer just before the holidays and Charlotte watched over her younger sisters with the jealous vigilance of some wild creature that changes her very nature if danger threatens her young.']
reference: BUT ANNE HAD BEGUN TO SUFFER JUST BEFORE THE HOLIDAYS AND CHARLOTTE WATCHED OVER HER YOUNGER SISTERS WITH THE JEALOUS VIGILANCE OF SOME WILD CREATURE THAT CHANGES HER VERY NATURE IF DANGER THREATENS HER YOUNG

transcript: [" Stung by anxiety for the little sister, she upbraided Miss W for her fancied indifference to Anne's state of health."]
reference: STUNG BY ANXIETY FOR THIS LITTLE SISTER SHE UPBRAIDED MISS W FOR HER FANCIED INDIFFERENCE TO ANNE'S STATE OF HEALTH

transcript: [" Still her heart had received a shock in the perception of anne's delicacy and all these holidays she watched over her with the longing fond anxiety which is so full of sudden pangs of fear."]
reference: STILL HER HEART HAD RECEIVED A SHOCK IN THE PERCEPTION OF ANNE'S DELICACY AND ALL THESE HOLIDAYS SHE WATCHED OVER HER WITH THE LONGING FOND ANXIETY WHICH IS SO FULL OF SUDDEN PANGS OF FEAR

transcript: [' I doubt whether branwell was maintaining himself at this time.']
reference: I DOUBT WHETHER BRANWELL WAS MAINTAINING HIMSELF AT THIS TIME

transcript: [' To celebrate the arrival of her son Sylvia gave a splendid supper to which she had invited all her relatives and it was a good opportunity for me to make their acquaintance.']
reference: TO CELEBRATE THE ARRIVAL OF HER SON SILVIA GAVE A SPLENDID SUPPER TO WHICH SHE HAD INVITED ALL HER RELATIVES AND IT WAS A GOOD OPPORTUNITY FOR ME TO MAKE THEIR ACQUAINTANCE

transcript: [' Without saying it positively, she made me understand that being herself an illustrious member of the republic of letters, she was well aware that she was speaking to an insect.']
reference: WITHOUT SAYING IT POSITIVELY SHE MADE ME UNDERSTAND THAT BEING HERSELF AN ILLUSTRIOUS MEMBER OF THE REPUBLIC OF LETTERS SHE WAS WELL AWARE THAT SHE WAS SPEAKING TO AN INSECT

transcript: [' In order to please her, I spoke to her of the Abbe Conti, and I had occasion to quote two lines of that profound writer.']
reference: IN ORDER TO PLEASE HER I SPOKE TO HER OF THE ABBE CONTI AND I HAD OCCASION TO QUOTE TWO LINES OF THAT PROFOUND WRITER

transcript: [' Madame corrected me with a patronizing air for my pronunciation of the word scevra which means divided saying that it ought to be pronounced sciura and she added that I ought to be very glad to have learned so much on the first day of my arrival in Paris telling me that it would be an important day in my life']
reference: MADAM CORRECTED ME WITH A PATRONIZING AIR FOR MY PRONUNCIATION OF THE WORD SCEVRA WHICH MEANS DIVIDED SAYING THAT IT OUGHT TO BE PRONOUNCED SCEURA AND SHE ADDED THAT I OUGHT TO BE VERY GLAD TO HAVE LEARNED SO MUCH ON THE FIRST DAY OF MY ARRIVAL IN PARIS TELLING ME THAT IT WOULD BE AN IMPORTANT DAY IN MY LIFE

transcript: [' Her face was an enigma for it inspired every one with the warmest sympathy and yet if you examined it attentively there was not one beautiful feature she could not be called handsome but no one could have thought her ugly']
reference: HER FACE WAS AN ENIGMA FOR IT INSPIRED EVERYONE WITH THE WARMEST SYMPATHY AND YET IF YOU EXAMINED IT ATTENTIVELY THERE WAS NOT ONE BEAUTIFUL FEATURE SHE COULD NOT BE CALLED HANDSOME BUT NO ONE COULD HAVE THOUGHT HER UGLY

transcript: [' Silvia was the adoration of france and her talent was the real support of all the comedies which the greatest authors wrote for her especially of the plays of Marivaux for without her his comedies would never have gone to posterity']
reference: SILVIA WAS THE ADORATION OF FRANCE AND HER TALENT WAS THE REAL SUPPORT OF ALL THE COMEDIES WHICH THE GREATEST AUTHORS WROTE FOR HER ESPECIALLY OF THE PLAYS OF MARIVAUX FOR WITHOUT HER HIS COMEDIES WOULD NEVER HAVE GONE TO POSTERITY

transcript: [' Sylvia did not think that her good conduct was a merit for she knew that she was virtuous only because her self love compelled her to be so and she never exhibited any pride or assumed any superiority towards her theatrical sisters although satisfied to shine by their talent or their beauty they cared little about rendering themselves conspicuous by their virtue']
reference: SILVIA DID NOT THINK THAT HER GOOD CONDUCT WAS A MERIT FOR SHE KNEW THAT SHE WAS VIRTUOUS ONLY BECAUSE HER SELF LOVE COMPELLED HER TO BE SO AND SHE NEVER EXHIBITED ANY PRIDE OR ASSUMED ANY SUPERIORITY TOWARDS HER THEATRICAL SISTERS ALTHOUGH SATISFIED TO SHINE BY THEIR TALENT OR THEIR BEAUTY THEY CARED LITTLE ABOUT RENDERING THEMSELVES CONSPICUOUS BY THEIR VIRTUE

transcript: [' Two years before her death I saw her perform the character of Mary Ann in the comedy of Mirabeau and in spite of her age and declining health the illusion was complete.']
reference: TWO YEARS BEFORE HER DEATH I SAW HER PERFORM THE CHARACTER OF MARIANNE IN THE COMEDY OF MARIVAUX AND IN SPITE OF HER AGE AND DECLINING HEALTH THE ILLUSION WAS COMPLETE

transcript: [' She was honourably buried in the church of saint Sauveur without the slightest opposition from the venerable priest who far from sharing the anti Christian intolerancy of the clergy in general said that her profession as an actress had not hindered her from being a good Christian and that the earth was a common mother of all human beings as Jesus Christ had been the saviour of all mankind.']
reference: SHE WAS HONOURABLY BURIED IN THE CHURCH OF SAINT SAUVEUR WITHOUT THE SLIGHTEST OPPOSITION FROM THE VENERABLE PRIEST WHO FAR FROM SHARING THE ANTI CHRISTAIN INTOLERANCY OF THE CLERGY IN GENERAL SAID THAT HER PROFESSION AS AN ACTRESS HAD NOT HINDERED HER FROM BEING A GOOD CHRISTIAN AND THAT THE EARTH WAS THE COMMON MOTHER OF ALL HUMAN BEINGS AS JESUS CHRIST HAD BEEN THE SAVIOUR OF ALL MANKIND

transcript: [' You will forgive me dear reader if I have made you attend the funeral of Silvia ten years before her death believe me I have no intention of performing a miracle you may console yourself with the idea that I shall spare you that unpleasant task when poor Silvia dies.']
reference: YOU WILL FORGIVE ME DEAR READER IF I HAVE MADE YOU ATTEND THE FUNERAL OF SILVIA TEN YEARS BEFORE HER DEATH BELIEVE ME I HAVE NO INTENTION OF PERFORMING A MIRACLE YOU MAY CONSOLE YOURSELF WITH THE IDEA THAT I SHALL SPARE YOU THAT UNPLEASANT TASK WHEN POOR SILVIA DIES

transcript: [' I never had any family.']
reference: I NEVER HAD ANY FAMILY

transcript: [' I had a name, I believe, in my young days, but I have forgotten it since I have been in service.']
reference: I HAD A NAME I BELIEVE IN MY YOUNG DAYS BUT I HAVE FORGOTTEN IT SINCE I HAVE BEEN IN SERVICE

transcript: [' I shall call you esprit.']
reference: I SHALL CALL YOU ESPRIT

transcript: [' You do me a great honor.']
reference: YOU DO ME A GREAT HONOUR

transcript: [' Here, go and get me change for a louis. I have it, sir.']
reference: HERE GO AND GET ME CHANGE FOR A LOUIS I HAVE IT SIR

transcript: [' At your service, sir.']
reference: AT YOUR SERVICE SIR

transcript: [' Madame Quinton besides can answer your inquiries.']
reference: MADAME QUINSON BESIDES CAN ANSWER YOUR ENQUIRIES

transcript: [' I see a quantity of chairs for hire at the rate of one sou men reading the newspaper under the shade of the trees girls and men breakfasting either alone or in company waiters who were rapidly going up and down a narrow staircase hidden under the foliage.']
reference: I SEE A QUANTITY OF CHAIRS FOR HIRE AT THE RATE OF ONE SOU MEN READING THE NEWSPAPER UNDER THE SHADE OF THE TREES GIRLS AND MEN BREAKFASTING EITHER ALONE OR IN COMPANY WAITERS WHO WERE RAPIDLY GOING UP AND DOWN A NARROW STAIRCASE HIDDEN UNDER THE FOLIAGE

transcript: [' I sit down at a small table. A waiter comes immediately to inquire my wishes.']
reference: I SIT DOWN AT A SMALL TABLE A WAITER COMES IMMEDIATELY TO ENQUIRE MY WISHES

transcript: [' I tell him to give me some coffee if it is good.']
reference: I TELL HIM TO GIVE ME SOME COFFEE IF IT IS GOOD

transcript: [' Then turning towards me he says that I look like a foreigner and when I say that I am an Italian he begins to speak to me of the court the city of the theatres and at last he offers to accompany me everywhere']
reference: THEN TURNING TOWARDS ME HE SAYS THAT I LOOK LIKE A FOREIGNER AND WHEN I SAY THAT I AM AN ITALIAN HE BEGINS TO SPEAK TO ME OF THE COURT OF THE CITY OF THE THEATRES AND AT LAST HE OFFERS TO ACCOMPANY ME EVERYWHERE

transcript: [' I thank him and take my leave.']
reference: I THANK HIM AND TAKE MY LEAVE

transcript: [' I address him in Italian and he answers very wittily, but his way of speaking makes me smile and I tell him why.']
reference: I ADDRESS HIM IN ITALIAN AND HE ANSWERS VERY WITTILY BUT HIS WAY OF SPEAKING MAKES ME SMILE AND I TELL HIM WHY

transcript: [' My remark pleases him, but I soon prove to him that it is not the right way to speak, however perfect may have been the language of that ancient writer.']
reference: MY REMARK PLEASES HIM BUT I SOON PROVE TO HIM THAT IT IS NOT THE RIGHT WAY TO SPEAK HOWEVER PERFECT MAY HAVE BEEN THE LANGUAGE OF THAT ANCIENT WRITER

transcript: [' I see a crowd in one corner of the garden everybody standing still and looking up.']
reference: I SEE A CROWD IN ONE CORNER OF THE GARDEN EVERYBODY STANDING STILL AND LOOKING UP

transcript: [' Is there not a meridian everywhere?']
reference: IS THERE NOT A MERIDIAN EVERYWHERE

transcript: [' Yes, but the meridian of the Palais Royal is the most exact.']
reference: YES BUT THE MERIDIAN OF THE PALAIS ROYAL IS THE MOST EXACT

transcript: [" That is true, bas d'Augri."]
reference: THAT IS TRUE BADAUDERIE

transcript: [' All these honest persons are waiting their turn to get their snuff boxes filled.']
reference: ALL THESE HONEST PERSONS ARE WAITING THEIR TURN TO GET THEIR SNUFF BOXES FILLED

transcript: [' It is sold everywhere, but for the last three weeks nobody will use any snuff but that sold at the Civette Cat.']
reference: IT IS SOLD EVERYWHERE BUT FOR THE LAST THREE WEEKS NOBODY WILL USE ANY SNUFF BUT THAT SOLD AT THE CIVET CAT

transcript: [' Is it better than anywhere else?']
reference: IS IT BETTER THAN ANYWHERE ELSE

transcript: [' But how did she manage to render it so fashionable?']
reference: BUT HOW DID SHE MANAGE TO RENDER IT SO FASHIONABLE

transcript: [' Simply by stopping her carriage two or three times before the shop to have her snuff box filled and by saying aloud to the young girl who handed back the box that her snuff was the very best in Paris.']
reference: SIMPLY BY STOPPING HER CARRIAGE TWO OR THREE TIMES BEFORE THE SHOP TO HAVE HER SNUFF BOX FILLED AND BY SAYING ALOUD TO THE YOUNG GIRL WHO HANDED BACK THE BOX THAT HER SNUFF WAS THE VERY BEST IN PARIS

transcript: [' You are now in the only country in the world where wit can make a fortune by selling either a genuine or a false article. In the first case it receives the welcome of intelligent and talented people, and in the second fools are always ready to reward it, for silliness is truly a characteristic of the people here, and however wonderful it may appear, silliness is the daughter of wit.']
reference: YOU ARE NOW IN THE ONLY COUNTRY IN THE WORLD WHERE WIT CAN MAKE A FORTUNE BY SELLING EITHER A GENUINE OR A FALSE ARTICLE IN THE FIRST CASE IT RECEIVES THE WELCOME OF INTELLIGENT AND TALENTED PEOPLE AND IN THE SECOND FOOLS ARE ALWAYS READY TO REWARD IT FOR SILLINESS IS TRULY A CHARACTERISTIC OF THE PEOPLE HERE AND HOWEVER WONDERFUL IT MAY APPEAR SILLINESS IS THE DAUGHTER OF WIT

transcript: [' Let a man run and everybody will run after him. The crowd will not stop unless the man is proved to be mad. But to prove it is indeed a difficult task because we have a crowd of men who mad from their birth are still considered wise.']
reference: LET A MAN RUN AND EVERYBODY WILL RUN AFTER HIM THE CROWD WILL NOT STOP UNLESS THE MAN IS PROVED TO BE MAD BUT TO PROVE IT IS INDEED A DIFFICULT TASK BECAUSE WE HAVE A CROWD OF MEN WHO MAD FROM THEIR BIRTH ARE STILL CONSIDERED WISE

transcript: [' It seems to me, I replied, that such approval, such ratification of the opinion expressed by the king, the princes of the blood, et cetera, is rather a proof of the affection felt for them by the nation, for the French carry that affection to such an extent that they believe them infallible.']
reference: IT SEEMS TO ME I REPLIED THAT SUCH APPROVAL SUCH RATIFICATION OF THE OPINION EXPRESSED BY THE KING THE PRINCES OF THE BLOOD ET CETERA IS RATHER A PROOF OF THE AFFECTION FELT FOR THEM BY THE NATION FOR THE FRENCH CARRY THAT AFFECTION TO SUCH AN EXTENT THAT THEY BELIEVE THEM INFALLIBLE

transcript: [' When the king comes to Paris, everybody calls out Vive le roi!']
reference: WHEN THE KING COMES TO PARIS EVERYBODY CALLS OUT VIVE LE ROI

transcript: [' She introduced me to all her guests and gave me some particulars respecting every one of them.']
reference: SHE INTRODUCED ME TO ALL HER GUESTS AND GAVE ME SOME PARTICULARS RESPECTING EVERY ONE OF THEM

transcript: [' What sir? I said to him, am I fortunate enough to see you?']
reference: WHAT SIR I SAID TO HIM AM I FORTUNATE ENOUGH TO SEE YOU

transcript: [' He himself recited the same passage in French and politely pointed out the parts in which he thought that I had improved on the original.']
reference: HE HIMSELF RECITED THE SAME PASSAGE IN FRENCH AND POLITELY POINTED OUT THE PARTS IN WHICH HE THOUGHT THAT I HAD IMPROVED ON THE ORIGINAL

transcript: [' For the first day, sir, I think that what you have done gives great hopes of you, and without any doubt you will make rapid progress.']
reference: FOR THE FIRST DAY SIR I THINK THAT WHAT YOU HAVE DONE GIVES GREAT HOPES OF YOU AND WITHOUT ANY DOUBT YOU WILL MAKE RAPID PROGRESS

transcript: [' I believe it sir, and that is what I fear. Therefore, the principal object of my visit here is to devote myself entirely to the study of the French language.']
reference: I BELIEVE IT SIR AND THAT IS WHAT I FEAR THEREFORE THE PRINCIPAL OBJECT OF MY VISIT HERE IS TO DEVOTE MYSELF ENTIRELY TO THE STUDY OF THE FRENCH LANGUAGE

transcript: [' I am a very unpleasant pupil always asking questions curious troublesome insatiable and even supposing that I could meet with the teacher I require I am afraid I am not rich enough to pay him']
reference: I AM A VERY UNPLEASANT PUPIL ALWAYS ASKING QUESTIONS CURIOUS TROUBLESOME INSATIABLE AND EVEN SUPPOSING THAT I COULD MEET WITH THE TEACHER I REQUIRE I AM AFRAID I AM NOT RICH ENOUGH TO PAY HIM

transcript: [' I reside in the Marais, rue de Duisport.']
reference: I RESIDE IN THE MARAIS RUE DE DOUZE PORTES

transcript: [' I will make you translate them into French and you need not be afraid of my finding you insatiable.']
reference: I WILL MAKE YOU TRANSLATE THEM INTO FRENCH AND YOU NEED NOT BE AFRAID OF MY FINDING YOU INSATIABLE

transcript: [' He had a good appetite could tell a good story without laughing was celebrated for his witty repartees and his sociable manners but he spent his life at home seldom going out and seeing hardly any one because he always had a pipe in his mouth and was surrounded by at least twenty cats with which he would amuse himself all day.']
reference: HE HAD A GOOD APPETITE COULD TELL A GOOD STORY WITHOUT LAUGHING WAS CELEBRATED FOR HIS WITTY REPARTEES AND HIS SOCIABLE MANNERS BUT HE SPENT HIS LIFE AT HOME SELDOM GOING OUT AND SEEING HARDLY ANYONE BECAUSE HE ALWAYS HAD A PIPE IN HIS MOUTH AND WAS SURROUNDED BY AT LEAST TWENTY CATS WITH WHICH HE WOULD AMUSE HIMSELF ALL DAY

transcript: [' His housekeeper had the management of everything she never allowed him to be in need of anything and she gave no account of his money which she kept altogether because he never asked her to render any accounts.']
reference: HIS HOUSEKEEPER HAD THE MANAGEMENT OF EVERYTHING SHE NEVER ALLOWED HIM TO BE IN NEED OF ANYTHING AND SHE GAVE NO ACCOUNT OF HIS MONEY WHICH SHE KEPT ALTOGETHER BECAUSE HE NEVER ASKED HER TO RENDER ANY ACCOUNTS

transcript: [' On the sixth of April, eighteen thirty, The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter day Saints was formally organized and thus took on a legal existence.']
reference: ON THE SIXTH OF APRIL EIGHTEEN THIRTY THE CHURCH OF JESUS CHRIST OF LATTER DAY SAINTS WAS FORMALLY ORGANIZED AND THUS TOOK ON A LEGAL EXISTENCE
