reference: RODOLFO ARRIVED AT HIS OWN HOUSE WITHOUT ANY IMPEDIMENT AND LEOCADIA'S PARENTS REACHED THEIRS HEART BROKEN AND DESPAIRING

transcript: [' Meanwhile, Rodolpho had Leocadia safe in his custody and in his own apartment.']
reference: MEANWHILE RODOLFO HAD LEOCADIA SAFE IN HIS CUSTODY AND IN HIS OWN APARTMENT

transcript: [' Who touches me? Am I in bed?']
reference: WHO TOUCHES ME AM I IN BED

transcript: [' Mother, dear father, do you hear me?']
reference: MOTHER DEAR FATHER DO YOU HEAR ME

transcript: [' It is the only amends I ask of you for the wrong you have done me.']
reference: IT IS THE ONLY AMENDS I ASK OF YOU FOR THE WRONG YOU HAVE DONE ME

transcript: [' She found the door, but it was locked outside.']
reference: SHE FOUND THE DOOR BUT IT WAS LOCKED OUTSIDE

transcript: [' She succeeded in opening the window, and the moonlight shone in so brightly that she could distinguish the color of some damask hanging in the room.']
reference: SHE SUCCEEDED IN OPENING THE WINDOW AND THE MOONLIGHT SHONE IN SO BRIGHTLY THAT SHE COULD DISTINGUISH THE COLOUR OF SOME DAMASK HANGINGS IN THE ROOM

transcript: [' She saw that the bed was gilded and so rich that it seemed that of a prince rather than of a private gentleman.']
reference: SHE SAW THAT THE BED WAS GILDED AND SO RICH THAT IT SEEMED THAT OF A PRINCE RATHER THAN OF A PRIVATE GENTLEMAN

transcript: [' Among other things on which she cast her eyes was a small crucifix of solid silver standing on a cabinet near the window.']
reference: AMONG OTHER THINGS ON WHICH SHE CAST HER EYES WAS A SMALL CRUCIFIX OF SOLID SILVER STANDING ON A CABINET NEAR THE WINDOW

transcript: [' This person was Rodolpho who though he had gone to look for his friends had changed his mind in that respect not thinking it advisable to acquaint them with what had passed between him and the girl.']
reference: THIS PERSON WAS RODOLFO WHO THOUGH HE HAD GONE TO LOOK FOR HIS FRIENDS HAD CHANGED HIS MIND IN THAT RESPECT NOT THINKING IT ADVISABLE TO ACQUAINT THEM WITH WHAT HAD PASSED BETWEEN HIM AND THE GIRL

transcript: [' On the contrary he resolved to tell them that repenting of his violence and moved by her tears he had only carried her half way towards his house and then let her go.']
reference: ON THE CONTRARY HE RESOLVED TO TELL THEM THAT REPENTING OF HIS VIOLENCE AND MOVED BY HER TEARS HE HAD ONLY CARRIED HER HALF WAY TOWARDS HIS HOUSE AND THEN LET HER GO

transcript: [' Choking with emotion, Leucadia made a sign to her parents that she wished to be alone with them.']
reference: CHOKING WITH EMOTION LEOCADI MADE A SIGN TO HER PARENTS THAT SHE WISHED TO BE ALONE WITH THEM

transcript: [' That would be very well my child replied her father if your plan were not liable to be frustrated by ordinary cunning but no doubt this image had been already missed by its owner and he will have set it down for certain that it was taken out of the room by the person he locked up there.']
reference: THAT WOULD BE VERY WELL MY CHILD REPLIED HER FATHER IF YOUR PLAN WERE NOT LIABLE TO BE FRUSTRATED BY ORDINARY CUNNING BUT NO DOUBT THIS IMAGE HAS BEEN ALREADY MISSED BY ITS OWNER AND HE WILL HAVE SET IT DOWN FOR CERTAIN THAT IT WAS TAKEN OUT OF THE ROOM BY THE PERSON HE LOCKED UP THERE

transcript: [' What you had best do, my child, is to keep it and pray to it that since it was a witness to your undoing, it will deign to vindicate your cause by its righteous judgment.']
reference: WHAT YOU HAD BEST DO MY CHILD IS TO KEEP IT AND PRAY TO IT THAT SINCE IT WAS A WITNESS TO YOUR UNDOING IT WILL DEIGN TO VINDICATE YOUR CAUSE BY ITS RIGHTEOUS JUDGMENT

transcript: [' Thus did the humane and right minded father comfort his unhappy daughter, and her mother embracing her again did all she could to soothe her feelings.']
reference: THUS DID THIS HUMANE AND RIGHT MINDED FATHER COMFORT HIS UNHAPPY DAUGHTER AND HER MOTHER EMBRACING HER AGAIN DID ALL SHE COULD TO SOOTHE HER FEELINGS

transcript: [' She meanwhile passed her life with her parents in the strictest retirement never letting herself be seen but shunning every eye lest it should read her misfortune in her face.']
reference: SHE MEANWHILE PASSED HER LIFE WITH HER PARENTS IN THE STRICTEST RETIREMENT NEVER LETTING HERSELF BE SEEN BUT SHUNNING EVERY EYE LEST IT SHOULD READ HER MISFORTUNE IN HER FACE

transcript: [' Time rolled on, the hour of her delivery arrived. It took place in the utmost secrecy, her mother taking upon her the office of midwife, and she gave birth to a son, one of the most beautiful ever seen.']
reference: TIME ROLLED ON THE HOUR OF HER DELIVERY ARRIVED IT TOOK PLACE IN THE UTMOST SECRECY HER MOTHER TAKING UPON HER THE OFFICE OF MIDWIFE AND SHE GAVE BIRTH TO A SON ONE OF THE MOST BEAUTIFUL EVER SEEN

transcript: [' When the boy walked through the streets blessings were showered upon him by all who saw him blessings upon his beauty upon the mother that bore him upon the father that begot him upon those who brought him up so well.']
reference: WHEN THE BOY WALKED THROUGH THE STREETS BLESSINGS WERE SHOWERED UPON HIM BY ALL WHO SAW HIM BLESSINGS UPON HIS BEAUTY UPON THE MOTHER THAT BORE HIM UPON THE FATHER THAT BEGOT HIM UPON THOSE WHO BROUGHT HIM UP SO WELL

transcript: [' One day when the boy was sent by his grandfather with a message to a relation, he passed along a street in which there was a great concourse of horsemen.']
reference: ONE DAY WHEN THE BOY WAS SENT BY HIS GRANDFATHER WITH A MESSAGE TO A RELATION HE PASSED ALONG A STREET IN WHICH THERE WAS A GREAT CONCOURSE OF HORSEMEN

transcript: [' The bed she too well remembered was there, and above all the cabinet on which had stood the image she had taken away was still on the same spot.']
reference: THE BED SHE TOO WELL REMEMBERED WAS THERE AND ABOVE ALL THE CABINET ON WHICH HAD STOOD THE IMAGE SHE HAD TAKEN AWAY WAS STILL ON THE SAME SPOT

transcript: [' Louis was out of danger in a fortnight in a month he rose from his bed and during all that time he was visited daily by his mother and grandmother and treated by the master and mistress of the house as if he was their own child.']
reference: LUIS WAS OUT OF DANGER IN A FORTNIGHT IN A MONTH HE ROSE FROM HIS BED AND DURING ALL THAT TIME HE WAS VISITED DAILY BY HIS MOTHER AND GRANDMOTHER AND TREATED BY THE MASTER AND MISTRESS OF THE HOUSE AS IF HE WAS THEIR OWN CHILD

transcript: [' Thus saying and pressing the crucifix to her breast, she fell fainting into the arms of Dona Estefania, who as a gentlewoman to whose sex pity is as natural as cruelty is to man, instantly pressed her lips to those of the fainting girl, shedding over her so many tears that there needed no other sprinkling of water to recover Leocadia from her swoon.']
reference: THUS SAYING AND PRESSING THE CRUCIFIX TO HER BREAST SHE FELL FAINTING INTO THE ARMS OF DONA ESTAFANIA WHO AS A GENTLEWOMAN TO WHOSE SEX PITY IS AS NATURAL AS CRUELTY IS TO MAN INSTANTLY PRESSED HER LIPS TO THOSE OF THE FAINTING GIRL SHEDDING OVER HER SO MANY TEARS THAT THERE NEEDED NO OTHER SPRINKLING OF WATER TO RECOVER LEOCADIA FROM HER SWOON

transcript: [' I have great things to tell you, senor, said dona Estefania to her husband, the cream and substance of which is this: the fainting girl before you is your daughter, and the boy is your grandson.']
reference: I HAVE GREAT THINGS TO TELL YOU SENOR SAID DONA ESTAFANIA TO HER HUSBAND THE CREAM AND SUBSTANCE OF WHICH IS THIS THE FAINTING GIRL BEFORE YOU IS YOUR DAUGHTER AND THAT BOY IS YOUR GRANDSON

transcript: [' This truth, which I have learned from her lips, is confirmed by his face, in which we have both beheld that of our son.']
reference: THIS TRUTH WHICH I HAVE LEARNED FROM HER LIPS IS CONFIRMED BY HIS FACE IN WHICH WE HAVE BOTH BEHELD THAT OF OUR SON

transcript: [" Just then Leocadia came to herself, and embracing the cross seemed changed into a sea of tears, and the gentleman remained in utter bewilderment until his wife had repeated to him from beginning to end Leocadia's whole story, and he believed it, through the blessed dispensation of heaven which had confirmed it by so many convincing testimonies."]
reference: JUST THEN LEOCADIA CAME TO HERSELF AND EMBRACING THE CROSS SEEMED CHANGED INTO A SEA OF TEARS AND THE GENTLEMAN REMAINED IN UTTER BEWILDERMENT UNTIL HIS WIFE HAD REPEATED TO HIM FROM BEGINNING TO END LEOCADIA'S WHOLE STORY AND HE BELIEVED IT THROUGH THE BLESSED DISPENSATION OF HEAVEN WHICH HAD CONFIRMED IT BY SO MANY CONVINCING TESTIMONIES

transcript: [" So persuasive were her entreaties and so strong her assurances that no harm whatever could result to them from the information she sought, they were induced to confess that one summer's night the same she had mentioned, themselves and another friend being out on a stroll with Rodolpho, they had been concerned in the abduction of a girl whom Rodolpho carried off, whilst the rest of them detained her family, who made a great outcry and would have defended her if they could."]
reference: SO PERSUASIVE WERE HER ENTREATIES AND SO STRONG HER ASSURANCES THAT NO HARM WHATEVER COULD RESULT TO THEM FROM THE INFORMATION SHE SOUGHT THEY WERE INDUCED TO CONFESS THAT ONE SUMMER'S NIGHT THE SAME SHE HAD MENTIONED THEMSELVES AND ANOTHER FRIEND BEING OUT ON A STROLL WITH RODOLFO THEY HAD BEEN CONCERNED IN THE ABDUCTION OF A GIRL WHOM RODOLFO CARRIED OFF WHILST THE REST OF THEM DETAINED HER FAMILY WHO MADE A GREAT OUTCRY AND WOULD HAVE DEFENDED HER IF THEY COULD

transcript: [" For God's sake, my lady mother, give me a wife who would be an agreeable companion, not one who will disgust me, so that we may both bear evenly and with mutual good will the yoke imposed on us by heaven instead of pulling this way and that way and fretting each other to death."]
reference: FOR GOD'S SAKE MY LADY MOTHER GIVE ME A WIFE WHO WOULD BE AN AGREEABLE COMPANION NOT ONE WHO WILL DISGUST ME SO THAT WE MAY BOTH BEAR EVENLY AND WITH MUTUAL GOOD WILL THE YOKE IMPOSED ON US BY HEAVEN INSTEAD OF PULLING THIS WAY AND THAT WAY AND FRETTING EACH OTHER TO DEATH

transcript: [' Her bearing was graceful and animated, she led her son by the hand, and before her walked two maids with wax lights and silver candlesticks.']
reference: HER BEARING WAS GRACEFUL AND ANIMATED SHE LED HER SON BY THE HAND AND BEFORE HER WALKED TWO MAIDS WITH WAX LIGHTS AND SILVER CANDLESTICKS

transcript: [' All rose to do her reverence as if something from heaven had miraculously appeared before them but gazing on her entranced with admiration not one of them was able to address a single word to her.']
reference: ALL ROSE TO DO HER REVERENCE AS IF SOMETHING FROM HEAVEN HAD MIRACULOUSLY APPEARED BEFORE THEM BUT GAZING ON HER ENTRANCED WITH ADMIRATION NOT ONE OF THEM WAS ABLE TO ADDRESS A SINGLE WORD TO HER

transcript: [' She reflected how near she stood to the crisis which was to determine whether she was to be blessed or unhappy for ever, and racked by the intensity of her emotions, she suddenly changed colour, her head dropped, and she fell forward in a swoon into the arms of the dismayed Estefania.']
reference: SHE REFLECTED HOW NEAR SHE STOOD TO THE CRISIS WHICH WAS TO DETERMINE WHETHER SHE WAS TO BE BLESSED OR UNHAPPY FOR EVER AND RACKED BY THE INTENSITY OF HER EMOTIONS SHE SUDDENLY CHANGED COLOUR HER HEAD DROPPED AND SHE FELL FORWARD IN A SWOON INTO THE ARMS OF THE DISMAYED ESTAFANIA

transcript: [' His mother had left her to him as being her destined protector, but when she saw that he too was insensible she was near making a third and would have done so had he not come to himself.']
reference: HIS MOTHER HAD LEFT HER TO HIM AS BEING HER DESTINED PROTECTOR BUT WHEN SHE SAW THAT HE TOO WAS INSENSIBLE SHE WAS NEAR MAKING A THIRD AND WOULD HAVE DONE SO HAD HE NOT COME TO HIMSELF

transcript: [' Know then son of my heart that this fainting lady is your real bride. I say real because she is the one whom your father and I have chosen for you, and the portrait was a pretence.']
reference: KNOW THEN SON OF MY HEART THAT THIS FAINTING LADY IS YOUR REAL BRIDE I SAY REAL BECAUSE SHE IS THE ONE WHOM YOUR FATHER AND I HAVE CHOSEN FOR YOU AND THE PORTRAIT WAS A PRETENCE

transcript: [' Just at the moment when the tears of the pitying beholders flowed fastest and their ejaculations were most expressive of despair Leucadia gave signs of recovery and brought back gladness to the hearts of all.']
reference: JUST AT THE MOMENT WHEN THE TEARS OF THE PITYING BEHOLDERS FLOWED FASTEST AND THEIR EJACULATIONS WERE MOST EXPRESSIVE OF DESPAIR LEOCADIA GAVE SIGNS OF RECOVERY AND BROUGHT BACK GLADNESS TO THE HEARTS OF ALL

transcript: [" When she came to her senses and blushing to find herself in Rodolpho's arms would have disengaged herself no signora he said that must not be strive not to withdraw from the arms of him who holds you in his soul"]
reference: WHEN SHE CAME TO HER SENSES AND BLUSHING TO FIND HERSELF IN RODOLFO'S ARMS WOULD HAVE DISENGAGED HERSELF NO SENORA HE SAID THAT MUST NOT BE STRIVE NOT TO WITHDRAW FROM THE ARMS OF HIM WHO HOLDS YOU IN HIS SOUL

transcript: [' This was done for the event took place at a time when the consent of the parties was sufficient for the celebration of a marriage without any of the preliminary formalities which are now so properly required.']
reference: THIS WAS DONE FOR THE EVENT TOOK PLACE AT A TIME WHEN THE CONSENT OF THE PARTIES WAS SUFFICIENT FOR THE CELEBRATION OF A MARRIAGE WITHOUT ANY OF THE PRELIMINARY FORMALITIES WHICH ARE NOW SO PROPERLY REQUIRED

transcript: [' Nor was Rodolfo less surprised than they, and the better to assure himself of so wonderful a fact he begged Leocadia to give him some token which should make perfectly clear to him that which indeed he did not doubt since it was authenticated by his parents.']
reference: NOR WAS RODOLFO LESS SURPRISED THAN THEY AND THE BETTER TO ASSURE HIMSELF OF SO WONDERFUL A FACT HE BEGGED LEOCADIA TO GIVE HIM SOME TOKEN WHICH SHOULD MAKE PERFECTLY CLEAR TO HIM THAT WHICH INDEED HE DID NOT DOUBT SINCE IT WAS AUTHENTICATED BY HIS PARENTS

transcript: [' You know captain lake?']
reference: YOU KNOW CAPTAIN LAKE

transcript: [' said Lord Chelford addressing me.']
reference: SAID LORD CHELFORD ADDRESSING ME

transcript: [" He had his hand upon Lake's shoulder."]
reference: HE HAD HIS HAND UPON LAKE'S SHOULDER

transcript: [' They are cousins, you know, we are all cousins.']
reference: THEY ARE COUSINS YOU KNOW WE ARE ALL COUSINS

transcript: [' Whatever Lord Chelford said, Miss Brandon received it very graciously and even with a momentary smile.']
reference: WHATEVER LORD CHELFORD SAID MISS BRANDON RECEIVED IT VERY GRACIOUSLY AND EVEN WITH A MOMENTARY SMILE

transcript: [' But her greeting to captain lake was more than usually haughty and frozen and her features I fancied particularly proud and pale.']
reference: BUT HER GREETING TO CAPTAIN LAKE WAS MORE THAN USUALLY HAUGHTY AND FROZEN AND HER FEATURES I FANCIED PARTICULARLY PROUD AND PALE

transcript: [' At dinner lake was easy and amusing.']
reference: AT DINNER LAKE WAS EASY AND AMUSING

transcript: [" I'm glad you like it says Wylder chuckling benignantly on it over his shoulder."]
reference: I'M GLAD YOU LIKE IT SAYS WYLDER CHUCKLING BENIGNANTLY ON IT OVER HIS SHOULDER

transcript: [' I believe I have a little taste that way. Those are all real, you know, those jewels.']
reference: I BELIEVE I HAVE A LITTLE TASTE THAT WAY THOSE ARE ALL REAL YOU KNOW THOSE JEWELS

transcript: [" And he placed it in that gentleman's fingers who now took his turn at the lamp and contemplated the little parallelogram with a gleam of sly amusement."]
reference: AND HE PLACED IT IN THAT GENTLEMAN'S FINGERS WHO NOW TOOK HIS TURN AT THE LAMP AND CONTEMPLATED THE LITTLE PARALLELOGRAM WITH A GLEAM OF SLY AMUSEMENT

transcript: [" I was thinking it's very like the Ace of Hearts answered the captain softly smiling on."]
reference: I WAS THINKING IT'S VERY LIKE THE ACE OF HEARTS ANSWERED THE CAPTAIN SOFTLY SMILING ON

transcript: [' Whereupon lake laughed quietly still looking on the ace of hearts with his sly eyes.']
reference: WHEREUPON LAKE LAUGHED QUIETLY STILL LOOKING ON THE ACE OF HEARTS WITH HIS SLY EYES

transcript: [' And Wilder laughed too more suddenly and noisily than the humor of the joke seemed quite to call for and glanced a grim look from the corners of his eyes on Lake but the gallant captain did not seem to perceive it and after a few seconds more he handed it very innocently back to missus Dorothy only remarking']
reference: AND WYLDER LAUGHED TOO MORE SUDDENLY AND NOISILY THAN THE HUMOUR OF THE JOKE SEEMED QUITE TO CALL FOR AND GLANCED A GRIM LOOK FROM THE CORNERS OF HIS EYES ON LAKE BUT THE GALLANT CAPTAIN DID NOT SEEM TO PERCEIVE IT AND AFTER A FEW SECONDS MORE HE HANDED IT VERY INNOCENTLY BACK TO MISSUS DOROTHY ONLY REMARKING

transcript: [" Do you know? Lake? Oh, I really can't tell, but he'll soon tire of country life."]
reference: DO YOU KNOW LAKE OH I REALLY CAN'T TELL BUT HE'LL SOON TIRE OF COUNTRY LIFE

transcript: [" He's not a man for country quarters."]
reference: HE'S NOT A MAN FOR COUNTRY QUARTERS

transcript: [' I had a horrid dream about him last night. That?']
reference: I HAD A HORRID DREAM ABOUT HIM LAST NIGHT THAT

transcript: [" Oh, I know, that's lorn brandon."]
reference: OH I KNOW THAT'S LORNE BRANDON

transcript: [' All the time he was talking to me, his angry little eyes were following Lake.']
reference: ALL THE TIME HE WAS TALKING TO ME HIS ANGRY LITTLE EYES WERE FOLLOWING LAKE

transcript: [' Miss Lake declined the carriage to night.']
reference: MISS LAKE DECLINED THE CARRIAGE TO NIGHT

transcript: [' And he added something still less complimentary.']
reference: AND HE ADDED SOMETHING STILL LESS COMPLIMENTARY

transcript: [" But don't these very wise things sometimes turn out very foolishly?"]
reference: BUT DON'T THESE VERY WISE THINGS SOMETIMES TURN OUT VERY FOOLISHLY

transcript: [' In the meantime I had formed a new idea of her.']
reference: IN THE MEANTIME I HAD FORMED A NEW IDEA OF HER

transcript: [' By this time Lord Chelford and Wylder returned, and disgusted rather with myself, I ruminated on my want of generalship.']
reference: BY THIS TIME LORD CHELFORD AND WYLDER RETURNED AND DISGUSTED RATHER WITH MYSELF I RUMINATED ON MY WANT OF GENERAL SHIP

transcript: [' And he made a little dip of his cane towards Brandon Hall over his shoulder.']
reference: AND HE MADE A LITTLE DIP OF HIS CANE TOWARDS BRANDON HALL OVER HIS SHOULDER

transcript: [' Yes, so they said, but that would, I think, have been worse.']
reference: YES SO THEY SAID BUT THAT WOULD I THINK HAVE BEEN WORSE

transcript: [" If a fellow's been a little bit wild, he is Beelzebub at once."]
reference: IF A FELLOW'S BEEN A LITTLE BIT WILD HE'S BEELZEBUB AT ONCE

transcript: [" Bracton's a very good fellow, I can assure you."]
reference: BRACTON'S A VERY GOOD FELLOW I CAN ASSURE YOU

transcript: [" I don't know and can't say how you fine gentlemen define wickedness only as an obscure female I speak according to my lights and he is generally thought the wickedest man in this county"]
reference: I DON'T KNOW AND CAN'T SAY HOW YOU FINE GENTLEMEN DEFINE WICKEDNESS ONLY AS AN OBSCURE FEMALE I SPEAK ACCORDING TO MY LIGHTS AND HE IS GENERALLY THOUGHT THE WICKEDEST MAN IN THIS COUNTY

transcript: [" Well, you know, Radie, women like wicked fellows. It is contrast, I suppose, but they do, and I'm sure from what Bracton has said to me, I know him intimately, that Dorcas likes him, and I can't conceive why they are not married."]
reference: WELL YOU KNOW RADIE WOMEN LIKE WICKED FELLOWS IT IS CONTRAST I SUPPOSE BUT THEY DO AND I'M SURE FROM WHAT BRACTON HAS SAID TO ME I KNOW HIM INTIMATELY THAT DORCAS LIKES HIM AND I CAN'T CONCEIVE WHY THEY ARE NOT MARRIED

transcript: [' Their walk continued silent for the greater part neither was quite satisfied with the other but rachel at last said']
reference: THEIR WALK CONTINUED SILENT FOR THE GREATER PART NEITHER WAS QUITE SATISFIED WITH THE OTHER BUT RACHEL AT LAST SAID

transcript: [" Now that's impossible, Radie, for I really don't think I once thought of him all this evening, except just while we were talking."]
reference: NOW THAT'S IMPOSSIBLE RADIE FOR I REALLY DON'T THINK I ONCE THOUGHT OF HIM ALL THIS EVENING EXCEPT JUST WHILE WE WERE TALKING

transcript: [' There was a bright moonlight broken by the shadows of overhanging boughs and withered leaves and the mottled lights and shadows glided oddly across his pale features.']
reference: THERE WAS A BRIGHT MOONLIGHT BROKEN BY THE SHADOWS OF OVERHANGING BOUGHS AND WITHERED LEAVES AND THE MOTTLED LIGHTS AND SHADOWS GLIDED ODDLY ACROSS HIS PALE FEATURES

transcript: [" Don't insult me, Stanley, by talking again as you did this morning."]
reference: DON'T INSULT ME STANLEY BY TALKING AGAIN AS YOU DID THIS MORNING

transcript: [' What I say is altogether on your own account.']
reference: WHAT I SAY IS ALTOGETHER ON YOUR OWN ACCOUNT

transcript: [" Mark my words, you'll find him too strong for you, aye, and too deep."]
reference: MARK MY WORDS YOU'LL FIND HIM TOO STRONG FOR YOU AYE AND TOO DEEP

transcript: [" I am very uneasy about it whatever it is. I can't help it."]
reference: I AM VERY UNEASY ABOUT IT WHATEVER IT IS I CAN'T HELP IT

transcript: [' To my mind there has always been something inexpressibly awful in family feuds.']
reference: TO MY MIND THERE HAS ALWAYS BEEN SOMETHING INEXPRESSIBLY AWFUL IN FAMILY FEUDS

transcript: [' The mystery of their origin, their capacity for evolving latent faculties of crime, and the steady vitality with which they survive the hearse and speak their deep mouthed malignities in every new born generation have associated them somehow in my mind with a spell of life exceeding and distinct from human and especially satanic action.']
reference: THE MYSTERY OF THEIR ORIGIN THEIR CAPACITY FOR EVOLVING LATENT FACULTIES OF CRIME AND THE STEADY VITALITY WITH WHICH THEY SURVIVE THE HEARSE AND SPEAK THEIR DEEP MOUTHED MALIGNITIES IN EVERY NEW BORN GENERATION HAVE ASSOCIATED THEM SOMEHOW IN MY MIND WITH A SPELL OF LIFE EXCEEDING AND DISTINCT FROM HUMAN AND A SPECIAL SATANIC ACTION

transcript: [' The floor more than anything else showed the great age of the room.']
reference: THE FLOOR MORE THAN ANYTHING ELSE SHOWED THE GREAT AGE OF THE ROOM

transcript: [' My bed was unexceptionably comfortable, but in my then mood I could have wished it a great deal more modern.']
reference: MY BED WAS UNEXCEPTIONABLY COMFORTABLE BUT IN MY THEN MOOD I COULD HAVE WISHED IT A GREAT DEAL MORE MODERN

transcript: [' Its curtains were of thick and faded tapestry.']
reference: ITS CURTAINS WERE OF THICK AND FADED TAPESTRY

transcript: [' All the furniture belonged to other times.']
reference: ALL THE FURNITURE BELONGED TO OTHER TIMES

transcript: [" I shan't trouble you about my train of thoughts or fancies, but I began to feel very like a gentleman in a ghost story watching experimentally in a haunted chamber."]
reference: I SHAN'T TROUBLE YOU ABOUT MY TRAIN OF THOUGHTS OR FANCIES BUT I BEGAN TO FEEL VERY LIKE A GENTLEMAN IN A GHOST STORY WATCHING EXPERIMENTALLY IN A HAUNTED CHAMBER

transcript: [' I did not even take the precaution of smoking up the chimney.']
reference: I DID NOT EVEN TAKE THE PRECAUTION OF SMOKING UP THE CHIMNEY

transcript: [' I boldly lighted my cheroot.']
reference: I BOLDLY LIGHTED MY CHEROOT

transcript: [' A cold bright moon was shining with clear sharp lights and shadows.']
reference: A COLD BRIGHT MOON WAS SHINING WITH CLEAR SHARP LIGHTS AND SHADOWS

transcript: [' The sombre old trees, like gigantic hearse plumes, black and awful.']
reference: THE SOMBRE OLD TREES LIKE GIGANTIC HEARSE PLUMES BLACK AND AWFUL

transcript: [' Somehow I had grown nervous.']
reference: SOMEHOW I HAD GROWN NERVOUS

transcript: [' A little bit of plaster tumbled down the chimney and startled me confoundedly.']
reference: A LITTLE BIT OF PLASTER TUMBLED DOWN THE CHIMNEY AND STARTLED ME CONFOUNDEDLY

transcript: [" It was not very much past eleven that morning when the pony carriage from Brandon drew up before the little garden wicket of Redman's farm."]
reference: IT WAS NOT VERY MUCH PAST ELEVEN THAT MORNING WHEN THE PONY CARRIAGE FROM BRANDON DREW UP BEFORE THE LITTLE GARDEN WICKET OF REDMAN'S FARM

transcript: [' Well, she was better, though she had had a bad night.']
reference: WELL SHE WAS BETTER THOUGH SHE HAD HAD A BAD NIGHT

transcript: [' So there came a step and a little rustling of feminine draperies the small door opened and rachel entered with her hand extended and a pale smile of welcome']
reference: SO THERE CAME A STEP AND A LITTLE RUSTLING OF FEMININE DRAPERIES THE SMALL DOOR OPENED AND RACHEL ENTERED WITH HER HAND EXTENDED AND A PALE SMILE OF WELCOME

transcript: [' Women can hide their pain better than we men and bear it better too, except when shame drops fire into the dreadful chalice.']
reference: WOMEN CAN HIDE THEIR PAIN BETTER THAN WE MEN AND BEAR IT BETTER TOO EXCEPT WHEN SHAME DROPS FIRE INTO THE DREADFUL CHALICE

transcript: [' But poor Rachel Lake had more than that stoical hypocrisy which enables the tortured spirits of her sex to lift a pale face through the flames and smile.']
reference: BUT POOR RACHEL LAKE HAD MORE THAN THAT STOICAL HYPOCRISY WHICH ENABLES THE TORTURED SPIRITS OF HER SEX TO LIFT A PALE FACE THROUGH THE FLAMES AND SMILE

transcript: [' This transient spring and lighting up are beautiful a glamour beguiling our senses.']
reference: THIS TRANSIENT SPRING AND LIGHTING UP ARE BEAUTIFUL A GLAMOUR BEGUILING OUR SENSES

transcript: [' There was something of sweetness and fondness in her tones and manner which was new to rachel and comforting and she returned the greeting as kindly and felt more like her former self.']
reference: THERE WAS SOMETHING OF SWEETNESS AND FONDNESS IN HER TONES AND MANNER WHICH WAS NEW TO RACHEL AND COMFORTING AND SHE RETURNED THE GREETING AS KINDLY AND FELT MORE LIKE HER FORMER SELF

transcript: [" Rachel's pale and sharpened features and dilated eye struck her with a painful surprise."]
reference: RACHEL'S PALE AND SHARPENED FEATURES AND DILATED EYE STRUCK HER WITH A PAINFUL SURPRISE

transcript: [' You have been so ill, my poor rachel.']
reference: YOU HAVE BEEN SO ILL MY POOR RACHEL

transcript: [' Ill and troubled, dear, troubled in mind and miserably nervous.']
reference: ILL AND TROUBLED DEAR TROUBLED IN MIND AND MISERABLY NERVOUS

transcript: [' Poor Rachel! her nature recoiled from deceit and she told at all events as much of the truth as she dared.']
reference: POOR RACHEL HER NATURE RECOILED FROM DECEIT AND SHE TOLD AT ALL EVENTS AS MUCH OF THE TRUTH AS SHE DARED

transcript: [' She spoke with a sudden energy which partook of fear and passion and flushed her thin cheek and made her languid eyes flash.']
reference: SHE SPOKE WITH A SUDDEN ENERGY WHICH PARTOOK OF FEAR AND PASSION AND FLUSHED HER THIN CHEEK AND MADE HER LANGUID EYES FLASH

transcript: [' thank you rachel my cousin rachel my only friend']
reference: THANK YOU RACHEL MY COUSIN RACHEL MY ONLY FRIEND

transcript: [' Chelford had a note from mister Wylder this morning, another note, his coming delayed, and something of his having to see some person who is abroad, continued Dorcas after a little pause.']
reference: CHELFORD HAD A NOTE FROM MISTER WYLDER THIS MORNING ANOTHER NOTE HIS COMING DELAYED AND SOMETHING OF HIS HAVING TO SEE SOME PERSON WHO IS ABROAD CONTINUED DORCAS AFTER A LITTLE PAUSE

transcript: [' Yes, something, everything, said Rachel hurriedly looking frowningly at a flower which she was twirling in her fingers.']
reference: YES SOMETHING EVERYTHING SAID RACHEL HURRIEDLY LOOKING FROWNINGLY AT A FLOWER WHICH SHE WAS TWIRLING IN HER FINGERS

transcript: [' Yes, said Rachel.']
reference: YES SAID RACHEL

transcript: [" And the wan oracle having spoken, she sat down in the same sort of abstraction again beside dorcas, and she looked full in her cousin's eyes."]
reference: AND THE WAN ORACLE HAVING SPOKEN SHE SATE DOWN IN THE SAME SORT OF ABSTRACTION AGAIN BESIDE DORCAS AND SHE LOOKED FULL IN HER COUSIN'S EYES

transcript: [' Of Mark Wylder I say this his name has been for years hateful to me and recently it has become frightful and you will promise me simply this that you will never ask me to speak again about him']
reference: OF MARK WYLDER I SAY THIS HIS NAME HAS BEEN FOR YEARS HATEFUL TO ME AND RECENTLY IT HAS BECOME FRIGHTFUL AND YOU WILL PROMISE ME SIMPLY THIS THAT YOU WILL NEVER ASK ME TO SPEAK AGAIN ABOUT HIM

transcript: [" It is an antipathy, an antipathy I cannot get over dear Dorcas. You may think it a madness, but don't blame me."]
reference: IT IS AN ANTIPATHY AN ANTIPATHY I CANNOT GET OVER DEAR DORCAS YOU MAY THINK IT A MADNESS BUT DON'T BLAME ME

transcript: [' I have very few to love me now, and I thought you might love me as I have begun to love you.']
reference: I HAVE VERY FEW TO LOVE ME NOW AND I THOUGHT YOU MIGHT LOVE ME AS I HAVE BEGUN TO LOVE YOU

transcript: [" And she threw her arms round her cousin's neck and brave rachel at last burst into tears."]
reference: AND SHE THREW HER ARMS ROUND HER COUSIN'S NECK AND BRAVE RACHEL AT LAST BURST INTO TEARS

transcript: [' Dorcas in her strange way was moved.']
reference: DORCAS IN HER STRANGE WAY WAS MOVED

transcript: [" I like you still rachel. I'm sure I'll always like you."]
reference: I LIKE YOU STILL RACHEL I'M SURE I'LL ALWAYS LIKE YOU

transcript: [' You resemble me, Rachel. You are fearless and inflexible and generous.']
reference: YOU RESEMBLE ME RACHEL YOU ARE FEARLESS AND INFLEXIBLE AND GENEROUS

transcript: [' Yes, Rachel, I do love you.']
reference: YES RACHEL I DO LOVE YOU

transcript: [' Thank you, Dorcas dear.']
reference: THANK YOU DORCAS DEAR

transcript: [' He began a confused complaint against the wizard who had vanished behind the curtain on the left.']
reference: HE BEGAN A CONFUSED COMPLAINT AGAINST THE WIZARD WHO HAD VANISHED BEHIND THE CURTAIN ON THE LEFT

transcript: [' Give not so earnest a mind to these mummeries child.']
reference: GIVE NOT SO EARNEST A MIND TO THESE MUMMERIES CHILD

transcript: [' a golden fortune and a happy life?']
reference: A GOLDEN FORTUNE AND A HAPPY LIFE

transcript: [' He was like unto my father in a way, and yet was not my father.']
reference: HE WAS LIKE UNTO MY FATHER IN A WAY AND YET WAS NOT MY FATHER

transcript: [' Also there was a stripling page who turned into a maid.']
reference: ALSO THERE WAS A STRIPLING PAGE WHO TURNED INTO A MAID

transcript: [' This was so sweet a lady sir, and in some manner I do think she died.']
reference: THIS WAS SO SWEET A LADY SIR AND IN SOME MANNER I DO THINK SHE DIED

transcript: [' But then the picture was gone as quickly as it came.']
reference: BUT THEN THE PICTURE WAS GONE AS QUICKLY AS IT CAME

transcript: [' Sister Nell, do you hear these marvels?']
reference: SISTER NELL DO YOU HEAR THESE MARVELS

transcript: [' Take your place and let us see what the crystal can show to you.']
reference: TAKE YOUR PLACE AND LET US SEE WHAT THE CRYSTAL CAN SHOW TO YOU

transcript: [' Like as not young master though I am an old man']
reference: LIKE AS NOT YOUNG MASTER THOUGH I AM AN OLD MAN

transcript: [' forthwith all ran to the opening of the tent to see what might be amiss but Master Will who peeped out first needed no more than one glance.']
reference: FORTHWITH ALL RAN TO THE OPENING OF THE TENT TO SEE WHAT MIGHT BE AMISS BUT MASTER WILL WHO PEEPED OUT FIRST NEEDED NO MORE THAN ONE GLANCE

transcript: [' He gave way to the others very readily and retreated unperceived by the squire and mistress Fitzooth to the rear of the tent.']
reference: HE GAVE WAY TO THE OTHERS VERY READILY AND RETREATED UNPERCEIVED BY THE SQUIRE AND MISTRESS FITZOOTH TO THE REAR OF THE TENT

transcript: [' Cries of a nottingham a nottingham!']
reference: CRIES OF A NOTTINGHAM A NOTTINGHAM

transcript: [' Before them fled the stroller and his three sons, capless and terrified.']
reference: BEFORE THEM FLED THE STROLLER AND HIS THREE SONS CAPLESS AND TERRIFIED

transcript: [' What is the tumult and rioting? cried out the squire authoritatively, and he blew twice on the silver whistle which hung at his belt.']
reference: WHAT IS THE TUMULT AND RIOTING CRIED OUT THE SQUIRE AUTHORITATIVELY AND HE BLEW TWICE ON A SILVER WHISTLE WHICH HUNG AT HIS BELT

transcript: [' "Nay, we refuse their request most politely, most nobly," said the little stroller.']
reference: NAY WE REFUSED THEIR REQUEST MOST POLITELY MOST NOBLE SAID THE LITTLE STROLLER

transcript: [' and then they became vexed and would have snatched your purse from us.']
reference: AND THEN THEY BECAME VEXED AND WOULD HAVE SNATCHED YOUR PURSE FROM US

transcript: [" I could not see my boy injured excellence for but doing his duty as one of cumberland's sons"]
reference: I COULD NOT SEE MY BOY INJURED EXCELLENCE FOR BUT DOING HIS DUTY AS ONE OF CUMBERLAND'S SONS

transcript: [' So I did push this fellow.']
reference: SO I DID PUSH THIS FELLOW

transcript: [' It is enough said George Gamewell sharply as he turned upon the crowd.']
reference: IT IS ENOUGH SAID GEORGE GAMEWELL SHARPLY AND HE TURNED UPON THE CROWD

transcript: [' Shame on you citizens cried he, I blush for my fellows of Nottingham.']
reference: SHAME ON YOU CITIZENS CRIED HE I BLUSH FOR MY FELLOWS OF NOTTINGHAM

transcript: [' surely we can submit with good grace']
reference: SURELY WE CAN SUBMIT WITH GOOD GRACE

transcript: [" 'tis fine for you to talk old man answered the lean sullen apprentice."]
reference: TIS FINE FOR YOU TO TALK OLD MAN ANSWERED THE LEAN SULLEN APPRENTICE

transcript: [' But I wrestled with this fellow and do know that he played unfairly in the second bout.']
reference: BUT I WRESTLED WITH THIS FELLOW AND DO KNOW THAT HE PLAYED UNFAIRLY IN THE SECOND BOUT

transcript: [' spoke the squire losing all patience, and it was to you that I gave another purse in consolation.']
reference: SPOKE THE SQUIRE LOSING ALL PATIENCE AND IT WAS TO YOU THAT I GAVE ANOTHER PURSE IN CONSOLATION

transcript: [' Come to me men! Here! Here! He raised his voice still louder.']
reference: COME TO ME MEN HERE HERE HE RAISED HIS VOICE STILL LOUDER

transcript: [' The strollers took their part in it with hearty zest now that they had some chance of beating off their foes.']
reference: THE STROLLERS TOOK THEIR PART IN IT WITH HEARTY ZEST NOW THAT THEY HAD SOME CHANCE OF BEATING OFF THEIR FOES

transcript: [' Robin and the little tumbler between them tried to force the squire to stand back and very valiantly did these two comport themselves.']
reference: ROBIN AND THE LITTLE TUMBLER BETWEEN THEM TRIED TO FORCE THE SQUIRE TO STAND BACK AND VERY VALIANTLY DID THESE TWO COMPORT THEMSELVES

transcript: [' The head in chief of the riot, the Nottingham apprentice, with clenched fists threatened Montfichet.']
reference: THE HEAD AND CHIEF OF THE RIOT THE NOTTINGHAM APPRENTICE WITH CLENCHED FISTS THREATENED MONTFICHET

transcript: [' The squire helped to thrust them all in and entered swiftly himself.']
reference: THE SQUIRE HELPED TO THRUST THEM ALL IN AND ENTERED SWIFTLY HIMSELF

transcript: [' Now be silent on your lives he began, but the captured apprentice set up an instant shout.']
reference: NOW BE SILENT ON YOUR LIVES HE BEGAN BUT THE CAPTURED APPRENTICE SET UP AN INSTANT SHOUT

transcript: [' Silence you knave cried Montfichet.']
reference: SILENCE YOU KNAVE CRIED MONTFICHET

transcript: [" He felt for and found the wizard's black cloth. The squire was quite out of breath."]
reference: HE FELT FOR AND FOUND THE WIZARD'S BLACK CLOTH THE SQUIRE WAS QUITE OUT OF BREATH

transcript: [' Thrusting open the proper entrance of the tent Robin suddenly rushed forth with his burden with a great shout.']
reference: THRUSTING OPEN THE PROPER ENTRANCE OF THE TENT ROBIN SUDDENLY RUSHED FORTH WITH HIS BURDEN WITH A GREAT SHOUT

transcript: [' Amont fichet Amont fichet gamewell to the rescue!']
reference: A MONTFICHET A MONTFICHET GAMEWELL TO THE RESCUE

transcript: [" Taking advantage of this the squire's few men redoubled their efforts and encouraged by robin's and the little stroller's cries fought their way to him."]
reference: TAKING ADVANTAGE OF THIS THE SQUIRE'S FEW MEN REDOUBLED THEIR EFFORTS AND ENCOURAGED BY ROBIN'S AND THE LITTLE STROLLER'S CRIES FOUGHT THEIR WAY TO HIM

transcript: [' George Montfichet will never forget this day.']
reference: GEORGE MONTFICHET WILL NEVER FORGET THIS DAY

transcript: [' What is your name, lording? asked the little stroller presently.']
reference: WHAT IS YOUR NAME LORDING ASKED THE LITTLE STROLLER PRESENTLY

transcript: [' Robin Fitzooth']
reference: ROBIN FITZOOTH

transcript: [' And mine is Will Stuteley. Shall we be comrades?']
reference: AND MINE IS WILL STUTELEY SHALL WE BE COMRADES

transcript: [' Right willingly for between us we have won the battle answered Robin.']
reference: RIGHT WILLINGLY FOR BETWEEN US WE HAVE WON THE BATTLE ANSWERED ROBIN

transcript: [' I like you will. You are the second will that I have met and liked within two days. Is there a sign in that?']
reference: I LIKE YOU WILL YOU ARE THE SECOND WILL THAT I HAVE MET AND LIKED WITHIN TWO DAYS IS THERE A SIGN IN THAT

transcript: [' Montfichet called out for Robin to give him an arm.']
reference: MONTFICHET CALLED OUT FOR ROBIN TO GIVE HIM AN ARM

transcript: [" Friends said Montfichet faintly to the wrestlers bear us escort so far as the sheriff's house."]
reference: FRIENDS SAID MONTFICHET FAINTLY TO THE WRESTLERS BEAR US ESCORT SO FAR AS THE SHERIFF'S HOUSE

transcript: [' It will not be safe for you to stay here now.']
reference: IT WILL NOT BE SAFE FOR YOU TO STAY HERE NOW

transcript: [" pray follow us with mine and my lord sheriff's men"]
reference: PRAY FOLLOW US WITH MINE AND MY LORD SHERIFF'S MEN

transcript: [' Nottingham Castle was reached and admittance was demanded.']
reference: NOTTINGHAM CASTLE WAS REACHED AND ADMITTANCE WAS DEMANDED

transcript: [' Master Monceux the sheriff of Nottingham was mightily put about when told of the rioting.']
reference: MASTER MONCEUX THE SHERIFF OF NOTTINGHAM WAS MIGHTILY PUT ABOUT WHEN TOLD OF THE RIOTING

transcript: [' and Henry might return to England at any moment.']
reference: AND HENRY MIGHT RETURN TO ENGLAND AT ANY MOMENT

transcript: [' Have your will child if the boy also wills it Montfichet answered feeling too ill to oppose anything very strongly just then']
reference: HAVE YOUR WILL CHILD IF THE BOY ALSO WILLS IT MONTFICHET ANSWERED FEELING TOO ILL TO OPPOSE ANYTHING VERY STRONGLY JUST THEN

transcript: [' He made an effort to hide his condition from them all and Robin felt his fingers tighten upon his arm.']
reference: HE MADE AN EFFORT TO HIDE HIS CONDITION FROM THEM ALL AND ROBIN FELT HIS FINGERS TIGHTEN UPON HIS ARM

transcript: [" beg me a room of the sheriff's child quickly"]
reference: BEG ME A ROOM OF THE SHERIFF CHILD QUICKLY

transcript: [' But who is this fellow plucking at your sleeve?']
reference: BUT WHO IS THIS FELLOW PLUCKING AT YOUR SLEEVE

transcript: [' He is my esquire, Your Excellency, returned Robin with dignity.']
reference: HE IS MY ESQUIRE EXCELLENCY RETURNED ROBIN WITH DIGNITY

transcript: [" Mistress Fitzooth had been carried off by the sheriff's daughter and her maids as soon as they had entered the house so that Robin alone had the care of Montfichet."]
reference: MISTRESS FITZOOTH HAD BEEN CARRIED OFF BY THE SHERIFF'S DAUGHTER AND HER MAIDS AS SOON AS THEY HAD ENTERED THE HOUSE SO THAT ROBIN ALONE HAD THE CARE OF MONTFICHET

transcript: [' Robin was glad when at length they were left to their own devices.']
reference: ROBIN WAS GLAD WHEN AT LENGTH THEY WERE LEFT TO THEIR OWN DEVICES

transcript: [" The wine did certainly bring back the color to the squire's cheeks."]
reference: THE WINE DID CERTAINLY BRING BACK THE COLOR TO THE SQUIRE'S CHEEKS

transcript: [' These escapades are not for old Gamewell lad, his day has come to twilight.']
reference: THESE ESCAPADES ARE NOT FOR OLD GAMEWELL LAD HIS DAY HAS COME TO TWILIGHT

transcript: [' Will you forgive me now?']
reference: WILL YOU FORGIVE ME NOW

transcript: [" It'll be no disappointment to me."]
reference: IT WILL BE NO DISAPPOINTMENT TO ME

transcript: [' No thanks, I am glad to give you such easy happiness.']
reference: NO THANKS I AM GLAD TO GIVE YOU SUCH EASY HAPPINESS

transcript: [' You are a worthy leech will presently whispered robin the wine has worked a marvel.']
reference: YOU ARE A WORTHY LEECH WILL PRESENTLY WHISPERED ROBIN THE WINE HAS WORKED A MARVEL

transcript: [' Aye, and show you some pretty tricks.']
reference: AY AND SHOW YOU SOME PRETTY TRICKS

transcript: [" Young Fitzooth had been commanded to his mother's chamber so soon as he had come out from his converse with the squire."]
reference: YOUNG FITZOOTH HAD BEEN COMMANDED TO HIS MOTHER'S CHAMBER SO SOON AS HE HAD COME OUT FROM HIS CONVERSE WITH THE SQUIRE

transcript: [" There befell an anxious interview, Mistress Fitzooth arguing for and against the squire's project in a breath."]
reference: THERE BEFELL AN ANXIOUS INTERVIEW MISTRESS FITZOOTH ARGUING FOR AND AGAINST THE SQUIRE'S PROJECT IN A BREATH

transcript: [' Most of all Robin thought of his father what would he counsel']
reference: MOST OF ALL ROBIN THOUGHT OF HIS FATHER WHAT WOULD HE COUNSEL

transcript: [' If for a whim you beggar yourself, I cannot stay you.']
reference: IF FOR A WHIM YOU BEGGAR YOURSELF I CANNOT STAY YOU

transcript: [" But take it whilst I live and wear Montfichet's shield in the days when my eyes can be rejoiced by so brave a sight for you will ne'er disgrace our duchin I warrant me."]
reference: BUT TAKE IT WHILST I LIVE AND WEAR MONTFICHET'S SHIELD IN THE DAYS WHEN MY EYES CAN BE REJOICED BY SO BRAVE A SIGHT FOR YOU WILL NE'ER DISGRACE OUR SCUTCHEON I WARRANT ME

transcript: [' The lad had checked him then.']
reference: THE LAD HAD CHECKED HIM THEN

transcript: [' Never that, sir, he had said.']
reference: NEVER THAT SIR HE HAD SAID

transcript: [' He was in deep converse with the clerk and entered the hall holding him by the arm.']
reference: HE WAS IN DEEP CONVERSE WITH THE CLERK AND ENTERED THE HALL HOLDING HIM BY THE ARM

transcript: [' Now to bed, boy.']
reference: NOW TO BED BOY

transcript: [' tis late, and I go myself within a short space.']
reference: TIS LATE AND I GO MYSELF WITHIN A SHORT SPACE

transcript: [" Dismiss your squire robin and bid me good e'en."]
reference: DISMISS YOUR SQUIRE ROBIN AND BID ME GOOD E E N

transcript: [' As any in England, I would say, said Gamewell proudly, that is, in his day.']
reference: AS ANY IN ENGLAND I WOULD SAY SAID GAMEWELL PROUDLY THAT IS IN HIS DAY

transcript: [' Yet he will teach you a few tricks when morning is come.']
reference: YET HE WILL TEACH YOU A FEW TRICKS WHEN MORNING IS COME

transcript: [" There was no chance to alter his sleeping room to one nearer to Gamewell's chamber."]
reference: THERE WAS NO CHANCE TO ALTER HIS SLEEPING ROOM TO ONE NEARER TO GAMEWELL'S CHAMBER

transcript: [' Presently he crossed the floor of his room with decided step.']
reference: PRESENTLY HE CROSSED THE FLOOR OF HIS ROOM WITH DECIDED STEP

transcript: [" Will! cried he softly, and Stuteley, who had chosen his couch across the door of his young master's chamber, sprang up at once in answer."]
reference: WILL CRIED HE SOFTLY AND STUTELEY WHO HAD CHOSEN HIS COUCH ACROSS THE DOOR OF HIS YOUNG MASTER'S CHAMBER SPRANG UP AT ONCE IN ANSWER

transcript: [' We will go out together to the Bower. There is a way down to the court from my window.']
reference: WE WILL GO OUT TOGETHER TO THE BOWER THERE IS A WAY DOWN TO THE COURT FROM MY WINDOW

transcript: [' Rest and be still until I warn you.']
reference: REST AND BE STILL UNTIL I WARN YOU

transcript: [' The hours passed wearily by and movement could yet be heard about the hall.']
reference: THE HOURS PASSED WEARILY BY AND MOVEMENT COULD YET BE HEARD ABOUT THE HALL

transcript: [' At last all was quiet and black in the courtyard of Gamewell.']
reference: AT LAST ALL WAS QUIET AND BLACK IN THE COURTYARD OF GAMEWELL

transcript: [' Will whispered robin opening his door as he spoke are you ready?']
reference: WILL WHISPERED ROBIN OPENING HIS DOOR AS HE SPOKE ARE YOU READY

transcript: [' They then renewed their journey and under the better light made a safe crossing of the stable roofs.']
reference: THEY THEN RENEWED THEIR JOURNEY AND UNDER THE BETTER LIGHT MADE A SAFE CROSSING OF THE STABLE ROOFS

transcript: [' Robin entered the hut dragging the unwilling esquire after him.']
reference: ROBIN ENTERED THE HUT DRAGGING THE UNWILLING ESQUIRE AFTER HIM

transcript: [' Be not so foolish friend, said Fitzooth crossly.']
reference: BE NOT SO FOOLISH FRIEND SAID FITZOOTH CROSSLY

transcript: [' They moved thereafter cautiously about the hut groping before and about them to find something to show that Warrenton had fulfilled his mission.']
reference: THEY MOVED THEREAFTER CAUTIOUSLY ABOUT THE HUT GROPING BEFORE AND ABOUT THEM TO FIND SOMETHING TO SHOW THAT WARRENTON HAD FULFILLED HIS MISSION

transcript: [' They were upon the verge of an open trap in the far corner of the hut and Stutley had tripped over the edge of the reversed flap mouth of this pit.']
reference: THEY WERE UPON THE VERGE OF AN OPEN TRAP IN THE FAR CORNER OF THE HUT AND STUTELEY HAD TRIPPED OVER THE EDGE OF THE REVERSED FLAP MOUTH OF THIS PIT

transcript: [" Fitzooth's hand rested at last upon the top rung of a ladder and slowly the truth came to him."]
reference: FITZOOTH'S HAND RESTED AT LAST UPON THE TOP RUNG OF A LADDER AND SLOWLY THE TRUTH CAME TO HIM

transcript: [' Robin carefully descended the ladder and found himself soon upon firm rocky ground']
reference: ROBIN CAREFULLY DESCENDED THE LADDER AND FOUND HIMSELF SOON UPON FIRM ROCKY GROUND

transcript: [' Stuteley was by his side in a flash and then they both began feeling about them to ascertain the shape and character of this vault.']
reference: STUTELEY WAS BY HIS SIDE IN A FLASH AND THEN THEY BOTH BEGAN FEELING ABOUT THEM TO ASCERTAIN THE SHAPE AND CHARACTER OF THIS VAULT

transcript: [" From the blackness behind the light they heard a voice Warrenton's"]
reference: FROM THE BLACKNESS BEHIND THE LIGHT THEY HEARD A VOICE WARRENTON'S

transcript: [' Save me, masters, but you startled me rarely.']
reference: SAVE ME MASTERS BUT YOU STARTLED ME RARELY

transcript: [' cried he, waving the lanthorn before him to make sure that these were no ghosts in front of him.']
reference: CRIED HE WAVING THE LANTHORN BEFORE HIM TO MAKE SURE THAT THESE WERE NO GHOSTS IN FRONT OF HIM

transcript: [' inquired Robin with his suspicion still upon him.']
reference: ENQUIRED ROBIN WITH HIS SUSPICIONS STILL UPON HIM

transcript: [' Truly such a horse would be worth much in Nottingham Fair.']
reference: TRULY SUCH A HORSE SHOULD BE WORTH MUCH IN NOTTINGHAM FAIR

transcript: [' nay nay, lording, answered Warrenton with a half laugh.']
reference: NAY NAY LORDING ANSWERED WARRENTON WITH A HALF LAUGH

transcript: [" Warrenton spoke thus with significance to show Robin that he was not to think Jeffrey's claims to the estate would be passed by."]
reference: WARRENTON SPOKE THUS WITH SIGNIFICANCE TO SHOW ROBIN THAT HE WAS NOT TO THINK GEOFFREY'S CLAIMS TO THE ESTATE WOULD BE PASSED BY

transcript: [' Robin Fitzooth saw that his doubts of Warrenton had been unfair and he became ashamed of himself for harbouring them.']
reference: ROBIN FITZOOTH SAW THAT HIS DOUBTS OF WARRENTON HAD BEEN UNFAIR AND HE BECAME ASHAMED OF HIMSELF FOR HARBORING THEM

transcript: [" His tones rang pleasantly on Warrenton's ears and forthwith a good fellowship was heralded between them."]
reference: HIS TONES RANG PLEASANTLY ON WARRENTON'S EARS AND FORTHWITH A GOOD FELLOWSHIP WAS HERALDED BETWEEN THEM

transcript: [' The old servant told him quietly as they crept back to Gamewell that this passageway led from the hut in the pleasance to Sherwood and that Geoffrey for the time was hiding with the outlaws in the forest.']
reference: THE OLD SERVANT TOLD HIM QUIETLY AS THEY CREPT BACK TO GAMEWELL THAT THIS PASSAGE WAY LED FROM THE HUT IN THE PLEASANCE TO SHERWOOD AND THAT GEOFFREY FOR THE TIME WAS HIDING WITH THE OUTLAWS IN THE FOREST

transcript: [' He implores us to be discreet as the grave in this matter, for in sooth his life is in the hollow of our hands.']
reference: HE IMPLORES US TO BE DISCREET AS THE GRAVE IN THIS MATTER FOR IN SOOTH HIS LIFE IS IN THE HOLLOW OF OUR HANDS

transcript: [' They regained their apartment apparently without disturbing the household of Gamewell.']
reference: THEY REGAINED THEIR APARTMENT APPARENTLY WITHOUT DISTURBING THE HOUSEHOLD OF GAMEWELL

transcript: [' Out in the woods stood a nice little fir tree.']
reference: OUT IN THE WOODS STOOD A NICE LITTLE FIR TREE

transcript: [' The place he had was a very good one the sun shone on him as to fresh air there was enough of that and round him grew many large sized comrades pines as well as firs']
reference: THE PLACE HE HAD WAS A VERY GOOD ONE THE SUN SHONE ON HIM AS TO FRESH AIR THERE WAS ENOUGH OF THAT AND ROUND HIM GREW MANY LARGE SIZED COMRADES PINES AS WELL AS FIRS

transcript: [' He did not think of the warm sun and of the fresh air. He did not care for the little cottage children that ran about and prattled when they were in the woods looking for wild strawberries.']
reference: HE DID NOT THINK OF THE WARM SUN AND OF THE FRESH AIR HE DID NOT CARE FOR THE LITTLE COTTAGE CHILDREN THAT RAN ABOUT AND PRATTLED WHEN THEY WERE IN THE WOODS LOOKING FOR WILD STRAWBERRIES

transcript: [' But this was what the tree could not bear to hear.']
reference: BUT THIS WAS WHAT THE TREE COULD NOT BEAR TO HEAR

transcript: [' In winter, when the snow lay glittering on the ground, a hare would often come leaping along and jump right over the little tree.']
reference: IN WINTER WHEN THE SNOW LAY GLITTERING ON THE GROUND A HARE WOULD OFTEN COME LEAPING ALONG AND JUMP RIGHT OVER THE LITTLE TREE

transcript: [' Oh, that made him so angry.']
reference: OH THAT MADE HIM SO ANGRY

transcript: [' To grow and grow to get older and be tall thought the tree, that after all is the most delightful thing in the world.']
reference: TO GROW AND GROW TO GET OLDER AND BE TALL THOUGHT THE TREE THAT AFTER ALL IS THE MOST DELIGHTFUL THING IN THE WORLD

transcript: [' In autumn the woodcutters always came and felled some of the largest trees.']
reference: IN AUTUMN THE WOOD CUTTERS ALWAYS CAME AND FELLED SOME OF THE LARGEST TREES

transcript: [' This happened every year and the young fir tree that had now grown to a very comely size trembled at the sight for the magnificent great trees fell to the earth with noise and cracking the branches were lopped off and the trees looked long and bare they were hardly to be recognised and then they were laid in carts and the horses dragged them out of the wood.']
reference: THIS HAPPENED EVERY YEAR AND THE YOUNG FIR TREE THAT HAD NOW GROWN TO A VERY COMELY SIZE TREMBLED AT THE SIGHT FOR THE MAGNIFICENT GREAT TREES FELL TO THE EARTH WITH NOISE AND CRACKING THE BRANCHES WERE LOPPED OFF AND THE TREES LOOKED LONG AND BARE THEY WERE HARDLY TO BE RECOGNISED AND THEN THEY WERE LAID IN CARTS AND THE HORSES DRAGGED THEM OUT OF THE WOOD

transcript: [' Have you not met them anywhere?']
reference: HAVE YOU NOT MET THEM ANYWHERE

transcript: [' Rejoice in thy growth, said the sunbeams.']
reference: REJOICE IN THY GROWTH SAID THE SUNBEAMS

transcript: [' And then, what happens then?']
reference: AND THEN WHAT HAPPENS THEN

transcript: [' I would fain know if I am destined for so glorious a career cried the tree rejoicing.']
reference: I WOULD FAIN KNOW IF I AM DESTINED FOR SO GLORIOUS A CAREER CRIED THE TREE REJOICING

transcript: [' I am now tall and my branches spread like the others that were carried off last year. Oh!']
reference: I AM NOW TALL AND MY BRANCHES SPREAD LIKE THE OTHERS THAT WERE CARRIED OFF LAST YEAR OH

transcript: [' Were I but already on the cart']
reference: WERE I BUT ALREADY ON THE CART

transcript: [' Were I in the warm room with all its splendour and magnificence?']
reference: WERE I IN THE WARM ROOM WITH ALL THE SPLENDOR AND MAGNIFICENCE

transcript: [' Yes, then something better, something still grander will surely follow. Or wherefore should they thus ornament me?']
reference: YES THEN SOMETHING BETTER SOMETHING STILL GRANDER WILL SURELY FOLLOW OR WHEREFORE SHOULD THEY THUS ORNAMENT ME

transcript: [' Something better something still grander must follow but what?']
reference: SOMETHING BETTER SOMETHING STILL GRANDER MUST FOLLOW BUT WHAT

transcript: [' Rejoice in our presence said the air and the sunlight.']
reference: REJOICE IN OUR PRESENCE SAID THE AIR AND THE SUNLIGHT

transcript: [' Rejoice in thy own fresh youth!']
reference: REJOICE IN THY OWN FRESH YOUTH

transcript: [' But the tree did not rejoice at all he grew and grew and was green both winter and summer.']
reference: BUT THE TREE DID NOT REJOICE AT ALL HE GREW AND GREW AND WAS GREEN BOTH WINTER AND SUMMER

transcript: [' and towards Christmas he was one of the first that was cut down.']
reference: AND TOWARDS CHRISTMAS HE WAS ONE OF THE FIRST THAT WAS CUT DOWN

transcript: [' The axe struck deep into the very pith. The tree fell to the earth with a sigh. He felt a pang. It was like a swoon. He could not think of happiness, for he was sorrowful at being separated from his home, from the place where he had sprung up.']
reference: THE AXE STRUCK DEEP INTO THE VERY PITH THE TREE FELL TO THE EARTH WITH A SIGH HE FELT A PANG IT WAS LIKE A SWOON HE COULD NOT THINK OF HAPPINESS FOR HE WAS SORROWFUL AT BEING SEPARATED FROM HIS HOME FROM THE PLACE WHERE HE HAD SPRUNG UP

transcript: [' He well knew that he should never see his dear old comrades the little bushes and flowers around him any more, perhaps not even the birds.']
reference: HE WELL KNEW THAT HE SHOULD NEVER SEE HIS DEAR OLD COMRADES THE LITTLE BUSHES AND FLOWERS AROUND HIM ANYMORE PERHAPS NOT EVEN THE BIRDS

transcript: [' The departure was not at all agreeable.']
reference: THE DEPARTURE WAS NOT AT ALL AGREEABLE

transcript: [" The tree only came to himself when he was unloaded in a courtyard with the other trees and heard a man say, That one is splendid, we don't want the others."]
reference: THE TREE ONLY CAME TO HIMSELF WHEN HE WAS UNLOADED IN A COURT YARD WITH THE OTHER TREES AND HEARD A MAN SAY THAT ONE IS SPLENDID WE DON'T WANT THE OTHERS

transcript: [' There too were large easy chairs silken sofas large tables full of picture books and full of toys worth hundreds and hundreds of crowns at least the children said so']
reference: THERE TOO WERE LARGE EASY CHAIRS SILKEN SOFAS LARGE TABLES FULL OF PICTURE BOOKS AND FULL OF TOYS WORTH HUNDREDS AND HUNDREDS OF CROWNS AT LEAST THE CHILDREN SAID SO

transcript: [' The servants as well as the young ladies decorated it.']
reference: THE SERVANTS AS WELL AS THE YOUNG LADIES DECORATED IT

transcript: [' This evening, they all said,']
reference: THIS EVENING THEY ALL SAID

transcript: [' How it will shine this evening!']
reference: HOW IT WILL SHINE THIS EVENING

transcript: [' Perhaps the other trees from the forest will come to look at me.']
reference: PERHAPS THE OTHER TREES FROM THE FOREST WILL COME TO LOOK AT ME

transcript: [' It blazed up famously. Help! Help!']
reference: IT BLAZED UP FAMOUSLY HELP HELP

transcript: [' cried the young ladies, and they quickly put out the fire.']
reference: CRIED THE YOUNG LADIES AND THEY QUICKLY PUT OUT THE FIRE

transcript: [' A story!']
reference: A STORY

transcript: [' A story! cried the children, drawing a little fat man towards the tree.']
reference: A STORY CRIED THE CHILDREN DRAWING A LITTLE FAT MAN TOWARDS THE TREE

transcript: [' But I shall tell only one story.']
reference: BUT I SHALL TELL ONLY ONE STORY

transcript: [' Humpty dumpty fell downstairs and yet he married the princess.']
reference: HUMPY DUMPY FELL DOWNSTAIRS AND YET HE MARRIED THE PRINCESS

transcript: [" That's the way of the world!"]
reference: THAT'S THE WAY OF THE WORLD

transcript: [' thought the fir tree, and believed it all because the man who told the story was so good looking. Well, well.']
reference: THOUGHT THE FIR TREE AND BELIEVED IT ALL BECAUSE THE MAN WHO TOLD THE STORY WAS SO GOOD LOOKING WELL WELL

transcript: [" I won't tremble to morrow thought the fir tree."]
reference: I WON'T TREMBLE TO MORROW THOUGHT THE FIR TREE

transcript: [' And the whole night the tree stood still and in deep thought.']
reference: AND THE WHOLE NIGHT THE TREE STOOD STILL AND IN DEEP THOUGHT

transcript: [' In the morning the servant and the housemaid came in.']
reference: IN THE MORNING THE SERVANT AND THE HOUSEMAID CAME IN

transcript: [' But they dragged him out of the room and up the stairs into the loft and here in a dark corner where no daylight could enter they left him.']
reference: BUT THEY DRAGGED HIM OUT OF THE ROOM AND UP THE STAIRS INTO THE LOFT AND HERE IN A DARK CORNER WHERE NO DAYLIGHT COULD ENTER THEY LEFT HIM

transcript: [" What's the meaning of this? thought the tree."]
reference: WHAT'S THE MEANING OF THIS THOUGHT THE TREE

transcript: [' And he leaned against the wall lost in revery.']
reference: AND HE LEANED AGAINST THE WALL LOST IN REVERIE

transcript: [' Time enough had he too for his reflections for days and nights passed on and nobody came up and when at last somebody did come it was only to put some great trunks in a corner out of the way.']
reference: TIME ENOUGH HAD HE TOO FOR HIS REFLECTIONS FOR DAYS AND NIGHTS PASSED ON AND NOBODY CAME UP AND WHEN AT LAST SOMEBODY DID COME IT WAS ONLY TO PUT SOME GREAT TRUNKS IN A CORNER OUT OF THE WAY

transcript: [" 'tis now winter out of doors,' thought the tree."]
reference: TIS NOW WINTER OUT OF DOORS THOUGHT THE TREE

transcript: [' How kind man is, after all!']
reference: HOW KIND MAN IS AFTER ALL

transcript: [' If it only were not so dark here, and so terribly lonely.']
reference: IF IT ONLY WERE NOT SO DARK HERE AND SO TERRIBLY LONELY

transcript: [' Squeak, squeak.']
reference: SQUEAK SQUEAK

transcript: [' They snuffed about the fir tree and rustled among the branches.']
reference: THEY SNUFFED ABOUT THE FIR TREE AND RUSTLED AMONG THE BRANCHES

transcript: [' I am by no means old, said the fir tree.']
reference: I AM BY NO MEANS OLD SAID THE FIR TREE

transcript: [" There's many a one considerably older than I am."]
reference: THERE'S MANY A ONE CONSIDERABLY OLDER THAN I AM

transcript: [' They were so extremely curious.']
reference: THEY WERE SO EXTREMELY CURIOUS

transcript: [' I know no such place, said the tree.']
reference: I KNOW NO SUCH PLACE SAID THE TREE

transcript: [' And then he told all about his youth. And the little mice had never heard the like before, and they listened and said,']
reference: AND THEN HE TOLD ALL ABOUT HIS YOUTH AND THE LITTLE MICE HAD NEVER HEARD THE LIKE BEFORE AND THEY LISTENED AND SAID

transcript: [' said the fir tree, thinking over what he had himself related.']
reference: SAID THE FIR TREE THINKING OVER WHAT HE HAD HIMSELF RELATED

transcript: [' Yes, in reality, those were happy times.']
reference: YES IN REALITY THOSE WERE HAPPY TIMES

transcript: [' Who is Humpty Dumpty asked the mice.']
reference: WHO IS HUMPY DUMPY ASKED THE MICE

transcript: [' Only that one answered the tree.']
reference: ONLY THAT ONE ANSWERED THE TREE

transcript: [' It is a very stupid story.']
reference: IT IS A VERY STUPID STORY

transcript: [" Don't you know one about bacon and tallow candles? Can't you tell any larder stories?"]
reference: DON'T YOU KNOW ONE ABOUT BACON AND TALLOW CANDLES CAN'T YOU TELL ANY LARDER STORIES

transcript: [' no, said the tree.']
reference: NO SAID THE TREE

transcript: [' Then good bye said the rats, and they went home.']
reference: THEN GOOD BYE SAID THE RATS AND THEY WENT HOME

transcript: [' At last the little mice stayed away also and the tree sighed after all it was very pleasant when the sleek little mice sat round me and listened to what I told them.']
reference: AT LAST THE LITTLE MICE STAYED AWAY ALSO AND THE TREE SIGHED AFTER ALL IT WAS VERY PLEASANT WHEN THE SLEEK LITTLE MICE SAT ROUND ME AND LISTENED TO WHAT I TOLD THEM

transcript: [' Now that too is over.']
reference: NOW THAT TOO IS OVER

transcript: [' Why one morning there came a quantity of people and set to work in the loft.']
reference: WHY ONE MORNING THERE CAME A QUANTITY OF PEOPLE AND SET TO WORK IN THE LOFT

transcript: [' The trunks were moved, the tree was pulled out and thrown, rather hard it is true, down on the floor, but a man drew him towards the stairs where the daylight shone.']
reference: THE TRUNKS WERE MOVED THE TREE WAS PULLED OUT AND THROWN RATHER HARD IT IS TRUE DOWN ON THE FLOOR BUT A MAN DREW HIM TOWARDS THE STAIRS WHERE THE DAYLIGHT SHONE

transcript: [' But it was not the fir tree that they meant.']
reference: BUT IT WAS NOT THE FIR TREE THAT THEY MEANT

transcript: [' It was in a corner that he lay among weeds and nettles.']
reference: IT WAS IN A CORNER THAT HE LAY AMONG WEEDS AND NETTLES

transcript: [' The golden star of tinsel was still on the top of the tree and glittered in the sunshine.']
reference: THE GOLDEN STAR OF TINSEL WAS STILL ON THE TOP OF THE TREE AND GLITTERED IN THE SUNSHINE

transcript: [' In the courtyard some of the merry children were playing who had danced at Christmas round the fir tree and were so glad at the sight of him.']
reference: IN THE COURT YARD SOME OF THE MERRY CHILDREN WERE PLAYING WHO HAD DANCED AT CHRISTMAS ROUND THE FIR TREE AND WERE SO GLAD AT THE SIGHT OF HIM

transcript: [" And the gardener's boy chopped the tree into small pieces. There was a whole heap lying there."]
reference: AND THE GARDENER'S BOY CHOPPED THE TREE INTO SMALL PIECES THERE WAS A WHOLE HEAP LYING THERE

transcript: [' The wood flamed up splendidly under the large brewing copper and it sighed so deeply.']
reference: THE WOOD FLAMED UP SPLENDIDLY UNDER THE LARGE BREWING COPPER AND IT SIGHED SO DEEPLY

transcript: [' However, that was over now. The tree gone. The story at an end.']
reference: HOWEVER THAT WAS OVER NOW THE TREE GONE THE STORY AT AN END

transcript: [" Kenneth and beth refrained from telling the other girls or uncle john of old will Rogers's visit, but they got mister Watson in the library and questioned him closely about the penalty for forging a check."]
reference: KENNETH AND BETH REFRAINED FROM TELLING THE OTHER GIRLS OR UNCLE JOHN OF OLD WILL ROGERS'S VISIT BUT THEY GOT MISTER WATSON IN THE LIBRARY AND QUESTIONED HIM CLOSELY ABOUT THE PENALTY FOR FORGING A CHECK

transcript: [" It was a serious crime indeed mister Watson told them, and Tom Gates bade fair to serve a lengthy term in the state's prison as a consequence of his rash act."]
reference: IT WAS A SERIOUS CRIME INDEED MISTER WATSON TOLD THEM AND TOM GATES BADE FAIR TO SERVE A LENGTHY TERM IN STATE'S PRISON AS A CONSEQUENCE OF HIS RASH ACT

transcript: [" I can't see it in that light, said the old lawyer."]
reference: I CAN'T SEE IT IN THAT LIGHT SAID THE OLD LAWYER

transcript: [' It was a deliberate theft from his employers to protect a girl he loved.']
reference: IT WAS A DELIBERATE THEFT FROM HIS EMPLOYERS TO PROTECT A GIRL HE LOVED

transcript: [' But they could not have proven a case against Lucy if she was innocent and all their threats of arresting her were probably a mere bluff.']
reference: BUT THEY COULD NOT HAVE PROVEN A CASE AGAINST LUCY IF SHE WAS INNOCENT AND ALL THEIR THREATS OF ARRESTING HER WERE PROBABLY MERE BLUFF

transcript: [" He was soft hearted and impetuous said beth and being in love he didn't stop to count the cost."]
reference: HE WAS SOFT HEARTED AND IMPETUOUS SAID BETH AND BEING IN LOVE HE DIDN'T STOP TO COUNT THE COST

transcript: [' If the prosecution were withdrawn and the case settled with the victim of the forged check, then the young man would be allowed his freedom.']
reference: IF THE PROSECUTION WERE WITHDRAWN AND THE CASE SETTLED WITH THE VICTIM OF THE FORGED CHECK THEN THE YOUNG MAN WOULD BE ALLOWED HIS FREEDOM

transcript: [' But under the circumstances, I doubt if such an arrangement could be made.']
reference: BUT UNDER THE CIRCUMSTANCES I DOUBT IF SUCH AN ARRANGEMENT COULD BE MADE

transcript: [" Fairview was twelve miles away, but by ten o'clock they drew up at the county jail."]
reference: FAIRVIEW WAS TWELVE MILES AWAY BUT BY TEN O'CLOCK THEY DREW UP AT THE COUNTY JAIL

transcript: [' They were received in the little office by a man named Markham, who was the jailer.']
reference: THEY WERE RECEIVED IN THE LITTLE OFFICE BY A MAN NAMED MARKHAM WHO WAS THE JAILER

transcript: [' We wish to talk with him answered kenneth talk!']
reference: WE WISH TO TALK WITH HIM ANSWERED KENNETH TALK

transcript: [' I\'m running for representative on the Republican ticket," said Kenneth quietly.']
reference: I'M RUNNING FOR REPRESENTATIVE ON THE REPUBLICAN TICKET SAID KENNETH QUIETLY

transcript: [" Oh, say that's different observed Markham, altering his demeanor."]
reference: OH SAY THAT'S DIFFERENT OBSERVED MARKHAM ALTERING HIS DEMEANOR

transcript: [' May we see gates at once asked Kenneth.']
reference: MAY WE SEE GATES AT ONCE ASKED KENNETH

transcript: [' They followed the jailer along a succession of passages.']
reference: THEY FOLLOWED THE JAILER ALONG A SUCCESSION OF PASSAGES

transcript: [" Sometimes I'm that yearnin for a smoke I'm nearly crazy an I dunno which is worse dyin one way or the other"]
reference: SOMETIMES I'M THAT YEARNING FOR A SMOKE I'M NEARLY CRAZY AN I DUNNO WHICH IS WORST DYIN ONE WAY OR ANOTHER

transcript: [" He unlocked the door and called, Here's visitors, Tom."]
reference: HE UNLOCKED THE DOOR AND CALLED HERE'S VISITORS TOM

transcript: [' Worse, Tom, worse in error, replied the jailer gloomily.']
reference: WORSE TOM WORSE N EVER REPLIED THE JAILER GLOOMILY

transcript: [" Miss de graf said Kenneth noticing the boy's face critically as he stood where the light from the passage fell upon it."]
reference: MISS DE GRAF SAID KENNETH NOTICING THE BOY'S FACE CRITICALLY AS HE STOOD WHERE THE LIGHT FROM THE PASSAGE FELL UPON IT

transcript: [" Sorry we haven't any reception room in the jail."]
reference: SORRY WE HAVEN'T ANY RECEPTION ROOM IN THE JAIL

transcript: [" Sit down please said Gates in a cheerful and pleasant voice there's a bench here."]
reference: SIT DOWN PLEASE SAID GATES IN A CHEERFUL AND PLEASANT VOICE THERE'S A BENCH HERE

transcript: [' A fresh wholesome looking boy was Tom Gates with steady gray eyes an intelligent forehead but a sensitive rather weak mouth.']
reference: A FRESH WHOLESOME LOOKING BOY WAS TOM GATES WITH STEADY GRAY EYES AN INTELLIGENT FOREHEAD BUT A SENSITIVE RATHER WEAK MOUTH

transcript: [' We have heard something of your story said kenneth and are interested in it.']
reference: WE HAVE HEARD SOMETHING OF YOUR STORY SAID KENNETH AND ARE INTERESTED IN IT

transcript: [" I didn't stop to think whether it was foolish or not. I did it, and I'm glad I did it."]
reference: I DIDN'T STOP TO THINK WHETHER IT WAS FOOLISH OR NOT I DID IT AND I'M GLAD I DID

transcript: [' Old will is a fine fellow, but poor and helpless since missus Rogers had her accident.']
reference: OLD WILL IS A FINE FELLOW BUT POOR AND HELPLESS SINCE MISSUS ROGERS HAD HER ACCIDENT

transcript: [" Then Rogers wouldn't do anything but lead her around and wait upon her and the place went to rack and ruin."]
reference: THEN ROGERS WOULDN'T DO ANYTHING BUT LEAD HER AROUND AND WAIT UPON HER AND THE PLACE WENT TO RACK AND RUIN

transcript: [' He spoke simply, but paced up and down the narrow cell in front of them.']
reference: HE SPOKE SIMPLY BUT PACED UP AND DOWN THE NARROW CELL IN FRONT OF THEM

transcript: [' "Whose name did you sign to the check?" asked Kenneth.']
reference: WHOSE NAME DID YOU SIGN TO THE CHECK ASKED KENNETH

transcript: [' He is supposed to sign all the checks of the concern.']
reference: HE IS SUPPOSED TO SIGN ALL THE CHECKS OF THE CONCERN

transcript: [" It's a stock company and rich."]
reference: IT'S A STOCK COMPANY AND RICH

transcript: [' I was bitkeeper, so it was easy to get a blank check and forge the signature.']
reference: I WAS BOOKKEEPER SO IT WAS EASY TO GET A BLANK CHECK AND FORGE THE SIGNATURE

transcript: [" As regards my robbing the company, I'll say that I saved him a heavy loss one day."]
reference: AS REGARDS MY ROBBING THE COMPANY I'LL SAY THAT I SAVED THEM A HEAVY LOSS ONE DAY

transcript: [' I discovered and put out a fire that would have destroyed the whole plant, but Martial never even thanked me.']
reference: I DISCOVERED AND PUT OUT A FIRE THAT WOULD HAVE DESTROYED THE WHOLE PLANT BUT MARSHALL NEVER EVEN THANKED ME

transcript: [' It was better for him to think the girl unfeeling than to know the truth.']
reference: IT WAS BETTER FOR HIM TO THINK THE GIRL UNFEELING THAN TO KNOW THE TRUTH

transcript: [" I'm going to see mister Marshall said kenneth and discover what I can do to assist you thank you sir"]
reference: I'M GOING TO SEE MISTER MARSHALL SAID KENNETH AND DISCOVER WHAT I CAN DO TO ASSIST YOU THANK YOU SIR

transcript: [" It won't be much, but I'm grateful to find a friend."]
reference: IT WON'T BE MUCH BUT I'M GRATEFUL TO FIND A FRIEND

transcript: [' They left him then, for the jailer arrived to unlock the door and escort them to the office.']
reference: THEY LEFT HIM THEN FOR THE JAILER ARRIVED TO UNLOCK THE DOOR AND ESCORT THEM TO THE OFFICE

transcript: [" I've seen lots o that kind in my day."]
reference: I'VE SEEN LOTS OF THAT KIND IN MY DAY

transcript: [" and it ruins a man's disposition."]
reference: AND IT RUINS A MAN'S DISPOSITION

transcript: [' He looked up rather ungraciously, but motioned them to be seated.']
reference: HE LOOKED UP RATHER UNGRACIOUSLY BUT MOTIONED THEM TO BE SEATED

transcript: [' Some girl has been in here twice to interview my men and I have refused to admit her.']
reference: SOME GIRL HAS BEEN HERE TWICE TO INTERVIEW MY MEN AND I HAVE REFUSED TO ADMIT HER

transcript: [" I'm not electioneering just now."]
reference: I'M NOT ELECTIONEERING JUST NOW

transcript: [' Oh well sir, what about him?']
reference: OH WELL SIR WHAT ABOUT HIM

transcript: [" and he deserves a term in state's prison."]
reference: AND HE DESERVES A TERM IN STATE'S PRISON

transcript: [' It has cost me twice sixty dollars in annoyance.']
reference: IT HAS COST ME TWICE SIXTY DOLLARS IN ANNOYANCE

transcript: [" I'll pay all the costs besides."]
reference: I'LL PAY ALL THE COSTS BESIDES

transcript: [" You're foolish. Why should you do all this?"]
reference: YOU'RE FOOLISH WHY SHOULD YOU DO ALL THIS

transcript: [' I have my own reasons, mister Marshall.']
reference: I HAVE MY OWN REASONS MISTER MARSHALL

transcript: [" Give me a check for a hundred and fifty and I'll turn over to you the forged check and quash further proceedings."]
reference: GIVE ME A CHECK FOR A HUNDRED AND FIFTY AND I'LL TURN OVER TO YOU THE FORGED CHECK AND QUASH FURTHER PROCEEDINGS

transcript: [' He detested the grasping disposition that would endeavor to take advantage of his evident desire to help young Gates.']
reference: HE DETESTED THE GRASPING DISPOSITION THAT WOULD ENDEAVOR TO TAKE ADVANTAGE OF HIS EVIDENT DESIRE TO HELP YOUNG GATES

transcript: [' Beth uneasy at his silence nudged him.']
reference: BETH UNEASY AT HIS SILENCE NUDGED HIM

transcript: [' There was a grim smile of amusement on his shrewd face.']
reference: THERE WAS A GRIM SMILE OF AMUSEMENT ON HIS SHREWD FACE

transcript: [" He might have had that forged check for the face of it if he'd been sharp."]
reference: HE MIGHT HAVE HAD THAT FORGED CHECK FOR THE FACE OF IT IF HE'D BEEN SHARP

transcript: [' And to think we can save all that misery and despair by the payment of a hundred and fifty dollars.']
reference: AND TO THINK WE CAN SAVE ALL THAT MISERY AND DESPAIR BY THE PAYMENT OF A HUNDRED AND FIFTY DOLLARS

transcript: [" So to the surprise of the Democratic committee and all his friends mister Hopkins announced that he would oppose forbes's aggressive campaign with an equal aggressiveness and spend as many dollars in doing so as might be necessary."]
reference: SO TO THE SURPRISE OF THE DEMOCRATIC COMMITTEE AND ALL HIS FRIENDS MISTER HOPKINS ANNOUNCED THAT HE WOULD OPPOSE FORBES'S AGGRESSIVE CAMPAIGN WITH AN EQUAL AGGRESSIVENESS AND SPEND AS MANY DOLLARS IN DOING SO AS MIGHT BE NECESSARY

transcript: [" One of mister hopkins's first tasks after calling his faithful henchmen around him was to make a careful canvass of the voters of his district to see what was still to be accomplished."]
reference: ONE OF MISTER HOPKINS'S FIRST TASKS AFTER CALLING HIS FAITHFUL HENCHMEN AROUND HIM WAS TO MAKE A CAREFUL CANVASS OF THE VOTERS OF HIS DISTRICT TO SEE WHAT WAS STILL TO BE ACCOMPLISHED

transcript: [' The weak need contingency must be strengthened and fortified and a couple of hundred votes in one way or the other secured from opposition.']
reference: THE WEAK KNEED CONTINGENCY MUST BE STRENGTHENED AND FORTIFIED AND A COUPLE OF HUNDRED VOTES IN ONE WAY OR ANOTHER SECURED FROM THE OPPOSITION

transcript: [' The Democratic committee figured out a way to do this.']
reference: THE DEMOCRATIC COMMITTEE FIGURED OUT A WAY TO DO THIS

transcript: [' Under ordinary conditions Reynolds was sure to be elected, but the committee proposed to sacrifice him in order to elect Hopkins.']
reference: UNDER ORDINARY CONDITIONS REYNOLDS WAS SURE TO BE ELECTED BUT THE COMMITTEE PROPOSED TO SACRIFICE HIM IN ORDER TO ELECT HOPKINS

transcript: [' The only thing necessary was to fix seth Reynolds and this hopkins arranged personally.']
reference: THE ONLY THING NECESSARY WAS TO FIX SETH REYNOLDS AND THIS HOPKINS ARRANGED PERSONALLY

transcript: [' And this was why Kenneth and Beth discovered him conversing with the young woman in the buggy.']
reference: AND THIS WAS WHY KENNETH AND BETH DISCOVERED HIM CONVERSING WITH THE YOUNG WOMAN IN THE BUGGY

transcript: [" The description she gave of the coming reception to the women's political league was so humorous and diverting that they were both laughing heartily over the thing when the young people passed them and thus mister hopkins failed to notice who the occupants of the other vehicle were."]
reference: THE DESCRIPTION SHE GAVE OF THE COMING RECEPTION TO THE WOMAN'S POLITICAL LEAGUE WAS SO HUMOROUS AND DIVERTING THAT THEY WERE BOTH LAUGHING HEARTILY OVER THE THING WHEN THE YOUNG PEOPLE PASSED THEM AND THUS MISTER HOPKINS FAILED TO NOTICE WHO THE OCCUPANTS OF THE OTHER VEHICLE WERE

transcript: [' These women were flattered by the attention of the young lady and had promised to assist in electing mister forbes.']
reference: THESE WOMEN WERE FLATTERED BY THE ATTENTION OF THE YOUNG LADY AND HAD PROMISED TO ASSIST IN ELECTING MISTER FORBES

transcript: [' Louise hoped for excellent results from this organization and wished the entertainment to be so effective in winning their good will that they would work earnestly for the cause in which they were enlisted.']
reference: LOUISE HOPED FOR EXCELLENT RESULTS FROM THIS ORGANIZATION AND WISHED THE ENTERTAINMENT TO BE SO EFFECTIVE IN WINNING THEIR GOOD WILL THAT THEY WOULD WORK EARNESTLY FOR THE CAUSE IN WHICH THEY WERE ENLISTED

transcript: [' The fairview band was engaged to discourse as much harmony as it could produce and the resources of the great house were taxed to entertain the guests.']
reference: THE FAIRVIEW BAND WAS ENGAGED TO DISCOURSE AS MUCH HARMONY AS IT COULD PRODUCE AND THE RESOURCES OF THE GREAT HOUSE WERE TAXED TO ENTERTAIN THE GUESTS

transcript: [' Tables were spread on the lawn, and a dainty but substantial repast was to be served.']
reference: TABLES WERE SPREAD ON THE LAWN AND A DAINTY BUT SUBSTANTIAL REPAST WAS TO BE SERVED

transcript: [' This was the first occasion within a generation when such an entertainment had been given at Elmhurst and the only one within the memory of man where the neighbors and country people had been the invited guests.']
reference: THIS WAS THE FIRST OCCASION WITHIN A GENERATION WHEN SUCH AN ENTERTAINMENT HAD BEEN GIVEN AT ELMHURST AND THE ONLY ONE WITHIN THE MEMORY OF MAN WHERE THE NEIGHBORS AND COUNTRY PEOPLE HAD BEEN INVITED GUESTS

transcript: [' The attendance was unexpectedly large and the girls were delighted foreseeing great success for their fight.']
reference: THE ATTENDANCE WAS UNEXPECTEDLY LARGE AND THE GIRLS WERE DELIGHTED FORESEEING GREAT SUCCESS FOR THEIR FETE

transcript: [' We ought to have more attendance, Beth, said louise approaching her cousin.']
reference: WE OUGHT TO HAVE MORE ATTENDANTS BETH SAID LOUISE APPROACHING HER COUSIN

transcript: [" Won't you run into the house and see if martha can't spare one or two more maids?"]
reference: WON'T YOU RUN INTO THE HOUSE AND SEE IF MARTHA CAN'T SPARE ONE OR TWO MORE MAIDS

transcript: [' She was very fond of the young ladies whom she had known when aunt Jane was their mistress here and beth was her especial favorite.']
reference: SHE WAS VERY FOND OF THE YOUNG LADIES WHOM SHE HAD KNOWN WHEN AUNT JANE WAS THE MISTRESS HERE AND BETH WAS HER ESPECIAL FAVORITE

transcript: [' The housekeeper led the way and Beth followed.']
reference: THE HOUSEKEEPER LED THE WAY AND BETH FOLLOWED

transcript: [' For a moment Beth stood staring while the new maid regarded her with composure and a slight smile upon her beautiful face.']
reference: FOR A MOMENT BETH STOOD STARING WHILE THE NEW MAID REGARDED HER WITH COMPOSURE AND A SLIGHT SMILE UPON HER BEAUTIFUL FACE

transcript: [' She was dressed in the regulation costume of the maids at Elmhurst, a plain black gown with a white apron and cap.']
reference: SHE WAS DRESSED IN THE REGULATION COSTUME OF THE MAIDS AT ELMHURST A PLAIN BLACK GOWN WITH WHITE APRON AND CAP

transcript: [" Then she gave a little laugh and replied, No, Miss Beth, I'm Elizabeth Parsons."]
reference: THEN SHE GAVE A LITTLE LAUGH AND REPLIED NO MISS BETH I'M ELIZABETH PARSONS

transcript: [' "But it can\'t be!" protested the girl.']
reference: BUT IT CAN'T BE PROTESTED THE GIRL

transcript: [' I attend to the household mending, you know, and care for the linen.']
reference: I ATTEND TO THE HOUSEHOLD MENDING YOU KNOW AND CARE FOR THE LINEN

transcript: [' You speak like an educated person said beth wonderingly where is your home?']
reference: YOU SPEAK LIKE AN EDUCATED PERSON SAID BETH WONDERINGLY WHERE IS YOUR HOME

transcript: [' For the first time the maid seemed a little confused and her gaze wandered from the face of her visitor.']
reference: FOR THE FIRST TIME THE MAID SEEMED A LITTLE CONFUSED AND HER GAZE WANDERED FROM THE FACE OF HER VISITOR

transcript: [" She sat down in a rocking chair and clasping her hands in her lap rocked slowly back and forth. I'm sorry said beth."]
reference: SHE SAT DOWN IN A ROCKING CHAIR AND CLASPING HER HANDS IN HER LAP ROCKED SLOWLY BACK AND FORTH I'M SORRY SAID BETH

transcript: [' Eliza Parsons shook her head.']
reference: ELIZA PARSONS SHOOK HER HEAD

transcript: [" They they excite me in some way and I can't bear them. You must excuse me."]
reference: THEY THEY EXCITE ME IN SOME WAY AND I I CAN'T BEAR THEM YOU MUST EXCUSE ME

transcript: [' She even seemed mildly amused at the attention she attracted.']
reference: SHE EVEN SEEMED MILDLY AMUSED AT THE ATTENTION SHE ATTRACTED

transcript: [' Beth was a beautiful girl, the handsomest of the three cousins by far, yet Eliza surpassed her in natural charm and seemed well aware of the fact.']
reference: BETH WAS A BEAUTIFUL GIRL THE HANDSOMEST OF THE THREE COUSINS BY FAR YET ELIZA SURPASSED HER IN NATURAL CHARM AND SEEMED WELL AWARE OF THE FACT

transcript: [' Her manner was neither independent nor assertive, but rather one of well bred composure and calm reliance.']
