reference: HER MANNER WAS NEITHER INDEPENDENT NOR ASSERTIVE BUT RATHER ONE OF WELL BRED COMPOSURE AND CALM RELIANCE

transcript: [" Her eyes wandered to the maid's hands."]
reference: HER EYES WANDERED TO THE MAID'S HANDS

transcript: [' However her features and form might repress any evidence of nervousness, these hands told a different story.']
reference: HOWEVER HER FEATURES AND FORM MIGHT REPRESS ANY EVIDENCE OF NERVOUSNESS THESE HANDS TOLD A DIFFERENT STORY

transcript: [' She rose quickly to her feet with an impetuous gesture that made her visitor catch her breath.']
reference: SHE ROSE QUICKLY TO HER FEET WITH AN IMPETUOUS GESTURE THAT MADE HER VISITOR CATCH HER BREATH

transcript: [' I wish I knew myself she cried fiercely.']
reference: I WISH I KNEW MYSELF SHE CRIED FIERCELY

transcript: [' Will you leave me alone in my own room or must I go away to escape you?']
reference: WILL YOU LEAVE ME ALONE IN MY OWN ROOM OR MUST I GO AWAY TO ESCAPE YOU

transcript: [' Eliza closed the door behind her with a decided slam and a key clicked in the lock.']
reference: ELIZA CLOSED THE DOOR BEHIND HER WITH A DECIDED SLAM AND A KEY CLICKED IN THE LOCK

transcript: [' Concord returned to its place amidst the tents.']
reference: CONCORD RETURNED TO ITS PLACE AMIDST THE TENTS

transcript: [' The English forwarded to the French baskets of flowers of which they had made a plentiful provision to greet the arrival of the young princess. The French in return invited the English to a supper which was to be given the next day.']
reference: THE ENGLISH FORWARDED TO THE FRENCH BASKETS OF FLOWERS OF WHICH THEY HAD MADE A PLENTIFUL PROVISION TO GREET THE ARRIVAL OF THE YOUNG PRINCESS THE FRENCH IN RETURN INVITED THE ENGLISH TO A SUPPER WHICH WAS TO BE GIVEN THE NEXT DAY

transcript: [' Congratulations were poured in upon the princess everywhere during her journey.']
reference: CONGRATULATIONS WERE POURED IN UPON THE PRINCESS EVERYWHERE DURING HER JOURNEY

transcript: [' From the respect paid her on all sides she seemed like a queen, and from the adoration with which she was treated by two or three she appeared an object of worship. The queen mother gave the French the most affectionate reception. France was her native country, and she had suffered too much unhappiness in England for England to have made her forget France.']
reference: FROM THE RESPECT PAID HER ON ALL SIDES SHE SEEMED LIKE A QUEEN AND FROM THE ADORATION WITH WHICH SHE WAS TREATED BY TWO OR THREE SHE APPEARED AN OBJECT OF WORSHIP THE QUEEN MOTHER GAVE THE FRENCH THE MOST AFFECTIONATE RECEPTION FRANCE WAS HER NATIVE COUNTRY AND SHE HAD SUFFERED TOO MUCH UNHAPPINESS IN ENGLAND FOR ENGLAND TO HAVE MADE HER FORGET FRANCE

transcript: [' She taught her daughter then by her own affection for it that love for a country where they had both been hospitably received and where a brilliant future opened for them.']
reference: SHE TAUGHT HER DAUGHTER THEN BY HER OWN AFFECTION FOR IT THAT LOVE FOR A COUNTRY WHERE THEY HAD BOTH BEEN HOSPITABLY RECEIVED AND WHERE A BRILLIANT FUTURE OPENED BEFORE THEM

transcript: [' The count had thrown himself back on his seat leaning his shoulders against the partition of the tent and remained thus, his face buried in his hands, with heaving chest and restless limbs.']
reference: THE COUNT HAD THROWN HIMSELF BACK ON HIS SEAT LEANING HIS SHOULDERS AGAINST THE PARTITION OF THE TENT AND REMAINED THUS HIS FACE BURIED IN HIS HANDS WITH HEAVING CHEST AND RESTLESS LIMBS

transcript: [' This has indeed been a harassing day, continued the young man, his eyes fixed upon his friend.']
reference: THIS HAS INDEED BEEN A HARASSING DAY CONTINUED THE YOUNG MAN HIS EYES FIXED UPON HIS FRIEND

transcript: [' You will be frank with me? I always am.']
reference: YOU WILL BE FRANK WITH ME I ALWAYS AM

transcript: [' Can you imagine why Buckingham has been so violent? I suspect.']
reference: CAN YOU IMAGINE WHY BUCKINGHAM HAS BEEN SO VIOLENT I SUSPECT

transcript: [' It is you who are mistaken, Raoul. I have read his distress in his eyes, in his every gesture and action the whole day.']
reference: IT IS YOU WHO ARE MISTAKEN RAOUL I HAVE READ HIS DISTRESS IN HIS EYES IN HIS EVERY GESTURE AND ACTION THE WHOLE DAY

transcript: [' I can perceive love clearly enough.']
reference: I CAN PERCEIVE LOVE CLEARLY ENOUGH

transcript: [' I am convinced of what I say, said the count.']
reference: I AM CONVINCED OF WHAT I SAY SAID THE COUNT

transcript: [' It is annoyance then.']
reference: IT IS ANNOYANCE THEN

transcript: [' In those very terms I even added more.']
reference: IN THOSE VERY TERMS I EVEN ADDED MORE

transcript: [' But continued Raoul not interrupted by this movement of his friend, heaven be praised, the French who are pronounced to be thoughtless and indiscreet, reckless even, are capable of bringing a calm and sound judgment to bear on matters of such high importance.']
reference: BUT CONTINUED RAOUL NOT INTERRUPTED BY THIS MOVEMENT OF HIS FRIEND HEAVEN BE PRAISED THE FRENCH WHO ARE PRONOUNCED TO BE THOUGHTLESS AND INDISCREET RECKLESS EVEN ARE CAPABLE OF BRINGING A CALM AND SOUND JUDGMENT TO BEAR ON MATTERS OF SUCH HIGH IMPORTANCE

transcript: [' Thus it is that the honor of three is saved our country our masters and our own.']
reference: THUS IT IS THAT THE HONOR OF THREE IS SAVED OUR COUNTRY'S OUR MASTER'S AND OUR OWN

transcript: [' Yes, I need repose. Many things have agitated me to day both in mind and body. When you return to morrow, I shall no longer be the same man.']
reference: YES I NEED REPOSE MANY THINGS HAVE AGITATED ME TO DAY BOTH IN MIND AND BODY WHEN YOU RETURN TO MORROW I SHALL NO LONGER BE THE SAME MAN

transcript: [' But in this friendly pressure Raoul could detect the nervous agitation of a great internal conflict.']
reference: BUT IN THIS FRIENDLY PRESSURE RAOUL COULD DETECT THE NERVOUS AGITATION OF A GREAT INTERNAL CONFLICT

transcript: [' The night was clear starlit and splendid the tempest had passed away and the sweet influences of the evening had restored life peace and security everywhere.']
reference: THE NIGHT WAS CLEAR STARLIT AND SPLENDID THE TEMPEST HAD PASSED AWAY AND THE SWEET INFLUENCES OF THE EVENING HAD RESTORED LIFE PEACE AND SECURITY EVERYWHERE

transcript: [' Upon the large square in front of the hotel the shadows of the tents intersected by the golden moonbeams formed as it were a huge mosaic of jet and yellow flagstones.']
reference: UPON THE LARGE SQUARE IN FRONT OF THE HOTEL THE SHADOWS OF THE TENTS INTERSECTED BY THE GOLDEN MOONBEAMS FORMED AS IT WERE A HUGE MOSAIC OF JET AND YELLOW FLAGSTONES

transcript: [' Bragelonne watched for some time the conduct of the two lovers listened to the loud and uncivil slumbers of Manicamp who snored as imperiously as though he was wearing his blue and gold instead of his violet suit']
reference: BRAGELONNE WATCHED FOR SOME TIME THE CONDUCT OF THE TWO LOVERS LISTENED TO THE LOUD AND UNCIVIL SLUMBERS OF MANICAMP WHO SNORED AS IMPERIOUSLY AS THOUGH HE WAS WEARING HIS BLUE AND GOLD INSTEAD OF HIS VIOLET SUIT

transcript: [' Goliath makes another discovery.']
reference: GOLIATH MAKES ANOTHER DISCOVERY

transcript: [' They were certainly no near the solution of their problem.']
reference: THEY WERE CERTAINLY NO NEARER THE SOLUTION OF THEIR PROBLEM

transcript: [' The poor little things cried Cynthia think of them having been turned to the wall all these years!']
reference: THE POOR LITTLE THINGS CRIED CYNTHIA THINK OF THEM HAVING BEEN TURNED TO THE WALL ALL THESE YEARS

transcript: [' Now what is the sense of it two innocent babies like that?']
reference: NOW WHAT WAS THE SENSE OF IT TWO INNOCENT BABIES LIKE THAT

transcript: [' But joyce had not been listening all at once she put down her candle on the table and faced her companion.']
reference: BUT JOYCE HAD NOT BEEN LISTENING ALL AT ONCE SHE PUT DOWN HER CANDLE ON THE TABLE AND FACED HER COMPANION

transcript: [" The twin brother did something she didn't like and she turned his picture to the wall."]
reference: THE TWIN BROTHER DID SOMETHING SHE DIDN'T LIKE AND SHE TURNED HIS PICTURE TO THE WALL

transcript: [" hers happened to be on the same frame too, but she evidently didn't care about it."]
reference: HERS HAPPENED TO BE IN THE SAME FRAME TOO BUT SHE EVIDENTLY DIDN'T CARE ABOUT THAT

transcript: [' Now what have you to say, Cynthia Sprague?']
reference: NOW WHAT HAVE YOU TO SAY CYNTHIA SPRAGUE

transcript: [" I thought we were stumped again when I first saw that picture, but it's been of some use after all."]
reference: I THOUGHT WE WERE STUMPED AGAIN WHEN I FIRST SAW THAT PICTURE BUT IT'S BEEN OF SOME USE AFTER ALL

transcript: [' Do you suppose the miniature was a copy of the same thing?']
reference: DO YOU SUPPOSE THE MINIATURE WAS A COPY OF THE SAME THING

transcript: [' "What in the world is it?" queried Joyce.']
reference: WHAT IN THE WORLD IS THAT QUERIED JOYCE

transcript: [" They worry me terribly and besides I'd like to see what this lovely furniture looks like without such quantities of dust all over it good scheme, sin!"]
reference: THEY WORRY ME TERRIBLY AND BESIDES I'D LIKE TO SEE WHAT THIS LOVELY FURNITURE LOOKS LIKE WITHOUT SUCH QUANTITIES OF DUST ALL OVER IT GOOD SCHEME CYN

transcript: [" We'll come in here this afternoon with old clothes on and have a regular house cleaning."]
reference: WE'LL COME IN HERE THIS AFTERNOON WITH OLD CLOTHES ON AND HAVE A REGULAR HOUSE CLEANING

transcript: [" It can't hurt anything, I'm sure, for we won't disturb things at all."]
reference: IT CAN'T HURT ANYTHING I'M SURE FOR WE WON'T DISTURB THINGS AT ALL

transcript: [' This thought however did not enter the heads of the enthusiastic pair.']
reference: THIS THOUGHT HOWEVER DID NOT ENTER THE HEADS OF THE ENTHUSIASTIC PAIR

transcript: [' Smuggling the house cleaning paraphernalia into the cellar window unobserved that afternoon proved no easy task for Cynthia had added a whisk broom and dust pan to the outfit.']
reference: SMUGGLING THE HOUSE CLEANING PARAPHERNALIA INTO THE CELLAR WINDOW UNOBSERVED THAT AFTERNOON PROVED NO EASY TASK FOR CYNTHIA HAD ADDED A WHISK BROOM AND DUST PAN TO THE OUTFIT

transcript: [" The lure proved too much for him and he came sporting after it as friskily as a young kitten much to Cynthia's delight when she caught sight of him."]
reference: THE LURE PROVED TOO MUCH FOR HIM AND HE CAME SPORTING AFTER IT AS FRISKILY AS A YOUNG KITTEN MUCH TO CYNTHIA'S DELIGHT WHEN SHE CAUGHT SIGHT OF HIM

transcript: [' Oh, let him come along she urged. I do love to see him about that old house.']
reference: OH LET HIM COME ALONG SHE URGED I DO LOVE TO SEE HIM ABOUT THAT OLD HOUSE

transcript: [' He makes it sort of cosier.']
reference: HE MAKES IT SORT OF COZIER

transcript: [" Now let's dust the furniture and pictures."]
reference: NOW LET'S DUST THE FURNITURE AND PICTURES

transcript: [' Yet little as it was, it had already made a vast difference in the aspect of the room.']
reference: YET LITTLE AS IT WAS IT HAD ALREADY MADE A VAST DIFFERENCE IN THE ASPECT OF THE ROOM

transcript: [' surface dust at least had been removed and the fine old furniture gave a hint of its real elegance and polish.']
reference: SURFACE DUST AT LEAST HAD BEEN REMOVED AND THE FINE OLD FURNITURE GAVE A HINT OF ITS REAL ELEGANCE AND POLISH

transcript: [' Then, she suddenly remarked,']
reference: THEN SHE SUDDENLY REMARKED

transcript: [" And my pocket money is getting low again and you haven't any left, as usual."]
reference: AND MY POCKET MONEY IS GETTING LOW AGAIN AND YOU HAVEN'T ANY LEFT AS USUAL

transcript: [' They say illumination by candle light is the prettiest in the world.']
reference: THEY SAY ILLUMINATION BY CANDLE LIGHT IS THE PRETTIEST IN THE WORLD

transcript: [" Why, it's Goliath as usual, they both cried peering in."]
reference: WHY IT'S GOLIATH AS USUAL THEY BOTH CRIED PEERING IN

transcript: [" Isn't he the greatest for getting into odd corners?"]
reference: ISN'T HE THE GREATEST FOR GETTING INTO ODD CORNERS

transcript: [' forgetting all their weariness they seized their candles and scurried through the house finding on occasional papers tucked away in some odd corner']
reference: FORGETTING ALL THEIR WEARINESS THEY SEIZED THEIR CANDLES AND SCURRIED THROUGH THE HOUSE FINDING AN OCCASIONAL PAPER TUCKED AWAY IN SOME ODD CORNER

transcript: [" Well, I'm convinced that the boarded up house mystery happened not earlier than April sixteenth eighteen sixty one and probably not much later."]
reference: WELL I'M CONVINCED THAT THE BOARDED UP HOUSE MYSTERY HAPPENED NOT EARLIER THAN APRIL SIXTEENTH EIGHTEEN SIXTY ONE AND PROBABLY NOT MUCH LATER

transcript: [' No words were spoken no language was uttered save that of wailing and hissing and that somehow was indistinct as if it existed in fancy and not in reality.']
reference: NO WORDS WERE SPOKEN NO LANGUAGE WAS UTTERED SAVE THAT OF WAILING AND HISSING AND THAT SOMEHOW WAS INDISTINCT AS IF IT EXISTED IN FANCY AND NOT IN REALITY

transcript: [' I heard a noise behind. I turned and saw Kaffir his black eyes shining, while in his hand he held a gleaming knife. He lifted it above his head as if to strike, but I had the strength of ten men and I hurled him from me.']
reference: I HEARD A NOISE BEHIND I TURNED AND SAW KAFFAR HIS BLACK EYES SHINING WHILE IN HIS HAND HE HELD A GLEAMING KNIFE HE LIFTED IT ABOVE HIS HEAD AS IF TO STRIKE BUT I HAD THE STRENGTH OF TEN MEN AND I HURLED HIM FROM ME

transcript: [' Onward! said a distant voice.']
reference: ONWARD SAID A DISTANT VOICE

transcript: [' No sound broke the stillness of the night.']
reference: NO SOUND BROKE THE STILLNESS OF THE NIGHT

transcript: [' The story of its evil influence came back to me and in my bewildered condition I wondered whether there was not some truth in what had been said.']
reference: THE STORY OF ITS EVIL INFLUENCE CAME BACK TO ME AND IN MY BEWILDERED CONDITION I WONDERED WHETHER THERE WAS NOT SOME TRUTH IN WHAT HAD BEEN SAID

transcript: [' What was that?']
reference: WHAT WAS THAT

transcript: [' What then a human hand large and shapely appeared distinctly on the surface of the pond']
reference: WHAT THEN A HUMAN HAND LARGE AND SHAPELY APPEARED DISTINCTLY ON THE SURFACE OF THE POND

transcript: [' nothing more, not even the wrist to which it might be attached.']
reference: NOTHING MORE NOT EVEN THE WRIST TO WHICH IT MIGHT BE ATTACHED

transcript: [' It did not beckon or indeed move at all. It was as still as the hand of death.']
reference: IT DID NOT BECKON OR INDEED MOVE AT ALL IT WAS AS STILL AS THE HAND OF DEATH

transcript: [' I awoke to consciousness fighting at first it seemed as if I was fighting with a phantom but gradually my opponent became more real to me it was kaffir']
reference: I AWOKE TO CONSCIOUSNESS FIGHTING AT FIRST IT SEEMED AS IF I WAS FIGHTING WITH A PHANTOM BUT GRADUALLY MY OPPONENT BECAME MORE REAL TO ME IT WAS KAFFAR

transcript: [' A sound of voices, a flash of light.']
reference: A SOUND OF VOICES A FLASH OF LIGHT

transcript: [' A feeling of freedom and I was awake! Where?']
reference: A FEELING OF FREEDOM AND I WAS AWAKE WHERE

transcript: [" said another voice which I recognised as Voltaire's kaffir"]
reference: SAID ANOTHER VOICE WHICH I RECOGNIZED AS VOLTAIRE'S KAFFAR

transcript: [' I had scarcely known what I had been saying or doing up to this time, but as he spoke I looked at my hand.']
reference: I HAD SCARCELY KNOWN WHAT I HAD BEEN SAYING OR DOING UP TO THIS TIME BUT AS HE SPOKE I LOOKED AT MY HAND

transcript: [' In the light of the moon I saw a knife red with blood and my hand too was also discoloured.']
reference: IN THE LIGHT OF THE MOON I SAW A KNIFE RED WITH BLOOD AND MY HAND TOO WAS ALSO DISCOLOURED

transcript: [' I do not know. I am dazed, bewildered.']
reference: I DO NOT KNOW I AM DAZED BEWILDERED

transcript: [" But that is Kaffir's knife."]
reference: BUT THAT IS KAFFAR'S KNIFE

transcript: [' I know he had it this very evening.']
reference: I KNOW HE HAD IT THIS VERY EVENING

transcript: [' I remembered saying have we been together?']
reference: I REMEMBER SAYING HAVE WE BEEN TOGETHER

transcript: [' Voltaire picked up something from the ground and looked at it.']
reference: VOLTAIRE PICKED UP SOMETHING FROM THE GROUND AND LOOKED AT IT

transcript: [' I say you do know what this means and you must tell us.']
reference: I SAY YOU DO KNOW WHAT THIS MEANS AND YOU MUST TELL US

transcript: [' A terrible thought flashed into my mind.']
reference: A TERRIBLE THOUGHT FLASHED INTO MY MIND

transcript: [" I had again been acting under the influence of this man's power."]
reference: I HAD AGAIN BEEN ACTING UNDER THE INFLUENCE OF THIS MAN'S POWER

transcript: [" perchance too Kaffir's death might serve him in good stead."]
reference: PERCHANCE TOO KAFFAR'S DEATH MIGHT SERVE HIM IN GOOD STEAD

transcript: [' My tongue refused to articulate, my power of speech left me.']
reference: MY TONGUE REFUSED TO ARTICULATE MY POWER OF SPEECH LEFT ME

transcript: [' My position was too terrible.']
reference: MY POSITION WAS TOO TERRIBLE

transcript: [' My over wrought nerves yielded at last.']
reference: MY OVERWROUGHT NERVES YIELDED AT LAST

transcript: [' For some time after that I remembered nothing distinctly.']
reference: FOR SOME TIME AFTER THAT I REMEMBERED NOTHING DISTINCTLY

transcript: [' The Three Modes of Management']
reference: THE THREE MODES OF MANAGEMENT

transcript: [' To suppose that the object of this work is to aid in effecting such a substitution as that is entirely to mistake its nature and design.']
reference: TO SUPPOSE THAT THE OBJECT OF THIS WORK IS TO AID IN EFFECTING SUCH A SUBSTITUTION AS THAT IS ENTIRELY TO MISTAKE ITS NATURE AND DESIGN

transcript: [' By reason and affection.']
reference: BY REASON AND AFFECTION

transcript: [' As the chaise drives away, Mary stands bewildered and perplexed on the doorstep, her mind in a tumult of excitement in which hatred of the doctor, distrust and suspicion of her mother, disappointment, vexation, and ill humor surge and swell among those delicate organizations on which the structure and development of the soul so closely depend, doing perhaps an irreparable injury.']
reference: AS THE CHAISE DRIVES AWAY MARY STANDS BEWILDERED AND PERPLEXED ON THE DOOR STEP HER MIND IN A TUMULT OF EXCITEMENT IN WHICH HATRED OF THE DOCTOR DISTRUST AND SUSPICION OF HER MOTHER DISAPPOINTMENT VEXATION AND ILL HUMOR SURGE AND SWELL AMONG THOSE DELICATE ORGANIZATIONS ON WHICH THE STRUCTURE AND DEVELOPMENT OF THE SOUL SO CLOSELY DEPEND DOING PERHAPS AN IRREPARABLE INJURY

transcript: [' The mother, as soon as the chase is so far turned that Mary can no longer watch the expression of her countenance, goes away from the door with a smile of complacency and satisfaction on her face at the ingenuity and success of her little artifice.']
reference: THE MOTHER AS SOON AS THE CHAISE IS SO FAR TURNED THAT MARY CAN NO LONGER WATCH THE EXPRESSION OF HER COUNTENANCE GOES AWAY FROM THE DOOR WITH A SMILE OF COMPLACENCY AND SATISFACTION UPON HER FACE AT THE INGENUITY AND SUCCESS OF HER LITTLE ARTIFICE

transcript: [" So you will be a good girl, I know, and not make any trouble, but will stay at home contentedly, won't you?"]
reference: SO YOU WILL BE A GOOD GIRL I KNOW AND NOT MAKE ANY TROUBLE BUT WILL STAY AT HOME CONTENTEDLY WON'T YOU

transcript: [' The mother in managing the case in this way relies partly on convincing the reason of the child and partly on an appeal to her affection.']
reference: THE MOTHER IN MANAGING THE CASE IN THIS WAY RELIES PARTLY ON CONVINCING THE REASON OF THE CHILD AND PARTLY ON AN APPEAL TO HER AFFECTION

transcript: [' If you should not be a good girl but should show signs of making us any trouble, I shall have to send you out somewhere to the back part of the house until we are gone.']
reference: IF YOU SHOULD NOT BE A GOOD GIRL BUT SHOULD SHOW SIGNS OF MAKING US ANY TROUBLE I SHALL HAVE TO SEND YOU OUT SOMEWHERE TO THE BACK PART OF THE HOUSE UNTIL WE ARE GONE

transcript: [' But this last supposition is almost always unnecessary for if Mary has been habitually managed on this principle she will not make any trouble.']
reference: BUT THIS LAST SUPPOSITION IS ALMOST ALWAYS UNNECESSARY FOR IF MARY HAS BEEN HABITUALLY MANAGED ON THIS PRINCIPLE SHE WILL NOT MAKE ANY TROUBLE

transcript: [' It is indeed true that the importance of tact and skill in the training of the young and of cultivating their reason and securing their affection can not be overrated.']
reference: IT IS INDEED TRUE THAT THE IMPORTANCE OF TACT AND SKILL IN THE TRAINING OF THE YOUNG AND OF CULTIVATING THEIR REASON AND SECURING THEIR AFFECTION CAN NOT BE OVERRATED

transcript: [' To such persons these indirect modes of training children in habits of subordination to their will or rather of yielding to their influence are specially useful.']
reference: TO SUCH PERSONS THESE INDIRECT MODES OF TRAINING CHILDREN IN HABITS OF SUBORDINATION TO THEIR WILL OR RATHER OF YIELDING TO THEIR INFLUENCE ARE SPECIALLY USEFUL

transcript: [' Della had a young sister named Maria and a cousin whose name was Jane.']
reference: DELLA HAD A YOUNG SISTER NAMED MARIA AND A COUSIN WHOSE NAME WAS JANE

transcript: [' Now Delia contrived to obtain a great influence and ascendancy over the minds of the children by means of these dolls.']
reference: NOW DELIA CONTRIVED TO OBTAIN A GREAT INFLUENCE AND ASCENDENCY OVER THE MINDS OF THE CHILDREN BY MEANS OF THESE DOLLS

transcript: [' To give an idea of these conversations, I will report one of them in full.']
reference: TO GIVE AN IDEA OF THESE CONVERSATIONS I WILL REPORT ONE OF THEM IN FULL

transcript: [" You have come, Andella, Andella was the name of Jane's doll, to make Rosalie a visit."]
reference: YOU HAVE COME ANDELLA ANDELLA WAS THE NAME OF JANE'S DOLL TO MAKE ROSALIE A VISIT

transcript: [' I am very glad.']
reference: I AM VERY GLAD

transcript: [' I expect you have been a very good girl, Andella, since you were here last.']
reference: I EXPECT YOU HAVE BEEN A VERY GOOD GIRL ANDELLA SINCE YOU WERE HERE LAST

transcript: [' Then turning to Jane she asked in a somewhat altered tone, Has she been a good girl, Jane?']
reference: THEN TURNING TO JANE SHE ASKED IN A SOMEWHAT ALTERED TONE HAS SHE BEEN A GOOD GIRL JANE

transcript: [' For instance, one day the children had been playing upon the piazza with blocks and other playthings and finally had gone into the house leaving all the things on the floor of the piazza instead of putting them away in their places as they ought to have done.']
reference: FOR INSTANCE ONE DAY THE CHILDREN HAD BEEN PLAYING UPON THE PIAZZA WITH BLOCKS AND OTHER PLAYTHINGS AND FINALLY HAD GONE INTO THE HOUSE LEAVING ALL THE THINGS ON THE FLOOR OF THE PIAZZA INSTEAD OF PUTTING THEM AWAY IN THEIR PLACES AS THEY OUGHT TO HAVE DONE

transcript: [' They were now playing with their dolls in the parlor.']
reference: THEY WERE NOW PLAYING WITH THEIR DOLLS IN THE PARLOR

transcript: [" Delia came to the parlour and with an air of great mystery beckoned the children aside and said to them in a whisper leave Andella and rosalie here and don't say a word to them"]
reference: DELIA CAME TO THE PARLOR AND WITH AN AIR OF GREAT MYSTERY BECKONED THE CHILDREN ASIDE AND SAID TO THEM IN A WHISPER LEAVE ANDELLA AND ROSALIE HERE AND DON'T SAY A WORD TO THEM

transcript: [' So saying she led the way on tiptoe followed by the children out of the room and round by a circuitous route to the piazza there']
reference: SO SAYING SHE LED THE WAY ON TIPTOE FOLLOWED BY THE CHILDREN OUT OF THE ROOM AND ROUND BY A CIRCUITOUS ROUTE TO THE PIAZZA THERE

transcript: [' said she, pointing to the playthings. See?']
reference: SAID SHE POINTING TO THE PLAYTHINGS SEE

transcript: [' Put these playthings all away quick and carefully and we will not let them know anything about your leaving them out.']
reference: PUT THESE PLAYTHINGS ALL AWAY QUICK AND CAREFULLY AND WE WILL NOT LET THEM KNOW ANY THING ABOUT YOUR LEAVING THEM OUT

transcript: [' And this method of treating the case was much more effectual in making them disposed to avoid committing a similar fault another time than any direct rebukes or expressions of displeasure addressed personally to them would have been.']
reference: AND THIS METHOD OF TREATING THE CASE WAS MUCH MORE EFFECTUAL IN MAKING THEM DISPOSED TO AVOID COMMITTING A SIMILAR FAULT ANOTHER TIME THAN ANY DIRECT REBUKES OR EXPRESSIONS OF DISPLEASURE ADDRESSED PERSONALLY TO THEM WOULD HAVE BEEN

transcript: [' Nature of the effect produced by early impressions.']
reference: NATURE OF THE EFFECT PRODUCED BY EARLY IMPRESSIONS

transcript: [' That is comparatively nothing.']
reference: THAT IS COMPARATIVELY NOTHING

transcript: [' They are chiefly formed from combinations of the impressions made in childhood.']
reference: THEY ARE CHIEFLY FORMED FROM COMBINATIONS OF THE IMPRESSIONS MADE IN CHILDHOOD

transcript: [' Vast importance and influence of this mental furnishing.']
reference: VAST IMPORTANCE AND INFLUENCE OF THIS MENTAL FURNISHING

transcript: [' Without going to any such extreme as this, we can easily see on reflection how vast an influence on the ideas and conceptions as well as on the principles of action in mature years must be exerted by the nature and character of the images which the period of infancy and childhood impress upon the mind.']
reference: WITHOUT GOING TO ANY SUCH EXTREME AS THIS WE CAN EASILY SEE ON REFLECTION HOW VAST AN INFLUENCE ON THE IDEAS AND CONCEPTIONS AS WELL AS ON THE PRINCIPLES OF ACTION IN MATURE YEARS MUST BE EXERTED BY THE NATURE AND CHARACTER OF THE IMAGES WHICH THE PERIOD OF INFANCY AND CHILDHOOD IMPRESSES UPON THE MIND

transcript: [' The pain produced by an act of hasty and angry violence to which a father subjects his son may soon pass away, but the memory of it does not pass away with the pain.']
reference: THE PAIN PRODUCED BY AN ACT OF HASTY AND ANGRY VIOLENCE TO WHICH A FATHER SUBJECTS HIS SON MAY SOON PASS AWAY BUT THE MEMORY OF IT DOES NOT PASS AWAY WITH THE PAIN

transcript: [' But anders cared nothing about that.']
reference: BUT ANDERS CARED NOTHING ABOUT THAT

transcript: [' He made a bow so deep that his back came near breaking and he was dumfounded I can tell you when he saw it was nobody but anders.']
reference: HE MADE A BOW SO DEEP THAT HIS BACK CAME NEAR BREAKING AND HE WAS DUMBFOUNDED I CAN TELL YOU WHEN HE SAW IT WAS NOBODY BUT ANDERS

transcript: [' He was such a big boy that he wore high boots and carried a jack knife.']
reference: HE WAS SUCH A BIG BOY THAT HE WORE HIGH BOOTS AND CARRIED A JACK KNIFE

transcript: [' Now this knife was a splendid one though half the blade was gone and the handle was a little cracked and anders knew that one is almost a man as soon as one has a jack knife.']
reference: NOW THIS KNIFE WAS A SPLENDID ONE THOUGH HALF THE BLADE WAS GONE AND THE HANDLE WAS A LITTLE CRACKED AND ANDERS KNEW THAT ONE IS ALMOST A MAN AS SOON AS ONE HAS A JACK KNIFE

transcript: [' Yes, why not thought Anders?']
reference: YES WHY NOT THOUGHT ANDERS

transcript: [' Seeing that I am so fine, I may as well go and visit the king.']
reference: SEEING THAT I AM SO FINE I MAY AS WELL GO AND VISIT THE KING

transcript: [' I am going to the court ball answered Anders.']
reference: I AM GOING TO THE COURT BALL ANSWERED ANDERS

transcript: [" And she took Anders's hand and walked with him up the broad marble stairs where soldiers were posted at every third step and through the magnificent halls where courtiers in silk and velvet stood bowing wherever he went."]
reference: AND SHE TOOK ANDERS HAND AND WALKED WITH HIM UP THE BROAD MARBLE STAIRS WHERE SOLDIERS WERE POSTED AT EVERY THIRD STEP AND THROUGH THE MAGNIFICENT HALLS WHERE COURTIERS IN SILK AND VELVET STOOD BOWING WHEREVER HE WENT

transcript: [' For, like as not, they must have thought him a prince when they saw his fine cap.']
reference: FOR LIKE AS NOT THEY MUST HAVE THOUGHT HIM A PRINCE WHEN THEY SAW HIS FINE CAP

transcript: [' At the farther end of the largest hall a table was set with golden cups and golden plates in long rows.']
reference: AT THE FARTHER END OF THE LARGEST HALL A TABLE WAS SET WITH GOLDEN CUPS AND GOLDEN PLATES IN LONG ROWS

transcript: [' On huge silver platters were pyramids of tarts and cakes, and red wine sparkled in glittering decanters.']
reference: ON HUGE SILVER PLATTERS WERE PYRAMIDS OF TARTS AND CAKES AND RED WINE SPARKLED IN GLITTERING DECANTERS

transcript: [' The princess sat down under a blue canopy with bouquets of roses and she let Anders sit in a golden chair by her side.']
reference: THE PRINCESS SAT DOWN UNDER A BLUE CANOPY WITH BOUQUETS OF ROSES AND SHE LET ANDERS SIT IN A GOLDEN CHAIR BY HER SIDE

transcript: [" But you must not eat with your cap on your head,' she said, and was going to take it off."]
reference: BUT YOU MUST NOT EAT WITH YOUR CAP ON YOUR HEAD SHE SAID AND WAS GOING TO TAKE IT OFF

transcript: [' The princess certainly was beautiful and he would have dearly liked to be kissed by her, but the cap which his mother had made he would not give up on any condition.']
reference: THE PRINCESS CERTAINLY WAS BEAUTIFUL AND HE WOULD HAVE DEARLY LIKED TO BE KISSED BY HER BUT THE CAP WHICH HIS MOTHER HAD MADE HE WOULD NOT GIVE UP ON ANY CONDITION

transcript: [' He only shook his head.']
reference: HE ONLY SHOOK HIS HEAD

transcript: [' Well, but now said the princess and she filled his pockets with cakes and put her own heavy gold chain around his neck and bent down and kissed him.']
reference: WELL BUT NOW SAID THE PRINCESS AND SHE FILLED HIS POCKETS WITH CAKES AND PUT HER OWN HEAVY GOLD CHAIN AROUND HIS NECK AND BENT DOWN AND KISSED HIM

transcript: [' That is a very fine cap you have, he said.']
reference: THAT IS A VERY FINE CAP YOU HAVE HE SAID

transcript: [' So it is, said Anders.']
reference: SO IT IS SAID ANDERS

transcript: [" And it is made of mother's best yarn. And she knitted it herself. And everybody wants to get it away from me."]
reference: AND IT IS MADE OF MOTHER'S BEST YARN AND SHE KNITTED IT HERSELF AND EVERYBODY WANTS TO GET IT AWAY FROM ME

transcript: [' With one jump, Anders got out of his chair.']
reference: WITH ONE JUMP ANDERS GOT OUT OF HIS CHAIR

transcript: [' He darted like an arrow through all the halls, down all the stairs, and across the yard.']
reference: HE DARTED LIKE AN ARROW THROUGH ALL THE HALLS DOWN ALL THE STAIRS AND ACROSS THE YARD

transcript: [" He still held onto it with both hands as he rushed into his mother's cottage."]
reference: HE STILL HELD ON TO IT WITH BOTH HANDS AS HE RUSHED INTO HIS MOTHER'S COTTAGE

transcript: [' And all his brothers and sisters stood round and listened with their mouths open.']
reference: AND ALL HIS BROTHERS AND SISTERS STOOD ROUND AND LISTENED WITH THEIR MOUTHS OPEN

transcript: [" But when his big brother heard that he had refused to give his cap for a king's golden crown, he said that anders was a stupid."]
reference: BUT WHEN HIS BIG BROTHER HEARD THAT HE HAD REFUSED TO GIVE HIS CAP FOR A KING'S GOLDEN CROWN HE SAID THAT ANDERS WAS A STUPID

transcript: [" Anders' face grew red."]
reference: ANDERS FACE GREW RED

transcript: [' But his mother hugged him close.']
reference: BUT HIS MOTHER HUGGED HIM CLOSE

transcript: [' No, my little son, she said.']
reference: NO MY LITTLE SON SHE SAID

transcript: [' If you dressed in silk and gold from top to toe, you could not look any nicer than in your little red cap.']
reference: IF YOU DRESSED IN SILK AND GOLD FROM TOP TO TOE YOU COULD NOT LOOK ANY NICER THAN IN YOUR LITTLE RED CAP

transcript: [" At the conclusion of the banquet which was served at five o'clock the king entered his cabinet where his tailors were awaiting him for the purpose of trying on the celebrated costume representing spring which was the result of so much imagination and had cost so many efforts of thought to the designers and ornament workers of the court."]
reference: AT THE CONCLUSION OF THE BANQUET WHICH WAS SERVED AT FIVE O'CLOCK THE KING ENTERED HIS CABINET WHERE HIS TAILORS WERE AWAITING HIM FOR THE PURPOSE OF TRYING ON THE CELEBRATED COSTUME REPRESENTING SPRING WHICH WAS THE RESULT OF SO MUCH IMAGINATION AND HAD COST SO MANY EFFORTS OF THOUGHT TO THE DESIGNERS AND ORNAMENT WORKERS OF THE COURT

transcript: [' Ah, very well.']
reference: AH VERY WELL

transcript: [' Let him come in then said the king and as if Colbert had been listening at the door for the purpose of keeping himself au courant with the conversation he entered as soon as the king had pronounced his name to the two courtiers']
reference: LET HIM COME IN THEN SAID THE KING AND AS IF COLBERT HAD BEEN LISTENING AT THE DOOR FOR THE PURPOSE OF KEEPING HIMSELF AU COURANT WITH THE CONVERSATION HE ENTERED AS SOON AS THE KING HAD PRONOUNCED HIS NAME TO THE TWO COURTIERS

transcript: [' Gentlemen to your posts whereupon Saint Aignan and Villeroy took their leave.']
reference: GENTLEMEN TO YOUR POSTS WHEREUPON SAINT AIGNAN AND VILLEROY TOOK THEIR LEAVE

transcript: [' Certainly sire, but I must have money to do that. What?']
reference: CERTAINLY SIRE BUT I MUST HAVE MONEY TO DO THAT WHAT

transcript: [' What do you mean? inquired Louise.']
reference: WHAT DO YOU MEAN INQUIRED LOUIS

transcript: [' He has given them with too much grace not to have others still to give if they are required, which is the case at the present moment.']
reference: HE HAS GIVEN THEM WITH TOO MUCH GRACE NOT TO HAVE OTHERS STILL TO GIVE IF THEY ARE REQUIRED WHICH IS THE CASE AT THE PRESENT MOMENT

transcript: [' It is necessary therefore that he should comply. The king frowned.']
reference: IT IS NECESSARY THEREFORE THAT HE SHOULD COMPLY THE KING FROWNED

transcript: [' Does your majesty then no longer believe the disloyal attempt?']
reference: DOES YOUR MAJESTY THEN NO LONGER BELIEVE THE DISLOYAL ATTEMPT

transcript: [' Not at all you are on the contrary most agreeable to me']
reference: NOT AT ALL YOU ARE ON THE CONTRARY MOST AGREEABLE TO ME

transcript: [" Your majesty's plan then, in this affair, is."]
reference: YOUR MAJESTY'S PLAN THEN IN THIS AFFAIR IS

transcript: [' You will take them from my private treasure.']
reference: YOU WILL TAKE THEM FROM MY PRIVATE TREASURE

transcript: [' The news circulated with the rapidity of lightning during its progress it kindled every variety of coquetry desire and wild ambition.']
reference: THE NEWS CIRCULATED WITH THE RAPIDITY OF LIGHTNING DURING ITS PROGRESS IT KINDLED EVERY VARIETY OF COQUETRY DESIRE AND WILD AMBITION

transcript: [" The king had completed his toilet by nine o'clock. He appeared in an open carriage decorated with branches of trees and flowers."]
reference: THE KING HAD COMPLETED HIS TOILETTE BY NINE O'CLOCK HE APPEARED IN AN OPEN CARRIAGE DECORATED WITH BRANCHES OF TREES AND FLOWERS

transcript: [' The queens had taken their seats upon a magnificent dais or platform erected upon the borders of the lake in a theatre of wonderful elegance of construction.']
reference: THE QUEENS HAD TAKEN THEIR SEATS UPON A MAGNIFICENT DIAS OR PLATFORM ERECTED UPON THE BORDERS OF THE LAKE IN A THEATER OF WONDERFUL ELEGANCE OF CONSTRUCTION

transcript: [' Suddenly, for the purpose of restoring peace and order, spring, accompanied by his whole court, made his appearance.']
reference: SUDDENLY FOR THE PURPOSE OF RESTORING PEACE AND ORDER SPRING ACCOMPANIED BY HIS WHOLE COURT MADE HIS APPEARANCE

transcript: [' The seasons allies of spring followed him closely to form a quadrille which after many words of more or less flattering import was the commencement of the dance.']
reference: THE SEASONS ALLIES OF SPRING FOLLOWED HIM CLOSELY TO FORM A QUADRILLE WHICH AFTER MANY WORDS OF MORE OR LESS FLATTERING IMPORT WAS THE COMMENCEMENT OF THE DANCE

transcript: [' His legs, the best shaped at court, were displayed to great advantage in flesh coloured silken hose of silk so fine and so transparent that it seemed almost like flesh itself.']
reference: HIS LEGS THE BEST SHAPED AT COURT WERE DISPLAYED TO GREAT ADVANTAGE IN FLESH COLORED SILKEN HOSE OF SILK SO FINE AND SO TRANSPARENT THAT IT SEEMED ALMOST LIKE FLESH ITSELF

transcript: [' There was something in his carriage which resembled the buoyant movements of an immortal and he did not dance so much as seemed to soar along.']
reference: THERE WAS SOMETHING IN HIS CARRIAGE WHICH RESEMBLED THE BUOYANT MOVEMENTS OF AN IMMORTAL AND HE DID NOT DANCE SO MUCH AS SEEM TO SOAR ALONG

transcript: [' Yes, it is suppressed.']
reference: YES IT IS SUPPRESSED

transcript: [' Far from it, sire. Your majesty having given no directions about it, the musicians have retained it.']
reference: FAR FROM IT SIRE YOUR MAJESTY HAVING GIVEN NO DIRECTIONS ABOUT IT THE MUSICIANS HAVE RETAINED IT

transcript: [' Yes, sire, and ready dressed for the ballet.']
reference: YES SIRE AND READY DRESSED FOR THE BALLET

transcript: [" Sire, he said, your majesty's most devoted servant approaches to perform a service on this occasion with similar zeal that he has already shown on the field of battle."]
reference: SIRE HE SAID YOUR MAJESTY'S MOST DEVOTED SERVANT APPROACHES TO PERFORM A SERVICE ON THIS OCCASION WITH SIMILAR ZEAL THAT HE HAS ALREADY SHOWN ON THE FIELD OF BATTLE

transcript: [' The king seemed only pleased with every one present.']
reference: THE KING SEEMED ONLY PLEASED WITH EVERY ONE PRESENT

transcript: [' Monsieur was the only one who did not understand anything about the matter.']
reference: MONSIEUR WAS THE ONLY ONE WHO DID NOT UNDERSTAND ANYTHING ABOUT THE MATTER

transcript: [' The ballet began. The effect was more than beautiful.']
reference: THE BALLET BEGAN THE EFFECT WAS MORE THAN BEAUTIFUL

transcript: [' When the music by its bursts of melody carried away these illustrious dancers, when the simple untutored pantomime of that period only the more natural on account of the very indifferent acting of the august actors had reached its culminating point of triumph, the theatre shook with tumultuous applause.']
reference: WHEN THE MUSIC BY ITS BURSTS OF MELODY CARRIED AWAY THESE ILLUSTRIOUS DANCERS WHEN THE SIMPLE UNTUTORED PANTOMIME OF THAT PERIOD ONLY THE MORE NATURAL ON ACCOUNT OF THE VERY INDIFFERENT ACTING OF THE AUGUST ACTORS HAD REACHED ITS CULMINATING POINT OF TRIUMPH THE THEATER SHOOK WITH TUMULTUOUS APPLAUSE

transcript: [' Disdainful of a success of which madame showed no acknowledgment, he thought of nothing but boldly regaining the marked preference of the princess.']
reference: DISDAINFUL OF A SUCCESS OF WHICH MADAME SHOWED NO ACKNOWLEDGEMENT HE THOUGHT OF NOTHING BUT BOLDLY REGAINING THE MARKED PREFERENCE OF THE PRINCESS

transcript: [' By degrees all his happiness, all his brilliancy subsided into regret and uneasiness so that his limbs lost their power, his arms hung heavily by his sides, and his head drooped as though he was stupefied.']
reference: BY DEGREES ALL HIS HAPPINESS ALL HIS BRILLIANCY SUBSIDED INTO REGRET AND UNEASINESS SO THAT HIS LIMBS LOST THEIR POWER HIS ARMS HUNG HEAVILY BY HIS SIDES AND HIS HEAD DROOPED AS THOUGH HE WAS STUPEFIED

transcript: [' The king, who had from this moment become in reality the principal dancer in the quadrille, cast a look upon his vanquished rival.']
reference: THE KING WHO HAD FROM THIS MOMENT BECOME IN REALITY THE PRINCIPAL DANCER IN THE QUADRILLE CAST A LOOK UPON HIS VANQUISHED RIVAL

transcript: [' everyone could observe his agitation and prostration, a prostration which was indeed the more remarkable since people were not accustomed to see him with his arms hanging listlessly by his side, his head bewildered, and his eyes with all their bright intelligence bedimmed.']
reference: EVERY ONE COULD OBSERVE HIS AGITATION AND PROSTRATION A PROSTRATION WHICH WAS INDEED THE MORE REMARKABLE SINCE PEOPLE WERE NOT ACCUSTOMED TO SEE HIM WITH HIS ARMS HANGING LISTLESSLY BY HIS SIDE HIS HEAD BEWILDERED AND HIS EYES WITH ALL THEIR BRIGHT INTELLIGENCE BEDIMMED

transcript: [' Upon this Madame deigned to turn her eyes languishingly towards the comte, observing,']
reference: UPON THIS MADAME DEIGNED TO TURN HER EYES LANGUISHINGLY TOWARDS THE COMTE OBSERVING

transcript: [' Do you think so? she replied with indifference.']
reference: DO YOU THINK SO SHE REPLIED WITH INDIFFERENCE

transcript: [' Yes, the character which your royal highness assumed is in perfect harmony with your own.']
reference: YES THE CHARACTER WHICH YOUR ROYAL HIGHNESS ASSUMED IS IN PERFECT HARMONY WITH YOUR OWN

transcript: [' Explain yourself?']
reference: EXPLAIN YOURSELF

transcript: [' I allude to the goddess.']
reference: I ALLUDE TO THE GODDESS

transcript: [' The princess inquired. No.']
reference: THE PRINCESS INQUIRED NO

transcript: [' She then rose humming the air to which she was presently going to dance.']
reference: SHE THEN ROSE HUMMING THE AIR TO WHICH SHE WAS PRESENTLY GOING TO DANCE

transcript: [' The arrow pierced his heart and wounded him mortally.']
reference: THE ARROW PIERCED HIS HEART AND WOUNDED HIM MORTALLY

transcript: [' A quarter of an hour afterwards he returned to the theatre, but it will be readily believed that it was only a powerful effort of reason over his great excitement that enabled him to go back, or perhaps, for love is thus strangely constituted, he found it impossible even to remain much longer separated from the presence of one who had broken his heart.']
reference: A QUARTER OF AN HOUR AFTERWARDS HE RETURNED TO THE THEATER BUT IT WILL BE READILY BELIEVED THAT IT WAS ONLY A POWERFUL EFFORT OF REASON OVER HIS GREAT EXCITEMENT THAT ENABLED HIM TO GO BACK OR PERHAPS FOR LOVE IS THUS STRANGELY CONSTITUTED HE FOUND IT IMPOSSIBLE EVEN TO REMAIN MUCH LONGER SEPARATED FROM THE PRESENCE OF ONE WHO HAD BROKEN HIS HEART

transcript: [' When she perceived the young man she rose like a woman surprised in the midst of ideas she was desirous of concealing from herself.']
reference: WHEN SHE PERCEIVED THE YOUNG MAN SHE ROSE LIKE A WOMAN SURPRISED IN THE MIDST OF IDEAS SHE WAS DESIROUS OF CONCEALING FROM HERSELF

transcript: [' Remain, I implore you. The evening is most lovely.']
reference: REMAIN I IMPLORE YOU THE EVENING IS MOST LOVELY

transcript: [' Indeed! — ah!']
reference: INDEED AH

transcript: [' I remember now, and I congratulate myself. Do you love anyone?']
reference: I REMEMBER NOW AND I CONGRATULATE MYSELF DO YOU LOVE ANY ONE

transcript: [' forgive me, I hardly know what I am saying a thousand times forgive me Madame was right, quite right, this brutal exile has completely turned my brain.']
reference: FORGIVE ME I HARDLY KNOW WHAT I AM SAYING A THOUSAND TIMES FORGIVE ME MADAME WAS RIGHT QUITE RIGHT THIS BRUTAL EXILE HAS COMPLETELY TURNED MY BRAIN

transcript: [' There cannot be a doubt he received you kindly for in fact you returned without his permission.']
reference: THERE CANNOT BE A DOUBT HE RECEIVED YOU KINDLY FOR IN FACT YOU RETURNED WITHOUT HIS PERMISSION

transcript: [' Oh, mademoiselle, why have I not a devoted sister or a true friend such as yourself?']
reference: OH MADEMOISELLE WHY HAVE I NOT A DEVOTED SISTER OR A TRUE FRIEND SUCH AS YOURSELF

transcript: [' What already here? they said to her.']
reference: WHAT ALREADY HERE THEY SAID TO HER

transcript: [' I have been here this quarter of an hour, replied la valliere.']
reference: I HAVE BEEN HERE THIS QUARTER OF AN HOUR REPLIED LA VALLIERE

transcript: [' Did not the dancing amuse you? No.']
reference: DID NOT THE DANCING AMUSE YOU NO

transcript: [' No more than the dancing.']
reference: NO MORE THAN THE DANCING

transcript: [' La Valliere is quite a poetess, said Tonnet Chiron.']
reference: LA VALLIERE IS QUITE A POETESS SAID TONNAY CHARENTE

transcript: [' I am a woman and there are few like me whoever loves me flatters me whoever flatters me pleases me and whoever pleases well said Montalais you do not finish']
reference: I AM A WOMAN AND THERE ARE FEW LIKE ME WHOEVER LOVES ME FLATTERS ME WHOEVER FLATTERS ME PLEASES ME AND WHOEVER PLEASES WELL SAID MONTALAIS YOU DO NOT FINISH

transcript: [' It is too difficult, replied Mademoiselle de Tenne Charente, laughing loudly.']
reference: IT IS TOO DIFFICULT REPLIED MADEMOISELLE DE TONNAY CHARENTE LAUGHING LOUDLY

transcript: [' Look yonder, do you not see the moon slowly rising, silvering the topmost branches of the chestnuts and the oaks?']
reference: LOOK YONDER DO YOU NOT SEE THE MOON SLOWLY RISING SILVERING THE TOPMOST BRANCHES OF THE CHESTNUTS AND THE OAKS

transcript: [' Exquisite soft turf of the woods, the happiness which your friendship confers upon me.']
reference: EXQUISITE SOFT TURF OF THE WOODS THE HAPPINESS WHICH YOUR FRIENDSHIP CONFERS UPON ME

transcript: [' Well, said Mademoiselle de Tonnaychalant, I also think a good deal, but I take care.']
reference: WELL SAID MADEMOISELLE DE TONNAY CHARENTE I ALSO THINK A GOOD DEAL BUT I TAKE CARE

transcript: [' To say nothing said Montalais so that when Mademoiselle de Tournay-Charente thinks, Athene is the only one who knows it.']
reference: TO SAY NOTHING SAID MONTALAIS SO THAT WHEN MADEMOISELLE DE TONNAY CHARENTE THINKS ATHENAIS IS THE ONLY ONE WHO KNOWS IT

transcript: [' quick quick then among the high reed grass said Montalais stoop Athenee you are so tall']
reference: QUICK QUICK THEN AMONG THE HIGH REED GRASS SAID MONTALAIS STOOP ATHENAIS YOU ARE SO TALL

transcript: [' The young girls had indeed made themselves small, indeed invisible.']
reference: THE YOUNG GIRLS HAD INDEED MADE THEMSELVES SMALL INDEED INVISIBLE

transcript: [' She was here just now said the count.']
reference: SHE WAS HERE JUST NOW SAID THE COUNT

transcript: [' You are positive then?']
reference: YOU ARE POSITIVE THEN

transcript: [' Yes, but perhaps I frightened her. In what way?']
reference: YES BUT PERHAPS I FRIGHTENED HER IN WHAT WAY

transcript: [' How is it la valliere said Mademoiselle de Tenerchyte that the vicomte de Bragelonne spoke of you as louise?']
reference: HOW IS IT LA VALLIERE SAID MADEMOISELLE DE TONNAY CHARENTE THAT THE VICOMTE DE BRAGELONNE SPOKE OF YOU AS LOUISE

transcript: [' It seems the king will not consent to it.']
reference: IT SEEMS THE KING WILL NOT CONSENT TO IT

transcript: [' Good gracious, has the king any right to interfere in matters of that kind?']
reference: GOOD GRACIOUS HAS THE KING ANY RIGHT TO INTERFERE IN MATTERS OF THAT KIND

transcript: [' I give my consent.']
reference: I GIVE MY CONSENT

transcript: [" Oh, I am speaking seriously replied Montalais, and my opinion in this case is quite as good as the king's, I suppose, is it not, Louise?"]
reference: OH I AM SPEAKING SERIOUSLY REPLIED MONTALAIS AND MY OPINION IN THIS CASE IS QUITE AS GOOD AS THE KING'S I SUPPOSE IS IT NOT LOUISE

transcript: [' Let us run then said all three and gracefully lifting up the long skirts of their silk dresses they lightly ran across the open space between the lake and the thickest covert of the park']
reference: LET US RUN THEN SAID ALL THREE AND GRACEFULLY LIFTING UP THE LONG SKIRTS OF THEIR SILK DRESSES THEY LIGHTLY RAN ACROSS THE OPEN SPACE BETWEEN THE LAKE AND THE THICKEST COVERT OF THE PARK

transcript: [" In fact, the sound of madame's and the queen's carriages could be heard in the distance upon the hard dry ground of the roads followed by the mountain cavaliers."]
reference: IN FACT THE SOUND OF MADAME'S AND THE QUEEN'S CARRIAGES COULD BE HEARD IN THE DISTANCE UPON THE HARD DRY GROUND OF THE ROADS FOLLOWED BY THE MOUNTED CAVALIERS

transcript: [' In this way the fete of the whole court was a fete also for the mysterious inhabitants of the forest for certainly the deer in the brake the pheasant on the branch the fox in its hole were all listening.']
reference: IN THIS WAY THE FETE OF THE WHOLE COURT WAS A FETE ALSO FOR THE MYSTERIOUS INHABITANTS OF THE FOREST FOR CERTAINLY THE DEER IN THE BRAKE THE PHEASANT ON THE BRANCH THE FOX IN ITS HOLE WERE ALL LISTENING

transcript: [' All about him was a tumult of bright and broken color scattered in broad splashes.']
reference: ALL ABOUT HIM WAS A TUMULT OF BRIGHT AND BROKEN COLOR SCATTERED IN BROAD SPLASHES

transcript: [' The merganser had a crested head of iridescent green black, a broad collar of lustrous white, black back, black and white wings, white belly, sides finely pencilled in black and white, and a breast of rich chestnut red streaked with black.']
reference: THE MERGANSER HAD A CRESTED HEAD OF IRIDESCENT GREEN BLACK A BROAD COLLAR OF LUSTROUS WHITE BLACK BACK BLACK AND WHITE WINGS WHITE BELLY SIDES FINELY PENCILLED IN BLACK AND WHITE AND A BREAST OF RICH CHESTNUT RED STREAKED WITH BLACK

transcript: [' His feet were red, his long narrow beak with its saw toothed edges and sharp hooked tip was bright red.']
reference: HIS FEET WERE RED HIS LONG NARROW BEAK WITH ITS SAW TOOTHED EDGES AND SHARP HOOKED TIP WAS BRIGHT RED

transcript: [' But here he was at a terrible disadvantage as compared with the owls hawks and eagles he had no rending claws.']
reference: BUT HERE HE WAS AT A TERRIBLE DISADVANTAGE AS COMPARED WITH THE OWLS HAWKS AND EAGLES HE HAD NO RENDING CLAWS

transcript: [' But suddenly straight and swift as a diving cormorant he shot down into the torrent and disappeared beneath the surface.']
reference: BUT SUDDENLY STRAIGHT AND SWIFT AS A DIVING CORMORANT HE SHOT DOWN INTO THE TORRENT AND DISAPPEARED BENEATH THE SURFACE

transcript: [' Once fairly a wing, however, he wheeled and made back hurriedly for his perch.']
reference: ONCE FAIRLY A WING HOWEVER HE WHEELED AND MADE BACK HURRIEDLY FOR HIS PERCH

transcript: [' It might have seemed that a trout of this size was a fairly substantial meal.']
reference: IT MIGHT HAVE SEEMED THAT A TROUT OF THIS SIZE WAS A FAIRLY SUBSTANTIAL MEAL

transcript: [' But such was his keenness that even while the wide flukes of his engorged victim were still sticking out at the corners of his beak his fierce red eyes were once more peering downward into the torrent in search of fresh prey.']
reference: BUT SUCH WAS HIS KEENNESS THAT EVEN WHILE THE WIDE FLUKES OF HIS ENGORGED VICTIM WERE STILL STICKING OUT AT THE CORNERS OF HIS BEAK HIS FIERCE RED EYES WERE ONCE MORE PEERING DOWNWARD INTO THE TORRENT IN SEARCH OF FRESH PREY

transcript: [' In despair he hurled himself downward too soon.']
reference: IN DESPAIR HE HURLED HIMSELF DOWNWARD TOO SOON

transcript: [' the great hawk followed hurriedly to retrieve his prey from the ground.']
reference: THE GREAT HAWK FOLLOWED HURRIEDLY TO RETRIEVE HIS PREY FROM THE GROUND

transcript: [' The cat growled softly picked up the prize in her jaws and trotted into the bushes to devour it.']
reference: THE CAT GROWLED SOFTLY PICKED UP THE PRIZE IN HER JAWS AND TROTTED INTO THE BUSHES TO DEVOUR IT

transcript: [" In fact, he had just finished it, the last of the trout's tail had just vanished with a spasm down his strained gullet, when the baffled hawk caught sight of him and swooped."]
reference: IN FACT HE HAD JUST FINISHED IT THE LAST OF THE TROUT'S TAIL HAD JUST VANISHED WITH A SPASM DOWN HIS STRAINED GULLET WHEN THE BAFFLED HAWK CAUGHT SIGHT OF HIM AND SWOOPED

transcript: [' The hawk alighted on the dead branch and sat upright motionless as if surprised.']
reference: THE HAWK ALIGHTED ON THE DEAD BRANCH AND SAT UPRIGHT MOTIONLESS AS IF SURPRISED

transcript: [' Like his unfortunate little cousin the teal, he too had felt the fear of death smitten into his heart and was heading desperately for the refuge of some dark overhanging bank deep fringed with weeds where the dreadful eye of the hawk should not discern him.']
reference: LIKE HIS UNFORTUNATE LITTLE COUSIN THE TEAL HE TOO HAD FELT THE FEAR OF DEATH SMITTEN INTO HIS HEART AND WAS HEADING DESPERATELY FOR THE REFUGE OF SOME DARK OVERHANGING BANK DEEP FRINGED WITH WEEDS WHERE THE DREADFUL EYE OF THE HAWK SHOULD NOT DISCERN HIM

transcript: [' The hawk sat upon the branch and watched his quarry swimming beneath the surface.']
reference: THE HAWK SAT UPON THE BRANCH AND WATCHED HIS QUARRY SWIMMING BENEATH THE SURFACE

transcript: [' Almost instantly he was forced to the top.']
reference: ALMOST INSTANTLY HE WAS FORCED TO THE TOP

transcript: [' straightway the hawk glided from his perch and darted after him.']
reference: STRAIGHTWAY THE HAWK GLIDED FROM HIS PERCH AND DARTED AFTER HIM

transcript: [' But at this point in the rapids it was impossible for him to stay down.']
reference: BUT AT THIS POINT IN THE RAPIDS IT WAS IMPOSSIBLE FOR HIM TO STAY DOWN

transcript: [' But this frequenter of the heights of air for all his savage valor was troubled at the leaping waves and the tossing foam of these mad rapids he did not understand them.']
reference: BUT THIS FREQUENTER OF THE HEIGHTS OF AIR FOR ALL HIS SAVAGE VALOR WAS TROUBLED AT THE LEAPING WAVES AND THE TOSSING FOAM OF THESE MAD RAPIDS HE DID NOT UNDERSTAND THEM

transcript: [" As he flew, his down reaching clutching talons were not half a yard above the fugitive's head."]
reference: AS HE FLEW HIS DOWN REACHING CLUTCHING TALONS WERE NOT HALF A YARD ABOVE THE FUGITIVE'S HEAD

transcript: [' Where the waves for an instant sank they came closer but not quite within grasping reach.']
reference: WHERE THE WAVES FOR AN INSTANT SANK THEY CAME CLOSER BUT NOT QUITE WITHIN GRASPING REACH

transcript: [' But as before the leaping waves of the rapids were too much for his pursuer and he was able to flap his way onward in a cloud of foam while doom hung low above his head yet hesitated to strike.']
reference: BUT AS BEFORE THE LEAPING WAVES OF THE RAPIDS WERE TOO MUCH FOR HIS PURSUER AND HE WAS ABLE TO FLAP HIS WAY ONWARD IN A CLOUD OF FOAM WHILE DOOM HUNG LOW ABOVE HIS HEAD YET HESITATED TO STRIKE

transcript: [' The hawk embittered by the loss of his first quarry had become as dogged in pursuit as a weasel not to be shaken off or evaded or deceived.']
reference: THE HAWK EMBITTERED BY THE LOSS OF HIS FIRST QUARRY HAD BECOME AS DOGGED IN PURSUIT AS A WEASEL NOT TO BE SHAKEN OFF OR EVADED OR DECEIVED

transcript: [' He had a lot of line out and the place was none too free for a long cast, but he was impatient to drop his flies again on the spot where the big fish was feeding.']
reference: HE HAD A LOT OF LINE OUT AND THE PLACE WAS NONE TOO FREE FOR A LONG CAST BUT HE WAS IMPATIENT TO DROP HIS FLIES AGAIN ON THE SPOT WHERE THE BIG FISH WAS FEEDING

transcript: [" The last drop fly as luck would have it caught just in the corner of the hawk's angrily open beak, hooking itself firmly."]
reference: THE LAST DROP FLY AS LUCK WOULD HAVE IT CAUGHT JUST IN THE CORNER OF THE HAWK'S ANGRILY OPEN BEAK HOOKING ITSELF FIRMLY

transcript: [' At the sudden sharp sting of it the great bird turned his head and noticed for the first time the fisherman standing on the bank.']
reference: AT THE SUDDEN SHARP STING OF IT THE GREAT BIRD TURNED HIS HEAD AND NOTICED FOR THE FIRST TIME THE FISHERMAN STANDING ON THE BANK

transcript: [' The drag upon his beak and the light check upon his wings were inexplicable to him and appalling.']
reference: THE DRAG UPON HIS BEAK AND THE LIGHT CHECK UPON HIS WINGS WERE INEXPLICABLE TO HIM AND APPALLING

transcript: [' Then the leader parted from the line']
reference: THEN THE LEADER PARTED FROM THE LINE

transcript: [' He is a welcome figure at the garden parties of the elect who are always ready to encourage him by accepting free seats for his play actor managers nod to him editors allow him to contribute without charge to a symposium on the price of golf balls']
reference: HE IS A WELCOME FIGURE AT THE GARDEN PARTIES OF THE ELECT WHO ARE ALWAYS READY TO ENCOURAGE HIM BY ACCEPTING FREE SEATS FOR HIS PLAY ACTOR MANAGERS NOD TO HIM EDITORS ALLOW HIM TO CONTRIBUTE WITHOUT CHARGE TO A SYMPOSIUM ON THE PRICE OF GOLF BALLS

transcript: [' In short, he becomes a prominent figure in London society, and if he is not careful somebody will say so.']
reference: IN SHORT HE BECOMES A PROMINENT FIGURE IN LONDON SOCIETY AND IF HE IS NOT CAREFUL SOMEBODY WILL SAY SO

transcript: [' But even the unsuccessful dramatist has his moments.']
reference: BUT EVEN THE UNSUCCESSFUL DRAMATIST HAS HIS MOMENTS

transcript: [' Your play must be not merely a good play but a successful one.']
reference: YOUR PLAY MUST BE NOT MERELY A GOOD PLAY BUT A SUCCESSFUL ONE

transcript: [' Frankly, I cannot always say.']
reference: FRANKLY I CANNOT ALWAYS SAY

transcript: [" But suppose you said, I'm fond of writing, my people always say my letters home are good enough for punch."]
reference: BUT SUPPOSE YOU SAID I'M FOND OF WRITING MY PEOPLE ALWAYS SAY MY LETTERS HOME ARE GOOD ENOUGH FOR PUNCH

transcript: [" I've got a little idea for a play about a man and a woman and another woman and, but perhaps I'd better keep the plot a secret for the moment."]
reference: I'VE GOT A LITTLE IDEA FOR A PLAY ABOUT A MAN AND A WOMAN AND ANOTHER WOMAN AND BUT PERHAPS I'D BETTER KEEP THE PLOT A SECRET FOR THE MOMENT

transcript: [" Anyhow, it's jolly exciting and I can do the dialogue all right."]
reference: ANYHOW IT'S JOLLY EXCITING AND I CAN DO THE DIALOGUE ALL RIGHT

transcript: [' lend me your ear for ten minutes and you shall learn just what stage craft is.']
reference: LEND ME YOUR EAR FOR TEN MINUTES AND YOU SHALL LEARN JUST WHAT STAGECRAFT IS

transcript: [' And I should begin with a short homily on soliloquy.']
reference: AND I SHOULD BEGIN WITH A SHORT HOMILY ON SOLILOQUY

transcript: [' him to be or not to be.']
reference: HAM TO BE OR NOT TO BE

transcript: [' Now the object of this soliloquy is plain.']
reference: NOW THE OBJECT OF THIS SOLILOQUY IS PLAIN

transcript: [" Indeed, irresolution being the keynote of Hamlet's soliloquy, a clever player could to some extent indicate the whole thirty lines by a silent working of the jaw. But at the same time, it would be idle to deny that he would miss the finer shades of the dramatist's meaning."]
reference: INDEED IRRESOLUTION BEING THE KEYNOTE OF HAMLET'S SOLILOQUY A CLEVER PLAYER COULD TO SOME EXTENT INDICATE THE WHOLE THIRTY LINES BY A SILENT WORKING OF THE JAW BUT AT THE SAME TIME IT WOULD BE IDLE TO DENY THAT HE WOULD MISS THE FINER SHADES OF THE DRAMATIST'S MEANING

transcript: [' We moderns, however, see the absurdity of it.']
reference: WE MODERNS HOWEVER SEE THE ABSURDITY OF IT

transcript: [' If it be granted first that the thoughts of a certain character should be known to the audience and secondly that soliloquy or the habit of thinking aloud is in opposition to modern stage technique, how shall a soliloquy be avoided without damage to the play?']
reference: IF IT BE GRANTED FIRST THAT THE THOUGHTS OF A CERTAIN CHARACTER SHOULD BE KNOWN TO THE AUDIENCE AND SECONDLY THAT SOLILOQUY OR THE HABIT OF THINKING ALOUD IS IN OPPOSITION TO MODERN STAGE TECHNIQUE HOW SHALL A SOLILOQUY BE AVOIDED WITHOUT DAMAGE TO THE PLAY

transcript: [' And so on till you get to the end when Ophelia might say ah yes or something non committal of that sort.']
reference: AND SO ON TILL YOU GET TO THE END WHEN OPHELIA MIGHT SAY AH YES OR SOMETHING NON COMMITTAL OF THAT SORT

transcript: [' This would be an easy way of doing it, but it would not be the best way for the reason that it is too easy to call attention to itself.']
reference: THIS WOULD BE AN EASY WAY OF DOING IT BUT IT WOULD NOT BE THE BEST WAY FOR THE REASON THAT IT IS TOO EASY TO CALL ATTENTION TO ITSELF

transcript: [' In the old badly made play, it was frequently necessary for one of the characters to take the audience into his confidence.']
reference: IN THE OLD BADLY MADE PLAY IT WAS FREQUENTLY NECESSARY FOR ONE OF THE CHARACTERS TO TAKE THE AUDIENCE INTO HIS CONFIDENCE

transcript: [' In the modern well constructed play, he simply rings up an imaginary confederate and tells him what he is going to do. Could anything be more natural?']
reference: IN THE MODERN WELL CONSTRUCTED PLAY HE SIMPLY RINGS UP AN IMAGINARY CONFEDERATE AND TELLS HIM WHAT HE IS GOING TO DO COULD ANYTHING BE MORE NATURAL

transcript: [' I want double nine. Hello?']
reference: I WANT DOUBLE NINE HAL LO

transcript: [' Double nine two three Elsinore double knot yes hello, is that you horatio? Hamlet speaking.']
reference: DOUBLE NINE TWO THREE ELSINORE DOUBLE NINE YES HALLO IS THAT YOU HORATIO HAMLET SPEAKING

transcript: [" I say I've been wondering about this business."]
reference: I SAY I'VE BEEN WONDERING ABOUT THIS BUSINESS

transcript: [' To be or not to be that is the question whether tis nobler in the mind to suffer the slings and arrows what? No, Hamlet speaking.']
reference: TO BE OR NOT TO BE THAT IS THE QUESTION WHETHER TIS NOBLER IN THE MIND TO SUFFER THE SLINGS AND ARROWS WHAT NO HAMLET SPEAKING

transcript: [' You gave me double five. I want double nine. Hello, is that you horatio? Hamlet speaking.']
reference: YOU GAVE ME DOUBLE FIVE I WANT DOUBLE NINE HALLO IS THAT YOU HORATIO HAMLET SPEAKING

transcript: [" To be or not to be that is the question whether 'tis nobler"]
reference: TO BE OR NOT TO BE THAT IS THE QUESTION WHETHER TIS NOBLER

transcript: [' It is to let Hamlet, if that happen to be the name of your character, enter with a small dog, pet falcon, mongoose, tame bear, or whatever animal is most in keeping with the part, and confide in this animal such sorrows, hopes, or secret history as the audience has got to know.']
reference: IT IS TO LET HAMLET IF THAT HAPPEN TO BE THE NAME OF YOUR CHARACTER ENTER WITH A SMALL DOG PET FALCON MONGOOSE TAME BEAR OR WHATEVER ANIMAL IS MOST IN KEEPING WITH THE PART AND CONFIDE IN THIS ANIMAL SUCH SORROWS HOPES OR SECRET HISTORY AS THE AUDIENCE HAS GOT TO KNOW

transcript: [' Enter Hamlet with his favorite boarhound.']
reference: ENTER HAMLET WITH HIS FAVOURITE BOAR HOUND

transcript: [' Lady Larkspur starts suddenly and turns toward him.']
reference: LADY LARKSPUR STARTS SUDDENLY AND TURNS TOWARDS HIM

transcript: [' Larkspur bit me again this morning for the third time.']
reference: LARKSPUR BIT ME AGAIN THIS MORNING FOR THE THIRD TIME

transcript: [' I want to get away from it all swoon.']
reference: I WANT TO GET AWAY FROM IT ALL SWOONS

transcript: [' Enter Lord Arthur Puffinose.']
reference: ENTER LORD ARTHUR FLUFFINOSE

transcript: [' And there you are. You will of course appreciate that the unfinished sentences not only save time but also make the maneuvering very much more natural.']
reference: AND THERE YOU ARE YOU WILL OF COURSE APPRECIATE THAT THE UNFINISHED SENTENCES NOT ONLY SAVE TIME BUT ALSO MAKE THE MANOEUVRING VERY MUCH MORE NATURAL

transcript: [' How, you may be wondering, are you to begin your masterpiece?']
reference: HOW YOU MAY BE WONDERING ARE YOU TO BEGIN YOUR MASTERPIECE

transcript: [' relapses into silence for the rest of the evening.']
reference: RELAPSES INTO SILENCE FOR THE REST OF THE EVENING

transcript: [' The Duchess of Southbridge to Lord reggie. Oh, reggie, what did you say?']
reference: THE DUCHESS OF SOUTHBRIDGE TO LORD REGGIE OH REGGIE WHAT DID YOU SAY

transcript: [' then, Lord Tupenny, well, what about auction?']
reference: THEN LORD TUPPENY WELL WHAT ABOUT AUCTION

transcript: [' The crowd drifts off leaving the hero and heroine alone in the middle of the stage, and then you can begin.']
reference: THE CROWD DRIFTS OFF LEAVING THE HERO AND HEROINE ALONE IN THE MIDDLE OF THE STAGE AND THEN YOU CAN BEGIN

transcript: [' Then is the time to introduce a meal on the stage.']
reference: THEN IS THE TIME TO INTRODUCE A MEAL ON THE STAGE

transcript: [' A stage meal is popular because it proves to the audience that the actors, even when called Charles Houltree or Owen Nares, are real people just like you and me.']
reference: A STAGE MEAL IS POPULAR BECAUSE IT PROVES TO THE AUDIENCE THAT THE ACTORS EVEN WHEN CALLED CHARLES HAWTREY OR OWEN NARES ARE REAL PEOPLE JUST LIKE YOU AND ME

transcript: [' tea please matthews butler impassively']
reference: TEA PLEASE MATTHEWS BUTLER IMPASSIVELY

transcript: [' Hostess replaces lump and inclines empty teapot over tray for a moment then hands him a cup painted brown inside thus deceiving the gentleman with the telescope in the upper circle.']
reference: HOSTESS REPLACES LUMP AND INCLINES EMPTY TEAPOT OVER TRAY FOR A MOMENT THEN HANDS HIM A CUP PAINTED BROWN INSIDE THUS DECEIVING THE GENTLEMAN WITH THE TELESCOPE IN THE UPPER CIRCLE

transcript: [' Reenter butler and three footmen who remove the tea things. Hostess, two guests.']
reference: RE ENTER BUTLER AND THREE FOOTMEN WHO REMOVE THE TEA THINGS HOSTESS TO GUEST

transcript: [' In novels the hero has often pushed his meals away untasted, but no stage hero would do anything so unnatural as this.']
reference: IN NOVELS THE HERO HAS OFTEN PUSHED HIS MEALS AWAY UNTASTED BUT NO STAGE HERO WOULD DO ANYTHING SO UNNATURAL AS THIS

transcript: [' Two bites are made and the bread is crumbled with an air of great eagerness indeed one feels that in real life the guest would clutch hold of the footman and say half a mile old chap i have it nearly finished but the actor is better schooled than this']
reference: TWO BITES ARE MADE AND THE BREAD IS CRUMBLED WITH AN AIR OF GREAT EAGERNESS INDEED ONE FEELS THAT IN REAL LIFE THE GUEST WOULD CLUTCH HOLD OF THE FOOTMAN AND SAY HALF A MO OLD CHAP I HAVEN'T NEARLY FINISHED BUT THE ACTOR IS BETTER SCHOOLED THAN THIS

transcript: [' But it is the cigarette which chiefly has brought the modern drama to its present state of perfection.']
reference: BUT IT IS THE CIGARETTE WHICH CHIEFLY HAS BROUGHT THE MODERN DRAMA TO ITS PRESENT STATE OF PERFECTION

transcript: [" Lord john taking out Gould's cigarette case from his left hand upper waistcoat pocket"]
reference: LORD JOHN TAKING OUT GOLD CIGARETTE CASE FROM HIS LEFT HAND UPPER WAISTCOAT POCKET

transcript: [' The bogus legislature numbered thirty six members.']
reference: THE BOGUS LEGISLATURE NUMBERED THIRTY SIX MEMBERS

transcript: [' This was at the March election, eighteen fifty five.']
reference: THIS WAS AT THE MARCH ELECTION EIGHTEEN FIFTY FIVE

transcript: [" That summer's emigration, however, being mainly from the free states, greatly changed the relative strengths of the two parties."]
reference: THAT SUMMER'S EMIGRATION HOWEVER BEING MAINLY FROM THE FREE STATES GREATLY CHANGED THE RELATIVE STRENGTH OF THE TWO PARTIES

transcript: [' For general service therefore requiring no special effort the numerical strength of the factions was about equal while on extraordinary occasions the two thousand border ruffian reserve lying a little farther back from the state line could at any time easily turn the scale.']
reference: FOR GENERAL SERVICE THEREFORE REQUIRING NO SPECIAL EFFORT THE NUMERICAL STRENGTH OF THE FACTIONS WAS ABOUT EQUAL WHILE ON EXTRAORDINARY OCCASIONS THE TWO THOUSAND BORDER RUFFIAN RESERVE LYING A LITTLE FARTHER BACK FROM THE STATE LINE COULD AT ANY TIME EASILY TURN THE SCALE

transcript: [' The free state men had only their convictions their intelligence their courage and the moral support of the north the conspiracy had its secret combination, the territorial officials, the legislature, the bogus laws, the courts, the militia officers, the president, and the army.']
reference: THE FREE STATE MEN HAD ONLY THEIR CONVICTIONS THEIR INTELLIGENCE THEIR COURAGE AND THE MORAL SUPPORT OF THE NORTH THE CONSPIRACY HAD ITS SECRET COMBINATION THE TERRITORIAL OFFICIALS THE LEGISLATURE THE BOGUS LAWS THE COURTS THE MILITIA OFFICERS THE PRESIDENT AND THE ARMY

transcript: [' This was a formidable array of advantages slavery was playing with loaded dice.']
reference: THIS WAS A FORMIDABLE ARRAY OF ADVANTAGES SLAVERY WAS PLAYING WITH LOADED DICE

transcript: [' Coming by way of the Missouri River towns, he fell first among border ruffian companionship and influences, and perhaps having his inclinations already molded by his Washington instructions, his early impressions were decidedly adverse to the free state cause.']
reference: COMING BY WAY OF THE MISSOURI RIVER TOWNS HE FELL FIRST AMONG BORDER RUFFIAN COMPANIONSHIP AND INFLUENCES AND PERHAPS HAVING HIS INCLINATIONS ALREADY MOLDED BY HIS WASHINGTON INSTRUCTIONS HIS EARLY IMPRESSIONS WERE DECIDEDLY ADVERSE TO THE FREE STATE CAUSE

transcript: [' His reception speech at Westport in which he maintained the legality of the legislature and his determination to enforce their laws delighted his pro slavery auditors.']
reference: HIS RECEPTION SPEECH AT WESTPORT IN WHICH HE MAINTAINED THE LEGALITY OF THE LEGISLATURE AND HIS DETERMINATION TO ENFORCE THEIR LAWS DELIGHTED HIS PRO SLAVERY AUDITORS

transcript: [' All the territorial dignitaries were present. Governor Shannon presided. John Calhoun, the surveyor general, made the principal speech a denunciation of the abolitionists supporting the Topeka movement. Chief Justice Lecomte dignified the occasion with approving remarks.']
reference: ALL THE TERRITORIAL DIGNITARIES WERE PRESENT GOVERNOR SHANNON PRESIDED JOHN CALHOUN THE SURVEYOR GENERAL MADE THE PRINCIPAL SPEECH A DENUNCIATION OF THE ABOLITIONISTS SUPPORTING THE TOPEKA MOVEMENT CHIEF JUSTICE LECOMPTE DIGNIFIED THE OCCASION WITH APPROVING REMARKS

transcript: [' All dissent, all non compliance, all hesitation, all mere silence even were in their stronghold towns like Leavenworth branded as abolitionism, declared to be hostility to the public welfare, and punished with proscription, personal violence, expulsion, and frequently death.']
reference: ALL DISSENT ALL NON COMPLIANCE ALL HESITATION ALL MERE SILENCE EVEN WERE IN THEIR STRONGHOLD TOWNS LIKE LEAVENWORTH BRANDED AS ABOLITIONISM DECLARED TO BE HOSTILITY TO THE PUBLIC WELFARE AND PUNISHED WITH PROSCRIPTION PERSONAL VIOLENCE EXPULSION AND FREQUENTLY DEATH

transcript: [' Of the lynchings, the mobs, and the murders, it would be impossible, except in a very extended work, to note the frequent and atrocious details']
reference: OF THE LYNCHINGS THE MOBS AND THE MURDERS IT WOULD BE IMPOSSIBLE EXCEPT IN A VERY EXTENDED WORK TO NOTE THE FREQUENT AND ATROCIOUS DETAILS

transcript: [" The present chapters can only touch upon the more salient movements of the civil war in Kansas which happily are not sanguinary. If however the individual and more isolated cases of bloodshed could be described, they would show a startling aggregate of barbarity and loss of life for opinion's sake."]
reference: THE PRESENT CHAPTERS CAN ONLY TOUCH UPON THE MORE SALIENT MOVEMENTS OF THE CIVIL WAR IN KANSAS WHICH HAPPILY WERE NOT SANGUINARY IF HOWEVER THE INDIVIDUAL AND MORE ISOLATED CASES OF BLOODSHED COULD BE DESCRIBED THEY WOULD SHOW A STARTLING AGGREGATE OF BARBARITY AND LOSS OF LIFE FOR OPINION'S SAKE

transcript: [' Several hundred free state men promptly responded to the summons.']
reference: SEVERAL HUNDRED FREE STATE MEN PROMPTLY RESPONDED TO THE SUMMONS

transcript: [' It was in fact the best weapon of its day.']
reference: IT WAS IN FACT THE BEST WEAPON OF ITS DAY

transcript: [' The leaders of the conspiracy became distrustful of their power to crush the town.']
reference: THE LEADERS OF THE CONSPIRACY BECAME DISTRUSTFUL OF THEIR POWER TO CRUSH THE TOWN

transcript: [' One of his militia generals suggested that the governor should require the outlaws at lawrence and elsewhere to surrender the sharps rifles another wrote asking him to call out the government troops at Fort Leavenworth.']
reference: ONE OF HIS MILITIA GENERALS SUGGESTED THAT THE GOVERNOR SHOULD REQUIRE THE OUTLAWS AT LAWRENCE AND ELSEWHERE TO SURRENDER THE SHARPS RIFLES ANOTHER WROTE ASKING HIM TO CALL OUT THE GOVERNMENT TROOPS AT FORT LEAVENWORTH

transcript: [' The governor on his part becoming doubtful of the legality of employing Missouri militia to enforce Kansas laws was also eager to secure the help of federal troops.']
reference: THE GOVERNOR ON HIS PART BECOMING DOUBTFUL OF THE LEGALITY OF EMPLOYING MISSOURI MILITIA TO ENFORCE KANSAS LAWS WAS ALSO EAGER TO SECURE THE HELP OF FEDERAL TROOPS

transcript: [' Sheriff Jones had his pockets always full of writs issued in the spirit of persecution but was often baffled by the sharp wits and ready resources of the free state people and sometimes defied outright']
reference: SHERIFF JONES HAD HIS POCKETS ALWAYS FULL OF WRITS ISSUED IN THE SPIRIT OF PERSECUTION BUT WAS OFTEN BAFFLED BY THE SHARP WITS AND READY RESOURCES OF THE FREE STATE PEOPLE AND SOMETIMES DEFIED OUTRIGHT

transcript: [' Little by little, however, the latter became hemmed and bound in the meshes of the various devices and proceedings which the territorial officials evolved from the bogus laws.']
reference: LITTLE BY LITTLE HOWEVER THE LATTER BECAME HEMMED AND BOUND IN THE MESHES OF THE VARIOUS DEVICES AND PROCEEDINGS WHICH THE TERRITORIAL OFFICIALS EVOLVED FROM THE BOGUS LAWS

transcript: [' To embarrass this damaging exposure, Judge Lecomte issued a writ against the ex governor on a frivolous charge of contempt.']
reference: TO EMBARRASS THIS DAMAGING EXPOSURE JUDGE LECOMPTE ISSUED A WRIT AGAINST THE EX GOVERNOR ON A FRIVOLOUS CHARGE OF CONTEMPT

transcript: [' The incident was not violent nor even dramatic no posse was summoned no further effort made and reader fearing personal violence soon fled in disguise.']
reference: THE INCIDENT WAS NOT VIOLENT NOR EVEN DRAMATIC NO POSSE WAS SUMMONED NO FURTHER EFFORT MADE AND REEDER FEARING PERSONAL VIOLENCE SOON FLED IN DISGUISE

transcript: [' But the affair was magnified as a crowning proof that the free state men were insurrectionists and outlaws.']
reference: BUT THE AFFAIR WAS MAGNIFIED AS A CROWNING PROOF THAT THE FREE STATE MEN WERE INSURRECTIONISTS AND OUTLAWS

transcript: [' From these again sprang barricaded and fortified dwellings camps and scout parties finally culminating in roving guerrilla bands half partisan half predatory.']
reference: FROM THESE AGAIN SPRANG BARRICADED AND FORTIFIED DWELLINGS CAMPS AND SCOUTING PARTIES FINALLY CULMINATING IN ROVING GUERRILLA BANDS HALF PARTISAN HALF PREDATORY

transcript: [' Their distinctive characters, however, display one broad and unfailing difference.']
reference: THEIR DISTINCTIVE CHARACTERS HOWEVER DISPLAY ONE BROAD AND UNFAILING DIFFERENCE

transcript: [' The free state men clung to their prairie towns and prairie ravines with all the obstinacy and courage of true defenders of their homes and firesides.']
reference: THE FREE STATE MEN CLUNG TO THEIR PRAIRIE TOWNS AND PRAIRIE RAVINES WITH ALL THE OBSTINACY AND COURAGE OF TRUE DEFENDERS OF THEIR HOMES AND FIRESIDES

transcript: [' Their assumed character changed with their changing opportunities or necessities.']
reference: THEIR ASSUMED CHARACTER CHANGED WITH THEIR CHANGING OPPORTUNITIES OR NECESSITIES

transcript: [' In the shooting of sheriff Jones in lawrence and in the refusal of ex governor Reeder to allow the deputy marshal to arrest him, they discovered grave offenses against the territorial and united states laws.']
reference: IN THE SHOOTING OF SHERIFF JONES IN LAWRENCE AND IN THE REFUSAL OF EX GOVERNOR BEEDER TO ALLOW THE DEPUTY MARSHAL TO ARREST HIM THEY DISCOVERED GRAVE OFFENSES AGAINST THE TERRITORIAL AND UNITED STATES LAWS

transcript: [' footnote Sumner to Shannon, may twelfth eighteen fifty six']
reference: FOOTNOTE SUMNER TO SHANNON MAY TWELFTH EIGHTEEN FIFTY SIX

transcript: [' Private persons who had leased the Free State Hotel vainly besought the various authorities to prevent the destruction of their property.']
reference: PRIVATE PERSONS WHO HAD LEASED THE FREE STATE HOTEL VAINLY BESOUGHT THE VARIOUS AUTHORITIES TO PREVENT THE DESTRUCTION OF THEIR PROPERTY

transcript: [' Ten days were consumed in these negotiations, but the spirit of vengeance refused to yield.']
reference: TEN DAYS WERE CONSUMED IN THESE NEGOTIATIONS BUT THE SPIRIT OF VENGEANCE REFUSED TO YIELD

transcript: [' He summoned half a dozen citizens to join his posse, who followed obeyed and assisted him.']
reference: HE SUMMONED HALF A DOZEN CITIZENS TO JOIN HIS POSSE WHO FOLLOWED OBEYED AND ASSISTED HIM

transcript: [' He continued his pretended search and to give color to his errand made two arrests.']
reference: HE CONTINUED HIS PRETENDED SEARCH AND TO GIVE COLOR TO HIS ERRAND MADE TWO ARRESTS

transcript: [' The Free State Hotel, a stone building in dimensions fifty by seventy feet, three stories high and handsomely furnished, previously occupied only for lodging rooms, on that day for the first time opened its table accommodations to the public and provided a free dinner in honor of the occasion.']
reference: THE FREE STATE HOTEL A STONE BUILDING IN DIMENSIONS FIFTY BY SEVENTY FEET THREE STORIES HIGH AND HANDSOMELY FURNISHED PREVIOUSLY OCCUPIED ONLY FOR LODGING ROOMS ON THAT DAY FOR THE FIRST TIME OPENED ITS TABLE ACCOMMODATIONS TO THE PUBLIC AND PROVIDED A FREE DINNER IN HONOR OF THE OCCASION

transcript: [' As he had promised to protect the hotel, the reassured citizens began to laugh at their own fears.']
reference: AS HE HAD PROMISED TO PROTECT THE HOTEL THE REASSURED CITIZENS BEGAN TO LAUGH AT THEIR OWN FEARS

transcript: [' To their sorrow they were soon undeceived.']
reference: TO THEIR SORROW THEY WERE SOON UNDECEIVED

transcript: [' The military force partly rabble partly organized had meanwhile moved into the town.']
reference: THE MILITARY FORCE PARTLY RABBLE PARTLY ORGANIZED HAD MEANWHILE MOVED INTO THE TOWN

transcript: [' He planted a company before the hotel and demanded a surrender of the arms belonging to the free state military companies.']
reference: HE PLANTED A COMPANY BEFORE THE HOTEL AND DEMANDED A SURRENDER OF THE ARMS BELONGING TO THE FREE STATE MILITARY COMPANIES

transcript: [" Half an hour later turning a deaf ear to all remonstrance, he gave the proprietors until five o'clock to remove their families and personal property from the free state hotel."]
reference: HALF AN HOUR LATER TURNING A DEAF EAR TO ALL REMONSTRANCE HE GAVE THE PROPRIETORS UNTIL FIVE O'CLOCK TO REMOVE THEIR FAMILIES AND PERSONAL PROPERTY FROM THE FREE STATE HOTEL

transcript: [' Acheson who had been haranguing the mob planted his two guns before the building and trained them upon it.']
reference: ATCHISON WHO HAD BEEN HARANGUING THE MOB PLANTED HIS TWO GUNS BEFORE THE BUILDING AND TRAINED THEM UPON IT

transcript: [' The inmates being removed, at the appointed hour a few cannon balls were fired through the stone walls.']
reference: THE INMATES BEING REMOVED AT THE APPOINTED HOUR A FEW CANNON BALLS WERE FIRED THROUGH THE STONE WALLS

transcript: [' In this incident contrasting the creative and the destructive spirit of the factions, the immigrant aid society of Massachusetts finds its most honorable and triumphant vindication.']
reference: IN THIS INCIDENT CONTRASTING THE CREATIVE AND THE DESTRUCTIVE SPIRIT OF THE FACTIONS THE EMIGRANT AID SOCIETY OF MASSACHUSETTS FINDS ITS MOST HONORABLE AND TRIUMPHANT VINDICATION

transcript: [' The whole proceeding was so childish, the miserable plot so transparent, the outrage so gross, as to bring disgust to the better class of border ruffians who were witnesses and accessories.']
reference: THE WHOLE PROCEEDING WAS SO CHILDISH THE MISERABLE PLOT SO TRANSPARENT THE OUTRAGE SO GROSS AS TO BRING DISGUST TO THE BETTER CLASS OF BORDER RUFFIANS WHO WERE WITNESSES AND ACCESSORIES

transcript: [' Relocated footnote Governor Robinson being on his way east, the steamboat on which he was traveling stopped at lexington, Missouri.']
reference: RELOCATED FOOTNOTE GOVERNOR ROBINSON BEING ON HIS WAY EAST THE STEAMBOAT ON WHICH HE WAS TRAVELING STOPPED AT LEXINGTON MISSOURI

transcript: [' In a few days an officer came with a requisition from governor Shannon and took the prisoner by land at westport and afterwards from there to kansas city and Leavenworth.']
reference: IN A FEW DAYS AN OFFICER CAME WITH A REQUISITION FROM GOVERNOR SHANNON AND TOOK THE PRISONER BY LAND TO WESTPORT AND AFTERWARDS FROM THERE TO KANSAS CITY AND LEAVENWORTH
