transcript: [' Here he was placed in the custody of captain martin of the Kickapoo Rangers who proved a kind jailer and materially assisted in protecting him from the dangerous intentions of the mob which at that time held Leavenworth under the reign of terror.']
reference: HERE HE WAS PLACED IN THE CUSTODY OF CAPTAIN MARTIN OF THE KICKAPOO RANGERS WHO PROVED A KIND JAILER AND MATERIALLY ASSISTED IN PROTECTING HIM FROM THE DANGEROUS INTENTIONS OF THE MOB WHICH AT THAT TIME HELD LEAVENWORTH UNDER A REIGN OF TERROR

transcript: [' Captain Martins said, I shall give you a pistol to help protect yourself if worst comes to worst.']
reference: CAPTAIN MARTIN SAID I SHALL GIVE YOU A PISTOL TO HELP PROTECT YOURSELF IF WORSE COMES TO WORST

transcript: [' In the early morning of the next day may twenty ninth, a company of dragoons with one empty saddle came down from the fort, and while the pro slavery men still slept, the prisoner and his escort were on their way across the prairies to Lecompton in the charge of officers of the United States Army.']
reference: IN THE EARLY MORNING OF THE NEXT DAY MAY TWENTY NINTH A COMPANY OF DRAGOONS WITH ONE EMPTY SADDLE CAME DOWN FROM THE FORT AND WHILE THE PRO SLAVERY MEN STILL SLEPT THE PRISONER AND HIS ESCORT WERE ON THEIR WAY ACROSS THE PRAIRIES TO LECOMPTON IN THE CHARGE OF OFFICERS OF THE UNITED STATES ARMY

transcript: [' Though thrown into prison for this enterprise and detained some time, he was not discouraged, but still continued by his countenance and protection to infuse spirit into the distressed royalists.']
reference: THOUGH THROWN INTO PRISON FOR THIS ENTERPRISE AND DETAINED SOME TIME HE WAS NOT DISCOURAGED BUT STILL CONTINUED BY HIS COUNTENANCE AND PROTECTION TO INFUSE SPIRIT INTO THE DISTRESSED ROYALISTS

transcript: [' Among other persons of distinction who united themselves to him was lord Napier of Merchiston, son of the famous inventor of the logarithms, the person to whom the title of a great man is more justly due than to any other whom his country ever produced.']
reference: AMONG OTHER PERSONS OF DISTINCTION WHO UNITED THEMSELVES TO HIM WAS LORD NAPIER OF MERCHISTON SON OF THE FAMOUS INVENTOR OF THE LOGARITHMS THE PERSON TO WHOM THE TITLE OF A GREAT MAN IS MORE JUSTLY DUE THAN TO ANY OTHER WHOM HIS COUNTRY EVER PRODUCED

transcript: [' While the former foretold that the Scottish covenanters were secretly forming a union with the English parliament, and inculcated the necessity of preventing them by some vigorous undertaking, the latter still insisted that every such attempt would precipitate them into measures to which otherwise they were not perhaps inclined.']
reference: WHILE THE FORMER FORETOLD THAT THE SCOTTISH COVENANTERS WERE SECRETLY FORMING A UNION WITH THE ENGLISH PARLIAMENT AND INCULCATED THE NECESSITY OF PREVENTING THEM BY SOME VIGOROUS UNDERTAKING THE LATTER STILL INSISTED THAT EVERY SUCH ATTEMPT WOULD PRECIPITATE THEM INTO MEASURES TO WHICH OTHERWISE THEY WERE NOT PERHAPS INCLINED

transcript: [" The king's ears were now open to montrose's counsels who proposed none but the boldest and most daring agreeably to the desperate state of the royal cause in Scotland."]
reference: THE KING'S EARS WERE NOW OPEN TO MONTROSE'S COUNSELS WHO PROPOSED NONE BUT THE BOLDEST AND MOST DARING AGREEABLY TO THE DESPERATE STATE OF THE ROYAL CAUSE IN SCOTLAND

transcript: [' Five hundred men more who had been levied by the covenanters were persuaded to embrace the royal cause, and with this combined force he hastened to attack Lord Elcho, who lay at Perth with an army of six thousand men assembled upon the first news of the Irish invasion.']
reference: FIVE HUNDRED MEN MORE WHO HAD BEEN LEVIED BY THE COVENANTERS WERE PERSUADED TO EMBRACE THE ROYAL CAUSE AND WITH THIS COMBINED FORCE HE HASTENED TO ATTACK LORD ELCHO WHO LAY AT PERTH WITH AN ARMY OF SIX THOUSAND MEN ASSEMBLED UPON THE FIRST NEWS OF THE IRISH INVASION

transcript: [' Dreading the superior power of argyle, who having joined his vassals to a force levied by the public, was approaching with a considerable army, Montrose hastened northward in order to rouse again the marquis of Huntly and the Gordons, who having before hastily taken arms had been instantly suppressed by the covenanters.']
reference: DREADING THE SUPERIOR POWER OF ARGYLE WHO HAVING JOINED HIS VASSALS TO A FORCE LEVIED BY THE PUBLIC WAS APPROACHING WITH A CONSIDERABLE ARMY MONTROSE HASTENED NORTHWARDS IN ORDER TO ROUSE AGAIN THE MARQUIS OF HUNTLEY AND THE GORDONS WHO HAVING BEFORE HASTILY TAKEN ARMS HAD BEEN INSTANTLY SUPPRESSED BY THE COVENANTERS

transcript: [" This nobleman's character though celebrated for political courage and conduct was very low for military prowess and after some skirmishes in which he was worsted he here allowed montrose to escape him."]
reference: THIS NOBLEMAN'S CHARACTER THOUGH CELEBRATED FOR POLITICAL COURAGE AND CONDUCT WAS VERY LOW FOR MILITARY PROWESS AND AFTER SOME SKIRMISHES IN WHICH HE WAS WORSTED HE HERE ALLOWED MONTROSE TO ESCAPE HIM

transcript: [' By quick marches through these inaccessible mountains that general freed himself from the superior forces of the covenanters.']
reference: BY QUICK MARCHES THROUGH THESE INACCESSIBLE MOUNTAINS THAT GENERAL FREED HIMSELF FROM THE SUPERIOR FORCES OF THE COVENANTERS

transcript: [" With these and some reenforcements of the athole men and macdonalds whom he had recalled, Montrose fell suddenly upon argyle's country and let loose upon it all the rage of war, carrying off the cattle, burning the houses, and putting the inhabitants to the sword."]
reference: WITH THESE AND SOME REENFORCEMENTS OF THE ATHOLEMEN AND MACDONALDS WHOM HE HAD RECALLED MONTROSE FELL SUDDENLY UPON ARGYLE'S COUNTRY AND LET LOOSE UPON IT ALL THE RAGE OF WAR CARRYING OFF THE CATTLE BURNING THE HOUSES AND PUTTING THE INHABITANTS TO THE SWORD

transcript: [' This severity by which Montrose sullied his victories was the result of private animosity against the chieftain as much as of zeal for the public cause Argyle collecting three thousand men marched in quest of the enemy who had retired with their plunder and he lay at Innerlochy supposing himself still at a considerable distance from them.']
reference: THIS SEVERITY BY WHICH MONTROSE SULLIED HIS VICTORIES WAS THE RESULT OF PRIVATE ANIMOSITY AGAINST THE CHIEFTAIN AS MUCH AS OF ZEAL FOR THE PUBLIC CAUSE ARGYLE COLLECTING THREE THOUSAND MEN MARCHED IN QUEST OF THE ENEMY WHO HAD RETIRED WITH THEIR PLUNDER AND HE LAY AT INNERLOCHY SUPPOSING HIMSELF STILL AT A CONSIDERABLE DISTANCE FROM THEM

transcript: [' By a quick and unexpected march Montrose hastened to innerlochy and presented himself in order of battle before the surprised but not affrighted covenanters.']
reference: BY A QUICK AND UNEXPECTED MARCH MONTROSE HASTENED TO INNERLOCHY AND PRESENTED HIMSELF IN ORDER OF BATTLE BEFORE THE SURPRISED BUT NOT AFFRIGHTENED COVENANTERS

transcript: [' His conduct and presence of mind in this emergence appeared conspicuous.']
reference: HIS CONDUCT AND PRESENCE OF MIND IN THIS EMERGENCE APPEARED CONSPICUOUS

transcript: [" Montrose weak in cavalry here lined his troops of horse with infantry and after putting the enemy's horse to rout fell with united force upon their foot who were entirely cut in pieces though with the loss of the gallant lord gordon on the part of the royalists"]
reference: MONTROSE WEAK IN CAVALRY HERE LINED HIS TROOPS OF HORSE WITH INFANTRY AND AFTER PUTTING THE ENEMY'S HORSE TO ROUT FELL WITH UNITED FORCE UPON THEIR FOOT WHO WERE ENTIRELY CUT IN PIECES THOUGH WITH THE LOSS OF THE GALLANT LORD GORDON ON THE PART OF THE ROYALISTS

transcript: [' From the same men new regiments and new companies were formed different officers appointed and the whole military force put into such hands as the independents could rely on.']
reference: FROM THE SAME MEN NEW REGIMENTS AND NEW COMPANIES WERE FORMED DIFFERENT OFFICERS APPOINTED AND THE WHOLE MILITARY FORCE PUT INTO SUCH HANDS AS THE INDEPENDENTS COULD RELY ON

transcript: [' Besides members of parliament who were excluded many officers unwilling to serve under the new generals threw up their commissions and unwarily facilitated the project of putting the army entirely into the hands of that faction.']
reference: BESIDES MEMBERS OF PARLIAMENT WHO WERE EXCLUDED MANY OFFICERS UNWILLING TO SERVE UNDER THE NEW GENERALS THREW UP THEIR COMMISSIONS AND UNWARILY FACILITATED THE PROJECT OF PUTTING THE ARMY ENTIRELY INTO THE HANDS OF THAT FACTION

transcript: [' Though the discipline of the former parliamentary army was not contemptible a more exact plan was introduced and rigorously executed by these new commanders.']
reference: THOUGH THE DISCIPLINE OF THE FORMER PARLIAMENTARY ARMY WAS NOT CONTEMPTIBLE A MORE EXACT PLAN WAS INTRODUCED AND RIGOROUSLY EXECUTED BY THESE NEW COMMANDERS

transcript: [' Valor indeed was very generally diffused over the one party as well as the other. During this period discipline also was attained by the forces of the parliament, but the perfection of the military art in concerting the general plans of action and the operations of the field seems still on both sides to have been in a great measure wanting.']
reference: VALOR INDEED WAS VERY GENERALLY DIFFUSED OVER THE ONE PARTY AS WELL AS THE OTHER DURING THIS PERIOD DISCIPLINE ALSO WAS ATTAINED BY THE FORCES OF THE PARLIAMENT BUT THE PERFECTION OF THE MILITARY ART IN CONCERTING THE GENERAL PLANS OF ACTION AND THE OPERATIONS OF THE FIELD SEEMS STILL ON BOTH SIDES TO HAVE BEEN IN A GREAT MEASURE WANTING

transcript: [' Historians at least, perhaps from their own ignorance and inexperience, have not remarked any thing but a headlong impetuous conduct each party hurrying to a battle where valor and fortune chiefly determined the success.']
reference: HISTORIANS AT LEAST PERHAPS FROM THEIR OWN IGNORANCE AND INEXPERIENCE HAVE NOT REMARKED ANY THING BUT A HEADLONG IMPETUOUS CONDUCT EACH PARTY HURRYING TO A BATTLE WHERE VALOR AND FORTUNE CHIEFLY DETERMINED THE SUCCESS

transcript: [' He passed through Henley, saint Albans, and came so near to London as Harrow on the Hill.']
reference: HE PASSED THROUGH HENLEY SAINT ALBANS AND CAME SO NEAR TO LONDON AS HARROW ON THE HILL

transcript: [' The Scottish generals and commissioners affected great surprise on the appearance of the king and though they paid him all the exterior respect due to his dignity they instantly set a guard upon him under color of protection and made him in reality a prisoner.']
reference: THE SCOTTISH GENERALS AND COMMISSIONERS AFFECTED GREAT SURPRISE ON THE APPEARANCE OF THE KING AND THOUGH THEY PAID HIM ALL THE EXTERIOR RESPECT DUE TO HIS DIGNITY THEY INSTANTLY SET A GUARD UPON HIM UNDER COLOR OF PROTECTION AND MADE HIM IN REALITY A PRISONER

transcript: [' They informed the English parliament of this unexpected incident and assured them that they had entered into no private treaty with the king.']
reference: THEY INFORMED THE ENGLISH PARLIAMENT OF THIS UNEXPECTED INCIDENT AND ASSURED THEM THAT THEY HAD ENTERED INTO NO PRIVATE TREATY WITH THE KING

transcript: [' Or hath he given us any gift?']
reference: OR HATH HE GIVEN US ANY GIFT

transcript: [' And the men of Israel answered the men of Judah and said, We have ten parts in the king, and we have also more right in David than ye. Why then did ye despise us that our advice should not be first had in bringing back our king?']
reference: AND THE MEN OF ISRAEL ANSWERED THE MEN OF JUDAH AND SAID WE HAVE TEN PARTS IN THE KING AND WE HAVE ALSO MORE RIGHT IN DAVID THAN YE WHY THEN DID YE DESPISE US THAT OUR ADVICE SHOULD NOT BE FIRST HAD IN BRINGING BACK OUR KING

transcript: [' Another preacher after reproaching him to his face with his misgovernment ordered this psalm to be sung.']
reference: ANOTHER PREACHER AFTER REPROACHING HIM TO HIS FACE WITH HIS MISGOVERNMENT ORDERED THIS PSALM TO BE SUNG

transcript: [' The king stood up and called for that psalm which begins with these words.']
reference: THE KING STOOD UP AND CALLED FOR THAT PSALM WHICH BEGINS WITH THESE WORDS

transcript: [' Have mercy, Lord, on me I pray, for men would me devour.']
reference: HAVE MERCY LORD ON ME I PRAY FOR MEN WOULD ME DEVOUR

transcript: [' The good natured audience in pity to fallen majesty showed for once greater deference to the king than to the minister and sung the psalm which the former had called for.']
reference: THE GOOD NATURED AUDIENCE IN PITY TO FALLEN MAJESTY SHOWED FOR ONCE GREATER DEFERENCE TO THE KING THAN TO THE MINISTER AND SUNG THE PSALM WHICH THE FORMER HAD CALLED FOR

transcript: [' The parliament and the scots laid their proposals before the king.']
reference: THE PARLIAMENT AND THE SCOTS LAID THEIR PROPOSALS BEFORE THE KING

transcript: [' Before the settlement of terms the administration must be possessed entirely by the parliaments of both kingdoms and how incompatible that scheme with the liberty of the king is easily imagined.']
reference: BEFORE THE SETTLEMENT OF TERMS THE ADMINISTRATION MUST BE POSSESSED ENTIRELY BY THE PARLIAMENTS OF BOTH KINGDOMS AND HOW INCOMPATIBLE THAT SCHEME WITH THE LIBERTY OF THE KING IS EASILY IMAGINED

transcript: [' The English, it is evident, had they not been previously assured of receiving the king, would never have parted with so considerable a sum, and while they weakened themselves by the same measure have strengthened a people with whom they must afterwards have so material an interest to discuss.']
reference: THE ENGLISH IT IS EVIDENT HAD THEY NOT BEEN PREVIOUSLY ASSURED OF RECEIVING THE KING WOULD NEVER HAVE PARTED WITH SO CONSIDERABLE A SUM AND WHILE THEY WEAKENED THEMSELVES BY THE SAME MEASURE HAVE STRENGTHENED A PEOPLE WITH WHOM THEY MUST AFTERWARDS HAVE SO MATERIAL AN INTEREST TO DISCUSS

transcript: [' If any still retained rancor against him in his present condition they passed in silence while his well-wishers more generous than prudent accompanied his march with tears with acclamations and with prayers for his safety.']
reference: IF ANY STILL RETAINED RANCOR AGAINST HIM IN HIS PRESENT CONDITION THEY PASSED IN SILENCE WHILE HIS WELL WISHERS MORE GENEROUS THAN PRUDENT ACCOMPANIED HIS MARCH WITH TEARS WITH ACCLAMATIONS AND WITH PRAYERS FOR HIS SAFETY

transcript: [' His death in this conjuncture was a public misfortune.']
reference: HIS DEATH IN THIS CONJUNCTURE WAS A PUBLIC MISFORTUNE

transcript: [' The analysis of knowledge will occupy us until the end of the thirteenth lecture and is the most difficult part of our whole enterprise.']
reference: THE ANALYSIS OF KNOWLEDGE WILL OCCUPY US UNTIL THE END OF THE THIRTEENTH LECTURE AND IS THE MOST DIFFICULT PART OF OUR WHOLE ENTERPRISE

transcript: [' What is called perception differs from sensation by the fact that the sensational ingredients bring up habitual associates, images and expectations of their usual correlates, all of which are subjectively indistinguishable from the sensation.']
reference: WHAT IS CALLED PERCEPTION DIFFERS FROM SENSATION BY THE FACT THAT THE SENSATIONAL INGREDIENTS BRING UP HABITUAL ASSOCIATES IMAGES AND EXPECTATIONS OF THEIR USUAL CORRELATES ALL OF WHICH ARE SUBJECTIVELY INDISTINGUISHABLE FROM THE SENSATION

transcript: [' Whether or not this principle is liable to exceptions everyone would agree that it has a broad measure of truth though the word exactly might seem an overstatement and it might seem more correct to say that ideas approximately represent impressions.']
reference: WHETHER OR NOT THIS PRINCIPLE IS LIABLE TO EXCEPTIONS EVERYONE WOULD AGREE THAT IS HAS A BROAD MEASURE OF TRUTH THOUGH THE WORD EXACTLY MIGHT SEEM AN OVERSTATEMENT AND IT MIGHT SEEM MORE CORRECT TO SAY THAT IDEAS APPROXIMATELY REPRESENT IMPRESSIONS

transcript: [' And what sort of evidence is logically possible?']
reference: AND WHAT SORT OF EVIDENCE IS LOGICALLY POSSIBLE

transcript: [' There is no logical impossibility in the hypothesis that the world sprang into being five minutes ago exactly as it then was with a population that remembered a wholly unreal past.']
reference: THERE IS NO LOGICAL IMPOSSIBILITY IN THE HYPOTHESIS THAT THE WORLD SPRANG INTO BEING FIVE MINUTES AGO EXACTLY AS IT THEN WAS WITH A POPULATION THAT REMEMBERED A WHOLLY UNREAL PAST

transcript: [' All that I am doing is to use its logical tenability as a help in the analysis of what occurs when we remember.']
reference: ALL THAT I AM DOING IS TO USE ITS LOGICAL TENABILITY AS A HELP IN THE ANALYSIS OF WHAT OCCURS WHEN WE REMEMBER

transcript: [' The behaviorist who attempts to make psychology a record of behavior has to trust his memory in making the record.']
reference: THE BEHAVIOURIST WHO ATTEMPTS TO MAKE PSYCHOLOGY A RECORD OF BEHAVIOUR HAS TO TRUST HIS MEMORY IN MAKING THE RECORD

transcript: [' Habit is a concept involving the occurrence of similar events at different times. If the behaviorist feels confident that there is such a phenomenon as habit, that can only be because he trusts his memory when it assures him that there have been other times.']
reference: HABIT IS A CONCEPT INVOLVING THE OCCURRENCE OF SIMILAR EVENTS AT DIFFERENT TIMES IF THE BEHAVIOURIST FEELS CONFIDENT THAT THERE IS SUCH A PHENOMENON AS HABIT THAT CAN ONLY BE BECAUSE HE TRUSTS HIS MEMORY WHEN IT ASSURES HIM THAT THERE HAVE BEEN OTHER TIMES

transcript: [' But I do not think such an inference is warranted.']
reference: BUT I DO NOT THINK SUCH AN INFERENCE IS WARRANTED

transcript: [' Our confidence or lack of confidence in the accuracy of a memory image must in fundamental cases be based upon a characteristic of the image itself since we cannot evoke the past bodily and compare it with the present image.']
reference: OUR CONFIDENCE OR LACK OF CONFIDENCE IN THE ACCURACY OF A MEMORY IMAGE MUST IN FUNDAMENTAL CASES BE BASED UPON A CHARACTERISTIC OF THE IMAGE ITSELF SINCE WE CANNOT EVOKE THE PAST BODILY AND COMPARE IT WITH THE PRESENT IMAGE

transcript: [" We sometimes have images that are by no means peculiarly vague which yet we do not trust for example under the influence of fatigue we may see a friend's face vividly and clearly but horribly distorted"]
reference: WE SOMETIMES HAVE IMAGES THAT ARE BY NO MEANS PECULIARLY VAGUE WHICH YET WE DO NOT TRUST FOR EXAMPLE UNDER THE INFLUENCE OF FATIGUE WE MAY SEE A FRIEND'S FACE VIVIDLY AND CLEARLY BUT HORRIBLY DISTORTED

transcript: [' Some images, like some sensations, feel very familiar, while others feel strange.']
reference: SOME IMAGES LIKE SOME SENSATIONS FEEL VERY FAMILIAR WHILE OTHERS FEEL STRANGE

transcript: [' Familiarity is a feeling capable of degrees.']
reference: FAMILIARITY IS A FEELING CAPABLE OF DEGREES

transcript: [' In an image of a well known face, for example, some parts may feel more familiar than others. When this happens, we have more belief in the accuracy of the familiar parts than in that of the unfamiliar parts.']
reference: IN AN IMAGE OF A WELL KNOWN FACE FOR EXAMPLE SOME PARTS MAY FEEL MORE FAMILIAR THAN OTHERS WHEN THIS HAPPENS WE HAVE MORE BELIEF IN THE ACCURACY OF THE FAMILIAR PARTS THAN IN THAT OF THE UNFAMILIAR PARTS

transcript: [' I come now to the other characteristic which memory images must have in order to account for our knowledge of the past.']
reference: I COME NOW TO THE OTHER CHARACTERISTIC WHICH MEMORY IMAGES MUST HAVE IN ORDER TO ACCOUNT FOR OUR KNOWLEDGE OF THE PAST

transcript: [' They must have some characteristic which makes us regard them as referring to more or less remote portions of the past.']
reference: THEY MUST HAVE SOME CHARACTERISTIC WHICH MAKES US REGARD THEM AS REFERRING TO MORE OR LESS REMOTE PORTIONS OF THE PAST

transcript: [' In actual fact there are doubtless various factors that concur in giving us the feeling of greater or less remoteness in some remembered event.']
reference: IN ACTUAL FACT THERE ARE DOUBTLESS VARIOUS FACTORS THAT CONCUR IN GIVING US THE FEELING OF GREATER OR LESS REMOTENESS IN SOME REMEMBERED EVENT

transcript: [' There may be a specific feeling which could be called the feeling of pastness, especially where immediate memory is concerned.']
reference: THERE MAY BE A SPECIFIC FEELING WHICH COULD BE CALLED THE FEELING OF PASTNESS ESPECIALLY WHERE IMMEDIATE MEMORY IS CONCERNED

transcript: [' There is of course a difference between knowing the temporal relation of a remembered event to the present and knowing the time order of two remembered events.']
reference: THERE IS OF COURSE A DIFFERENCE BETWEEN KNOWING THE TEMPORAL RELATION OF A REMEMBERED EVENT TO THE PRESENT AND KNOWING THE TIME ORDER OF TWO REMEMBERED EVENTS

transcript: [' It would seem that only rather recent events can be placed at all accurately by means of feelings giving their temporal relation to the present, but it is clear that such feelings must play an essential part in the process of dating remembered events.']
reference: IT WOULD SEEM THAT ONLY RATHER RECENT EVENTS CAN BE PLACED AT ALL ACCURATELY BY MEANS OF FEELINGS GIVING THEIR TEMPORAL RELATION TO THE PRESENT BUT IT IS CLEAR THAT SUCH FEELINGS MUST PLAY AN ESSENTIAL PART IN THE PROCESS OF DATING REMEMBERED EVENTS

transcript: [' If we had retained the subject or act in knowledge, the whole problem of memory would have been comparatively simple.']
reference: IF WE HAD RETAINED THE SUBJECT OR ACT IN KNOWLEDGE THE WHOLE PROBLEM OF MEMORY WOULD HAVE BEEN COMPARATIVELY SIMPLE

transcript: [' Remembering has to be a present occurrence in some way resembling or related to what is remembered.']
reference: REMEMBERING HAS TO BE A PRESENT OCCURRENCE IN SOME WAY RESEMBLING OR RELATED TO WHAT IS REMEMBERED

transcript: [' Some points may be taken as fixed and such as any theory of memory must arrive at.']
reference: SOME POINTS MAY BE TAKEN AS FIXED AND SUCH AS ANY THEORY OF MEMORY MUST ARRIVE AT

transcript: [' In this case, as in most others, what may be taken as certain in advance is rather vague.']
reference: IN THIS CASE AS IN MOST OTHERS WHAT MAY BE TAKEN AS CERTAIN IN ADVANCE IS RATHER VAGUE

transcript: [' The first of our vague but indubitable data is that there is knowledge of the past.']
reference: THE FIRST OF OUR VAGUE BUT INDUBITABLE DATA IS THAT THERE IS KNOWLEDGE OF THE PAST

transcript: [' We might provisionally though perhaps not quite correctly define memory as that way of knowing about the past which has no analogue in our knowledge of the future. Such a definition would at least serve to mark the problem with which we are concerned, though some expectations may deserve to rank with memory as regards immediacy.']
reference: WE MIGHT PROVISIONALLY THOUGH PERHAPS NOT QUITE CORRECTLY DEFINE MEMORY AS THAT WAY OF KNOWING ABOUT THE PAST WHICH HAS NO ANALOGUE IN OUR KNOWLEDGE OF THE FUTURE SUCH A DEFINITION WOULD AT LEAST SERVE TO MARK THE PROBLEM WITH WHICH WE ARE CONCERNED THOUGH SOME EXPECTATIONS MAY DESERVE TO RANK WITH MEMORY AS REGARDS IMMEDIACY

transcript: [' This distinction is vital to the understanding of memory, but it is not so easy to carry out in practice as it is to draw in theory.']
reference: THIS DISTINCTION IS VITAL TO THE UNDERSTANDING OF MEMORY BUT IT IS NOT SO EASY TO CARRY OUT IN PRACTICE AS IT IS TO DRAW IN THEORY

transcript: [' A gramophone by the help of suitable records might relate to us the incidents of its past and people are not so different from gramophones as they like to believe.']
reference: A GRAMOPHONE BY THE HELP OF SUITABLE RECORDS MIGHT RELATE TO US THE INCIDENTS OF ITS PAST AND PEOPLE ARE NOT SO DIFFERENT FROM GRAMOPHONES AS THEY LIKE TO BELIEVE

transcript: [' I can set to work now to remember things I never remembered before, such as what I had to eat for breakfast this morning, and it can hardly be wholly habit that enables me to do this.']
reference: I CAN SET TO WORK NOW TO REMEMBER THINGS I NEVER REMEMBERED BEFORE SUCH AS WHAT I HAD TO EAT FOR BREAKFAST THIS MORNING AND IT CAN HARDLY BE WHOLLY HABIT THAT ENABLES ME TO DO THIS

transcript: [' The fact that a man can recite a poem does not show that he remembers any previous occasion on which he has recited or read it.']
reference: THE FACT THAT A MAN CAN RECITE A POEM DOES NOT SHOW THAT HE REMEMBERS ANY PREVIOUS OCCASION ON WHICH HE HAS RECITED OR READ IT

transcript: [" Simmons' two books, mentioned in an earlier lecture, do not touch knowledge memory at all closely."]
reference: SEMON'S TWO BOOKS MENTIONED IN AN EARLIER LECTURE DO NOT TOUCH KNOWLEDGE MEMORY AT ALL CLOSELY

transcript: [' They give laws according to which images of past occurrences come into our minds but do not discuss our belief that these images refer to past occurrences, which is what constitutes knowledge memory.']
reference: THEY GIVE LAWS ACCORDING TO WHICH IMAGES OF PAST OCCURRENCES COME INTO OUR MINDS BUT DO NOT DISCUSS OUR BELIEF THAT THESE IMAGES REFER TO PAST OCCURRENCES WHICH IS WHAT CONSTITUTES KNOWLEDGE MEMORY

transcript: [' It is this that is of interest to theory of knowledge.']
reference: IT IS THIS THAT IS OF INTEREST TO THEORY OF KNOWLEDGE

transcript: [' It is by no means always reliable almost everybody has at some time experienced the well known illusion that all that is happening now happened before at some time.']
reference: IT IS BY NO MEANS ALWAYS RELIABLE ALMOST EVERYBODY HAS AT SOME TIME EXPERIENCED THE WELL KNOWN ILLUSION THAT ALL THAT IS HAPPENING NOW HAPPENED BEFORE AT SOME TIME

transcript: [' Whenever the sense of familiarity occurs without a definite object, it leads us to search the environment until we are satisfied that we have found the appropriate object, which leads us to the judgment this is familiar.']
reference: WHENEVER THE SENSE OF FAMILIARITY OCCURS WITHOUT A DEFINITE OBJECT IT LEADS US TO SEARCH THE ENVIRONMENT UNTIL WE ARE SATISFIED THAT WE HAVE FOUND THE APPROPRIATE OBJECT WHICH LEADS US TO THE JUDGMENT THIS IS FAMILIAR

transcript: [' Thus no knowledge as to the past is to be derived from the feeling of familiarity alone.']
reference: THUS NO KNOWLEDGE AS TO THE PAST IS TO BE DERIVED FROM THE FEELING OF FAMILIARITY ALONE

transcript: [' A further stage is recognition.']
reference: A FURTHER STAGE IS RECOGNITION

transcript: [' Recognition in this sense does not necessarily involve more than a habit of association. The kind of object we are seeing at the moment is associated with the word cat or with an auditory image of purring or whatever other characteristic we may happen to recognize in the cat of the moment.']
reference: RECOGNITION IN THIS SENSE DOES NOT NECESSARILY INVOLVE MORE THAN A HABIT OF ASSOCIATION THE KIND OF OBJECT WE ARE SEEING AT THE MOMENT IS ASSOCIATED WITH THE WORD CAT OR WITH AN AUDITORY IMAGE OF PURRING OR WHATEVER OTHER CHARACTERISTIC WE MAY HAPPEN TO RECOGNIZE IN THE CAT OF THE MOMENT

transcript: [' We are of course in fact able to judge when we recognize an object that we have seen it before, but this judgment is something over and above recognition in this first sense and may very probably be impossible to animals that nevertheless have the experience of recognition in this first sense of the word.']
reference: WE ARE OF COURSE IN FACT ABLE TO JUDGE WHEN WE RECOGNIZE AN OBJECT THAT WE HAVE SEEN IT BEFORE BUT THIS JUDGMENT IS SOMETHING OVER AND ABOVE RECOGNITION IN THIS FIRST SENSE AND MAY VERY PROBABLY BE IMPOSSIBLE TO ANIMALS THAT NEVERTHELESS HAVE THE EXPERIENCE OF RECOGNITION IN THIS FIRST SENSE OF THE WORD

transcript: [' This knowledge is memory in one sense though in another it is not.']
reference: THIS KNOWLEDGE IS MEMORY IN ONE SENSE THOUGH IN ANOTHER IT IS NOT

transcript: [' There are, however, several points in which such an account of recognition is inadequate to begin with, it might seem at first sight more correct to define recognition as I have seen this before than as this has existed before.']
reference: THERE ARE HOWEVER SEVERAL POINTS IN WHICH SUCH AN ACCOUNT OF RECOGNITION IS INADEQUATE TO BEGIN WITH IT MIGHT SEEM AT FIRST SIGHT MORE CORRECT TO DEFINE RECOGNITION AS I HAVE SEEN THIS BEFORE THAN AS THIS HAS EXISTED BEFORE

transcript: [' The definition of my experience is difficult broadly speaking it is everything that is connected with what I am experiencing now by certain links of which the various forms of memory are among the most important.']
reference: THE DEFINITION OF MY EXPERIENCE IS DIFFICULT BROADLY SPEAKING IT IS EVERYTHING THAT IS CONNECTED WITH WHAT I AM EXPERIENCING NOW BY CERTAIN LINKS OF WHICH THE VARIOUS FORMS OF MEMORY ARE AMONG THE MOST IMPORTANT

transcript: [' Thus if I recognize a thing, the occasion of its previous existence in virtue of which I recognize it forms part of my experience by definition. Recognition will be one of the marks by which my experience is singled out from the rest of the world.']
reference: THUS IF I RECOGNIZE A THING THE OCCASION OF ITS PREVIOUS EXISTENCE IN VIRTUE OF WHICH I RECOGNIZE IT FORMS PART OF MY EXPERIENCE BY DEFINITION RECOGNITION WILL BE ONE OF THE MARKS BY WHICH MY EXPERIENCE IS SINGLED OUT FROM THE REST OF THE WORLD

transcript: [' Of course, the words this has existed before are a very inadequate translation of what actually happens when we form a judgment of recognition, but that is unavoidable. Words are framed to express a level of thought which is by no means primitive, and are quite incapable of expressing such an elementary occurrence as recognition.']
reference: OF COURSE THE WORDS THIS HAS EXISTED BEFORE ARE A VERY INADEQUATE TRANSLATION OF WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENS WHEN WE FORM A JUDGMENT OF RECOGNITION BUT THAT IS UNAVOIDABLE WORDS ARE FRAMED TO EXPRESS A LEVEL OF THOUGHT WHICH IS BY NO MEANS PRIMITIVE AND ARE QUITE INCAPABLE OF EXPRESSING SUCH AN ELEMENTARY OCCURRENCE AS RECOGNITION

transcript: [' I remained there alone for many hours, but I must acknowledge that before I left the chambers I had gradually brought myself to look at the matter in another light.']
reference: I REMAINED THERE ALONE FOR MANY HOURS BUT I MUST ACKNOWLEDGE THAT BEFORE I LEFT THE CHAMBERS I HAD GRADUALLY BROUGHT MYSELF TO LOOK AT THE MATTER IN ANOTHER LIGHT

transcript: [' Had Eva Cressweller not been good looking? Had Jack been still at college? Had Sir Kennington Oval remained in England? Had mister Bunnett and the barkeeper not succeeded in stopping my carriage on the hill? Should I have succeeded in arranging for the final departure of my old friend?']
reference: HAD EVA CRASWELLER NOT BEEN GOOD LOOKING HAD JACK BEEN STILL AT COLLEGE HAD SIR KENNINGTON OVAL REMAINED IN ENGLAND HAD MISTER BUNNIT AND THE BAR KEEPER NOT SUCCEEDED IN STOPPING MY CARRIAGE ON THE HILL SHOULD I HAVE SUCCEEDED IN ARRANGING FOR THE FINAL DEPARTURE OF MY OLD FRIEND

transcript: [' On arriving at home at my own residence, I found that our salon was filled with a brilliant company.']
reference: ON ARRIVING AT HOME AT MY OWN RESIDENCE I FOUND THAT OUR SALON WAS FILLED WITH A BRILLIANT COMPANY

transcript: [' As I spoke, I made him a gracious bow, and I think I showed him by my mode of address that I did not bear any grudge as to my individual self.']
reference: AS I SPOKE I MADE HIM A GRACIOUS BOW AND I THINK I SHOWED HIM BY MY MODE OF ADDRESS THAT I DID NOT BEAR ANY GRUDGE AS TO MY INDIVIDUAL SELF

transcript: [' I have come to your shores, mister president, with the purpose of seeing how things are progressing in this distant quarter of the world.']
reference: I HAVE COME TO YOUR SHORES MISTER PRESIDENT WITH THE PURPOSE OF SEEING HOW THINGS ARE PROGRESSING IN THIS DISTANT QUARTER OF THE WORLD

transcript: [' We have our little struggles here as elsewhere, and all things cannot be done by rose water.']
reference: WE HAVE OUR LITTLE STRUGGLES HERE AS ELSEWHERE AND ALL THINGS CANNOT BE DONE BY ROSE WATER

transcript: [' We are quite satisfied now, Captain Battleaxe, said my wife.']
reference: WE ARE QUITE SATISFIED NOW CAPTAIN BATTLEAX SAID MY WIFE

transcript: [' Quite satisfied, said Eva.']
reference: QUITE SATISFIED SAID EVA

transcript: [' The ladies in compliance with that softness of heart which is their characteristic are on one side and the men by whom the world has to be managed are on the other.']
reference: THE LADIES IN COMPLIANCE WITH THAT SOFTNESS OF HEART WHICH IS THEIR CHARACTERISTIC ARE ON ONE SIDE AND THE MEN BY WHOM THE WORLD HAS TO BE MANAGED ARE ON THE OTHER

transcript: [' No doubt, in process of time, the ladies will follow.']
reference: NO DOUBT IN PROCESS OF TIME THE LADIES WILL FOLLOW

transcript: [" They're masters, said missus Neverbend."]
reference: THEIR MASTERS SAID MISSUS NEVERBEND

transcript: [' I did not mean, said Captain Battleaxe, to touch upon public subjects at such a moment as this.']
reference: I DID NOT MEAN SAID CAPTAIN BATTLEAX TO TOUCH UPON PUBLIC SUBJECTS AT SUCH A MOMENT AS THIS

transcript: [' Missus Neverbend, you must indeed be proud of your son.']
reference: MISSUS NEVERBEND YOU MUST INDEED BE PROUD OF YOUR SON

transcript: [' Jack had been standing in the far corner of the room talking to eva and was now reduced to silence by his praises.']
reference: JACK HAD BEEN STANDING IN THE FAR CORNER OF THE ROOM TALKING TO EVA AND WAS NOW REDUCED TO SILENCE BY HIS PRAISES

transcript: [' Sir Kennington Oval is a very fine player, said my wife.']
reference: SIR KENNINGTON OVAL IS A VERY FINE PLAYER SAID MY WIFE

transcript: [' I and my wife and son and the two cresswellers and three or four others agreed to dine on board the ship on the next.']
reference: I AND MY WIFE AND SON AND THE TWO CRASWELLERS AND THREE OR FOUR OTHERS AGREED TO DINE ON BOARD THE SHIP ON THE NEXT

transcript: [' This I felt was paid to me as being president of the republic, and I endeavored to behave myself with such mingled humility and dignity as might befit the occasion, but I could not but feel that something was wanting to the simplicity of my ordinary life.']
reference: THIS I FELT WAS PAID TO ME AS BEING PRESIDENT OF THE REPUBLIC AND I ENDEAVOURED TO BEHAVE MYSELF WITH SUCH MINGLED HUMILITY AND DIGNITY AS MIGHT BEFIT THE OCCASION BUT I COULD NOT BUT FEEL THAT SOMETHING WAS WANTING TO THE SIMPLICITY OF MY ORDINARY LIFE

transcript: [' My wife, on the spur of the moment, managed to give the gentlemen a very good dinner.']
reference: MY WIFE ON THE SPUR OF THE MOMENT MANAGED TO GIVE THE GENTLEMEN A VERY GOOD DINNER

transcript: [' This she said was true hospitality, and I am not sure that I did not agree with her.']
reference: THIS SHE SAID WAS TRUE HOSPITALITY AND I AM NOT SURE THAT I DID NOT AGREE WITH HER

transcript: [' Then there were three or four leading men of the community with their wives who were for the most part the fathers and mothers of the young ladies.']
reference: THEN THERE WERE THREE OR FOUR LEADING MEN OF THE COMMUNITY WITH THEIR WIVES WHO WERE FOR THE MOST PART THE FATHERS AND MOTHERS OF THE YOUNG LADIES

transcript: [" Oh yes, said Jack, and I'm nowhere."]
reference: OH YES SAID JACK AND I'M NOWHERE

transcript: [' But I mean to have my innings before long.']
reference: BUT I MEAN TO HAVE MY INNINGS BEFORE LONG

transcript: [' Of what missus neverbend had gone through in providing birds beasts and fishes not to talk of tarts and jellies for the dinner of that day no one but myself can have any idea, but it must be admitted that she accomplished her task with thorough success.']
reference: OF WHAT MISSUS NEVERBEND HAD GONE THROUGH IN PROVIDING BIRDS BEASTS AND FISHES NOT TO TALK OF TARTS AND JELLIES FOR THE DINNER OF THAT DAY NO ONE BUT MYSELF CAN HAVE ANY IDEA BUT IT MUST BE ADMITTED THAT SHE ACCOMPLISHED HER TASK WITH THOROUGH SUCCESS

transcript: [' We sat with the officers some little time after dinner and then went ashore.']
reference: WE SAT WITH THE OFFICERS SOME LITTLE TIME AFTER DINNER AND THEN WENT ASHORE

transcript: [' How much of evil, of real accomplished evil, had there not occurred to me during the last few days?']
reference: HOW MUCH OF EVIL OF REAL ACCOMPLISHED EVIL HAD THERE NOT OCCURRED TO ME DURING THE LAST FEW DAYS

transcript: [' What could I do now but just lay myself down and die?']
reference: WHAT COULD I DO NOW BUT JUST LAY MYSELF DOWN AND DIE

transcript: [' And the death of which I dreamt could not alas!']
reference: AND THE DEATH OF WHICH I DREAMT COULD NOT ALAS

transcript: [' When this captain should have taken himself and his vessel back to England, I would retire to a small farm which I possessed at the further side of the island, and there in seclusion would I end my days.']
reference: WHEN THIS CAPTAIN SHOULD HAVE TAKEN HIMSELF AND HIS VESSEL BACK TO ENGLAND I WOULD RETIRE TO A SMALL FARM WHICH I POSSESSED AT THE FARTHEST SIDE OF THE ISLAND AND THERE IN SECLUSION WOULD I END MY DAYS

transcript: [" Jack would become eva's happy husband and would remain amidst the hurried duties of the eager world."]
reference: JACK WOULD BECOME EVA'S HAPPY HUSBAND AND WOULD REMAIN AMIDST THE HURRIED DUTIES OF THE EAGER WORLD

transcript: [' Thinking of all this, I went to sleep.']
reference: THINKING OF ALL THIS I WENT TO SLEEP

transcript: [' mister neverbend began the captain and I observed that up to that moment he had generally addressed me as president it cannot be denied that we have come here on an unpleasant mission']
reference: MISTER NEVERBEND BEGAN THE CAPTAIN AND I OBSERVED THAT UP TO THAT MOMENT HE HAD GENERALLY ADDRESSED ME AS PRESIDENT IT CANNOT BE DENIED THAT WE HAVE COME HERE ON AN UNPLEASANT MISSION

transcript: [' You have received us with all that courtesy and hospitality for which your character in England stands so high.']
reference: YOU HAVE RECEIVED US WITH ALL THAT COURTESY AND HOSPITALITY FOR WHICH YOUR CHARACTER IN ENGLAND STANDS SO HIGH

transcript: [' It is a duty, said i.']
reference: IT IS A DUTY SAID I

transcript: [' But your power is so superior to any that I can advance as to make us here feel that there is no disgrace in yielding to it.']
reference: BUT YOUR POWER IS SO SUPERIOR TO ANY THAT I CAN ADVANCE AS TO MAKE US HERE FEEL THAT THERE IS NO DISGRACE IN YIELDING TO IT

transcript: [' not a doubt but had your force been only double or treble our own I should have found it my duty to struggle with you']
reference: NOT A DOUBT BUT HAD YOUR FORCE BEEN ONLY DOUBLE OR TREBLE OUR OWN I SHOULD HAVE FOUND IT MY DUTY TO STRUGGLE WITH YOU

transcript: [' That is all quite true, mister Neverbend, said Sir Ferdinando Brown.']
reference: THAT IS ALL QUITE TRUE MISTER NEVERBEND SAID SIR FERDINANDO BROWN

transcript: [' I can afford to smile because I am absolutely powerless before you, but I do not the less feel that in a matter in which the progress of the world is concerned, I or rather we have been put down by brute force.']
reference: I CAN AFFORD TO SMILE BECAUSE I AM ABSOLUTELY POWERLESS BEFORE YOU BUT I DO NOT THE LESS FEEL THAT IN A MATTER IN WHICH THE PROGRESS OF THE WORLD IS CONCERNED I OR RATHER WE HAVE BEEN PUT DOWN BY BRUTE FORCE

transcript: [' You have come to us threatening us with absolute destruction.']
reference: YOU HAVE COME TO US THREATENING US WITH ABSOLUTE DESTRUCTION

transcript: [' Therefore, I feel myself quite able as president of this republic to receive you with the courtesy due to the servants of a friendly ally.']
reference: THEREFORE I FEEL MYSELF QUITE ABLE AS PRESIDENT OF THIS REPUBLIC TO RECEIVE YOU WITH A COURTESY DUE TO THE SERVANTS OF A FRIENDLY ALLY

transcript: [' I can assure you he has not even allowed me to see the trigger since I have been on board.']
reference: I CAN ASSURE YOU HE HAS NOT EVEN ALLOWED ME TO SEE THE TRIGGER SINCE I HAVE BEEN ON BOARD

transcript: [' Then said Sir Ferdinando there is nothing for it but that we must take you with him.']
reference: THEN SAID SIR FERDINANDO THERE IS NOTHING FOR IT BUT THAT HE MUST TAKE YOU WITH HIM

transcript: [' There came upon me a sudden shock when I heard these words, which exceeded anything which I had yet felt.']
reference: THERE CAME UPON ME A SUDDEN SHOCK WHEN I HEARD THESE WORDS WHICH EXCEEDED ANYTHING WHICH I HAD YET FELT

transcript: [' You hear what Sir Ferdinando Browne has said replied Captain Battleaxe.']
reference: YOU HEAR WHAT SIR FERDINANDO BROWN HAS SAID REPLIED CAPTAIN BATTLEAX

transcript: [' But what is the delicate mission? I asked.']
reference: BUT WHAT IS THE DELICATE MISSION I ASKED

transcript: [' I was to be taken away and carried to England or elsewhere or drowned upon the voyage it mattered not which.']
reference: I WAS TO BE TAKEN AWAY AND CARRIED TO ENGLAND OR ELSEWHERE OR DROWNED UPON THE VOYAGE IT MATTERED NOT WHICH

transcript: [' Then the republic of Britannula was to be declared as non existent and the british flag was to be exalted and a british governor installed in the executive chambers.']
reference: THEN THE REPUBLIC OF BRITANNULA WAS TO BE DECLARED AS NON EXISTENT AND THE BRITISH FLAG WAS TO BE EXALTED AND A BRITISH GOVERNOR INSTALLED IN THE EXECUTIVE CHAMBERS

transcript: [" You may be quite sure it's there said captain battle axe and that I can so use it as to half obliterate your town within two minutes of my return on board."]
reference: YOU MAY BE QUITE SURE IT'S THERE SAID CAPTAIN BATTLEAX AND THAT I CAN SO USE IT AS TO HALF OBLITERATE YOUR TOWN WITHIN TWO MINUTES OF MY RETURN ON BOARD

transcript: [' You propose to kidnap me, I said.']
reference: YOU PROPOSE TO KIDNAP ME I SAID

transcript: [' What would become of your gun were I to kidnap you?']
reference: WHAT WOULD BECOME OF YOUR GUN WERE I TO KIDNAP YOU

transcript: [' Lieutenant Crosstrees is a very gallant officer.']
reference: LIEUTENANT CROSSTREES IS A VERY GALLANT OFFICER

transcript: [' One of us always remains on board while the other is on shore.']
reference: ONE OF US ALWAYS REMAINS ON BOARD WHILE THE OTHER IS ON SHORE

transcript: [' What world wide iniquity such a speech as that discloses said I still turning myself to the captain for though I would have crushed them both by my words had it been possible my dislike centred itself on Sir Ferdinando']
reference: WHAT WORLD WIDE INIQUITY SUCH A SPEECH AS THAT DISCLOSES SAID I STILL TURNING MYSELF TO THE CAPTAIN FOR THOUGH I WOULD HAVE CRUSHED THEM BOTH BY MY WORDS HAD IT BEEN POSSIBLE MY DISLIKE CENTRED ITSELF ON SIR FERDINANDO

transcript: [' You will allow me to suggest said he that that is a matter of opinion.']
reference: YOU WILL ALLOW ME TO SUGGEST SAID HE THAT THAT IS A MATTER OF OPINION

transcript: [' Were I to comply with your orders without expressing my own opinion, I should seem to have done so willingly hereafter.']
reference: WERE I TO COMPLY WITH YOUR ORDERS WITHOUT EXPRESSING MY OWN OPINION I SHOULD SEEM TO HAVE DONE SO WILLINGLY HEREAFTER

transcript: [' The letter ran as follows.']
reference: THE LETTER RAN AS FOLLOWS

transcript: [' When the blueskins saw ghip ghisizzle they raised another great shout for he was the favorite of the soldiers and very popular with all the people.']
reference: WHEN THE BLUESKINS SAW GHIP GHISIZZLE THEY RAISED ANOTHER GREAT SHOUT FOR HE WAS THE FAVORITE OF THE SOLDIERS AND VERY POPULAR WITH ALL THE PEOPLE

transcript: [' Since last Thursday, I, Ghip ghisizzle, have been the lawful boolooroo of the blue country. But now that you are conquered by Queen Trot, I suppose I am conquered too, and you have no boolooroo at all.']
reference: SINCE LAST THURSDAY I GHIP GHISIZZLE HAVE BEEN THE LAWFUL BOOLOOROO OF THE BLUE COUNTRY BUT NOW THAT YOU ARE CONQUERED BY QUEEN TROT I SUPPOSE I AM CONQUERED TOO AND YOU HAVE NO BOOLOOROO AT ALL

transcript: [' When he finished she said cheerfully']
reference: WHEN HE FINISHED SHE SAID CHEERFULLY

transcript: [" don't worry sizzle dear it'll all come right pretty soon"]
reference: DON'T WORRY SIZZLE DEAR IT'LL ALL COME RIGHT PRETTY SOON

transcript: [" Now then, let's enter the city and enjoy the great feast that's being cooked. I'm nearly starved myself, for this conquering kingdoms is hard work."]
reference: NOW THEN LET'S ENTER THE CITY AN ENJOY THE GRAND FEAST THAT'S BEING COOKED I'M NEARLY STARVED MYSELF FOR THIS CONQUERIN KINGDOMS IS HARD WORK

transcript: [' Then she gave rosalie back her magic ring thanking the kind witch for all she had done for them.']
reference: THEN SHE GAVE ROSALIE BACK HER MAGIC RING THANKING THE KIND WITCH FOR ALL SHE HAD DONE FOR THEM

transcript: [' You are, mate, replied the sailor.']
reference: YOU ARE MATE REPLIED THE SAILOR

transcript: [' It will be such a satisfaction.']
reference: IT WILL BE SUCH A SATISFACTION

transcript: [' The guards had a terrible struggle with the goat which was loose in the room and still wanted to fight but finally they subdued the animal and then they took the boolooroo out of the frame he was tied in and brought both him and the goat before queen trot who awaited them in the throne room of the palace.']
reference: THE GUARDS HAD A TERRIBLE STRUGGLE WITH THE GOAT WHICH WAS LOOSE IN THE ROOM AND STILL WANTED TO FIGHT BUT FINALLY THEY SUBDUED THE ANIMAL AND THEN THEY TOOK THE BOOLOOROO OUT OF THE FRAME HE WAS TIED IN AND BROUGHT BOTH HIM AND THE GOAT BEFORE QUEEN TROT WHO AWAITED THEM IN THE THRONE ROOM OF THE PALACE

transcript: [" I'll gladly do that promised the new boolooroo and I'll feed the honorable goat all the shavings and leather and tin cans he can eat besides the grass"]
reference: I'LL GLADLY DO THAT PROMISED THE NEW BOOLOOROO AND I'LL FEED THE HONORABLE GOAT ALL THE SHAVINGS AND LEATHER AND TIN CANS HE CAN EAT BESIDES THE GRASS

transcript: [" Excuse me, said trot. I neglected to tell you that you're not the Boolooroo any more."]
reference: SCUSE ME SAID TROT I NEGLECTED TO TELL YOU THAT YOU'RE NOT THE BOOLOOROO ANY MORE

transcript: [' The former Boolooroo groaned.']
reference: THE FORMER BOOLOOROO GROANED

transcript: [" I'll not be wicked any more, sighed the old Boolooroo. I'll reform."]
reference: I'LL NOT BE WICKED ANY MORE SIGHED THE OLD BOOLOOROO I'LL REFORM

transcript: [' As a private citizen I shall be a model of deportment because it would be dangerous to be otherwise.']
reference: AS A PRIVATE CITIZEN I SHALL BE A MODEL OF DEPORTMENT BECAUSE IT WOULD BE DANGEROUS TO BE OTHERWISE

transcript: [' When first they entered the throne room they tried to be as haughty and scornful as ever, but the blues who were assembled there all laughed at them and jeered at them, for there was not a single person in all the blue country who loved the princesses the least little bit.']
reference: WHEN FIRST THEY ENTERED THE THRONE ROOM THEY TRIED TO BE AS HAUGHTY AND SCORNFUL AS EVER BUT THE BLUES WHO WERE ASSEMBLED THERE ALL LAUGHED AT THEM AND JEERED THEM FOR THERE WAS NOT A SINGLE PERSON IN ALL THE BLUE COUNTRY WHO LOVED THE PRINCESSES THE LEAST LITTLE BIT

transcript: [' So Ghip ghisizzle ordered the captain to take a file of soldiers and escort the raving beauties to their new home.']
reference: SO GHIP GHISIZZLE ORDERED THE CAPTAIN TO TAKE A FILE OF SOLDIERS AND ESCORT THE RAVING BEAUTIES TO THEIR NEW HOME

transcript: [' That evening trot gave a grand ball in the palace to which the most important of the pinkies and blueskins were invited']
reference: THAT EVENING TROT GAVE A GRAND BALL IN THE PALACE TO WHICH THE MOST IMPORTANT OF THE PINKIES AND THE BLUESKINS WERE INVITED

transcript: [' The combined bands of both countries played the music and a fine supper was served.']
reference: THE COMBINED BANDS OF BOTH THE COUNTRIES PLAYED THE MUSIC AND A FINE SUPPER WAS SERVED

transcript: [" Brighter than early dawn's most brilliant dye are blown clear bands of color through the sky that swirl and sweep and meet to break and foam like rainbow veils upon a bubble's dome."]
reference: BRIGHTER THAN EARLY DAWN'S MOST BRILLIANT DYE ARE BLOWN CLEAR BANDS OF COLOR THROUGH THE SKY THAT SWIRL AND SWEEP AND MEET TO BREAK AND FOAM LIKE RAINBOW VEILS UPON A BUBBLE'S DOME

transcript: [" Guided by you, how we might stroll towards death, our only music one another's breath, through gardens intimate with hollyhocks where silent poppies burn between the rocks, by pools where birches bend to confidants above green waters scummed with lily plants."]
reference: GUIDED BY YOU HOW WE MIGHT STROLL TOWARDS DEATH OUR ONLY MUSIC ONE ANOTHER'S BREATH THROUGH GARDENS INTIMATE WITH HOLLYHOCKS WHERE SILENT POPPIES BURN BETWEEN THE ROCKS BY POOLS WHERE BIRCHES BEND TO CONFIDANTS ABOVE GREEN WATERS SCUMMED WITH LILY PLANTS

transcript: [' Venice']
reference: VENICE

transcript: [" In a sunset glowing of crimson and gold she lies the glory of the world a beached king's galley whose sails are furled who is hung with tapestries rich and old."]
reference: IN A SUNSET GLOWING OF CRIMSON AND GOLD SHE LIES THE GLORY OF THE WORLD A BEACHED KING'S GALLEY WHOSE SAILS ARE FURLED WHO IS HUNG WITH TAPESTRIES RICH AND OLD

transcript: [' The pity that we must come and go.']
reference: THE PITY THAT WE MUST COME AND GO

transcript: [' While the old gold in the marble stays, for ever gleaming its soft strong blaze calm in the early evening glow.']
reference: WHILE THE OLD GOLD AND THE MARBLE STAYS FOREVER GLEAMING ITS SOFT STRONG BLAZE CALM IN THE EARLY EVENING GLOW

transcript: [' The pleasant graveyard of my soul with sentimental cypress-trees and flowers is filled that i may stroll in meditation at my ease']
reference: THE PLEASANT GRAVEYARD OF MY SOUL WITH SENTIMENTAL CYPRESS TREES AND FLOWERS IS FILLED THAT I MAY STROLL IN MEDITATION AT MY EASE

transcript: [' It is my heart hung in the sky and no clouds ever float between the grave flowers and my heart on high.']
reference: IT IS MY HEART HUNG IN THE SKY AND NO CLOUDS EVER FLOAT BETWEEN THE GRAVE FLOWERS AND MY HEART ON HIGH

transcript: [' Over the track lined city street the young men, the grinning men, pass.']
reference: OVER THE TRACK LINED CITY STREET THE YOUNG MEN THE GRINNING MEN PASS

transcript: [' Ho ye sails that seem to wander in dream filled meadows say is the shore where I stand the only field of struggle or are ye hit and battered out there by waves and wind gusts as ye tack over a clashing sea of watery echoes?']
reference: HO YE SAILS THAT SEEM TO WANDER IN DREAM FILLED MEADOWS SAY IS THE SHORE WHERE I STAND THE ONLY FIELD OF STRUGGLE OR ARE YE HIT AND BATTERED OUT THERE BY WAVES AND WIND GUSTS AS YE TACK OVER A CLASHING SEA OF WATERY ECHOES

transcript: [' Old dances are simplified of their yearning, bleached by time.']
reference: OLD DANCES ARE SIMPLIFIED OF THEIR YEARNING BLEACHED BY TIME

transcript: [' He had got into her courtyard.']
reference: HE HAD GOT INTO HER COURTYARD

transcript: [' Through the black night rain he sang to her window bars.']
reference: THROUGH THE BLACK NIGHT RAIN HE SANG TO HER WINDOW BARS

transcript: [' That was but rustling of dripping plants in the dark.']
reference: THAT WAS BUT RUSTLING OF DRIPPING PLANTS IN THE DARK

transcript: [' She was alone that night.']
reference: SHE WAS ALONE THAT NIGHT

transcript: [' He had broken into her courtyard.']
reference: HE HAD BROKEN INTO HER COURTYARD

transcript: [' To fade away like morning beauty from her mortal day down by the river of Adana her soft voice is heard and thus her gentle lamentation falls like morning dew']
reference: TO FADE AWAY LIKE MORNING BEAUTY FROM HER MORTAL DAY DOWN BY THE RIVER OF ADONA HER SOFT VOICE IS HEARD AND THUS HER GENTLE LAMENTATION FALLS LIKE MORNING DEW

transcript: [' O life of this our spring,']
reference: O LIFE OF THIS OUR SPRING

transcript: [' Why fades the lotus of the water?']
reference: WHY FADES THE LOTUS OF THE WATER

transcript: [' Why fade these children of the spring']
reference: WHY FADE THESE CHILDREN OF THE SPRING

transcript: [" Thel is like a watery bow and like a parting cloud like a reflection in a glass like shadows in the water like dreams of infants like a smile upon an infant's face"]
reference: THEL IS LIKE A WATRY BOW AND LIKE A PARTING CLOUD LIKE A REFLECTION IN A GLASS LIKE SHADOWS IN THE WATER LIKE DREAMS OF INFANTS LIKE A SMILE UPON AN INFANTS FACE

transcript: [" Like the dove's voice, like transient day, like music in the air. Ah!"]
reference: LIKE THE DOVES VOICE LIKE TRANSIENT DAY LIKE MUSIC IN THE AIR AH

transcript: [' And gentle sleep the sleep of death and gently hear the voice of him that walketh in the garden in the evening time.']
reference: AND GENTLE SLEEP THE SLEEP OF DEATH AND GENTLY HEAR THE VOICE OF HIM THAT WALKETH IN THE GARDEN IN THE EVENING TIME

transcript: [' The lily of the valley breathing in the humble grass answered the lovely maid and said I am a watery weed and I am very small and love to dwell in lowly vales so weak the gilded butterfly scarce perches on my head yet I am visited from heaven and he that smiles on all walks in the valley and each morn over me spreads his hand saying rejoice thou humble grass thou new born lily flower']
reference: THE LILLY OF THE VALLEY BREATHING IN THE HUMBLE GRASS ANSWERD THE LOVELY MAID AND SAID I AM A WATRY WEED AND I AM VERY SMALL AND LOVE TO DWELL IN LOWLY VALES SO WEAK THE GILDED BUTTERFLY SCARCE PERCHES ON MY HEAD YET I AM VISITED FROM HEAVEN AND HE THAT SMILES ON ALL WALKS IN THE VALLEY AND EACH MORN OVER ME SPREADS HIS HAND SAYING REJOICE THOU HUMBLE GRASS THOU NEW BORN LILY FLOWER

transcript: [" thou gentle maid of silent valleys and of modest brooks, for thou shalt be clothed in light and fed with morning manna till summer's heat melts thee beside the fountains and the springs to flourish in eternal vales. They, why shouldst thou complain?"]
reference: THOU GENTLE MAID OF SILENT VALLEYS AND OF MODEST BROOKS FOR THOU SHALL BE CLOTHED IN LIGHT AND FED WITH MORNING MANNA TILL SUMMERS HEAT MELTS THEE BESIDE THE FOUNTAINS AND THE SPRINGS TO FLOURISH IN ETERNAL VALES THEY WHY SHOULD THEL COMPLAIN

transcript: [' Why should the mistress of the vales of Har utter a sigh?']
reference: WHY SHOULD THE MISTRESS OF THE VALES OF HAR UTTER A SIGH

transcript: [' She ceased and smiled in tears then sat down in her silver shrine']
reference: SHE CEASD AND SMILD IN TEARS THEN SAT DOWN IN HER SILVER SHRINE

transcript: [' Which thou dost scatter on every little blade of grass that springs revives the milked cow and tames the fire breathing steed']
reference: WHICH THOU DOST SCATTER ON EVERY LITTLE BLADE OF GRASS THAT SPRINGS REVIVES THE MILKED COW AND TAMES THE FIRE BREATHING STEED

transcript: [' But vel is like a faint cloud kindled at the rising sun I vanish from my pearly throne and who shall find my place?']
reference: BUT THEL IS LIKE A FAINT CLOUD KINDLED AT THE RISING SUN I VANISH FROM MY PEARLY THRONE AND WHO SHALL FIND MY PLACE

transcript: [' And why it scatters its bright beauty through the humid air.']
reference: AND WHY IT SCATTERS ITS BRIGHT BEAUTY THRO THE HUMID AIR

transcript: [' Descend o little cloud and hover before the eyes of Thel.']
reference: DESCEND O LITTLE CLOUD AND HOVER BEFORE THE EYES OF THEL

transcript: [' O little cloud the virgin said, I charge thee to tell me why thou complainest now when in one hour thou fade away then we shall seek thee but not find. Ah, fell is like to thee.']
reference: O LITTLE CLOUD THE VIRGIN SAID I CHARGE THEE TO TELL ME WHY THOU COMPLAINEST NOW WHEN IN ONE HOUR THOU FADE AWAY THEN WE SHALL SEEK THEE BUT NOT FIND AH THEL IS LIKE TO THEE

transcript: [' I pass away, yet I complain, and no one hears my voice.']
reference: I PASS AWAY YET I COMPLAIN AND NO ONE HEARS MY VOICE

transcript: [' The cloud then showed his golden head and his bright form emerged.']
reference: THE CLOUD THEN SHEWD HIS GOLDEN HEAD AND HIS BRIGHT FORM EMERG'D

transcript: [' And fearest thou because I vanish and am seen no more?']
reference: AND FEAREST THOU BECAUSE I VANISH AND AM SEEN NO MORE

transcript: [' It is to tenfold life, to love, to peace, and raptures holy. Unseen descending, weigh my light wings upon balmy flowers, and court the fair eyed dew to take me to her shining tent. The weeping virgin trembling kneels before the risen sun.']
reference: IT IS TO TENFOLD LIFE TO LOVE TO PEACE AND RAPTURES HOLY UNSEEN DESCENDING WEIGH MY LIGHT WINGS UPON BALMY FLOWERS AND COURT THE FAIR EYED DEW TO TAKE ME TO HER SHINING TENT THE WEEPING VIRGIN TREMBLING KNEELS BEFORE THE RISEN SUN

transcript: [' Till we arise linked in a golden band and never part but walk united bearing food to all our tender flowers']
reference: TILL WE ARISE LINK'D IN A GOLDEN BAND AND NEVER PART BUT WALK UNITED BEARING FOOD TO ALL OUR TENDER FLOWERS

transcript: [' Lives not alone nor of itself. Fear not and I will call the weak worm from its lowly bed and thou shalt hear its voice.']
reference: LIVES NOT ALONE NOR OR ITSELF FEAR NOT AND I WILL CALL THE WEAK WORM FROM ITS LOWLY BED AND THOU SHALT HEAR ITS VOICE

transcript: [' Come forth worm and the silent valley to thy pensive queen.']
reference: COME FORTH WORM AND THE SILENT VALLEY TO THY PENSIVE QUEEN

transcript: [" The helpless worm arose and sat upon the lily's leaf and the bright cloud sailed on to find his partner in the vale."]
reference: THE HELPLESS WORM AROSE AND SAT UPON THE LILLYS LEAF AND THE BRIGHT CLOUD SAILD ON TO FIND HIS PARTNER IN THE VALE

transcript: [' Image of weakness, art thou but a worm?']
reference: IMAGE OF WEAKNESS ART THOU BUT A WORM

transcript: [" I see they lay helpless and naked weeping and none to answer none to cherish thee with mother's smiles."]
reference: I SEE THEY LAY HELPLESS AND NAKED WEEPING AND NONE TO ANSWER NONE TO CHERISH THEE WITH MOTHERS SMILES

transcript: [' and says, Thou mother of my children, I have loved thee, and I have given thee a crown that none can take away.']
reference: AND SAYS THOU MOTHER OF MY CHILDREN I HAVE LOVED THEE AND I HAVE GIVEN THEE A CROWN THAT NONE CAN TAKE AWAY

transcript: [' And lay me down in thy cold bed and leave my shining lot.']
reference: AND LAY ME DOWN IN THY COLD BED AND LEAVE MY SHINING LOT

transcript: [' Or an eye of gifts and graces showering fruits in coined gold.']
reference: OR AN EYE OF GIFTS AND GRACES SHOWRING FRUITS AND COINED GOLD

transcript: [" Why a tongue impress'd with honey from every wind?"]
reference: WHY A TONGUE IMPRESS'D WITH HONEY FROM EVERY WIND

transcript: [' Why an ear a whirlpool fierce to draw creations in?']
reference: WHY AN EAR A WHIRLPOOL FIERCE TO DRAW CREATIONS IN

transcript: [' All is said without a word.']
reference: ALL IS SAID WITHOUT A WORD

transcript: [' I sit beneath thy looks as children do in the noon sun with souls that tremble through their happy eyelids from an unavered yet prodigal inward joy.']
reference: I SIT BENEATH THY LOOKS AS CHILDREN DO IN THE NOON SUN WITH SOULS THAT TREMBLE THROUGH THEIR HAPPY EYELIDS FROM AN UNAVERRED YET PRODIGAL INWARD JOY

transcript: [' I did not wrong myself so, but I placed a wrong on thee.']
reference: I DID NOT WRONG MYSELF SO BUT I PLACED A WRONG ON THEE

transcript: [' When called before, I told how hastily I dropt my flowers or break off from a game.']
reference: WHEN CALLED BEFORE I TOLD HOW HASTILY I DROPPED MY FLOWERS OR BRAKE OFF FROM A GAME

transcript: [' Shall I never miss home talk and blessing and the common kiss that comes to each in turn nor count it strange when I look up to drop on a new range of walls and floors another home than this']
reference: SHALL I NEVER MISS HOME TALK AND BLESSING AND THE COMMON KISS THAT COMES TO EACH IN TURN NOR COUNT IT STRANGE WHEN I LOOK UP TO DROP ON A NEW RANGE OF WALLS AND FLOORS ANOTHER HOME THAN THIS

transcript: [' Alas, I have grieved so I am hard to love.']
reference: ALAS I HAVE GRIEVED SO I AM HARD TO LOVE

transcript: [' Open thy heart wide and fold within the wet wings of thy dove.']
reference: OPEN THY HEART WIDE AND FOLD WITHIN THE WET WINGS OF THY DOVE

transcript: [' Could it mean to last a love set pendulous between sorrow and sorrow?']
reference: COULD IT MEAN TO LAST A LOVE SET PENDULOUS BETWEEN SORROW AND SORROW

transcript: [' Nay, I rather thrilled distrusting every light that seemed to gild the onward path and feared to overlean a finger even.']
reference: NAY I RATHER THRILLED DISTRUSTING EVERY LIGHT THAT SEEMED TO GILD THE ONWARD PATH AND FEARED TO OVERLEAN A FINGER EVEN

transcript: [' And though I have grown serene and strong since then, I think that God has willed a still renewable fear.']
reference: AND THOUGH I HAVE GROWN SERENE AND STRONG SINCE THEN I THINK THAT GOD HAS WILLED A STILL RENEWABLE FEAR

transcript: [' O love, O troth,']
reference: O LOVE O TROTH

transcript: [' And love be false.']
reference: AND LOVE BE FALSE

transcript: [" If he to keep one oath must lose one joy by his life's star foretold."]
reference: IF HE TO KEEP ONE OATH MUST LOSE ONE JOY BY HIS LIFE'S STAR FORETOLD

transcript: [' slow to world greetings, quick with its o list when the angels speak.']
reference: SLOW TO WORLD GREETINGS QUICK WITH ITS O LIST WHEN THE ANGELS SPEAK

transcript: [' A ring of amethyst I could not wear here plainer to my sight than that first kiss.']
reference: A RING OF AMETHYST I COULD NOT WEAR HERE PLAINER TO MY SIGHT THAN THAT FIRST KISS

transcript: [" That was the chrism of love which love's own crown with sanctifying sweetness did proceed the third upon my lips was folded down in perfect purple state since when indeed I have been proud and said my love my own"]
reference: THAT WAS THE CHRISM OF LOVE WHICH LOVE'S OWN CROWN WITH SANCTIFYING SWEETNESS DID PRECEDE THE THIRD UPON MY LIPS WAS FOLDED DOWN IN PERFECT PURPLE STATE SINCE WHEN INDEED I HAVE BEEN PROUD AND SAID MY LOVE MY OWN

transcript: [' Dearest, teach me so to pour out gratitude as thou dost good.']
reference: DEAREST TEACH ME SO TO POUR OUT GRATITUDE AS THOU DOST GOOD

transcript: [' Mussulmans and giaours throw kerchiefs at a smile and have no ruth for any weeping.']
reference: MUSSULMANS AND GIAOURS THROW KERCHIEFS AT A SMILE AND HAVE NO RUTH FOR ANY WEEPING

transcript: [' But thou art not such a lover, my beloved.']
reference: BUT THOU ART NOT SUCH A LOVER MY BELOVED

transcript: [' Thou canst wait through sorrow and sickness to bring souls to touch and think it soon when others cry too late.']
reference: THOU CANST WAIT THROUGH SORROW AND SICKNESS TO BRING SOULS TO TOUCH AND THINK IT SOON WHEN OTHERS CRY TOO LATE

transcript: [' I thank all who have loved me in their hearts with thanks and love from mine.']
reference: I THANK ALL WHO HAVE LOVED ME IN THEIR HEARTS WITH THANKS AND LOVE FROM MINE

transcript: [" Oh to shoot my soul's full meaning into future years that they should lend it utterance and salute love that endures from life that disappears!"]
reference: OH TO SHOOT MY SOUL'S FULL MEANING INTO FUTURE YEARS THAT THEY SHOULD LEND IT UTTERANCE AND SALUTE LOVE THAT ENDURES FROM LIFE THAT DISAPPEARS

transcript: [" Then I long tried by natural ills received the comfort fast while budding at thy sight my pilgrim's staff gave out green leaves with morning dews impearled."]
reference: THEN I LONG TRIED BY NATURAL ILLS RECEIVED THE COMFORT FAST WHILE BUDDING AT THY SIGHT MY PILGRIM'S STAFF GAVE OUT GREEN LEAVES WITH MORNING DEWS IMPEARLED

transcript: [' I love thee freely as men strive for right. I love thee purely as they turn from praise.']
reference: I LOVE THEE FREELY AS MEN STRIVE FOR RIGHT I LOVE THEE PURELY AS THEY TURN FROM PRAISE

transcript: [" I love thee with the passion put to use and my old griefs and with my childhood's faith."]
reference: I LOVE THEE WITH THE PASSION PUT TO USE IN MY OLD GRIEFS AND WITH MY CHILDHOOD'S FAITH

transcript: [' I love thee with a love I seemed to lose with my lost saints. I love thee with the breath, smiles, tears of all my life, and if God choose, I shall but love thee better after death.']
reference: I LOVE THEE WITH A LOVE I SEEMED TO LOSE WITH MY LOST SAINTS I LOVE THEE WITH THE BREATH SMILES TEARS OF ALL MY LIFE AND IF GOD CHOOSE I SHALL BUT LOVE THEE BETTER AFTER DEATH
